```
application.
```

```
One of the original members of the COBOL Committee was an English major.
```

```
In article <oFVp4.2503$Ez.59564@news2.mia>, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>Jerry P wrote:
>>
>>If you want to be a professional
>>programmer or system analyst or programming manager or
vice-president
>>or entrepreneur, take all the mathematics (at least through
>>differential equations) and business courses you can find.
>
>While I agree with the general thrust of your post, and my own
formal
>educational background is in physics and math, and I am for
learning
>as much as possible; other than algebra and statistics, I have
never
>had the need for higher math in business programming.  I have
used
>calculus on some personal projects, but never for business.  In
the
>sciences, yes.  But business?  I would be interested in hearing
what
>needs others may have encountered in business programming for
math
>beyond algebra and statistics?
>--
>Judson McClendon      judmc@bellsouth.net
>Sun Valley Systems    http://www.sunvaley.com
>"For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son,
that
>whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting
life."
>
I once worked for a financial organization that bought a
collection of economic data from a research house.  In order to
performs transformations or look for various kinds of
relationships, I contributed a COBOL logarithm to my supervisor's
program.

As for math beyond algebra and statistics, it is my point of view
that, although you may USE statistics with only a background in
algebra, in order to UNDERSTAND statistics (pdf's etc.) you need
three semesters of calculus.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** brian2048@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89ifin$3u8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <38AAA4D7.4E4F5AA8@bellsouth.net>`

```
The professors were telling us that COBOL was dead in 1984 when I did my
undergraduate work in CS.  I've been working with it ever since then.
Now, much of our work involves putting Java and VB front ends on the
existing COBOL systems.

   -B Sullivan

In article <38AAA4D7.4E4F5AA8@bellsouth.net>,
  Tim Hopper <hoppertr@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> I just finishe a master's program in CIS.  They teach advanced Cobol
at the
> university and I took it.  I graduated undergraduate in 1983 and had
the
> standard two courses is Cobol 74, but have worked as  a machinist
since.
> After being layed off and fired several times over the years, I
decided to
> go back to graduate school and get a masters.  I fini8shed Dec. 1999.
> Beside the numerrous graduate research papers required in the
curriculum,
> the Cobol course required a term paper for completetion.  The subject
I
> chose was the future of Cobol and OOCobol.  It seems as if you asked
an
> "expert" five years ago if Cobol would be dead today, they would have
said
> yes.  But not today (judging from the articles I read).  Cobol seems
to have
> a future, and it did seem that not many universitites (I in typing
> withdrawal) are teaching it anymore.  This worried me as I approached
> graduation that I was learning legacies again, but most of the
articles
> confirmed that Cobol is indeed alive and well, and on tap for object
> orientation.  I sure hope so.  Since I graduated I have been teaching
myself
> C++, Visual Basic and Java,  But I still love Cobol.  I'm still in
shock at
> being able to use my home computer to program these languages at home,
but
> it sure beats having to wait for a terminal at the university
> lab(1981-1982)!!!!!!!
```

```
Ed Guy wrote:

> Howard Brazee wrote:
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> decided they want to be called "Nations".  I read of one that has only one member.

They do here as well, and historically they WERE self governing bodies.  They could just as
easily have called themselves countries.  Before the U.S. was formed, the word "State" did
not mean a province or a commonwealth the way it does here.  While it has kept its old
meaning, its new meaning is similar to a British "nation".  Or at least it was two centuries
ago...


> Words change their meanings more quickly these days than they used to.

Everything changes more quickly - but words changed their meanings often enough in the past
that fogies such as myself were complaining in any century you care to choose.
```

```

Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:EA1FWCAFMmv4EwBE@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...
> In article <38BC2537.97537E43@cusys.edu>, Howard Brazee
> <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> writes
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >I always thought country and nation were synonyms.  I guess for most
places they
> >are.  I
> >would have guessed that your nation was the UK because I think of
national flags
> >as
> >opposed to country flags.
> The national flag that you are refering to is the Union Jack and I
> understand from a friend that it is not the official flag for the UK.
> Rather, it originated as a naval flag and has come into common usage.
<snip>

> Alistair Maclean
>

Almost correct.  In fact it is officially the Union Flag (the flag of the
union).  It should only be called the Union Jack when flown from the jack
staff of a ship.  So it's the name that is naval not the flag.  However I
must admit that most of us British (well me) tend to call it the Union Jack
unless we're being pedantic (or talking to Yanks).

As far as I can remember from my history lessons, the original Union Flag,
made up of the English and Scottish Flags originated c1604 when the Scottish
crown took over (combined with) England.  This would have been the Flag of
the combined American Colonies pre the revolution.  The current flag dates
from c1804 when the Irish cross of St. Patrick was added.

Just goes to show that good design doesn't have to be planned.  Whoops!
dangerously on topic.

BTW, totally OT.  Something I've often wondered (but not enough to look it
up); did our Civil War have any affect on the American Colonies?  Were there
royalist colonies and parliamentarian colonies?  Were any battles fought on
American soil?  Or were they still so new that they just worried about their
own survival.

Rick
```

- **References:** `<85so2t$e18$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38823B5F.77A3643@home.com> <86v1ad$5pg$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <389BAB33.3B61FB57@home.com> <QpZm4.119$as3.1500485@news.netdirect.net> <1C13CF69D33C81C9.810F4F83202A5A84.FB5B1267262EB3CA@lp.airnews.net> <389EE5B5.7BA9A12@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <D8E7E712ADE10346.09CC1815F73863D1.3BF06A83584171E5@lp.airnews.net> <87qpjt$208$1@news.igs.net> <38A17A89.D2A272C5@cusys.edu> <87rvu6$bn8m$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com> <AWxo4.50$n02.1692@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <87v2ra$jdo$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <tcGo4.903$n02.18719@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <881cta$q3m$1@news.igs.net> <38A51643.F5658A15@worldnet.att.net> <91ep4.104$ek5.5385@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38A64120.E399AE38@worldnet.att.net> <38A81971.8A20D018@cusys.edu> <38B89D5B.59ED34FE@home.com> <MPG.1326007d1cb779ca989917@news.mersinet.co.uk> <38BC2537.97537E43@cusys.edu>`

```
I noticed that Howard Brazee as howard.brazee@cusys.edu said...
> Charles F Hankel wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> other place where there is a distinction between these two words?

Without resorting to a dictionary, I'd say that nation is the people 
whereas country is where people live and may be little more than an 
administrative or political convenience.  Thus, the English, Welsh, Scots 
and the Cornish share the same island (Great Britain) and indeed move 
freely about the island with no obvious physical borders.  Similarly, the 
French, the Basques and the Bretons share the same country (France).  
Slightly differently, a country can be shared between nations that 
predominate in other countries; for example Belgium is inhabited by 
French, Dutch and Germans and yet there are indigenous Belgians who 
might be French, Dutch or German speakers.
```

```
I noticed that James J. Gavan as jjgavan@home.com said...
> 
> 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> wonder how many others picked up on it ?

I deliberately left out Ireland as my argument was based on the land mass 
of Great Britain.  If you want a similar scenario for Ireland there are 
at least two distinctive nations with waters muddied by association with 
particular churches.  It's always been a big question, the solution for 
which seems to have evaded everyone in power for the past couple of 
centuries or so.  Oh, and mentioning it is a bit like opening Pandora's 
box.  Anyway, if I brought in Ireland, then I'd have to include other 
places such as the Isle of Man.
```

```
territory of the British Crown.
```

```
Back in the sixties I was hitching through France and got a lift in a pretty
smart car.  I obviously talked to the driver and he turned out to be a local
Baron (according to him).   He emphasised that he was an English Baron not a
French one i.e. his family were originally Barons when the English kings
ruled that part of France.  Ain't history grand; and strange?
```

```
in the part of Frnace that is called Normandie.  I guess they just 
stopped off there for a bit of partying on their way to join their 
relatives in England.  In the Great Scheme Of Things, you understand.
```

```
Charles F Hankel wrote:
> 
> I noticed that Ed Guy as ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com said...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> relatives in England.  In the Great Scheme Of Things, you understand.

True, originally the Normans were the "Northmen".  However, when William led his forces 
in the conquest of England in 1066 he took a lot more than Vikings with him.

Two surname examples:  
Lamont - originally Lomond - were people who had moved from Scotland to France before 
returning with William as "Normans" to have a bash at the Anglo-Saxons.

Guy - started off in the area of Germany as "Wido" (means woodsmen).  Moved to France 
and morphed to "Guido", then "Guy".  Showed in up the Domesday Book entry for my old 
home village in England as having "two ploughs of land and the right to take 1500 eels a 
year from the river" (but we can only do a direct father-to-son trace since 1580 'cos 
we can't find the records in between).
```

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:
> 
> In article <38B89B1B.DDB8ABC@home.com>, "James J. Gavan"
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Sorry Alistair, I disagree and I now go back to my 12 year stint in the
RAF. I'm guessing, but I'll bet the original definition for 'Western
Oriental Gentleman' is a particularly military/imperial derisive term -
regardless of what you may have picked up from a dictionary. 

As former US Secretary of State Dean Rusk said, 'Britain carried the
seeds of the destruction of it's Empire in it's knapsack' - i.e. "We
teach these bloody locals to become British subjects, and then the
cheeky buggers turn around and expect British justice'. Where the WOG
derisive comes in is when the locals 'aped' their British betters and
took on British education and mannerisms, tea parties and cricket old
boy. The boys in khaki looked on them with contempt - stupid Western
Oriental Gentlemen. One specific phrase I recall, 'Honours degree
Bombay, Failed". 

And when you include the Arabic countries you get remembrances of filthy
little rowing boats pulling alongside a troopship and Ahmed giving a
toothsome grin, looks up at you and in his very best attempt at English,
says, "Queen Victoria, him very good man. You like f..... my sister ?"
And your mental response, "Bloody WOG".

No connotation of "Wise" here.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

PS: Just checked Oxford Concise - says 'Native of a Middle Eastern
country, especially Egypt'. So I think my reference to Ahmed is close.
```

```
Charles F Hankel wrote:
> 
> I noticed that James J. Gavan as jjgavan@home.com said...
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
And talking of of the unacceptable - remember Idi Amin and also that
Robertson's jams and marmalades used to have a golliwog trade-mark on
the labels. (For our American brethren, if you don't know what a
golliwog doll is - think of the minstrel shows in the late 1800s).

Story goes, Idi got uppity and made the decision to remove the Queen's
effigy from his stamps. Robertsons haughtily responded, "If that's the
case, we are going to remove your President's effigy from our jam jars.
So there !".

Mind you I think I'd be a bit cautious carrying a golliwog around in
Brixton or some less salubrious spots in Liverpool or Leeds :-)

PS: And being politically correct my Netscape spell-checker doesn't
include 'golliwog' :-)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Know of any Internships, Charities that need COBOL Prgmrs?

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38bd5e01.3849680@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<89gmh2$s3v$1@news.cerf.net> <20000229174329.21186.00000055@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
merhottin@aol.com (Merhottin) wrote:

>So how would you suggest I present myself, Cheesle?
>I would really like to know your working situation. Heres mine, I Just
>graduated, and am looking to get my foot in the door.  Think I should look for
>40K to start? Also wonder where you got that great name CHEESLE

If you submit your resume to him and it shows that you are an
outstanding programmer, perhaps Cheesle will offer you a job for 40K
NOK annually.  I think that it may even be possible to get a job for
as much as 50K NOK or 60K NOK annually!  You would, of course, be
responsible for the expense of your daily commute or should you decide
to move closer to Cheesle's offices, you would have to pay for all
moving expense.

How about it, Cheesle?  Do you think that he might be worth 40K NOK?
He didn't specify currency.

Regarding your prior comment about a "low paying COBOL position"... 

My company has a strong working relationship with several consulting
firms who all happily report that they always charge MORE for their
experienced COBOL people than they can for their VB; Powerbuilder;
Delphi people.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Know of any Internships, Charities that need COBOL Prgmrs?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89md0s$m8$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<89gmh2$s3v$1@news.cerf.net> <20000229174329.21186.00000055@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38bd5e01.3849680@news.epix.net>`

```
"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
news:38bd5e01.3849680@news.epix.net...
> merhottin@aol.com (Merhottin) wrote:
>
> >So how would you suggest I present myself, Cheesle?
> >I would really like to know your working situation. Heres mine, I Just
> >graduated, and am looking to get my foot in the door.  Think I should
look for
> >40K to start? Also wonder where you got that great name CHEESLE

To merhottin@aol.com:

I haven't seen your response, it does not show in my thread listing, that is
why I haven't responded to it previously.
The intention of my comment was to say that you exposed a negative attitude
for Cobol, and I just thought that I would probably not have hired you in a
Cobol position no matter salary because of the attitude. No offense
intended.

When it comes to what to start with, I really have no idea what should be
the ideal, I have seen Computerworld US offer statistics, you might want to
look up there.

About getting the foot inside the door, when I was in the position to hire
people, I was always looking for personal initiative, that is, anyone
contacting me without necessarily having seen an ad that we were looking for
people would be considered up front, just because of the initiative. It is
my experience that good papers from school and lots of experience may not be
as good as the right attitude.

I will give you one good advice:
Remember you are selling a product. The fact that the product is you should
not matter. Actually it is beneficial, because you are the only one offering
the product. Have belief in yourself. If you don't believe in you, who is
going to believe in you then. This is actually were I commented you, it is
all about attitude, with the wrong attitude you ain't going nowhere.

A short one about CV's.
Remember those doing the hireing are reading tons of resumes. To emphasize
one self from 500 others, you don't necessarily have to like an TV ad, just
simple. From my own experience, when I do the first evaluation I look for
the obvious, hence I am not interested in what compiler switch you used here
and there. The best CV / resume is the one that fits one page. Details are
for the following meeting.

Thanks for the comment on my nickname, it is a pretty long story behind that
one, I am most certain the NG will not appreciate that story  :-)

> outstanding programmer, perhaps Cheesle will offer you a job for 40K
> NOK annually.  I think that it may even be possible to get a job for

That would be a poor pay, 40K NOK is approximately 5K $US.

> How about it, Cheesle?  Do you think that he might be worth 40K NOK?

I am most positive that he will do good where ever he lands. I am
unfortunately not in the position to hire someone.

> My company has a strong working relationship with several consulting
> firms who all happily report that they always charge MORE for their
> experienced COBOL people than they can for their VB; Powerbuilder;
> Delphi people.

This makes sense, as Cobol developers rarely are youngsters, Delphi, VB at
least are crowded with youngsters that is willing to work for pennies to get
experienced.

Cheesle
```

```
is

>for Cobol, and I just thought that I would probably not have hired you in a
>Cobol position no matter salary because of the attitude. No offense

As a further comment to that, my experience has taught me that the best
people to hire are always the ones that love their work.  The second that I
hear somebody say "I do not like" in reference to the job I am hiring them
to do, a giant red flag goes up.  What I want to hear in an interview is
someone that tells me they really *like* Cobol, and can say enough about why
they like it that I believe them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Know of any Internships, Charities that need COBOL Prgmrs?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38CD4122.42DA3AC5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<89gmh2$s3v$1@news.cerf.net> <20000229174329.21186.00000055@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38bd5e01.3849680@news.epix.net> <89md0s$m8$1@news.cerf.net> <MPG.132d2a3744084cc7989942@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89vf56$hi$1@news.cerf.net> <MPG.13363fe97e4ebfa6989968@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```


Charles F Hankel wrote:
> 
> I noticed that Cheesle as cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no said...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> want to be involved in, not in any way.

I assume from the way you worded it that you are going into these sites
as a contractor ? Unfortunately if you are a salaried employee as part
of the beast, regrettably, sometimes you just can't ignore the politics.
EDP should be a 'pure science', (I prefer 'art').

I'm not telling tales out of school - because it was such a long time
ago (around VAT '73). Debenhams bought out a family supermarket group in
SE. Gave the family directors a whole bunch of money - and get this,
then gracefully allowed them to stay on as directors. (Portents of the
future - you can see where this is heading ?). Their thinking just
didn't jive with ours - they wanted to do it their way. We already had a
successful system for A/P (Bought Ledger) that told the merchandisers
what they were buying. It was agreed they (the subsidiary), would run
the Food A/P for the group. 

Initially I did it, but then I had a lead Systems Analyst who used to
travel weekly from the S.W up to London. He was coming back saying, "But
it isn't going to work". I understood perfectly what he was saying but I
had no hard proof facts on which to base anything, just his feelings and
mine.

What I should have done was follow my instincts, write a letter DIRECTLY
to the chairman, copies to others, and let the proverbial hit the fan. I
didn't - guess what, it was a complete cock-up. The external auditors
warned that if the mess wasn't cleaned up for the following year - they
would qualify Debenhams accounts. (To those not appreciative of that
last little bit - that's like seeing 'this company's accounts are
questionable' - your shares take a real nose-dive on the stock
exchange). And to cap it all, the Financial Director, one of the
instigators of the take-over, announced 'it was because we had a
computer boo-boo'. That comment together with all the navel-gazing that
used to go on by decision makers - is the reason I said to hell with it
and made a fresh start here in the Great White North. (I could have
happily seen out my time there, but they got taken over by Burtons the
tailors anyway).

I love it here, but funny thing is I get the distinct impression that
down in the zippity-zippty oil patch they don't navel-gaze, but actually
try to look in their bloody navels.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Know of any Internships, Charities that need COBOL Prgmrs?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.133cb445ecc18651989988@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<89gmh2$s3v$1@news.cerf.net> <20000229174329.21186.00000055@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38bd5e01.3849680@news.epix.net> <89md0s$m8$1@news.cerf.net> <MPG.132d2a3744084cc7989942@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89vf56$hi$1@news.cerf.net> <MPG.13363fe97e4ebfa6989968@news.mersinet.co.uk> <38CD4122.42DA3AC5@home.com>`

```
I noticed that James J. Gavan as jjgavan@home.com said...
> 
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> EDP should be a 'pure science', (I prefer 'art').

My first installation back in the mists of time had a five page standards 
manual in which computer programming was described as "disciplined 
creativity".  Personally I see it the entire development and maintenance 
cycles as all as simple problem solving exercises.

Salaried employee is a status I enjoyed until June 1976.  Then I joined 
the other three hundred contractors in the UK mostly working through four 
agencies.  My how times have changed.  What I despise most is politics 
because the client isn't paying me to be political, even if I was a 
salaried employee.

The zenith of this disgust was during a contract with a really well-known 
global corporation where we were in a formal meeting to discuss and 
decide on just which route to take with the migration of a complete 
system and it was constantly interrupted with phone calls about who just 
got fired and who was going to get which job depending on who got which 
other job, etc.  Even worse, the migration route was left undecided.  
This sort of thing was almost a daily event on that contract.
 
> I love it here, but funny thing is I get the distinct impression that
> down in the zippity-zippty oil patch they don't navel-gaze, but actually
> try to look in their bloody navels.

They're probably still trying to work out where this oil patch is.  :-)
```

```

Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote in message
news:MPG.13260cd87c2dfbb989919@news.mersinet.co.uk...
> I noticed that Howard Brazee as howard.brazee@cusys.edu said...
> > Some arithmetic works with them directly.  But if I want look at a date,
add a year to it,
> > and place this new date on a report,  Julian isn't the most efficient.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Back to COBOL, the COBOL standard has Julian dates available, but IBM's
language environment
> > is about 11 years off (if I remember correctly) with a Lilian date.
Some spread sheets
> > start with days since 1900, and I believe Unix has built in date from
some time recent.
> > Gregorian may be safer for portability (and it is easier for me to look
at a file and
> > recognize last Sunday's date), it just isn't efficient at comparing the
number of days
> > between two dates (and as you say, it takes up more space)
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's only net news but I like it.

Disk space may be cheap in financial terms but its not necessarily cheap in
processing times.   We started having the view 'its only DASD' a few years
ago,  but now we have some clients with such large data bases that we have
to be careful of the effect of  enhancements on record size.   When clients
get a new release of our software they're unhappy when we've increased the
time of their backups and batch processes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software - a contrarian view

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Se6v4.6755$JL3.1991633@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia> <38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au> <UsSs4.7634$zu3.118468@news3.mia> <38B40BB5.2BE7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38B42C8A.64A8CDEF@cusys.edu> <MPG.13260cd87c2dfbb989919@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89ip2i$4p2$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
Rick Price wrote:
>
>Disk space may be cheap in financial terms but its not necessarily cheap in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>time of their backups and batch processes.

A few years ago we developed a large and highly complex system for a
client.  The system has pretty demanding technical requirements, and
the user was implacable in their demands that the system meet every
little detail they could think of.  In order to satisfy their many
demands, we had to create a number of high volume transaction files.
The user needed to see, and utilize in processing, very detailed
breakdowns of transactions two years old, with a couple of million
transactions a year.  This is because they must be able to back out
transactions up to two years old, and because of tax law changes, it
is no longer possible to recalculate exactly what the breakdown of
the transaction was.  Time after time we stressed in meetings and
writing that, though we could meet their requirements, it was going
to consume a significant amount of system resources.  We were told,
literally "We are not concerned about storage or cycle time."  The
system came up and worked from the first day, so well the users were
ecstatic.  One prime design goal was to minimize the programmer
maintenance time.  The old system had five people assigned full time
to maintenence, and they couldn't keep up.  The new system takes only
one person, part time.  Another primary goal was making balancing much
easier.  The new system has been up 18 months and hasn't been out of
balance once.  But after it was up about a year the client called to
ask "Is there anything we can do to reduce the size of the transaction
files? They sure take up a lot of disk space."  Sigh.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software - a contrarian view

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aPQv4.5750$6o.57785@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia> <38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au> <UsSs4.7634$zu3.118468@news3.mia> <38B40BB5.2BE7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38B42C8A.64A8CDEF@cusys.edu> <MPG.13260cd87c2dfbb989919@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89ip2i$4p2$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <Se6v4.6755$JL3.1991633@news4.mia> <ui2sbscokmd5avrb68iaqckgiqec40f55s@4ax.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>cache the top of the btree).

It is an automobile license (tag) system.  In Alabama, a person can
come in and claim an error on a tag up to two years old, or cancel a
tag up to two years old (I know this is strange, but laws don't have
to make sense :).  This county has about 43 different municipalities.
The cost of a tag is based on a whole host of possible charges, taxes,
fees, penalties and interest.  Every one of these charges must be
split out into the appropriate fund.  One single tag can have any
number of these items.  The average is probably about 6 or 8, but some
have 30 or more.  This is for each of about 2 million transactions a
year.  Alabama has over 200 kinds of tags you can buy, many of them
optional, such as personalized or specialized tags.  Almost every
university has it's own special tag.  Actually, many of them have two
types, one with an assigned tag number, the other with a personalized
tag number (e.g. "THE KING", "HOTSTUFF", "BIG MAMA", etc :).  You can
buy Environmental tags, Children's Trust Fund tags, Square and Round
Dance tags, Alabama Shakespeare Festival Tags, etc., etc.  There is
even a Helping School tag where the extra $50 fee will be to be sent
arbitrarily to the school district of your choice, from any of the
hundreds of school districts in the state, so we have to keep up with
those too.  And the number of tags is increasing constantly.  Any club
or organization can get so many signatures on a petition, and the state
will set up a potential special tag just for that group.  People come
in and request the new tags, paying the extra fee.  If enough people
buy them to meet the minimum requirement, the state will then stamp and
issue the special tags, which become a new tag type for us.  If not
enough people buy the new tags, no new tag is issued, and the fees are
refunded.  This is why it took 5 people full time to maintain the old
system, because all this stuff was hard coded.  One of the ways we
addressed the maintenance issue is to virtually totally define a tag,
its numbering scheme, the requirements and restrictions, and the charges,
all in table files maintained by the user department.  When a new tag
type is introduced, usually it is simply a matter of adding table entries,
on the fly, with the date they are scheduled to be active (and end, if
applicable).  But it is mandatory that when a tag is cancelled, the
charges which were applied originally be reversed exactly.  Since the
user is responsible for table maintenance, it is possible that a user
could modify an older charge table record or, when a tax rate is changed,
to modify the table entry rather than create a new one to become active
on the appropriate date, superceding the old one (the correct way to do
it).  Because of this, it is impossible to guarantee that our table
records are still correct for a certain date, after the two year period.
In other words, we can't guarantee that we can still recalculate the
exact charges for a tag two years later, in order to reverse them.
However, we must store the detailed charges in a write-only file for
accounting purposes.  So, by keeping records in this file for two years,
we can simply retrieve the original charges for that tag and reverse
them online.  The old tag system tried to recalculate such charges and
was often incorrect, so the users were very adamant that we be able to
do it precisely.  Believe me, the above complication is simply one small
item of *many*, *many* such, and worse, complications.  In 31 years of
designing and writing computer systems, this was the most difficult one
I ever did.  The system collects about $140 million each year in this
county.  The head of the county Revenue Department is held personally
responsible for the money (the checks are actually made out to him),
and collecting it correctly, which is why they're so picky. :-)

If you would like to see a few of the many specialized tags available,
check out http://www.jeffcointouch.com/ieindex.asp.  The county website
is www.jefcointouch.com.  We didn't do the web stuff, just the mainframe
backend for tag renewals.  In this county, you can now renew your tag
over the internet, or at kiosks across the county, using your credit
card.  That part was a piece of cake. :-)
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
>Hats off to Alabama. Seems like they invented their own money printing
>machine :-)

Actually, it's more than that.  Many years ago, Alabama passed laws
that restricted liquor sales to only state owned stores for bottled
booze, though restaurants and bars can sell by the drink.  There
are privately owned package stores but, as I understand it, everybody
must buy their stock from the state store.  However, Alabama has
among the lowest sales (4%), property and income taxes in the nation.
I don't drink, so it's win-win for me, but it always bothers me when
government gets into competition with private enterprise.
```

```
Couldn't agree with you more about the private enterprise bit. And
Canucks tend to think English drinking laws are quaint, but here in
Alberta we too have a provincially controlled liquor board. Our current
premier is a very popular former mayor of Calgary but his cohorts are
somewhere to the extreme right of Maggie Thatcher - Michael M. would
love it !

They did switch to privately owned liquor stores some 3/4 years ago BUT
the Province still controls the liquor warehousing - which means in
effect choice is restricted to a bureaucrat's preferences. The other bit
of nonsense is that Safeway wanted to include liquor in their
supermarkets, just as existed in England before I left, and last time I
was there, was also the situation in Montana. The private liquor stores
pounded the Province with objections - compromise - Safeway can sell
liquor, but the building has to be PHYSICALLY separated from the
supermarket. Not what that merchandising outfit was after, and I don't
think they've bothered to build any separate liquor stores.

Now either you have 'free enterprise' or you don't - how stupid.

PS: Unlike your 4% - Albertans do like their booze, though it isn't a
hot bed of drunks, just very affluent drinkers.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Paul Knudsen wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>It is an automobile license (tag) system.
>
>Well done, Judson!   And let them try porting THAT to a server, eh?

Actually, I am looking forward to that very thing, as long as there
is plenty of room between myself and the action. :-)  A few years ago
Alabama moved their driver's license system (run by the state, not
county) from mainframe to client/server.  Response time dropped from
10-20 seconds to over 4 minutes.  The tag system has a response time
of from less than 1 second to perhaps 10 to 15 seconds at peak.  I
can just imagine 750,000 people trying to renew vehicle licenses with
a 4 minute response time, or when the servers go down for a day.  A
riot is not out of the question, really.  We rednecks can get awfully
impatient. :-)
```

```
Judson, Enlighten me briefly, the difference between state and county .

UK - Federal(Central Government) - just about everything including 
	taxation
     County - geographical area, going back to the old 'shires' hence
	shire reeve and sheriff of Nottingham(shire)

	Not to clear - but think they get a hand-out for education and
	assistance on transpiration (road building etc..). Other than
	local property taxes I would think the rest is done from London.
	Any contradiction from you folks in Albion -

	Above ignores current move to 'separate' functions occurring
	in Scottish or Welsh environments

Canada - Federal (Ottawa or THEM as so many refer to them in Alberta)
	 All tax collection, (well Quebec is a bit different, but they 
	 would have to be, wouldn't they :-) )

	 They supposedly pass on hand-outs to provinces for healthier
	 and education

	 Province - lost count, it is now around 12 entities (??)
	 With the exception of Alberta, they all have a sales tax.
	 They bugger around having a 'local parliament', (Legislature)
	 and introduce Bills as they see fit - providing they don't 
	 conflict with federal laws - and the new twist providing the
	 damned judges don't decide it conflicts with our beloved
	 'Charter of Rights'. (It's amazing, UK in its various 	 	 transitions,
has happily existed based on common law. -
	 Do you see the problem with 'Standards' William K ?)

	 Below Province - towns/cities collecting property taxes, to
	 run city, transit services, education.

So where does your county fit into the picture. Next question, same for
all states or do you differ ?. (I betcha you're gonna tell me the oldies
'New England' do it different to the rest or newcomers).

Jimmy
```

```
In article <38CAA8B0.765EE8DA@home.com>, "James J. Gavan"
<jjgavan@home.com> writes
>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>       local property taxes I would think the rest is done from London.
UK Counties are responsible for a lot of things:
 local road networks maintenance, virge maintenance, road safety and
   sign-posting
 social services (eg OAP homes, home visitors, monitoring children 
   at risk, etc.)
 education (the grant does come from Central Government and constitutes
   about 60 percent of local expenditure) 
 community education (evening classes)
 provision of cemetaries and crematoria
 county planning (location of new villages and housing stock, 
   administering and reviewing the construction, siteing of industrial 
   premises, shops and infrastructure)
 provision and control of local police forces (we don't have FBI)
 provision of fire fighting facilities
 refuse disposal/recycling facilities
 environmental health (pollution, slum clearance, abbattoir control)
 provision of parks and leisure facilities
 provision of community halls/meeting facilities
 civil defense (natural disasters, etc)
 library facilities
 registrars of births, deaths and marriages
 benefits agencies (job seekers, welfare)
 cultural services
 university grants to students
 trading standards
 some provide computing facilities to district councils
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software - a contrarian view

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fE7z4.34154$wk.1489021@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia> <38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au> <UsSs4.7634$zu3.118468@news3.mia> <38B40BB5.2BE7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38B42C8A.64A8CDEF@cusys.edu> <MPG.13260cd87c2dfbb989919@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89ip2i$4p2$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <Se6v4.6755$JL3.1991633@news4.mia> <ui2sbscokmd5avrb68iaqckgiqec40f55s@4ax.com> <aPQv4.5750$6o.57785@news3.mia> <38c95fce.3348228@news.gw.total-web.net> <SFuy4.15056$aw3.242563@news2.mia> <38CAA8B0.765EE8DA@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
>
>Judson, Enlighten me briefly, the difference between state and county.

I wrote a very careful reply to this on Saturday, but I accidentally
deleted it before sending.  Grrr!

In the United States, the states, at least originally, were very similar
in power to the European Union countries now, relative to the Federal
government.  Over time, particularly during wars, the Federal government
has taken more and more power to itself.  This has been done primarily
through the process of heavy taxation at the Federal level, with much
of the monies returned to the states, but earmarked for specific things.
Since the U.S. Constitution gives the Federal Government little direct
power over the states, the Federal government mostly imposes its will
on the states by threat of withholding Federal money from the states
who do not comply.  This is how the countrywide 55 mph speed limit was
imposed in the 70's oil embargo crisis.  But most of the laws are made
and enforced by the states.  Murder, robbery, larceny, etc. are all
state laws.  Most laws are pretty similar from state to state, but
gambling is legal only in some states, and only in certain areas in
some states.  Prostitution is legal (and licensed) only in Nevada, I
believe.  Federal laws tend to deal mostly with taxation, crimes such
as assassination of Federal officials or crimes carried out across state
lines.  The latter is so Federal authorities (FBI) can coordinate the
effective prosecution of the case.

Each state is also divided into counties (Alabama has 73), though they
are called 'Parishes' in Louisiana.  Counties are primarily agents of
the state to bring actual state government function closer to the local
level.  Counties, at least in Alabama, are 'creatures of the state',
and their boundaries and existence are entirely determined by the state.
But they do have their own governments and lawmaking authority, and
collect virtually all county and state taxes.  County school districts
are under state school board authority.  Municipal governments also
have government and lawmaking and taxing authority, but are not directly
under state authority, as are counties.  Cities derive their authority
from the consent of their citizens, county authority derives from the
state.  Most county 'ordinances', as their laws are called (at least
in Alabama), involve things like zoning laws and taxes.


For example, one of my great, great uncles lived in one county, but
many of the politicos in a neighboring county wanted him to run for
judge in that
```

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qX7z4.34178$wk.1489507@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia> <38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au> <UsSs4.7634$zu3.118468@news3.mia> <38B40BB5.2BE7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38B42C8A.64A8CDEF@cusys.edu> <MPG.13260cd87c2dfbb989919@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89ip2i$4p2$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <Se6v4.6755$JL3.1991633@news4.mia> <ui2sbscokmd5avrb68iaqckgiqec40f55s@4ax.com> <aPQv4.5750$6o.57785@news3.mia> <38c95fce.3348228@news.gw.total-web.net> <SFuy4.15056$aw3.242563@news2.mia> <38CAA8B0.765EE8DA@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
>
>Judson, Enlighten me briefly, the difference between state and county.

I wrote a very careful reply to this on Saturday, but I accidentally
deleted it before sending.  Grrr!

In the United States, the states, at least originally, were very similar
in power to the European Union countries now, relative to the Federal
government.  Over time, particularly during wars, the Federal government
has taken more and more power to itself.  This has been done primarily
through the process of heavy taxation at the Federal level, with much
of the monies returned to the states, but earmarked for specific things.
Since the U.S. Constitution gives the Federal Government little direct
power over the states, the Federal government mostly imposes its will
on the states by threat of withholding Federal money from the states
who do not comply.  This is how the countrywide 55 mph speed limit was
imposed in the 70's oil embargo crisis.  But most of the laws are made
and enforced by the states.  Murder, robbery, larceny, etc. are all
state laws.  Most laws are pretty similar from state to state, but
gambling is legal only in some states, and only in certain areas in
some states.  Prostitution is legal (and licensed) only in Nevada, I
believe.  Federal laws tend to deal mostly with taxation, crimes such
as assassination of Federal officials or crimes carried out across state
lines.  The latter is so Federal authorities (FBI) can coordinate the
effective prosecution of the case.  Each state also has its own constitution, which ordains the fundamental lawmaking authority of
the
state, very similar to the U.S. constitution.  As the United States,
each state has executive, judicial and legislative bodies, with the
state Governor being analogous to the President of the U.S.

Each state is also divided into counties (Alabama has 73), though they
are called 'Parishes' in Louisiana.  Counties are primarily agents of
the state to bring actual state government function closer to the local
level.  Counties, at least in Alabama, are 'creatures of the state',
and their boundaries and existence are entirely determined by the state.
But they do have their own governments and lawmaking authority, and
collect virtually all county and state taxes.  County school districts
are under state school board authority.  Municipal governments also
have government and lawmaking and taxing authority, but are not directly
under state authority, as are counties.  Cities derive their authority
from the consent of their citizens, county authority derives from the
state.  Most county 'ordinances', as their laws are called (at least
in Alabama), involve things like zoning and taxes.  There are city
courts and state courts, which start at the county level.  There are
also Federal courts.  I believe that, as a rule, Federal courts only
become involved when Federal law or Constitutional issues are involved.
Each state has its own Supreme Court, as does the United States.

Personal anecdote: One of my great great uncles lived in Marshall
County, but some of the politicos in neighboring Dekalb County wanted
him to run for judge in Dekalb County.  To do this you must be a
resident in that county.  Since he lived only a short distance from
the Dekalb County line, and my great great uncle was not willing to
move, the Dekalb County state legislators got a law passed changing
the Marshall and Dekalb county lines to include the section of land
including my great great uncle's property in Dekalb County.  If you
have access to an Alabama map showing county lines, look at the
Eastern Marshall County line Northeast of the town of Albertville
(my old hometown).  You can see a rectangular notch in the county
lines where that chunk of Marshall County was transferred into Dekalb
County.  I grew up about a mile west of that notch, in Marshall
County. :-)
```

```
I noticed that Judson McClendon as judmc@bellsouth.net said...
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> government.

This isn't quite right.  The EU comprises several sovereign states who do 
their own thing.  There is a move towards federalism by some of them and 
a resistance to it by others, notably the UK.  The original idea was the 
Common Market and that is how most Brits still see it, as a free trade 
area.  Theoretically, "harmonisation" took place a few years ago but you 
will find more national differences than the term "harmonisation" might 
imply.  In short, the EU is a long way off from even the original ideas 
of federalism in the USA.  Still, I'm just being pedantic :-)

Anyway, thanks for the rest of the post - very interesting as used to be 
said on Rowan & Martin's Laugh-In.
```

- **References:** `<cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia> <38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au> <UsSs4.7634$zu3.118468@news3.mia> <38B40BB5.2BE7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38B42C8A.64A8CDEF@cusys.edu> <MPG.13260cd87c2dfbb989919@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89ip2i$4p2$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <Se6v4.6755$JL3.1991633@news4.mia> <ui2sbscokmd5avrb68iaqckgiqec40f55s@4ax.com> <aPQv4.5750$6o.57785@news3.mia> <38c95fce.3348228@news.gw.total-web.net> <SFuy4.15056$aw3.242563@news2.mia> <38CAA8B0.765EE8DA@home.com> <qX7z4.34178$wk.1489507@news1.mia> <MPG.13376aa8e7261560989976@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
Charles F Hankel wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> James J. Gavan wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>of federalism in the USA.  Still, I'm just being pedantic :-)

What you describe above sounds very much like what I studied in U.S.
history about the original 13 states. :-)  As I understand it, there
was a lot of heat and fire over the very idea of federalism.  If it
were not for the potential threat from European countries, they may
not have done it.  It was done with some difficulty, even then.

The individual states were so fiercely determined to have their own
will on internal matters, that we fought a terrible civil war over
it.  The American Civil War was really as much about 'States Rights'
as slavery.  In 1860, the year before the Civil War began, the
Virginia legislature came within 1 vote of abolishing slavery in
Virginia.  Even if for no other reason, the Industrial Revolution
was about to make slavery unprofitable anyway.  Slavery was a cruel,
horrible disgrace to this country, and I rejoice that it was
abolished long before my time.  Our society still suffers from the
repercussions.  But if the idealists of the time had just had more
wisdom and a little patience, and not tried to force the idea down
the South's throats overnight, slavery would have been abolished
within a decade or so, with no bloodshed.  Even southern people who
did not like slavery were in a very difficult position.  Remember
that those plantation owners had enormous resources tied up in
slaves, with no readily available way to replace them, and it would
have ruined them financially to have abolished slavery overnight.
A destroyed economy would have been (and was) very harmful to the
freed slaves, as well as the South, and the North, who needed
Southern cotton and so forth.  Instead of putting political and
financial pressure on the South to abolish slavery, while offering
financial incentives for freeing slaves, which would have done the
trick with no bloodshed over time, hundreds of thousands died in a
terrible war, including many blacks, destroying the economy of
half of the country.  Ironically, it was largely wealthy northern
ship owners who brought many of the slaves to America.  And when
the market became glutted, joined in the indignant demands that the
institution be abolished immediately.  Politically, the Civil War
dealt a tremendous blow to the idea of 'States Rights'.  Sadly, so
many of those who wish to eliminate social problems today have no
more wisdom about how to do it than they did 140 years ago. :-(

Are we off topic yet? :-)
```

```
I noticed that Judson McClendon as judmc@bellsouth.net said...
> Charles F Hankel wrote:
> >Judson McClendon wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> not have done it.  It was done with some difficulty, even then.

There's plenty of heat and fire over here at the moment, and has been for 
some years and probably will be for some time to come.  I'm ambivalent 
because I can't see the direct advantage in going the full federalism 
route.

Maybe my time in Missouri all those years ago has coloured my decision-
making, but I'd really like someone to show me that it's better than what 
we have now.  At least with the original 13 states there was a large 
degree of common culture whereas here the cultures can be quite different 
from one country to the next.  Heck, we think quite differently to the 
French and nobody thinks quite like the Irish or Italians or any other 
country in the EU.

<snip lots more enlightening stuff about the US Civil War issues>

My surname is pretty unusual - there aren't many of us around - and yet 
here in the Liverpool area a chap called Allen Stuart Hanckel (sometimes 
spelled Hankel) came over from the Confederacy being involved in the 
Trent affair.  When Mason & Slidell, the Confederate Consuls, where taken 
off by the Union Navy, he took their papers on to Liverpool where he 
continued to be involved in such matters as the commissioning of the CSS 
Alabama, which was constructed about a mile from where I now live.  He 
married a local girl here and they had three children that I know of and  
he's buried in Liverpool himself in Wavertree cemetary.  As far as I'm 
aware, he is not a relative but like most Hankels, I have a sneaking 
suspicion that we are all related somewhere down the line.
 
> Are we off topic yet? :-)

Only if I point out that you missed out the ENVIRONMENT DIVISION, so I 
won't.  :-)
```

```
I noticed that Rick Price as rick@hpd.co.uk said...
> 
> Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote in message
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> time of their backups and batch processes.

There's no pleasing people, is there?  My point about DASD is that it's 
while it's cheaper than it used to be, it's one of the prime concerns of 
IT managers because just adding more and more of it to soak up the demand 
isn't a viable option.  That's why we see some really aggressive archive 
policies nowadays compared to ten years ago.

<topic swerve>
I worked on a site that archived datasets that were unused for more than 
three days - the bad idea faltered on the first day back after a public 
holiday was tacked onto a weekend, with a couple of hundred programmer 
types hanging around for the best part of the Tuesday morning (some even 
into the afternoon) just waiting for their TSO environments to restore.  
This knocked on when the US teams turned up for work and experienced 
similar delays - and they were queued up behind us.  Another victory for 
the Department of Terribly Bright Ideas.
```

<a id="t-04f01f6eda"></a>
## Thread: SAS sucks — 16 this month / 18 total

_Thread started [2000-02](2000-02.md#t-04f01f6eda)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SAS sucks

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000229212636.05847.00001592@ng-fa1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<D7Ru4.4503$JL3.1968267@news4.mia>`

```
>Subject: Re: SAS sucks
>From: "Judson McClendon" judmc@bellsouth.net 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Unfortunately, as many companies have found out via lawsuits (e.g., McDonald's
coffee), there are limits to genius, but seemingly none on stupidity...

Even among software "professionals", I've seen some doozies that have been
written off as SAS performance problems:

1. One VP dropped SAS after complaining about performance problems with their
datamart, the design of which (by non-SAS programmers) included huge data base
tables (millions of rows) with thousands of variables per table that were
rebuilt from scratch every single time a report request was made...

2. A consulting company recommended that SAS be dropped (for performance
reasons of course!) after programmers, ignoring hundreds of warning messages,
deleted all of the client's source code and complained that the system couldn't
magically regenerate the source code (because they also discovered they failed
to make any backups)...

3. A Big Six firm recommended that a major insurance company drop SAS in favor
of an Oracle, Peoplesoft, Hyperion, Essbase, NT, Unix combination to speed up
turnaround...Scores of millions of  dollars later, reports that took an hour
with SAS could now be produced in five days with the new "state of the art"
system...  

4. A former IT VP expressed his outrage to me after SAS Institute declined to
jump to his demands for free tuning of his (self-developed) marketing reporting
system. I had to fight back the laughter when he showed me the "outrageous"
letter he had received from the (too easy going to a fault IMHO) SAS Technical
Suport folks - "Dear Sir...we are afraid that we will be unable to respond to
your demand for free consulting services...However, we did notice that you have
defined more than 7,000 separate variables (e.g., AK9801,
AK9802,...AK9912...WY9801...WY9912..) in your program...If your budget cannot
support training or consulting services at this time, perhaps you could have
someone in your IT department take some time to demonstrate the use of tables
or arrays..."... 

In most cases (there are horrific COBOL implementations as well!), SAS will
consume more CPU conservative than a comparable COBOL-based solution. However,
IMHO it will be developed much faster, be much easier to maintain, and have a
much better chance of being correct. In the absence of an off-the-wall internal
chargeback system (unfortuanately, there are way still too many!), the CPU
requirements should not pose a real obstacle for most business systems.

HTH
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SAS sucks

- **From:** dgrampsas@aol.com (DGrampsas)
- **Date:** 2000-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000314231336.01692.00002557@ng-bg1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20000229212636.05847.00001592@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

```
Recently, a COBOL programmer in my company decided it would be wise to convert
a SAS program that processes masterfile data into an equivalent COBOL program.
Keep in mind that the SAS program produced one-way frequency tables  and was
only 15 pages or so. He completed the COBOL program (several hundred lines
long) and proclaimed, "I just saved the company $3000 dollars in processing
costs!" He was asked to adapt his COBOL program to produce two-way frequencies
and promptly dusted off the SAS program and abandoned his COBOL program.


Sometimes CPU throughput isn't everything...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SAS sucks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Brian Maher" <maher@progress.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ansvf$7jq$1@progress.progress.com>`
- **References:** `<20000229212636.05847.00001592@ng-fa1.aol.com> <20000314231336.01692.00002557@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
I have to add a comment to this thread.  Years ago I wrote SAS in a bank
down south
and the other developers were complaining that a particular program I wrote
used 4,096K
of memory and sucked down around 80% of the CPU cycles of an IBM 3090.  They
wanted
us to use an Easytrieve program that they wrote that used only 200K and
around 2 to 3% of
the CPU resources of the machine.  We ended up taking the argument to the
head of Technical
Services (i.e. the guy who managed the mainframe).  I'll never forget his
response after looking
over the job logs for both programs.  His response was "the SAS program ran
in 5 minutes & the
Easytrieve program ran for 1 hour.  I'll take the program that sits in the
machine for the smallest
amount of time anyday & I don't give a darn how much memory or CPU the SAS
program uses
because it still is more efficient for overall thru-put than the Easytrieve
program".

The Easytrieve programmers were not happy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SAS sucks

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BDF16C.36E09D8A@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<89fcen$q3f@netaxs.com> <D7Ru4.4503$JL3.1968267@news4.mia>`

```
Judson,

It is true for those products that SI supplies with GUI, for instance,
SAS/Insight. They are marketed in about the same way as a spreadsheet or
word processor would be marketed. In such cases, the emphasis is
naturally off procedural programming. However, SI does not market Base
SAS, SAS Language, and Macro Language this way. This is serious stuff
intended for serious programmers, and SI keeps improving, streamlining,
and enriching the language. The current Version 8 has received a myriad
of enhancements. For COBOL NG folks touching SAS sporadically, the most
notable ones would perhaps be:

* The limit on the identifier name is SAS has been raised from 8 to 32.
* The maximum length of a character variable has been expanded from 200
to 32767.
* About 50 new functions and call routines have been added.
* ODS (Output Delivery System) has been introduced. In a nutshell, now,
instead of directing a reporting output straight to a print file, one can
create an "output object", edit it at will, and spit the object to a
print file, RTF, HTML, etc.

Generally, it appears to me that even if SI did market SAS as a tool
"even a non-programmer could use" it would be as poor an excuse for
writing crappy SAS programs as for writing horrible COBOL code just
because there is a book offering one to teach himself COBOL in 24 hours.
Such claims, as every rational person of course understands, are simply
marketing (and fully legitimate) means to attract attention, while
becoming either professional COBOL or SAS programmer demands plenty of
hard work, dedication, and practical coding. To become a "guru", one
should add obsession with the subject and a lot of insatiable curiosity.

Kind regards,
=====================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl
=====================

Judson McClendon wrote:

> Lisa nor Jeff wrote:
> >SAS gets a bad rap for performance primarily because
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SAS sucks

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BE7435.D1593973@cusys.edu>`

```
In our shop, we have SAS as option 2 on our TSO menu.  As far as I know, the
only thing people have done with it here is to accidentally go into
interactive TSO when they meant to be in ISPF option 2, then try to figure
out what is EXIT in SAS, to get out.

I've been in shops which used SAS for reporting (normally not the best tool
for that), but interactive SAS seems designed for what SAS is designed for -
statistics.  But I haven't seen it used.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SAS sucks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BF65E2.94F2810A@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<89fcen$q3f@netaxs.com> <D7Ru4.4503$JL3.1968267@news4.mia> <38BDF16C.36E09D8A@mediaone.net> <38BE7435.D1593973@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> In our shop, we have SAS as option 2 on our TSO menu.  As far as I know, the
> only thing people have done with it here is to accidentally go into
> interactive TSO when they meant to be in ISPF option 2, then try to figure
> out what is EXIT in SAS, to get out.

Since you have now digressed into the anecdotal domain of the discussion, let me
not take myself too seriously and explain to those poor lads unfortunate to have
accidentally entered SAS Display Manager System that it is not necessary to call
the operator asking for killing the TSO session. Simply type BYE or ENDSAS in
the command line and hit ENTER.

> I've been in shops which used SAS for reporting (normally not the best tool
> for that),

Yeah, but maybe abnormally, it is?

> but interactive SAS seems designed for what SAS is designed for -
> statistics.  But I haven't seen it used.

"Interactrive SAS" (to a SAS professional, sounds similar to calling CICS
something like "interactive mainframe" but let us not be picky here) is designed
exactly what "batch SAS" is designed for. The difference is the same as running
a DB2 query from a SPUFI panel interactively or as DSN batch - the same program
is running, only in the first case, your terminal gets locked until the program
is done, in the second - you do whatever else you want while it is executing in
the background. However, additionally, there are SAS product, such as FSP, IML,
or Insight,  specifically designed to be run interactively, in a GUI manner.
Lastly, to say that SAS is designed for statistics is the same as to say that
COBOL is designed to calculate the number of records in a flat file.

Kind regards,
===================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
===================
```

#### ↳ Re: SAS sucks

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BDFCCD.794996D1@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<89fcen$q3f@netaxs.com>`

```
Lisa nor Jeff wrote:

> SAS gets a bad rap for performance primarily because
> managers think it can be written by anybody, not necessarily a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL, and most programmers have lots of experience in it.

Therein lies a sizable stretch right there. Not _everybody_ knows COBOL,
and as a matter of fact, _most_ programmers have no vaguest idea how a
COBOL program looks like. This is one of the reasons why experienced
COBOL programmers dictate premium compensation on the market and are
highly sought after.

>  The
> advantage of COBOL is that it's a universal language.

Define "universal". If it is in the sense that "everybody knows COBOL",
see above. If it is in the sense that COBOL can be used to program just
about anything, it is true, for it is a Turing complete language!
However, the tradition of its usage and the intent with which it was
created limit its versatility pretty much to standard business
applications.

> Like it or not, SAS is a specialized language.

Define "specialized". In fact, because of the breadth of its
applications, it is much less specialized than other languages, say
COBOL and FORTRAN _specifically_ aimed at business and
scientific/engineering computations, respectively. And its syntax is no
more specialized than that of PL/I from which it has been derived.

> Not everyone
> knows it, certainly not to the level you speak of to use it
> properly.  Unless an organization standardizes on SAS and trains
> everyone in it _thoroughly_, the organization won't develop the
> expertise.

This is absolutely true. However, you might be surprised how many
organizations standardize on SAS. For a small change, it pertains to
_all_ pharmaceutical companies. For a number of reasons, there SAS is
Language One. These are big players, and they invest gobs of money and
time in SAS training. SAS enjoyes the same status in any marketing,
credit policy, modelling, forecasting, data warehousing, in other words,
decision support or trend setting department of any large organization
serious about keeping to do business. 4000 SAS people from all around
the world do not assemble for their annual SUGI Conference for
nothing.

> Regarding your comment of coding complex routines, my point is
> that if coding is required, you might as well code in COBOL
> rather than SAS.

Sure, as well as any other tongue. If an entire application is written
in COBOL, it is silly to stick a SAS piece there just for the heck of
it. However, if it reduces the number of JCL steps from 20 to 1, or NLOC
from 20,000 to 200, it may be well worth it. Many SAS procs have become
so prolific and well-known even to non-SAS programmers (e.g. PRINT,
FREQ, SUMMARY, SQL) that most folks know what they do and recognize them
with an unaided eye. You may of course disagree, but I am sure that
several lines of TABULATE or SQL are much more self-explanatory and
infinitely easier to maintain than 10,000 lines of COBOL program they
supplant. In fact, sinful as I am, I have convinced more than one COBOL
programmer that it is faster to take time to learn the entire TABULATE
than even to sketch a COBOL program that would implement maybe 1 per
cent of TABULATE's functionality.

Kind regards,
===================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
===================
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SAS sucks

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BE7611.74EB7F4E@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<89fcen$q3f@netaxs.com> <38BDFCCD.794996D1@mediaone.net>`

```
"Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:

> Sure, as well as any other tongue. If an entire application is written
> in COBOL, it is silly to stick a SAS piece there just for the heck of
> it. However, if it reduces the number of JCL steps from 20 to 1, or NLOC
> from 20,000 to 200, it may be well worth it.

The big problem with this is that it can become costly to use the best tool
for a task - instead we use the best tool for the job because we can't find
or build programmers who are competent with all tools.  So most shops have
one primary language (nowadays shops are often split into segments - the
Unix/Oracle/C box, the IBM/IMS/COBOL box, the NT/Pearl/Java box...) which a
programmer needs to be competent, and a couple or few other languages which
they can get by in.  Often SAS is one of these "get by" languages.  But if a
programmer already works in COBOL, ADS/O, Easytrieve, and Java (my shop),
adding another tool can cost valuable competence.
```

```
news:38BDFCCD.794996D1@mediaone.net...

> >  The
> > advantage of COBOL is that it's a universal language.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> applications.

It may not have been the original poster's intent, but I read this as meaning
that COBOL (as a "language") is available across operating systems (both
"proprietary" and "open").  This is because there are ANSI/ISO Standards for
it and NO specific vendor "owns" the language definition.  I know that over
the years, SAS has been ported to more systems, but I still don't think that
it is available on NEARLY as many platforms as COBOL is (and certainly not
with the same "universal" semantics).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SAS sucks

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BF60B0.47486E79@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<89fcen$q3f@netaxs.com> <38BDFCCD.794996D1@mediaone.net> <89n0os$r2u$2@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

I have been under the impression that SAS has been an undisputed portability
champion. Usually, if I need to test a piece of code and S/390 is kind of busy, I
cut and paste the code and run it under NT; or if it is too slow to my taste, I
do the same in UNIX. However, I may be wrong, and indeed SAS is not available on
_nearly_ as many platforms as COBOL. Just for my information, let me list the
operating systems SAS currently runs under, and I implore you to expand the list
by telling me on what operating systems, in addition to ones listed below, COBOL
is capable of running:

 1. OS/390 (TSO and CICS shells)
 2. VM/VSE
 3. CMS
 4. Open VMS
 5. VAX
 6. OS/400
 7. DOS
 8. Windows (3.x, 95, 98, NT, NT Server, 2000)
 9. OS/2 (any version)
10. Mac (any version)
11. UNIX

You are correct in SAS being _very_ proprietory. However, from the standpoint of
standards, I am not sure it is all that bad. There is just one, and only one, SAS
standard, namely the one set by The SAS Institute, and all changes and
enhancements to the standard are thoroughly documented by SI. The situation when
an implementation may be different from vendor to vendor is simply non-existent.
I do not really understand the argument of "universal semantics" since within the
accuracy of OS specifics like file name conventions, the semantics of the entire
SAS Software is precisely the same. SAS's SQL implementation completely conforms
to the ANSI standard _and_ additionally allows for use of all functions, formats,
and informats available in SAS.

Kind regards,
====================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl
====================

"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Paul M. Dorfman <sashole@mediaone.net> wrote in message
> news:38BDFCCD.794996D1@mediaone.net...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SAS sucks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rich0850@aol.com (RICH0850)
- **Date:** 2000-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000304194507.02603.00000103@ng-fs1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38BF60B0.47486E79@mediaone.net>`

```
I have found SAS to be *my* ultimate scripting language. One time shot and
such.

Along the lines of "I need an answer now!"
Very few can match SAS.

It also scales well when I have to process HUGE amounts of data (gigabytes
here).

SAS is great if you need fast results in a dynamic environment.  Sometimes not
so good if CPU load is a factor (although I think with good programming, speed
will improve).  Most examples of SAS being poor in relation to other languages
(in terms of speed) are usually due to lousy design.

It's a very powerfull language.  My only regret is that others don't know about
it.  And the fact that it is so expensive.

One of the problems I've seen is that new programmers have a problem with the
"table" nature of SAS.  The fact of the dataset.  The idea that observations
are dealt with automagically.

--Richard
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SAS sucks

_(thread depth: 5)_

```


RICH0850 wrote:
> 
> I have found SAS to be *my* ultimate scripting language. One time shot and
> such.
> 
I'm always intrigued by the various innovations; I know nothing of SAS
nor SQL - but isn't the latter another attempt to do the former, or by
comparison is SQL hard coded ?

Some 30 years ago on an NCR mainframe I recall an analyst taking the A/R
element of a system, (don't know the specific details), but you could
flag which fields you wanted, in which order, plus ranges - users
checked off boxes on the request form, key-punched and hey presto,
customized report produced. Do these 'glittering' packages do more than

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SAS sucks

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C2037B.684E9664@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<38BF60B0.47486E79@mediaone.net> <20000304194507.02603.00000103@ng-fs1.aol.com> <38C1B55A.562A2DC5@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> RICH0850 wrote:
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> comparison is SQL hard coded ?

No. SQL is a self-sustained part of Base SAS. In SAS, a programmer has the
lattitude of achieving the same result by a variety of very different means. The
choice may be dictated by performance considerations, personal choice, or both.
Those coming to SAS with procedural language experience tend to code in SAS DATA
step - the closest SAS equivalent of a COBOL, PL/I, or whatever,  program.
People with DBA background naturally tend to code in SQL. Professional SAS
programmers use both and/or a variety of SAS procedures (from the standpoint of
SAS, SQL is a SAS proc, too). Additionally, SAS allows to run programs
"external" to SAS, say IBM Utilities, from within a SAS program as if they were
native SAS procs, e.g.

PROC IDCAMS; RUN;

The control cards required by an external program, if any,  can be supplied
either in JCL or via SAS's intrinsic FILENAME statements. Likewise, any OS
commands can be issued from within a SAS program, etc. As far as SQL goes, one
may think of it as more 'hard coded' than DATA step which is simply a full
procedural programming language (admittedly, with a myriad of automatic features
built into it - contrary to the popular belief, they can always be 'turned off',
if the programmer so desires). However, I do not think it is a correct way of
putting things. SQL concentrates on relations between the objects like files or
tables and operates on those relations - it is up to the SQL optimizer to figure
out how to implement the query; so it builds the program by deduction.
Procedural philosophy is rather inductive in comparison. SAS allows both to
coexist peacefully within the same package.


> Some 30 years ago on an NCR mainframe I recall an analyst taking the A/R
> element of a system, (don't know the specific details), but you could
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> that analyst was doing 30 years ago ?

Judge for yourself. To erect a building, SAS gives one all the traditional tools
and pieces (bricks, sand, cement, shovels, nails,...); also, it provides
preassembled blocks of every imaginable shape along with very well designed,
unified way of meshing them together; and, finally, a myriad of automatic tools,
machinery from light to heavy, portable powerstation, etc. How to use all these
tools, is up to the programmer. In the hands of an experienced master, they
become a programming miracle, and a beautiful building is erected in a very
short time within the budget allotted. In the hands of a dumb ass, they become
dangerous. One who uses a bulldozer where only a shovel is needed will surely
produce a lousy result and waste gobs of materials and fuel for no reason. One
who, having failed to learn how to match blocks, uses bricks only, runs out of
the bricks, cement, and time. In such cases, a CIO receives a report about SAS
being a "resource hog". If CIO's intellectual capabilities match the ones of the
"resource hog" culprit, SAS gets cursed, uninstalled, etc.; otherwise, the dumb
ass guilty of raping SAS gets "uninstalled", and a professional SAS programmer
is hired to do the job the way it should be done, i.e. right.

Kind regards,
==================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
==================
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **Message-ID:** `<38C2093D.677289C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38BF60B0.47486E79@mediaone.net> <20000304194507.02603.00000103@ng-fs1.aol.com> <38C1B55A.562A2DC5@home.com> <38C2037B.684E9664@mediaone.net>`

```


"Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> lattitude of achieving the same result by a variety of very different means. <snip>......

Many Thanks Paul for the explanation. I always enjoy reading your
insightful messages.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Karl/Oscar - I loved it. A thing that puzzles me is that we see here,
"I'm changing COBOL programs that were written before I was born". How
come, it really is difficult to visualize a program that hasn't
necessitated fairly dramatic changes to meet new business needs. And
that so often goes beyond just tweaking one individual program, with
sometimes re-arrangement of a file structure, or maybe even a sub module
to handle the new business requirement.

Certainly back in the late 60s/early 70s we had the phrase, "an
application's life cycle is some 4-5 years". 

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
