###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89p31o$jck$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <#NboHaCg$GA.252@cpmsnbbsa05> <s_Qt4.104$oO6.3002@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38BA9334.250F358A@cusys.edu> <38BF2B78.341DE007@home.com> <38BFCD59.2407C59F@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <38BFCD59.2407C59F@cusys.edu>...
>

>> > What happens in OO shops when a bug is found in a basic component?
Does
>> > everything which uses that object get a thorough test?  How expensive
is that?
>>
>> I don't know of any OO shops. So you tell me, 'cos I guess you do.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>matter.

It is no better or worse than a subroutine, in that respect.  So the answer
is yes, every type of use should be retested.  However, there is one thing
to keep in mind.  The objects that contain business logic are quite
different than the ones that have only to do with screen display (for
example).

A Cobol object can be looked at as a set of subroutines with several
advantages.  First of all, the parameters are rigorously checked. You
cannot, for example, invoke a method if the calling parameters are not
*exactly* matched to the called parameters. That is done at compile time.
Secondly, you can have many "instances" of the routine.  Picture a
subroutine with it's own data.

You know that the data is internally available on successive calls ...
while local to the subroutine, it is not going to be in the same state on
the second call as on the first unless declared as INITIAL.  That is
standard Cobol, and quite different than most languages.

That is roughly analogous to a single instance of an object, and, in fact,
you can use OOP coding in exactly that way ... create a single instance of
the "object" at the start of your program, then destroy it at the end.  The
methods then replace the subroutine entry points, so you have the equivalent
of a subroutine with multiple entry points and persistent data for the
duration of the run. You gain the advantage of far more rigourous parameter
checking than with subroutine calls. If you do nothing more than replace
your subroutines with single instances of OOP routines, you will make a
system far more solid, and eliminate many potential bugs.

The next step is to simply duplicate the subroutine as many times as you
want it, so that each of the subroutines has it's own data. Picture a
subroutine that manages a screen.  I now need two screens managed by the
same Cobol program, so I make two copies of my subroutine and change the
program-id on the second
from "SCREEN" to "SCREEN1". I then call them using different initialization
routines, and do my IO to one or the other.

That is an object with two instances. Each inherits the original code of the
prototype, plus the "value" statements of the prototype, but once created,
each behaves differently, and each has independent data.

Try to maintain that code.  Change the subroutine "SCREEN", and you have to
also change the subroutine "SCREEN1".  With OOP's, you do not.  The
instances are created at the object level, rather than at the source level.


So debugging and testing is halved.

Now, take our program, and design it so that it can handle ten open windows
at the same time.  I can do that with ten subroutines called WINDOW1 through
WINDOW10, or I can write it as OOPS and at run time create ten instances, or
I could do it with one subroutine having a table of ten cursor positions,
etc., and pass the window number as a parameter on each call.  Which of the
three is going to be easier to debug and maintain?

There are ways around the above, of course.  You can get into copy libraries
to ensure that different programs/subroutines are co-ordinated. However, if
you fail to compile one module that uses the copy, you are screwed.   If you
take that copy statement, and hard code it into an object, then each program
that uses it can create an instance, and use the data in the exact same way.
Nothing needs to be recompiled, and every program starts to use the new copy
at the object level. The added data names are automatically initialized in
one place ... the object. One of the best possible examples of OOPs would be
for the replacement of working storage copy modules used throughout the
system.

That only covers methods and attributes.  When you get to inheritance, it
gets even better, and the payoff is even greater. Used wisely, OOP's has the
potential to *greatly* reduce maintenance and testing.  The real problem is
that people have been conned into thinking of it as a GUI tool, instead of a
Cobol tool.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<38BF2949.7D8FDE90@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```


Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> Richard Plinston wrote in message <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net>...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Well Mr. Paintbrush, you are technicaly correct but most of us just
think of them as 'Linkage items' - and ensure you get them in the right
sequence for the sender/receiver.

Same applies with OO collections/dictionaries - there must be 'pointers'
to where they are in a collection/dictionary, as the syntax indicates,
though the literature never uses the word 'pointer' :-

add to  - invoke MyCollection "add"  using MyObject 

	  Note : no 'n-value' used as this is the next one in sequence
	  being added (ordered or sorted); there are features to "add 		 
before", "add after" etc..		

get	- invoke MyCollection "at"   using n returning MyObject

	  Note : Depends what you are doing; 

	  (a) Selecting from a Listbox/DropList :-

	FIRST - creating the Listbox control. Another method adds the 		actual
collection contents to the control.

       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "z-create-ListBox".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       copy "listflag.cpy".
       01 ls-object                         object reference.
       01 ls-event                          pic x(4) comp-5.

       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-id                        pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnk-field                     pic x(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division using lnk-id lnk-field.

          move p2ce-select        to ls-event
          invoke self "getObjectFromID"
                 using lnk-ID returning ls-object
          invoke ls-object "noAdjust"
          invoke ls-object "NotSorted"
          invoke ls-object "addFlags"
              using by value lbf-use-tabstops
          invoke ls-object "setEventto" using
             ls-event, self, "return-listbox-object " lnk-Field

          invoke os-ControlCollection "add" using ls-object

       Exit Method
       End Method "z-create-Listbox".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------

	SECOND - getting what the user selected from the Listbox :-

       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "return-Listbox-object".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-index                          pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-object                         object reference.
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-Field                         pic x(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division using lnk-Field.

        invoke os-ControlCollection "at"
               using lnk-field returning ls-object
        invoke ls-object "selectedItem" returning ls-index

        if     ls-index <> zeroes
               invoke os-Caller "return-ListIndex"
                      using lnk-Field ls-index
        End-if

       Exit Method
       End Method "return-Listbox-object".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------


	  (b) selecting an entryfield from a trigger event. I use 
	      "check-character-code" below because of BLOODY Windows 		     
substituting TAB for ENTER Key - for the past 20 years my 
	      end-user has been using the ENTER key :-

       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "z-create-Entryfield".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-event                      pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-object                     object reference.
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-id                        pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnk-field                     pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnk-length                    pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnk-type			pic x.

       Procedure Division using lnk-id lnk-field lnk-length lnk-type.

        invoke self "getObjectFromId"
               using lnk-ID returning ls-object

        if  lnk-length <> zeroes
            invoke ls-object "setLength" using lnk-length
            invoke ls-object "accessSystemEvents"
            move Button1Down to ls-event

            Evaluate true
              when lnk-type <> "x"
                   invoke ls-object "setEventto" using
                     ls-event, self, "set-nextfocus-empty ", lnk-Field
              when other
                   invoke ls-object "setEventto" using
                     ls-event, self, "set-nextfocus-insert ", lnk-Field
            End-evaluate

            move p2ce-functionKey to ls-event
            invoke ls-object "setEventto" using
              ls-event, self, "check-character-code "

        else invoke ls-object "readonly"
        End-if

        invoke os-ControlCollection "add" using ls-object

       Exit Method
       End Method "z-create-Entryfield".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------

Call 'em what you will - they are all 'pointers'.

Jimmy, Calgary AB










		
if selecting an item 	  from a Listbox/DropList collection, then you
start by 	  retrieving the "selectedItem", checking if the selection
is 		  zero. If not zero then it is 	  null),cxollectionuse of 'n' here
indicates you have stored the n-value
	  elsewhere at the time of adding
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BE0FD2.212A@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <MPG.13261830426e4ace98991b@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89jdjr$k92$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
> > list when most of the time it is neither necessary nor efficient?
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> than one cycle is more efficient with a linked list.

You are quite right.  It is better to have anything that is not stored
or reused as a linked list.  This includes student test data, and not
much else.

Cobol is for business use.  Businesses require their data to be stored
and to be used by all that require it.  This means that all data should
be shared resources.  Linked lists in memory cannot be shared.


> I really can't understand how you can say something like this.

Perhaps it is because he has experience in real environments and has
forgotten his school days.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89joud$1up$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <MPG.13261830426e4ace98991b@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89jdjr$k92$1@news.cerf.net> <38BE0FD2.212A@ibm.net>`

```


> Cobol is for business use.


I guess I should be writing COGOL and it would have pointers just like
C. The 'G' being for government. And COGOL will be blindingly fast and
easy to write, with a rich set of operators but hard to read 6 months
later and wildly expensive to maintain as a government language should
be. <g>

Sometimes I think you guys just like to argue.


In article <38BE0FD2.212A@ibm.net>,
  riplin@ibm.net wrote:
> > > list when most of the time it is neither necessary nor efficient?
> >
> > For Windows, which I usually work with, the limitation is virtual
memory,
> > that is, both disk space AND physical memory. Hence, the best out of
two
> > worlds.
> >
> > As regards to insertion job. With the proper copy book that is a
write once
> > use many scenario. Easy, easy.
> >
> > The linked list construction is *not* any more wasted time than
doing your
> > disk aksess per definition. Depending on the context each way is the
most
> > efficient one. Anything that isn't going to permanent storage and
used more
> > than one cycle is more efficient with a linked list.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> and to be used by all that require it.  This means that all data
should
> be shared resources.  Linked lists in memory cannot be shared.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89mbsj$sqk$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <MPG.13261830426e4ace98991b@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89jdjr$k92$1@news.cerf.net> <38BE0FD2.212A@ibm.net>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:38BE0FD2.212A@ibm.net...
> You are quite right.  It is better to have anything that is not stored
> or reused as a linked list.  This includes student test data, and not
> much else.

Seems like I am into a unique scenario then.
When I added the "spreadsheet" functionality to our accounting package. It
was basically to allow the end user to implement basic formulas on his own.
These formulas were allowed to be nested. This implies that there had to be
a lot of temporary storage and multiple cycles as formula references could
have been repeated. Remember this was three dimentional too.

Obviously, the "spreadsheet" solution could have been solved using temporary
sort files, but to what cost, one of the more complex tests that were ran
was noticeably slow due to the heavy load. And I can imagine using a
temporary sort file would have made the disk glow.

I am not saying that linked lists are the only solution, but contrary to
others here, I believe in linked lists as an option also for Cobol.

Cheesle
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** William Reed <adamreed@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BD85F7.8351478C@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <mAnt4.1334$dm5.26510@news.swbell.net> <D9xt4.35348$po2.361772@news1.mia> <38B6A88E.CBE706F9@cusys.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> True, but this doesn't quite contradict what the first poster said.  C++
> is significantly closer to COBOL in a count of new programs being
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> programs, and also programming jobs using different languages.

This hits the nail on the head!  I would be extremely interested in learning
to know how
much mainenance is being done to C/C++ programs.  I have NEVER heard of a job

posting for a C++ maintenance programmer (HUNDREDS for COBOL), and I doubt
that they even exist!

One of the true tests of the acceptance of a language is NOT how programmers
can understand it, but how non-programmers understand it.  I have explained
many a COBOL program to a user about how the program works vs. how the user
perceives it should work.  Try to explain the workings of a C/C++ program to
a user--he may go away scratching his head and wondering about your sanity...
```

```
> Try explaining any OO language you like to a non programmer. :-)

It depends on which level you attack from.

By saying that a car has 4 wheels and they are used to make the car move, OO
is definitely a better choice.

But, by saying that the object has to instantiate itself, inherit from this
and that, and that the rubber in the wheels are inherited from the tree
class, which has expanded into the rubber tree class and bla, bla, bla, I
don't know... :-)

Cheesle
```

```
Well done - couldn't agree more. Get away from the guff and talk in

somebody asks me, "What is a bit or byte ?". I don't automatically
respond - it isn't second nature to me and I have to translate in my
mind into layman's terms. (And in practical terms with regard to the
latter, in my 20 years-odd in COBOL I have never once had the necessity
to worry about a bit or a byte - and because I know Judson is 'big' on
math, it wasn't because I was trying to avoid the issue).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message

>Bias, bias. Yews suggestin my end-user can't reed me OO COBOL :-)

Speaking of which, I am having the most fun with Cobol that I have had for
years.  The more I do OOPs, the better I like it.  I just about have a OOPs
screen section emulator done ... the next step is going to be my own GUI
designer.  I am thinking that a Cobol interpeter can be written quite easily
using a Cobol Compiler ...

The code for the screen section emulator should read old screen section
code, and convert each item on a screen to an object that data can be moved
into and out of. The screens themselves become higher level objects, etc.

The more I get used to thinking about it, the more I can "see" a system that
reads Cobol code, and builds a data dictionary of "Cobol objects", each with
a length, picture clause, editing clause, and all the associated logic that
goes with it.

Theoretical question for you.  What is a minimum set of oop objects that can
be used to design a complete oop system?  IE:-is there an equivalent of a
high level Turing set?  In effect, can I start with "x" bootstrap objects,
and design a complete software system in terms of those objects, threaded
fashion?

For Cobol, we can start with the object represented by a single character
picture clause, for example.  All other Cobol picture clauses can be built
from that ...

Donald <having fun>
```

```
> Speaking of which, I am having the most fun with Cobol that I have had for
> years.  The more I do OOPs, the better I like it.  I just about have a OOPs
> screen section emulator done ... the next step is going to be my own GUI
> designer.  I am thinking that a Cobol interpeter can be written quite easily
> using a Cobol Compiler ...

...


> Donald <having fun>

I imagine so.  But what I want to see is how much fun maintenance programmers
have.  I bet they like their jobs better with OO than with spaghetti code
maintenance.  But I have found that hidden code (procedure division copies), and
interdependencies generally make maintenance harder.  It would be nice to
compare maintenance done for comparable (recent) structured non OO code with OO
Cobol in a large environment.
```

```
programmers
> have.  I bet they like their jobs better with OO than with spaghetti code

copies), and
> interdependencies generally make maintenance harder.  It would be nice to
> compare maintenance done for comparable (recent) structured non OO code

> Cobol in a large environment.

Since the library was defined so that COPY would be useful, I've had my
doubts about it because of the importance of data names.  Data dictionaries
seem to offer a method for centralized control that would allow for no
change is field sizes, descriptions, etc.  Yet, I feel the absents of all
of the data/procedure code in a source listing for a program is not
acceptable.  How does anyone like to look up the meaning of a word, and
have it use another definition reference instead of give the definition?
I welcome rules that require a source program contain all of the code used
to compile it.

Just my opinion.

Warren Simmons
w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net
```

- **Message-ID:** `<38BF161D.F4A6F640@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <mAnt4.1334$dm5.26510@news.swbell.net> <D9xt4.35348$po2.361772@news1.mia> <38B6A88E.CBE706F9@cusys.edu> <38BD85F7.8351478C@bellsouth.net> <oZgv4.4502$0l.39205@news2.mia> <38BDE300.A0E60193@home.com> <89lul1$qru$1@news.igs.net> <38BE8228.870F913B@cusys.edu> <L6Dv4.606$xZ.62566@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
> "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just my opinion.
 
You old reprobate :-). You'll get some sympathy from the gent wot says
'It's the artist not the paintbrush' - he doesn't like copyfiles either.
But I'm using them all the time, and one record format using 'copy
replace' appears three times in my general coding concept in OO :-

	business logic	 - replace ==(tag)==  with ==CUSTOMER==
	DB Interface	 - replace ==(tag)==  with either "ws" or "ls"
				Working-storage 'global' to the class,
				or Local-storage section, relevant only
				to the current method
	Data File	 - replace ==(tag)== with ==Data==

Same record, same field-lengths, and should they include redefinition,
then it is common to all three.

Now if you look at Donald's next message, I think he is heading towards
your Data Dictionary concept using OO - but it's a big task for starters
using the new methodology.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO is fun

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BF0B45.A57AA5E7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <mAnt4.1334$dm5.26510@news.swbell.net> <D9xt4.35348$po2.361772@news1.mia> <38B6A88E.CBE706F9@cusys.edu> <38BD85F7.8351478C@bellsouth.net> <oZgv4.4502$0l.39205@news2.mia> <38BDE300.A0E60193@home.com> <89lul1$qru$1@news.igs.net> <38BE8228.870F913B@cusys.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> > Speaking of which, I am having the most fun with Cobol that I have had for
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cobol in a large environment.

Sorry Howard, but you've got a real hang-up about this 'hidden code'. As
others have pointed out we already use it extensively for things like
date functions. But to concede your point, I prefer 'public domain'
source code so that you can see what the hell it is doing - and the big
plus - seeing others' code gives you tips on how to write your own new
functions, or even sub-class that code to add features particular to
your installation.

This classing business is nothing new to EDP, as Ken Foskey pointed out
in another message, reusability has been a staple of EDP since the
sixties - long before the dreaded initials 'OO'. Yours and many other
installations call pre-canned stuff, which has been well destruction
tested over a period of time.

As regards maintenance, I really see no dramatic difference between
viewing structured or OO. You have my source for OO flat-file handling -
one standard class for each file, which contains all verbs or only those
applicable to this particular file, an intermediary DBI 'topped' by a
generic DBI which they all inherit from. Get these two elements right,
and frankly, the only screw-up that can occur is within the
'business-logic' program in the system.

I'm adapting the same approach to GUIs. Pass parameters to a generic
Dialog Maker (MyDialog), get that right and all the dialogs work. If I
get it wrong the same error will occur in every 'business-logic' program
that calls that routine - you can narrow the maintenance change down to
one class (MyDialog) to make the correction, unless of course the error
starts in your 'business-logic' program.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO is fun

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BFCCDC.E61F0F91@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <mAnt4.1334$dm5.26510@news.swbell.net> <D9xt4.35348$po2.361772@news1.mia> <38B6A88E.CBE706F9@cusys.edu> <38BD85F7.8351478C@bellsouth.net> <oZgv4.4502$0l.39205@news2.mia> <38BDE300.A0E60193@home.com> <89lul1$qru$1@news.igs.net> <38BE8228.870F913B@cusys.edu> <38BF0B45.A57AA5E7@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> > I imagine so.  But what I want to see is how much fun maintenance programmers
> > have.  I bet they like their jobs better with OO than with spaghetti code
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sorry Howard, but you've got a real hang-up about this 'hidden code'.

I guess I do.  Because I in environments I have worked, it has been a major problem.

But my main want is an open question.  I can try out OO in development (and agree that
it can be fun).  I have no idea whether maintenance is better or worse in an OO
environment.  I recognize that it may be hard to find this out.

A shop with the discipline to create an OO environment well designed for maintenance
would probably be well disciplined to do the same thing in a non OO environment.  Or
maybe not, maybe it would slack off because it didn't HAVE to be as well disciplined.
I don't know.

But I want to know.  I want to have facts and figures comparing maintenance costs and
problems for a large shop in an OO environment, with costs in a similar non OO
environment.  I am particularly interested when a shop buys a software package and
integrates it into an existing environment.  That's where we would be likely to
consider going OO or not.  As it is, the current packages we buy are obviously
developed in a style from the mid 1980s, ported over from other languages and/or
databases by entry level programmers. I wince at the code and integrate different
packages with our home built systems.

We wouldn't be moving to an all OO shop (not all at once), but we could easily be
adding OO packages into our environment.  I have to make recommendations about such
packages and methodologies based upon our major cost needs - maintenance.  But
excluding papers done on implementation problems, I haven't seen comparisons on
enterprise level systems.  Theory on how hard or easy maintenance will be isn't
sufficient.  I want to see real life success and/or failure stories for large systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO is fun

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BFF936.A152125A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <mAnt4.1334$dm5.26510@news.swbell.net> <D9xt4.35348$po2.361772@news1.mia> <38B6A88E.CBE706F9@cusys.edu> <38BD85F7.8351478C@bellsouth.net> <oZgv4.4502$0l.39205@news2.mia> <38BDE300.A0E60193@home.com> <89lul1$qru$1@news.igs.net> <38BE8228.870F913B@cusys.edu> <38BF0B45.A57AA5E7@home.com> <38BFCCDC.E61F0F91@cusys.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> sufficient.  I want to see real life success and/or failure stories for large systems.

Point taken. And as you say it really boils down to (whether structured
or OO) how disciplined the design implementation has been. We are not
going to know until such time as there are OO packages available.

My own thoughts are that what we should be looking for are modules not
necessarily packages. One area I can think of that is 'packaged' is
payroll, primarily this is now an area in which banks have introduced
some clout (the objective being to get your money into their coffers).
That invariably presents a situation where you take the whole shebang,
(including any deficiencies), or sit down and write your own.

I can see, say, in inventory systems, where you might be interested in
adding a forecasting module - you are after the mathematical expertise -
as opposed to the merchandising/distribution, which you can design
effectively within your own organization. For the forecasting module -
you've got the same possibility of cock-ups whether structured or OO.

Only time is going to give us the answer. Any maintenance problems that
do arise will result from the complaint that the 'package' or 'module'
was badly designed in the first place - regardless of whether structured
or OO.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO is fun

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C18F1F.20393876@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <mAnt4.1334$dm5.26510@news.swbell.net> <D9xt4.35348$po2.361772@news1.mia> <38B6A88E.CBE706F9@cusys.edu> <38BD85F7.8351478C@bellsouth.net> <oZgv4.4502$0l.39205@news2.mia> <38BDE300.A0E60193@home.com> <89lul1$qru$1@news.igs.net> <38BE8228.870F913B@cusys.edu> <38BF0B45.A57AA5E7@home.com> <38BFCCDC.E61F0F91@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> We wouldn't be moving to an all OO shop (not all at once), but we
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> for large systems.

First a reference.  If you look at the extreme programming site you
will find information about the C4 project.  This project is the
chrysler payroll system.  The system has been in constant development
for two years.

Extreme programming attracted my interest because it does focus on the
maintenance cycle.  How many times have we walked into a new shop to
find that the system documentation was good about three years ago, the
test plans have not been updated since, and finally the original test
data was accidentally deleted 6 months ago and now the backups have
been purged as well.

There have been some spectacular attempts to do OO systems such as the
IBM / Westpac (Australia) CS90 project.  It was OO cobol without the
OO infrastructure, it cost millions and was cancelled with only a very
minor system implemented (term deposits).  This has been repeated in
several attempts.

I think that the first part of a change over is to seriously look at
OO modelling.  This allows you to very the project in a new way but
not necessarily to develop the code in OO terms.  We must remember
that there is a large critical mass of non-OO developers.  This is
going to cause some problems.  I have seen some changes done to an OO
system that made me cringe.  If it was standard code then it was the
way we have all done it.

I think that the main thing that OO gives you that you can probably
see is procedure pointers.  You can change the subroutine or method
that is executed using the same driver code.  The most obvious use of
this is to have the code that handles the file item in a menu.  It
will call the save routine, open routine, etc.  You can create an
object to handle this and simply call the modules that the programmer
defines.  This logic of the main driver is fairly simple that is true,
but why rewrite the same logic many times.

OO is a way of life,  old programming habits are hard to break.  Like
life, once you have given up smoking and starting jogging again you
can look back at your old life and wonder why you did not switch
earlier.  I really think OO is the same.  I am currently working on a
non-OO project and I am frustrated because some easy things in OO are
really hard in procedural.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote in message
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

That's the real difficult part, Don, explaining the "attractiveness" of
OO COBOL - we are both travelling slightly different paths, because of
compilers, but are seeing a tremendous number of advantages, and we are
just on the threshold.

Like you I'm developing a MyDialog to handle "all" of my individual
dialogs (or at least as many features as I can accommodate) in one
called class. N/E has the Smalltalk feature 'dictionaries/classes' so as
I call MyDialog, I create an Ordered Collection and 'attach' each of the
Windows controls to the collection as individual objects, accessing them
by their 'placement' sequence within the collection.

As for your 'theoretical question' - you are asking the wrong guy -
would be nice if either Fujitsu or Merant would respond to your question
in general terms. (Steve in Newbury ???). Taking a stab at what you are
asking re Screen Section - you could view it like a Christmas tree
starting at the 'top' as one object and literally 'branching out' from
there. In OO terminology of course, the tree is viewed bottom to top to
get inheritance. (Does that help ?)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL was Re: cobol for free ??

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89mq4d$atq$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <mAnt4.1334$dm5.26510@news.swbell.net> <D9xt4.35348$po2.361772@news1.mia> <38B6A88E.CBE706F9@cusys.edu> <38BD85F7.8351478C@bellsouth.net> <oZgv4.4502$0l.39205@news2.mia> <38BDE300.A0E60193@home.com> <89lul1$qru$1@news.igs.net> <38BECE55.864BD1DE@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <38BECE55.864BD1DE@home.com>...
>That's the real difficult part, Don, explaining the "attractiveness" of
>OO COBOL - we are both travelling slightly different paths, because of
>compilers, but are seeing a tremendous number of advantages, and we are
>just on the threshold.

Yes.  Finally, my code writing speed is increasing exponentially.
(after thirty years of attempting to be generalized enough to do that)  It
has been a long slow start though.
>
>Like you I'm developing a MyDialog to handle "all" of my individual
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>by their 'placement' sequence within the collection.

I am starting at the system level, and building a data base of program
objects.  Those contain screen objects, which in turn contain item objects.
The item objects that get placed on a given screen contain segments from all
programs of the old system. For example, the "enter customer" box has
attributes that allow everything in the system that can be done to a
customer,  from printing a statement to changing the master record, to
looking up the customer telephone number.

What I am *trying* to do is write code that will extract all that from an
entire legacy system ... in effect read the old source code, parse it, and
construct system wide XREF tables.  Those can be used to extract all code
required for the new object from the old programs, and build an entirely new
"object" with everything that has to do with a customer imbedded in that
object.

My problem is that I have to build the tool set to do it with, and I am
jumping back and forth between very low level objects that I need to build
with to very high level code that can write programs in terms of of those
low level objects.  I am writing a compiler in code that is compiled by the
compiler I am writing. I've got into the habit of backing up after each
successful "compile", as bad versions trash the code needed to fix them.
Great fun.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL was Re: cobol for free ??

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BF06DD.B3CB0D0@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <mAnt4.1334$dm5.26510@news.swbell.net> <D9xt4.35348$po2.361772@news1.mia> <38B6A88E.CBE706F9@cusys.edu> <38BD85F7.8351478C@bellsouth.net> <oZgv4.4502$0l.39205@news2.mia> <38BDE300.A0E60193@home.com> <89lul1$qru$1@news.igs.net> <38BECE55.864BD1DE@home.com> <89mq4d$atq$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote in message <38BECE55.864BD1DE@home.com>...
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> Great fun.

I'm quite sure it is great fun. However, from what you have described I
would suggest it is pretty ambitious, but not impossible, as a first
attempt. (Bear in mind at one stage I used Screen section to generate
menus and popups, so I'm sure this is something you could meld into OO). 

As you have no current model to work from, I think it inevitable it will
crash on you at some stage, which will mean you will need to
re-orientate and take a second crack. But it is by you producing a
reasonably good working model that others can use, that OO COBOL can
progress.

If you reflect on what I've previously written, my file handling was not
my original thought; that goes down to Wilson Price of objectz.com.
Looking at his model, which was based on accessing one flat-file, I was
then able to adapt it to cover all COBOL flat files - hasn't failed me
yet - but I'm not claiming it is the final approach, nor may it be
applicable to the mainframe world without a considerable amount of
tuning. Somebody else taking what I have done can use my model to make
further improvements, and so on, and so on....

And the above implies that we are all producing 'public domain' source
code acceptable to EVERY OO COBOL compiler.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
program is written and maintained entirely on punch cards and compiler
listings.  No editor or on line source of any sort.  You can end up with
every card being filled out all the way to column 72.
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dD8x4.4758$aw3.72465@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <mAnt4.1334$dm5.26510@news.swbell.net> <D9xt4.35348$po2.361772@news1.mia> <38B6A88E.CBE706F9@cusys.edu> <38BD85F7.8351478C@bellsouth.net> <oZgv4.4502$0l.39205@news2.mia> <DmDv4.1125$1i.197050@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <89omve$ghv$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.132d2bbf539a51a9989944@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
Charles F Hankel wrote:
>RUSSELL STYLES wrote:
>>     How many of you remember the sort of mess that can result when a large
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Especially when you're picking it up from the machine room floor.

I never tried to minimize cards by 'filling' them with code.  But
when I was in the USAF I once wrote a very large (three full 36 inch
long metal trays) SPS (IBM 1401 assembly) program once.  There were
only 5 digits for the sequence number, and I wrapped through the
numbers more than 3 times.  I was headed for the computer room to
test the thing when a full colonel abruptly opened a door into the
hall, catching the crook of my elbow.  It looked like real big snow
for a few seconds, as I looked on in horror.  Never before did I have
so many interesting things I wanted to say, but couldn't.  Most of
the program was tables of numbers.  It took me over 12 hours to sort
all the cards by sequence number, then use my latest listing to divide
each set of cards with duplicate sequence numbers into the 4 sets.
```

```
    I must confess that I have never done any PAID (unless you count me
paying them at UGA) using punch cards, but I observed a lot of it goining
around, and have seen ample evidence of same in later years.  The main
reason for 'filling' a card would be to avoid having to repunch cards that
otherwise would not need to be bothered just to keep sequence numbers in
order.  The more typing you do, the greater the chance for typos.

    It is a pity that you could not use all 6 digits, or use cols 73-80 to
help resort the deck.
```

```
All you have to do is to pick them up, align them with the diagonal
stripes you should have drawn on the outside of the deck, and then run
them through the card sorter.  You did use sequence numbers, didn't
you?
```

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c6f61f_4@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <mAnt4.1334$dm5.26510@news.swbell.net> <D9xt4.35348$po2.361772@news1.mia> <38B6A88E.CBE706F9@cusys.edu> <38BD85F7.8351478C@bellsouth.net> <oZgv4.4502$0l.39205@news2.mia> <DmDv4.1125$1i.197050@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <89omve$ghv$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.132d2bbf539a51a9989944@news.mersinet.co.uk> <38c699d8.7177373@news1.frb.gov>`

```
> >>     How many of you remember the sort of mess that can result when a
large
> >> program is written and maintained entirely on punch cards and compiler
> >> listings.  No editor or on line source of any sort.  You can end up
with
> >> every card being filled out all the way to column 72.
> >>     Even well written code can be hard to understand when this happens.
> >Especially when you're picking it up from the machine room floor.

write your program like this:
copy aaa
0000.  move a to b.       go to 0001
0001.  if a > 0 move 0 to c.      go to 0002.
etc.

aaa contains the data division and stuff.
now all you need to do is to have the first two
cars first. They should have a different color,
e.g. red. The rest can be jumbled and the
program will still run.
```

```
See, capitalization counts!
Basque

Basque (bask) noun

1.A member of a people of unknown origin inhabiting the western
Pyrenees and the Bay of Biscay in France and Spain.
2.The language of the Basques, of no known linguistic affiliation.

basque (bask) noun
A woman's close-fitting bodice.

[French, skirt of a garment, alteration (perhaps influenced by Basque,
Basque).

Which kinda reminds me of the opening sentence to a novel:

Sir Carlton had already removed three petticoats, a chemise, a basque,
a corset, two camisoles, and a teddy and, with a lot of linen to go,
growled "Good God, woman, are you nothing but skivvies?"
```

<a id="t-1e3356a600"></a>
## Thread: PIC S9(6)V99 help please — 3 this month / 6 total

_Thread started [2000-02](2000-02.md#t-1e3356a600)._

#### ↳ Re: PIC S9(6)V99 help please

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dumobs48kgar6hsin6rb6n36pps945gcig@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<89glte$vsn$1@minus.oleane.net>`

```
On Tue, 29 Feb 2000 15:43:48 +0100 "Polaire" <p.bureau@dial.oleane.com> wrote:

:>I have to transfert a cobol database.

:>One field have this picture : S9(6)V99

:>I think that S is for 'signed' but in my file one of the record is
:>'p0015115'

:>What 'p' mean. Plus ? or is it an error in the record ?

If the PICTURE specifies "SIGN IS LEADING" the P, having code x'D7' would
indicate that the value is -700151.15. x'C0' and A-I and indicate +0 to +9 and
x'D0' and J-R would indicate -0 to -9. 

Otherwise the value is bad.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PIC S9(6)V99 help please

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89j7us$b5j$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`
- **References:** `<89glte$vsn$1@minus.oleane.net> <dumobs48kgar6hsin6rb6n36pps945gcig@4ax.com>`

```

Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:dumobs48kgar6hsin6rb6n36pps945gcig@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 29 Feb 2000 15:43:48 +0100 "Polaire" <p.bureau@dial.oleane.com>
wrote:
>
> :>I have to transfert a cobol database.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> indicate that the value is -700151.15. x'C0' and A-I and indicate +0 to +9
and
> x'D0' and J-R would indicate -0 to -9.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Director, Dissen Software, Bar & Grill - Israel

Of course the hex value X'D7' only applies if this is EBCDIC.  But letter
equivalents are the same in ASCII and EBCDIC.  So P would indicate -7.   I
don't know what -0 would be in ASCII.  I guess it may still be X'D0'?
```

#### ↳ Re: PIC S9(6)V99 help please

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ji9w4.113$ER3.36296@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<89glte$vsn$1@minus.oleane.net>`

```
You can download a free tutorial on COBOL datatypes by selecting file
COBDATA.ZIP from the Flexus web site at
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html.

One problem you may have, though, is that some of the COBOL datatypes are
not IEEE standard datatypes, so you may not be able to pass convert those
values to another storage product (i.e., database) 'as is.'

But..  I have another free document on converting COBOL data to IEEE data.
That (html) document is not available on-line, so you'll have to write me
for it. And, if you are working in a Windows/32 environment, I have written
a DLL you can call from a "c" program, supplying the PICTURE and USAGE from
the COBOL FD, and the procedure will return the data size; you then pass
that many bytes of data and the DLL returns the value of that data in
multiple IEEE fields. Yes, that's free too, and comes with the (BASIC)
source code to a conversion program showing exactly how to use it. Write for
this, too, if interested.

Michael Mattias
Racine WI
michael.mattias@gte.net

Polaire wrote in message <89glte$vsn$1@minus.oleane.net>...
>I have to transfert a cobol database.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-a50561af0b"></a>
## Thread: Index Usage — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Index Usage

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89ho7m$kmg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I'm maintaining an IBM Mainframe System, written in COBOL2, that has
extensive use of setting the index of table to the index of a
completely different table.

I have always set an intermediate halfword or fullword workarea (in
COBOL) to the first index, followed by setting the second index to the
intermediate workarea.

Is the setting of an index to a completely different index common
practice and will it result in the same displacement value as using the
intermediate method described above ? Or, will it yield inconsistent
displacement results, difficult to pinpoint and/or ascertain ?

All opinions welcome, scathing or otherwise.

TIA....

Cheers,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Index Usage

- **From:** "Mark L. Bartlett" <mbartlet@sound.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BD3666.A0555064@sound.net>`
- **References:** `<89ho7m$kmg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
When you set an intermediate field to an index, the value in the field will
be that of the ordinal occurance position in the table referenced.

If you set the index for table 1 to the index for table 2, the occurance
numbers after the set will be identical.

COBOL stores the index as a byte offest into the referenced table but any
external reference to the value is given the occurance number.

Mark L. Bartlett


WOB Consulting wrote:

> I'm maintaining an IBM Mainframe System, written in COBOL2, that has
> extensive use of setting the index of table to the index of a
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Index Usage

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89n0ok$r2u$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<89ho7m$kmg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38BD3666.A0555064@sound.net>`

```
Mark L. Bartlett <mbartlet@sound.net> wrote in message
news:38BD3666.A0555064@sound.net...
> When you set an intermediate field to an index, the value in the field will
> be that of the ordinal occurance position in the table referenced.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The preceding is true for IBM COBOLs (and I think some others).  HOWEVER,
there is no requirement in the COBOL Standard that it work this way.  It is
true that you can set an integer data-item to an index and then set another
index to that integer-data item to get the same ordinal number.  It is *not*
true, however, that indexes are always stored as offsets or that you will get
"usable" values if you set an index from one table to an index in another
table.  This is most likely to be a problem if the tables have different size
elements, but it MAY give you bad results - even if the elements are the same
size.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Index Usage

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89ocqt$bv2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<89ho7m$kmg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38BD3666.A0555064@sound.net> <89n0ok$r2u$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> The preceding is true for IBM COBOLs (and I think some others).
HOWEVER,
> there is no requirement in the COBOL Standard that it work this way.
It is
> true that you can set an integer data-item to an index and then set
another
> index to that integer-data item to get the same ordinal number.  It
is *not*
> true, however, that indexes are always stored as offsets or that you
will get
> "usable" values if you set an index from one table to an index in
another
> table.  This is most likely to be a problem if the tables have
different size
> elements, but it MAY give you bad results - even if the elements are
the same
> size.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bill,

The "idea" of setting one index to another is dicussed in the IBM
COBOL2 manual, but it eludes to the requirement that the tables must be
of equal to size (as you've pointed out). But, I'm not buying into it
at all.

I agree that this method has its potential "gotcha's" and as I find
each one of these, I'm going to change them to the way I've been doing
it all along, using the intermediate workarea. At least I know this
works correctly since I've been coding it this way since the mid-
seventies and have never encountered a problem. Granted, this method is
less efficient, but we're not talking a great deal of extra
instructions or even a Call to a COBOL Run-Time module.

I'll get over it :-)

Cheers,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-f01f6d9ed1"></a>
## Thread: PIC S9(6)V99 help please — 1 msgs

### Re: PIC S9(6)V99 help please

- **From:** glendsmith@aol.com (GlenDSmith)
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000301182125.03640.00002580@ng-cs1.aol.com>`

```
You mention that only 1 record has this value? Why don't you stop guessing what
the data could be and check out the complete definition which will be found in
the program. You need to know exactly which usage it is. Then you can write the
conversion routine that was mentioned. Move picin to picout etc.

More importantly with cobol records you might not even be on the right data
field. To learn how to compute exact field lengths with mixes of packed,
signed, synchronized etc, you should get yourself the cobol ref manual and
spend an evening reading the section on data formats! It may not be as simple a
problem as you seem to describe.
```

<a id="t-95a5944214"></a>
## Thread: Need help translating from S/390 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Need help translating from S/390

- **From:** "SoftwareTailor" <SoftwareTailor@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OtAPfO$g$GA.89@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
I'm getting some values from a COBOL program, which are formatted as
PIC S9(5)V99.

000000{
000123E
001234F

Can you give me a hint.

TIA

Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: Need help translating from S/390

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89kqjk$2kps$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<OtAPfO$g$GA.89@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```

SoftwareTailor <SoftwareTailor@email.msn.com> wrote in message
news:OtAPfO$g$GA.89@cpmsnbbsa02...
> I'm getting some values from a COBOL program, which are formatted as
> PIC S9(5)V99.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bob

What you show are the EBCDIC representations of:
+0
+12.35
+123.46
The hexadecimal representations are:
X'F0F0F0F0F0F0C0'
X'F0F0F0F1F2F3C5'
X'F0F0F1F2F3F4C6'
Hex F0 thru F9 display as 0 thru 9;
Hex C0 thru C9 display as {ABCDEFGHI;
Hex D0 thru D9 display as }JKLMNOPQR;
A 'C' in the left half of the low-order byte indicates a positive sign;
A 'D' in the left half of the low-order byte indicates a negative sign;
An 'F' in the left half of the low-order byte indicates unsigned.
```

<a id="t-18f308161c"></a>
## Thread: cobol programming — 12 this month / 54 total

_Thread started [2000-02](2000-02.md#t-18f308161c)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BF6243.A413924C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Gods4.25750$po2.257991@news1.mia> <88s0p1$80l$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <l1is4.26403$po2.265329@news1.mia>`

```


Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> One of the points I made at the time was that it is a pretty stupid policy
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> complete waste of time, from my personal perspective. <sigh>

As usual Judson I agree with the majority of what you've written but
disagree about the OO bit. As regards the C-itification of the language,
that I'm sure is a dubious area. Looking at COBOL as a systems designer
rather than a hot-shot programmer, I am extremely leery of messing
around with usage of memory, particularly if you use the word pointer in
its COBOL definition, rather than in general terms as a reference 'which
points to something' - and if that is the area you are kicking at, then
I totally agree.

Tim Josling has rightly complained that the 'new' COBOL offers so many
features, it is becoming a real challenge to write an effective
compiler. Similarly, Bill has stressed it contains so many features, he
doubts on average that most of us wont use some 50% of what's available.

OO offers you another methodology to do things, and truly, I don't think
it bloats the language; 90% plus of its usage is traditional COBOL
usage, if you exclude GUI classes. One thing I don't like, but so much
of the software is going that route, is that it demands you carry around
a lot of baggage in the sense of 'support classes' written by the
compiler developers.

For my money I never considered COBOL a truck, a good workhorse maybe, 
but with a few enhancements it can be used as a racing stallion - why
let these 'newbie' languages take over. If you thoughts are coloured at
all by looking at the NetExpress demos - chuck 'em out the window, (the
demos, not your thoughts :-) ). Unfortunately they attempt to show you
every conceivable feature of OO that they can embrace in one demo and
you get lost in the process. It is only by usage that I found there can
be tremendous shortcuts, same technology they are demonstrating but with
shortcuts. As Don Tees as indicated, he's finding it fun, not 'fun to
play with', but fun in the sense that it is a joy to work in COBOL and
OO is illustrating new avenues for him to use COBOL, his language of
first choice, in a much more effective way and simplify his coding.

As regards your comments about COBOL taking on a C-image I can't comment
- I don't know the language. But as regards Smalltalk it would probably
be true to say that we've embraced the invoke "method-name" concept and
certainly within NetExpress collections/dictionaries - BUT - the rest of
Smalltalk has been ignored - we are already well armoured on text
handling pic x/pic 9 etc - and Smalltalk treating all strings as objects 
has represented no benefit to COBOL and therefore has not been included
in our standard.

Whichever way you plunk, you can stay completely structured, or mix and
match as you see fit, providing Screen Section for Customer A or GUIs
for Customer B, both without any OO or both with OO.
 
Your concerns are legit about the possibility of it going ass over head
because of the 'bloating effect' you refer to - but I'm hoping sincerely
that your misgivings are dead wrong. Only time will tell.

> Bill, by the time the new standard is finalized, and more changes are
> considered, I will probably be retired, or even dead.  It would be a
> complete waste of time, from my personal perspective. <sigh>

Wanna bet - I'll meet the Great Reaper before you do. Assuming after
Newbury we get COBOL 2001 - that means release earliest 2002 (that makes
me 71); somewhere around 2004 they'll be discussing the next Standard,
assuming they've been able to clear the air on standard/public domain
classes for OO in the time span 2001 - 2004. Now they release COBOL
2005/6, (I'm getting up there now 74/75). If they delay it beyond that
I'll probably have gone ga-ga anyway, and apart from which I'll probably
need a white stick to feel where my screen is :-)

Just think, 2006 you will still be a mere youth, even if you do have
grandkids !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BEE813.E31A7423@home.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> OK,  let me try and summarize:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<snip>

The above contains a real flaw because ideas are 'nationalized'. Cheesle
proposes something to his Norwegian confreres, Judson in the States
likes it, as does yours truly up here in the Great White North. Hell,
even Pete Dashwood in NZ with his aversion to bureaucratic BS also likes
Cheelse's idea. If Cheesle can't persuade his Norwegian brethren, it
doesn't go forward. Only alternative, he tells us that it aint going
nowhere in Norway, so Judson, myself and Pete try and wing it across on
our national groups - now if that aint bureaucratic BS...... 

I don't know how others feel, but this is where I think Bill could
prepare a draft indicating our (CLC viewers) thoughts about J4/WG4
processes - he has a pretty clear idea of our views already ????

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89n0v1$ovq$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Gods4.25750$po2.257991@news1.mia> <88s0p1$80l$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <38B1A23E.2112064C@melbpc.org.au> <88sf73$56p$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <38BEE813.E31A7423@home.com>`

```
The point is that as long as the Standard is being developed as an ISO
Standard, then the input ABSOLUTELY POSITIVELY *must* come from the different
national bodies.  If you think that the COBOL Standard should *not* be
developed as an ISO Standard (and I have some sympathy with that point of
view), then *THAT* needs to be communicated to ISO *via* your national body
organization.
```

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:38BEE813.E31A7423@home.com...
>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > As far as WG4 goes (the ISO-ish group), individuals are NEVER welcome
to
> > submit comments or input documents.  Instead what you need to do is to
send
> > something to your "national body" (or its WG4 membership) for THEM to
submit
> > to the group.  I have frequently put out a web-page where you can find
your
> > national body, but if you have trouble finding information on this,
send me
> > private email and I will help you. As far as I know most (almost all?)
of the
> > active WG4 member countries are QUITE willing to pass on individual
comments
> > for the entire committee to consider - but this is a "country by
country"
> > decision and there is no guarantee of this. (Only your specific
national body
> > can tell you how to provide input via this channel.)
> >
> > This restriction on WG4 input applies at all time but is particularly
> > important during "official" public review periods.  You need to submit
your
> > comments to your country and they will submit a "single" country ballot
on
> > the revision.
> >
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

I hate to include all of the above, but I believe you ALL have hit upon the
problem. This problem has several names that I'll try to avoid, but it
boils down to the fact that there is distain among groups about working
together thus the committee problem.

I'm the first to agree that committees take time and involving a lot of
people in defining something is at best difficult. However, the nature of
most people is to let someone else do it, and pick at it if it doesn't
satisfy.  Sort of committee work in absentcia (SP).

Consequently, I believe that you people are in a place where you have a
golden opportunity to do something as a community.  Even become the same as
the old COBOL Committee.  There is a void. Fill it.

Warren Simmons
w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a15ev$fhd$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <38BDE629.18179068@home.com> <gUQv4.5759$6o.57810@news3.mia> <MPG.132d28f92c46f74e989941@news.mersinet.co.uk> <38C3357A.6941359A@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:38C3357A.6941359A@home.com...

>
> So let's re-address my question 'cos he obviously missed it. Sir
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Mostly NO.  Several of the members of J4 and/or WG4 have and/or do teach at
universities or politechnics (part time).  One active member of WG4 is a
full-time professor.  During the "Public Review" period of the '85 Standard,
there were a couple of EXCELLENT comment papers submitted by European
Universities (and possibly MacGill in Canada?).  HOWEVER, they do not
actively participate in the development of new proposals (as far as I know).
```

```
Your best bet for this type of application is any one of many ActiveX
controls that you can call from your COBOL program (depending on your
COBOL vendor).  If you have sp2 (A GUI tool from
http://www.flexus.com) you can use ActiveX controls (with an sp2
addon) from any 32bit COBOL.  http://www.ulead.com offers some of the
more robust and capable tools.
```

<a id="t-5bea89b39d"></a>
## Thread: Need help translating from S/390 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Need help translating from S/390

- **From:** hancock4@bbs.cpcn.com (Lisa nor Jeff)
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89mudm$s8q@netaxs.com>`

```
The right most character is an "overpunch" for the sign field.  It's
there because the field is defined with an "S" (signed).  I presume
you're getting this via a DISPLAY statement.  If you were to print
it out using numeric edited with a sign character, it would look
correct.
```

## Thread: TCS2000: Testing Computer Software Conference — 1 msgs

### TCS2000: Testing Computer Software Conference

- **From:** uspdi@earthlink.net (USPDI)
- **Date:** 2000-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<uspdi-0103001944580001@ip197.washington13.dc.pub-ip.psi.net>`

```

     PARTICIPATE IN THE CONFERENCE THAT WILL LET YOU MASTER THE
  CHALLENGES OF FUTURE TRENDS AND DEVELOPMENTS IN SOFTWARE TESTING!

  Mark your calendar for the 17th annual event. The final Conference
program is available on the Web at:

                 http://www.uspdi.org/conference

  Brochure requests and downloading the printed brochure can also be done
from this web site.

  The detailed printed brochure is available. You may request the brochure
and other information by e-mail to: admin@uspdi.org
  
++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

            17th International Conference and Exposition on

                      TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE
         Theme: Testing Technology vs. Testers' Requirements

                         June 12-16, 2000
          Washington, D.C. * Hyatt Regency (Bethesda) * USA
      (Conference: June 14-16; Workshops/Tutorials: June 12-13)

+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++ 

IN COOPERATION WITH: 

Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) SIGSoft
American Society for Quality (ASQ) Software Division
IEEE Reliability Society
Software Technology Support Center (STSC) 

CONFERENCE PROGRAM BY: 
Frontier Technologies, Inc.

CONFERENCE MANAGEMENT BY: 
U.S. Professional Development Institute

+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

Message from Conference Chair, Genevieve Houston-Ludlam:

            TESTING TECHNOLOGY VS. TESTERS' REQUIREMENTS

Technology for testing and automated solutions have grown remarkably in
the past decade. But the question is still before us -- do we have the
support from technology that we need? Has technology solved the right
problems? Is the technology accessible enough to today's tester? What are
the pitfalls of using such technology? This year, we will be looking at
the experiences with various aspects of testing technology -- some
successful, and some not.

We look forward to having you share your experiences and learn from
others. I look forward to seeing you there.

+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

                       ABOUT THE CONFERENCE

SCOPE AND PURPOSE

  The Seventeenth International Conference on TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE
will provide an opportunity to participate in the leading international
conference on software testing. The goal of the 2000 Conference will be to
explore which testing technologies we will need to take into the new
millennium and which ones we will not. Learn how testing professionals and
experts have used advanced methods and testing applications to improve the
effectiveness of their testing, reduce testing costs and meet schedule
deadlines. Share your own experiences in testing with others. You will be
able to exchange knowledge and ideas with people from a wide variety of
environments and locations including those from industry, academia and
government from the United States and abroad. 

  Conference tracks will address topics concerning the management of
software testing, the application of a variety of software testing
techniques, testing case studies, and additional topics that will
introduce more advanced ideas.

  This conference will provide essential information to all who are
involved with or have an interest in software testing. This includes not
only practitioners but managers and researchers as well. 

  The Seventeenth International Conference on Testing Computer Software is
where testing practitioners and managers from the commercial and
applications software community can learn:

* The impact of increasingly complex software on testing
* Ways to improve the testing process
* Practical experience with the various techniques
* Which efforts have been most successful
* Which efforts have been least successful.

  The conference brings together a set of experienced software testers to
share their experiences and what they have learned from them. You will
have the opportunity to hear about effective software testing techniques,
methods and technologies that have benefited the organizations where they
are used.

GENERAL SESSIONS and FEATURED SPEAKERS:

"From Art to Practice: Transferring Testing (and Other) Technology" by
   Shari Lawrence Pfleeger
"When Helping Doesn't Help: Software Testing as Codependent Behavior" by
   Lee Copeland
"Testing Object-Oriented (OO) Systems: Lessons Learned" by Bob Binder
"Is an Ounce of Test-plan Worth a Ton of Tools?" by Simon Mills
"Powerful and Pitiful Measures of Software" by Tom Gilb
"Luncheon Address: If I Knew Then What I Know Now" by Louise Tamres
"Wrap-up Session: Conference Reflections" by Marnie Hutcheson

PRE-CONFERENCE WORKSHOPS and TUTORIALS (June 12-13) will present
comprehensive technical sessions for software testing practitioners:

        WORKSHOPS (2 days)
1. "MITS Test Management Method" by Marnie Hutcheson
2. "Practical Guide to Software Test Management: Task and People Issues"
    by Lloyd Roden
3. "Test Automation" by Mark Fewster

        TUTORIALS (1 day)
4. "Testing Distributed Systems" by Lee Copeland
5. "Overview of Testing" by Simon Mills and Louise Tamres
6. "Advanced Testing Tactics: Unconventional Methods to Complement
    and Supplement" by Tom Gilb
7. "Testing Object Oriented (OO) Systems" by Lee Copeland
8. "Fundamentals of Testing Software-Intensive Systems" by Selim Aissi
9. "Specification-Based Testing" by Jens Grabowski and Thomas Walter

CONCURRENT SESSIONS will be divided into four parallel track sessions,
presenting a variety of management, technical and advanced topics and
issues.

       MANAGEMENT TRACK
Managing Automated Software Testing and the Web
Building a QA and Testing Organization from Scratch
The Pitfalls and Benefits of Introducing Test Automation Early in
  Project Development
Managing Test Automation
Experiences with Risk Based Testing
Rules Governing Testing of Web Based Systems
"Rechecking the Test Software" -- An ISO Enigma and a Practical Solution
Management Definition and Control of the Benchmark for Quality Improvement
BEFORE Testing

       ADVANCED TOPICS
Advanced Integration Testing of Object-Oriented (OO) Programs
Issues and Approaches in Testing Mission-Critical COTS Systems
A Performance Evaluation of Microcontrollers for Engine Control
Mobile Agent Genetic Algorithms: Routing Test in a Telecommunication
   Network Simulator
Continuous Testing
Integrated Technical Computing Environments as a Tool for Testing
Algorithmically Complicated Software
Automated Failure Modeling of Embedded Systems
When 100% Coverage Isn't

       PRACTITIONER
Testing Access Control Systems in Chip Cards
TTCN3 -- A New Test Specification Language for Black-Box Testing of
   Distributed Systems
Using Dependency Charts to Improve Scenario-Based Testing
Software Usability Assessment and Improvement
Challenges of System Test: Beyond Pass/Fail 
Functional Testing of the International Space Station
Emerging Test Technologies: Shifting Tester Requirements
Industrial Experience in Using Formal Methods for Object Oriented (OO)

       AUTOMATION 
Software Design for Testability and Test Automation
Automated GUI Testing at ANSYS
Testing Automation: Fighting the Ever-changing Devil
Automated Console Port Testing
Life After Death: An Update on Testing Standards for the New Millennium
It's a Bug's Life
The Automated Testing Life-Cycle Methodology (ATLM)
Getting More From Existing Tests

VENDOR EXHIBIT of products and services will provide valuable support
services and software information to your computer software testing
efforts.

CONFERENCE VOLUME A set of conference proceedings, papers and view graphs
will be provided to each participant of the Conference. These materials
are useful not only during the Conference as learning guides, but also as
useful references when the conference is completed.

NETWORKING:

Birds-of-a-Feather Sessions allow you to meet and network with the
speakers and other conference attendees, and discuss areas of mutual
interest.

Lunch Discussion Groups will facilitate the exploration of common interest
topics among the participants.

SPECIAL EVENT:
 
CAPITOL STEPS (Thursday, June 15, 2000 * 6:00 pm)

The Capitol Steps will provide an evening performance of entertainment.
Featured will be the travails of testers, a bipartisan musical political
satire of the 2000 election year, and other events taking place in the
nation's capital and elsewhere.

CONFERENCE LOCATION:

The Testing Computer Software Conference will be held in suburban
Washington, D.C., USA, a major center for government agencies and
educational institutions. The Conference location will be the Hyatt
Regency Hotel in Bethesda, Maryland, easily accessible from interstate
highways or the Metro subway. There is a convenient subway stop beneath
the hotel with elevator access. Washington, D.C. is served by three major
international airports. The Washington area also offers many extraordinary
museums, memorials, parks and other attractions for visitors. 

                                * * * * *

For additional information please contact: 

Regular Mail: USPDI, 612 Ethan Allen Avenue, Suite 100;
              Takoma Park, MD 20912-5400, USA.
Phone:        301-270-1033
Fax:          301-270-1040 
E-mail:       admin@uspdi.org (Subject: TCS2000)
Web:          http://www.uspdi.org/conference

                                * * * * *
```
