```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> and asking for the "idea" to be discussed at J4.

Bill, I think the idea of everyone putting in their oar is
non-productive. Even assuming folks did write to Don, he really doesn't
want to be inundated with a 1,000 messages saying the same thing, or in
a slightly different flavour.

People in the group do have 'pet' issues - let them thrash a particular
interest around in a sub-group and see if they can come up with a
generally agreed idea as to how they see an improvement could be made.
Then, by all means, you could jump in, indicate previous history,
rejection or modification of the idea etc... Then they can discuss do
they still want to 'push' for this idea regardless of previous
rejections. Then by all means, don you Horace Rumpole outfit and flesh
it out for the folks so it is in a nice convoluted form for J4/WG4 to
read in a style they understand :-)

OK here's one for starters - I don't currently use Screen Section having
gotten into %%$$!! Windows tied in with OO - BUT - I am very sympathetic
to Judson's idea of a common set, (and I would suggest a limited set) of
'Screen controls' in COBOL Screen Section, which overrides Thane's
sensible observation, 'but we are looking at a moving target'. Cheesle
the AcuCOBOL 'dual screen' has to surely be the basis for what Judson is
after.

Short quote re the above, having quickly browsed a book which arrived 
to-day :-

'(a) - Keep design really, really simple. Cutting-edge controls are
great but.....  keep the cool and obscure controls to a minimum.....
(b) - stick to basics, don't go overboard shifting them around.....
(c) - use role model for consistency of style (looks like, feels
like)...
(d) - don't use a bunch of crazy colours.... 
(e) - At times desirable and highly appropriate to deviate from the 
norm (i.e. don't slavishly follow the Windows model).....
(f)....... and goes on an on........

The book ? Waite Group's Visual Basic 6 Super Bible - seems I've heard
that name before - isn't VB a Microsoft product ???

Jimmy
```

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
> >
> > I have said it before and I'll say it again.  It really REALLY does
matter if
> > each individual (not a group) submits their ideas.  Getting them
submitted to
> > J4 is as easy as sending an email note to
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Believe what you will - but I don't believe that any "combined" input will
have nearly the value of LOTS of individual comments (not just "copied" but
reflecting the individual input on the same topic).  I think that any
comments on extended the Accept/Display/Screen Section (that is already in
the draft) should make it clear where you stand on the following issues:

1) Do you think this next revision should be delayed until the GUI stuff
could be added?

2) If the revision doesn't wait until the GUI portion is developed, do you
think that the character based stuff should still be included?

3) If you are thinking of an "enhancment" for a future Standard (not the
revision under work right now), then make that clear (and be aware that it
will PROBABLY not even be looked at for a couple of years)

4) What is your "view" on OO Standard class library versus
Accept/Display/Screen Section support for GUIs (in some future Standard)?  Do
you want BOTH - or just one and if just one, which one (and why)?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D1E27E.8BE335D8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <7j43cson8697j65qdemgviu001u28gnqge@4ax.com> <38C1F245.BCE36D2B@home.com> <jjt5cskgfmu26ko4etdpk04ivmpgnk24oc@4ax.com> <8a16i4$l5k$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <1398cskr7docc5o2cg8qccagqbu2tmaep5@4ax.com> <38C531C9.5378276@home.com> <qRax4.4645$5Y2.70748@news3.mia> <Zkgx4.2615$HN.138687@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> the draft) should make it clear where you stand on the following issues:


OK Bill - you are on - this is what I will write to Don :-

> 1) Do you think this next revision should be delayed until the GUI stuff
> could be added?

No <repeat> No - it should continue as currently planned. I would like
to see a new COBOL 2000X before I die ! Imperfect or not go with what
we've got. Don't delay the issue any further.
 

> 2) If the revision doesn't wait until the GUI portion is developed, do you
> think that the character based stuff should still be included?

Yes it should - because there are (as indicated in the NewsGroup) people
using character - off the top of my head, Judson, Richard Plinston and
Don experimenting, combining with Fujitsu OO.
 
> 3) If you are thinking of an "enhancment" for a future Standard (not the
> revision under work right now), then make that clear (and be aware that it
> will PROBABLY not even be looked at for a couple of years)

That's the bit that really bothers me - are you implying they already
have a shopping list (apart from OO and the 'Finalizer' problem) which
carries them through to May 2002 (basing that on Newbury this coming
May) ? 

> 4) What is your "view" on OO Standard class library versus
> Accept/Display/Screen Section support for GUIs (in some future Standard)?  Do
> you want BOTH - or just one and if just one, which one (and why)?

Tricky, tricky. People should not be 'forced' into using OO GUIs. Given
an adequate set of standard controls, and OO's ability to add additional
classes, then my preference would be for OO GUIs - but please compiler
vendors a VERY COMPREHENSIVE set of examples showing how to use the damn
things. But again, we shouldn't jeopardize text mode users.

Now having said that (Jeez, I'm sounding like you :-), what can we
anticipate as a result. OK I'll accept that Don is up to his proverbial
in alligators, first priority Newbury, but on a separate issue when can
Ken and I realistically expect a response to our volunteering to put in
our two cent's worth on OO ? Now don't tell me I've got to chase him to
remind him - because if so, then the bloody system is BROKE !

It would be interesting to know how the system works, so grade my
guesstimate on a rating from 1 - 10 :-

- (a) They use Roberts Rules of Order to conduct meetings - oh my gawd !
Washington DC yes - but in business ???

- (b) "Let's read and approve last minutes" - somebody nit-picks,
wanting to cross 'i's and dot 't's - a good half hour wasted, if not
more.

- (c) Every topic is followed with a proposed, seconded, show of hands

- (d) there's always one of them Bill - some jerk takes an issue and
takes the meeting off at a tangent

- (e) 'Physically' you meet very infrequently - hence you've got to cram
as much as possible into a one day session - and damn well feel mentally
drained when it's finished - and probably much is carried over as being
inconclusive.

- (f) - I sincerely hope that the J4 committee, as an example, doesn't
try to tackle every topic; if topics are not covered by sub-committees
you are on a hiding to nothing. There is no way the main committee can
absorb all the issues in COBOL - just consider that 'Finalizer' jazz -
not knocking people, but would suggest it truly is beyond the ken of
many - with a direct interest in the topic I had to read really hard
what those two Japanese gentlemen had written - and they seemed to know
their stuff.

So what's my grade teach' ? Pass or otherwise ? If you mark me high -
then I would suggest, before anybody does any more standardizing, that
they take a real serious look at how their organization is structured.

Hope your ears aren't plugged - I'll be 'bending' them at Newbury, given
half a chance :-)

PS: Judson - join the fray. Hell ! I'm happy with OO GUIs - it's you
that wants the Screen Section text :-)

Jimmy
```

```
How would you implement a screen section in an IBM (or other vendor)
mainframe environment?  Will it only work with CICS shops?  Will it work
with client server environments?  Will it work with a mainframe as a Web
server?

This is a serious question hoping to find a way from stopping us from
having two Cobols, PC Cobol and mainframe Cobol.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D2C3C6.FF57D381@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <jjt5cskgfmu26ko4etdpk04ivmpgnk24oc@4ax.com> <8a16i4$l5k$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <1398cskr7docc5o2cg8qccagqbu2tmaep5@4ax.com> <38C531C9.5378276@home.com> <qRax4.4645$5Y2.70748@news3.mia> <Zkgx4.2615$HN.138687@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1E27E.8BE335D8@home.com> <38D24241.F2D5A696@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> How would you implement a screen section in an IBM (or other vendor)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> having two Cobols, PC Cobol and mainframe Cobol.

There should a base guaranteed set (similar to ISPF):

1.  Text fields
2.  Buttons to push  (where is cursor when enter is pressed on
mainframe)
3.  Menus that pop up. (mainframe / Windows top line X does it
slightly differently)
4.  Selection lists  (emulated on mainframe with PF key and careful
design).
5.  Screen close, minimize, windowing (simple split on mainframe as
per ISPF).
6. Standard messages:  Close, scroll up, scroll down, help.
7.  Window size information.
8.  Context sensitive help (where is the cursor).

True GUI would be a superset of this, image handling, mouse position,
etc.

The problems would be that Gui would handle a 90 character scrolling
screen field and IBM just would not work well.  Positioning tends to
be system specific (characters on MVS, dots on screen in windows,
etc.).

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b0qas$jdf$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1E27E.8BE335D8@home.com> <38D24241.F2D5A696@cusys.edu> <tpi4dso5tsol3m09rms9v8ndpudnaotls5@4ax.com>`

```
This will be handled by the revision by the use of the Standard "conditional
compilation" facility.  It doesn't give you a way of "testing" which compiler
you are using, but if you set up your own "parameter" on the compile you can
(will be able to) code something like:

>>IF compiler = "mainframe"
     Perform xyz
>>ELSE
      Perform ABC
>>END-IF

This is not a run-time test of the compiler but actually tells the compiler
ONLY to compile (treat as if coded) the relevant part of your source code.
```

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
>
> > 3) If you are thinking of an "enhancment" for a future Standard (not the
> > revision under work right now), then make that clear (and be aware that
it
> > will PROBABLY not even be looked at for a couple of years)
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> May) ?

This comment definitely requires a response.  The "procedures" required to
move from the current draft to an approved Standard will take ALMOST all the
(meeting) time for the next 2 years.  There are both ANSI and ISO rules
concerning the processing of comments that are extremely time consuming.

Having said that, I do expect SOME initial work to be proceding on:

A) definition of what will be in the "Standard Class library" technical
report (I expect serious work on this to being in the 2nd half of 2001 - if
there are no surprises during the review process of the current revision).

B) There are BUNCHES of "interpretation requests" on the current ('85)
Standard that are still outstanding and J4 has "put on the back burner"
during development work on the revision.  I *think* that some time will be
spent on trying to clear these up - before the next Standard becomes
"official".

  ***

HOWEVER, it is still worth getting your ideas into J4 (and more importantly
into WG4) on what priorities you think should be next on their schedules).
There simply is a (relatively) long "lead time" - but if you don't submit
your ideas now, they will be too late for the next work.
```

- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <7j43cson8697j65qdemgviu001u28gnqge@4ax.com> <38C1F245.BCE36D2B@home.com> <jjt5cskgfmu26ko4etdpk04ivmpgnk24oc@4ax.com> <8a16i4$l5k$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <1398cskr7docc5o2cg8qccagqbu2tmaep5@4ax.com> <38C531C9.5378276@home.com> <qRax4.4645$5Y2.70748@news3.mia> <Zkgx4.2615$HN.138687@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> 1) Do you think this next revision should be delayed until the GUI stuff
> could be added?
> 

This has been something I've been wondering about.  In the mainframe
world most shops I've seen are reluctant to upgrade compilers.  They
don't want to convert all existing programs (expensive to do and test),
so they end up with lots of versions (expensive to maintain).  

I think I need to get a better idea on how the world would accept
continuous upgrades vs being left behind because we will NEVER finish
developing technology.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <38C1F245.BCE36D2B@home.com> <jjt5cskgfmu26ko4etdpk04ivmpgnk24oc@4ax.com> <8a16i4$l5k$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <1398cskr7docc5o2cg8qccagqbu2tmaep5@4ax.com> <38C531C9.5378276@home.com> <qRax4.4645$5Y2.70748@news3.mia> <Zkgx4.2615$HN.138687@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu>`

```
I noticed that Howard Brazee as howard.brazee@cusys.edu said...
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
> > 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> so they end up with lots of versions (expensive to maintain).  

It was only the advent of LE that caused any major ructions like this 
and likely more to do with y2k than LE per se.  Before then, we just 
changed to a new compiler and ran the programs through it when they were 
changed or developed, thus most sites ended up with a fair mix of source 
types.  There was no need to recompile every program just to cater to the 
new compiler.
 
> I think I need to get a better idea on how the world would accept
> continuous upgrades vs being left behind because we will NEVER finish
> developing technology.

As you said, there's a real danger of ending up with two or more versions 
of COBOL.

There is one scenario that I plain don't understand.  It was obvious that 
the 3270s were only enhanced with GDDM for graphic presentations and that 
was, let's face it, not the best solution regardless of how appropriate 
it may have been when designed.  When Java came along, my thought was 
that we'd see a series of replacements for the 3270 terminal series with 
Java onboard and that we'd be presenting data to these new terminals that 
would be interpreted by the Java-based terminal speedily, efficiently and 
with something to satisfy the aesthete.

Instead, what I see in actuality is a proliferation of crappy VB-based 
GUI presentations on networked PCs that the ordinary users don't even 
like.  Why don't they like them?  First, the change has been imposed on 
them; secondly, they are invariably slower and occasionally less 
functional than the text-based 3270 screens that they had before; and, 
thirdly, the users find that their job takes longer all for the sake of 
something that is purely cosmetic.  OK, it's a wild generalisation but 
I've witnessed all three of these and usually concurrently.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <jjt5cskgfmu26ko4etdpk04ivmpgnk24oc@4ax.com> <8a16i4$l5k$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <1398cskr7docc5o2cg8qccagqbu2tmaep5@4ax.com> <38C531C9.5378276@home.com> <qRax4.4645$5Y2.70748@news3.mia> <Zkgx4.2615$HN.138687@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu> <MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
Charles F Hankel wrote:
>
>Instead, what I see in actuality is a proliferation of crappy VB-based
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>I've witnessed all three of these and usually concurrently.

This is my observation as well.  This breakneck race toward 'GUI Only'
reminds me of (pardon me) a bunch of sheep, or lemmings.  "Everybody
says it is Always Better, so it must always be better!"  Reminds me of
the fashion industry. ;-)  No tool ever made by man has ever been
"Always Better".  Spending billions of dollars and untold billions of
manhours on cosmetics and flash is pretty drastic action, in my opinion.
I have yet to see a situation where a GUI approach to typical prosaic
business applications, such as entering payroll or inventory data, is
more cost efficient than a text based one.  Drop down menus, combo
boxes, and other features can improve training, but you can do a pretty
good job with character based interfaces too.  I made a character based
help system that allows the user to get online help on any field on a
screen, search tables of codes (by code or description, try that with a
combo box with 20,000 codes!), and select one which is automatically
placed in the screen field, etc.  You can drop it into an application
easily, and the users can update the help information on the fly.  And
nobody but nobody can move a mouse and click a button faster than they
can type a character; it takes at least ten times more time and energy.
I don't view the character based interface as obsolete, but as the
simple and efficient form of interface, with GUI being the more complex
and flexible interface.  By all means, use GUI where it is needed or
truly helpful, but don't abandon all character based interfaces just
because they aren't as flashy.  For many uses, GUI is not needed, and
actually less desirable. :-)
```

```
I noticed that RUSSELL STYLES as RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM said...
>     Things might be better when dealing with "internal" stuff (Ie any
> program that does not need to
> be marketed), but I fail to see the problem with a "crappy" VB-based user
> interface.

The systems that I deal with are almost always in-house developments 
although I have been involved in packages as well.  My point, as with 
Judson's is that a GUI is fine for some applications but for systems used 
by people who know what they're doing, the text-based system is hard to 
better.
 
>     Ask your marketing department which would they prefer to sell:
> 1. A fast, well written, stable character mode program that does everything
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     They can probably make 10 times as much money on the GUI.

Mainly because most of the people they are selling to are not the users 
themselves, or not "expert" users.  More often than not, decision makers 
have little or no experience of the job in hand and are easily swayed by 
buzzwords and window-dressing.

>     Before you flame, I agree with the writers below, personnally.  However,
> if marketing can not sell ...

True - I just wish that more actual users were asked before management 
makes decisions that affects them.  Still, it's not a clear blue sky 
every day, is it?
```

- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <38C531C9.5378276@home.com> <qRax4.4645$5Y2.70748@news3.mia> <Zkgx4.2615$HN.138687@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu> <MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk> <yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```

Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote in message
news:MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk...
> I noticed that RUSSELL STYLES as RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM said...
> >     Things might be better when dealing with "internal" stuff (Ie any
> > program that does not need to
> > be marketed), but I fail to see the problem with a "crappy" VB-based
user
> > interface.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> better.

There are several benefits to GUI front-end systems.

The GUI application tends to be (semi-)intelligent. The I/O traffic may be
reduced because the user is prevented from entering incorrect, or
inconsistent data by the terminal.

There are limitations to how much data can be processed on a single screen.
A Customer data entry process may have 4 screens in a text based system. A
GUI application can handle all the data in one shot. This should reduce the
number of modules needed to process the Customer data.

> >     Ask your marketing department which would they prefer to sell:
> > 1. A fast, well written, stable character mode program that does
everything
> > the user wants.
> > 2. A slow, unstable, buggy GUI that is low on features.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I worked on a Purchase Order package for the vendor.
I would meet with the User Group Steering Committee at least twice a year.
We would discuss possible improvements to the system, and set priorities for
their implementation. ALL the members of the committee were users of the
system (Purchasing Managers, or Accounting). Believe me, they were not
swayed by window-dressing. They wanted features, and functions that would
improve their job performance.

Having said that, I should also point out that software marketing has
decided to move up the food chain when trying to sell products. If the
person they are targeting isn't smart enough to solicit the opinions of the
system users, they deserve what they get.
```

```
DennisHarley wrote:
>Charles F Hankel wrote:
>> RUSSELL STYLES wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>There are several benefits to GUI front-end systems.

I don't think any of us are questioning this.  The issue is whether
or not GUI should be the *only* approach, or would many applications
work as well, or better, with character mode interfaces. :-)

>The GUI application tends to be (semi-)intelligent. The I/O traffic may be
>reduced because the user is prevented from entering incorrect, or
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>number of modules needed to process the Customer data.

The above points are more a function of system architecture than GUI
or character based interfaces.  After all, if you put the intelligence
to accept and validate data on the client PC, then why could it not
have a character based interface to do the job?  Right now, it would
only be because of a lack of character based tools. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 36)_

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eKDVOJfk$GA.214@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <Zkgx4.2615$HN.138687@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu> <MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk> <yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk> <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <Ol6B4.108$l34.9414@news3.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:Ol6B4.108$l34.9414@news3.mia...
>
> The above points are more a function of system architecture than GUI
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> --
A character based interface can be GUI.
There is no requirement to use Drop Downs, Spin Controls etc.
In many instances they are unnecessary, and sometimes impractical.
You don't want a drop down list for 250,000 customers.

I do character based presentations in CICS. It is a royal pain to change
screen maps.
Programming for getting data in bits and pieces is more challenging than it
has to be.
If I can process all the data in one shot, I can concentrate on the business
logic, rather than the administration of 4 related screens.

A good VB, C++ etc. programmer can produce quality presentations of data
much faster than I can code CICS maps, and the supporting programs. I can
pass a Customer Master record to the desktop, and let them present it 100
different ways.

I don't believe that we should place the enforcement of business rules on
the desktop. Data has to be verified before it is placed in the system. The
verification should be done at  the data storage level. If the files are on
a server, use the facilities on the server to provide a final check that the
data is good.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> The above points are more a function of system architecture than GUI
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> only be because of a lack of character based tools. :-)

OK Judson don't know answer as I never tried it - so yea or nay. My
system (lousy #$$5@@!! Windows) - I know what my user is after
EVENTUALLY - display my 10 x 24 table of values - THEN put a pretty
little graph up their top-right translating the values in the array;
also maybe bring up an AutoCad drawing showing the Item being inspected. 
Wait for it - at some stage he's going to say show me a pretty picture
(photograph) of the Oil plant being inspected. I know I can EVENTUALLY
do it in Windows - probably with great agonizing on my part. 

Can you do that with your text-based screen - not teasing - just asking.

PS: Oh yes - and while I am it - REMEMBER gentlmen - to help William K.
I started this thread on the basis that we should ALL get involved with
the standards - that includes YOU down in Alabama too :-) What the hell
- let's talk about the derivation of the word Durex with its different
meanings in UK and Australia ! Come to think of it - you guys in Albion
care to go off at a tangent and tell me the meaning of the word 'narf' -
probably spelled incorrectly - but you should recognise it.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 37)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<V4LB4.2999$z22.234622@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu> <MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk> <yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk> <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <Ol6B4.108$l34.9414@news3.mia> <38D6F4DC.6732DF07@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
>
> PS: Oh yes - and while I am it - REMEMBER gentlmen - to help William K.
> I started this thread on the basis that we should ALL get involved with
> the standards - that includes YOU down in Alabama too :-)

Beyond what I believe is the obvious need for COBOL based GUI, I had
not thought a great deal about what the COBOL syntax would look like.
But I am beginning to think it might be simpler than some here have
suggested.  I have not seen any AcuCOBOL GUI code, so pardon me if I
am reiterating their approach.

Starting with the Screen Section as it is in the new draft standard,
I don't see why it could not be implemented as it is in GUI.  This
would be a boring GUI, but pretty straightforward to implement.  And
beyond that, of course, you would want to assign additional properties
to items on the screen (relative font size and positioning based on
screen size, associated event handling code, etc.), and allow for
graphics, both drawn (lines, circles, etc.) and pictures.  I think
most of this could be handled as additional option phrases in the
Screen Section.  But the event handling might be better specified in
the Declaratives, or some other facility made for the purpose.  If
this would be sufficient, then it could be integrated with only a
reasonable amount of thought and effort.  There is already much
experience in what is required for a GUI interface.  Creating COBOL
syntax and semantics to support a modest, or perhaps not so modest,
GUI capability seems fairly straightforward.  Now I will don my
asbestos pants and wait for the storm of rebuttal. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 38)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D78211.83036036@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu> <MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk> <yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk> <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <Ol6B4.108$l34.9414@news3.mia> <38D6F4DC.6732DF07@home.com> <V4LB4.2999$z22.234622@news2.mia>`

```
TSO/SPF has a drop down menu option in text mode.  I don't know anybody
who uses it, but it is there.  ADSO has a slightly better design for
generating maps and dialogs - programmers don't have any choice, they
have to use those drop down windows.  Colors are available for mainframe
programming, as are italics (which italics sometimes cover up the next
non-italic character).

What I haven't seen is ability to keep focus on different windows, but I
don't see why that can't be emulated.  And of course, we don't have the
ability to load graphics such as 3-d windows.  But since everything is
text, everything can be cut and pasted into an e-mail.  (some Windows
error messages cannot be cut and pasted as text - it is a pain to  write
it down in pencil just to type it in again into an e-mail).

Forgetting whether these emulated windows features are useful - it may
be worth considering what can be emulated in a text window.
```

```
On Tue, 21 Mar 2000 07:47:34 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>  Creating COBOL
>syntax and semantics to support a modest, or perhaps not so modest,
>GUI capability seems fairly straightforward.  Now I will don my
>asbestos pants and wait for the storm of rebuttal. :-)

FLAME ON!  Oh wait, that's from a comic book.  

I'm still not convinced it should be standardized at all.  If we
standardize a limited GUI then this will be seen as a limit because
it's COBOL.  Thus COBOL should not be used.  By leaving the interface
entirely external this will not be a valid complaint about COBOL.  I'd
rather see it absent than half way.  

I find my applications in direct competition with other desktop
applications.  I need to get the users attention to get sales.  A
boring text interface does not do it - ignoring the argument that it
might actually be a BETTER interface.  I need to be able to use ALL of
the features in my target platform.  Now, I know that wether the GUI
is standardized or not, I will have the GUI I need because of the
tools I will continue to use.  However, the PERCEPTION among our peers
will be that COBOL is limited because of the half baked GUI
implementation.  We don't need that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 39)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wnOB4.46852$wk.1860901@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu> <MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk> <yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk> <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <Ol6B4.108$l34.9414@news3.mia> <38D6F4DC.6732DF07@home.com> <V4LB4.2999$z22.234622@news2.mia> <38d79192.1080588118@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> >Creating COBOL
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> implementation.  We don't need that.

No one is suggesting that your ability to use your current methods
be curtailed. :-)  But why should everyone who could benefit from
a standardized COBOL GUI capability be penalized, when those of
you who want will still (and always) have the same options as you
do now? :-)  Dont throw the baby out with the wash. ;-)  And COBOL
has *always* been thought of as limited, and it is - intentionally.
We also willingly limit ourselves to carefully using structured
(and/or OO) techniques because of the benefits.  We willingly don't
use GO TO.  Limitation is not necessarily a disadvantage, especially
when the 'limitation' is actually specialization, as is the case
for COBOL.  Specialized tools are generally more efficient than
general purpose tools, because their specialization (limitation)
permits a more focused approach.  If nothing else, specialization
gets rid of the clutter of seldom used capabilities, something we
need In A Big Way in our GUI tools!  Every GUI programmer has at
some time or other bemoaned the complexities of GUI programming. :-)
Also by your same logic, Indexed Files capability should not have
been added to COBOL, because it wasn't sufficient for every database
need. ;-)
```

- **Message-ID:** `<38D7CC51.C4459DC1@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu> <MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk> <yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk> <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <Ol6B4.108$l34.9414@news3.mia> <38D6F4DC.6732DF07@home.com> <V4LB4.2999$z22.234622@news2.mia> <38d79192.1080588118@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:

> I find my applications in direct competition with other desktop
> applications.  I need to get the users attention to get sales.  A
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> implementation.  We don't need that.

GUI standards may always be a moving target.  Applications designed with
a GUI in mind are designed to be replaced with next year's GUI style. 
This is a big argument in favor of breaking our applications into a
business component and a display component.  I am thinking more and more
that the interface should be something like JDBC or ODBC.  

If so, then we may be better off WITHOUT a screen section - but WITH an
ODBC interface section.  I don't know who and what should control the
communications/security though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Business data to user interface

```
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> it one step furhter removed than Judson does!

Let's analyze this in business terms.  What do the users want and what
will they want over the long term?

They want reports.
They want to do data entry.
They want to change their reports, to massage their data, and to
summarize on different criteria.
They want real-time access to data.
They want real-time updating of data.
They want their order system to know whether or not the product being
sold is in stock.
They want audit trails.
They want automatic ordering of low supplies when a product is removed
from stock.
They want invoicing, pricing, etc.
They want security.

This is a starting list.  Some of these needs are solved on the central
computer (server or mainframe, who cares?).  Some of these are solved on
a PC (spread sheet).  So far, if our COBOL can talk to databases and to
ODBC, everything is hunky dory.  Then we have the real time needs:

A salesman scans a product code on a scanner.  The program checks the
price, marks inventory down one, checks to see if more products should
be ordered, checks the credit card, offers the good customer discount,
asks if the customer wants batteries, calculates taxes, and prints out
an invoice.  This looks like a job for OO.  What tools are best for the
interface?  Screen sections?  HTML/XTML generators?  CORBA objects?

You just wrote the above programs, and your company decides to do the
same thing on the Web.  What do you have to rewrite?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Business data to user interface

_(thread depth: 42)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38d82742.1118913017@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu> <MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk> <yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk> <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <Ol6B4.108$l34.9414@news3.mia> <38D6F4DC.6732DF07@home.com> <V4LB4.2999$z22.234622@news2.mia> <38d79192.1080588118@news.cox-internet.com> <38D7CC51.C4459DC1@cusys.edu> <38d7df87.1100548057@news.cox-internet.com> <38D7F2DE.16894C8A@cusys.edu>`

```
On Tue, 21 Mar 2000 15:08:30 -0700, Howard Brazee
<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>> 
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>same thing on the Web.  What do you have to rewrite?

Oh boy.  You hit my hot button.   BECAUSE I externalized everything
using SP2, I CAN DO THIS ALREADY.  I can take an application written
using sp2 and without changing a line of source, relink it and have a
"Thin Client" application.  Next, with some minor source changes
because Web browsers do not support all the windows controls, I can
make it an HTML application.  Not a line of business logic needs to
change.

Now, how this fits into the standard.  Unless vendors are going to
have screen sections that generate XML/HTML, and offer TERMINAL
EMULATION wherever there is a screen section display/accept - they
cannot get to one common interface WITHIN the language.  By
externalizing it with calls to an external system, you CAN easily do
all these things.  However, when it is embedded within the syntax of
the language, things like Thin client become very very hard to do, if
not impossible - the interface is back to being part of the actual
business logic.
```

```
>same claim using Activex  :-)

It's the same thing.  ActiveX is what OLE (Object Linking and
Embedding) has become.  It's a formalized method for sharing access to
common objects - without sharing source code.
```

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 

> >You just wrote the above programs, and your company decides to do the
> >same thing on the Web.  What do you have to rewrite?
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> business logic.

Externalizing the interface certainly is the way to go.  We don't care
if it is a moving target.  We don't care if we change platforms.  This
is sort of like the logic behind TCP/IP - layered interfaces allow us to
write the part we care about, and plug in the part that someone else has
already developed for the other platform.
```

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> Howard Brazee wrote:
> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> it one step furhter removed than Judson does!

I'm certainly not against standardizing an ODBC-type section.  But
we can separate disk or screen I/O now, with separate programs.  I
don't see why any of this would be impacted negatively having a GUI
Screen Section.  I believe you guys are looking primarily at the
'extreme end', where applications must do all kinds of GUI tricks
to make sales.  No problem with that, but remember that COBOL has
always been a mostly in-house language, and by far the most code is
written by IS or consultants for internal use.  Unless you have some
reason for pushing the envelope, a reasonably vanilla GUI capability
would be all you need for virtually all those applications.
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> I'm certainly not against standardizing an ODBC-type section.  But
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> would be all you need for virtually all those applications.

We're very far from pushing the envelope.  Heck, we can't even SEE the
envelope.  And we do not use COBOL at all for screens (We use ADSO
instead).  But we do write comma delimited files and FTP them to users
to be read into PC applications.  We do have people use other
applications to input data which we copy to files so that we can read
them.  This is ODBC stuff.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 44)_

- **From:** "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ghgC4.7108$Ys3.110605@nnrp1.ptd.net>`
- **References:** `<38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu> <MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk> <yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk> <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <Ol6B4.108$l34.9414@news3.mia> <38D6F4DC.6732DF07@home.com> <V4LB4.2999$z22.234622@news2.mia> <38d79192.1080588118@news.cox-internet.com> <38D7CC51.C4459DC1@cusys.edu> <38d7df87.1100548057@news.cox-internet.com> <d0TB4.47179$wk.1867140@news1.mia> <HvVB4.6735$Ys3.100583@nnrp1.ptd.net> <38D84D57.4AA8A6C6@home.com>`

```
Jimmy,

Fujitsu PowerCobol, which has been around for about 2 years
now (maybe more?) is basically what this thread has been
discussing.

You build GUI elements using a WSIWYG editor.  You then
write cobol code for each of the "events" that occur when a
control is clicked, changed, etc.

Each event is its own standalone
mini-program (called a "scriplet").  Code that would be duplicated
in multpile events is simply put in its own scriplet, and CALLed
by the individual events.

Each scriplet also has its own local variables.  Global variables
may also be declared, which are useable from within any
of the scriplets.

Its still good old cobol.

The WSIWYG editor actually generates cobol code to define
the elements, but you don't have to do the "row#, column#,
raised.." as Cheesle mentioned.  That stuff is in there, but
you don't have to look at it.

Earlier versions of PowerCobol were very buggy.  The one
they give away for free, V3, is one of those early versions.
If you really want to build something commercial, don't bother
with that version.  But then, you didn't expect to get a
solid compiler for free, now....

The current version is very stable, and does *exactly* what
most of this thread "wishes" for in the future.  What it doesn't
do, of course, is set a "standard".  Much if it is specific to
this product, and the learning curve is *very* steep.  It has
taken me a full year to get my productivity back to the level
it was with previous character-based cobol products (I've been
playing this musical instrument for 20 years).

If you've ever used VisualBasic, this product will feel very familiar.
The graphical setup tools are an exact copy.  I tried to learn
VisualBasic a few years ago, and spent a lot of time going
nowhere.  Cobol is very good for what we do (lots of I-O,
error-checking, the usual business stuff...).  VisualBasic is
much harder to use for those applications.


don ferrario
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> would be all you need for virtually all those applications.


Originally as a result of something you wrote earlier I was going to
comment as to what Thane's reaction would be - he beat me to it !
Although I find the 'sub-set' idea of a Screen Section containing GUIs
extremely attractive - Thane is right. He doesn't need a half-baked
approach - simply all or nothing, and we know the former is unattainable
because 'Windows is a moving target' due to M/S marketing generating the
suitable consumer demand.

Consider the on-again off-again situation with COBOL R/W - has to be a
minority who use it, (and I'll acknowledge from my perusing years ago
it was a good tool). Assume we arrived at a COBOL 'sub-set' of GUIs
containing 30 controls deemed really necessary. In view of Bill K's
comments elsewhere in this thread, how long would it take for COBOL to
catch-up. Closer to home, M/S now allows for left, centre and
right justified with single line entryfields - I'm still waiting for
Merant to play catch-up with that one little detail in the Dialog Editor
!

One thing we should remember when we get into Microsoft Windows bashing
- Bill Gates didn't start it - it's been a natural progression, where he
has come out current victor. (Not all progression is naturally good - I
enjoyed some of the earlier days of TV when we looked at a poky little
black and white screen - looking at most of the junk on TV now I would
prefer their colours were grey on grey !)

OK so there was Simula first, then Smalltalk. Can't find the exact
reference, but the feisty young Adele Goldberg (reminisces of Grace),
whisked a few 'desktops' out the front door, and gave kids at a local
school the chance to play with 'figures' on screens. The experiment
being a success, then she and her accomplices asked for formal
permission :-)

Now continue the story in Adele's own words :-

"1979 was year of ominous events......Steve Jobs then head of the
fledgling Apple Corporation visited PARC - twice. First a courtesy visit
hosted by Xerox. Then Jobs came back for a second demo ... with the
entire Lisa programming team. He and the Apple programming team waited
three hours... while I argued in vain that a demo for the programming
team was an inappropriate give-away. Worse, Job's insisted that I give
the demo, knowing I was the group's teacher. My manager ordered me to
give the demo, claiming later that he was ordered to do so from
corporate. The plans for Lisa were revised..........."

OO Programming Languages 1998.

Doesn't need spelling out does it - Bill G. latched on to what Steve was
doing and gave us Windows. Now it was Steve's turn to be PO'd.
So come on Linus give Bill's tree a real good shake !

Jimmy
```

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D8E06A.A85D3D9C@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu> <MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk> <yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk> <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <Ol6B4.108$l34.9414@news3.mia> <38D6F4DC.6732DF07@home.com> <V4LB4.2999$z22.234622@news2.mia> <38d79192.1080588118@news.cox-internet.com> <38D7CC51.C4459DC1@cusys.edu> <38d7df87.1100548057@news.cox-internet.com> <d0TB4.47179$wk.1867140@news1.mia> <38D86623.D72D8A57@home.com>`

```

I have been reading this thread and I think that most people are missing the
point about "GUI SCREEN" I-O.

There are a couple of standards around now that can be used to map function
onto the native GUI drivers for whatever system you are running on.

Firstly there is "Display Postscript"
This can be used to define the layout and content of the screen/window etc.

Then there's "Display PDF"
Apple has started using a variant of PDF to display all of it's windows. This
this has the advantage that any screen or window can be captured to a PDF
document.
This function is purportedly being incorporated into the OS X operating system.

Finally, there's good old HTML (the thing that started it all off).
Currently, the only way a program can format a screen via HTML is to be invoked
by a WEB Server. It would be really nice if one or all of the above could be
invoked by COBOL program directly. Maybe some direct pipe to NetScape or IE? Of
course you could use
LOCALHOST to send the HTML to Netscape and imbed a Web Server in the Driver code
that the COBOL program would use for HTML.

I envision something like:

Identification Division.
Program-id. Blah.
Environment Division.
Input-Output Section.
    Select Program-Window
      Assign to HTML-PROCESSOR
      Processing Mode is Server|Application
      Window Status is Window-Status1 Window-Status2.
Data Division.
Window Section.
WD  Program-Window
    Value of CONTENT-TYPE is Program-Window-Content-Type
             CONTENT-LENGTH is Program-Window-Content-Length
             PATH-INFO is Program-Window-Path
    Data Records are Program-Window-Form1
                     Program-Window-Form2
                     Program-Window-Data.
01  Program-Window-Form1.
    05 Input-Field-1   PIC X(20) Varying Window Name is "INPUT1"
    ...
01  Program-Window-Form2.
    05 Input-Field-2   PIC X(30) Varying Window Name is "INPUT2"
    ...
01  Program-Window-Data PIC X(150) Varying.
  
Procedure Division.

    Open Program-Window for I-O.
    Read Program-Window into
         Program-Window-Form1 When Program-Window-Path contains "PATH1"
         Program-Window-Form2 When Program-Window-Path contains "PATH2".

    ... (logic)
    
    Move "Content-type: text/html" to Program-Window-Data.
    Write Program-Window-Data.
    Move Spaces to Program-Window-Data.
    Write Program-Window-Data.
    Move '<!doctype html public "-//w3c//dtd html 4.0 transitional//en">' to
         Program-Window-Data.
    Write Program-Window-Data.

    ...
    You get the drift.

Now, whilst the above is for a HTML windowing system.
The Window type could be changed to ADOBE-PDF or DISPLAY-POSTSCRIPT or whatever
other markup language you think would be usefull (note: this works for producing
reports as well).

Then the logic (Procedure division) would need to be changed for each type of
markup language. 

Once we had this type of feature, we could write a generic module that could
call a driver module based on the type of display system being used.

Then (and this is the good bit) it would be up to the programmer to decide what
level of dumbness the display's would have to enable the code to work with
different markup scheme's. Any given markup scheme should be implemented
identically across platforms (sort of like NetScape on WIN, Unix, etc).
```

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> I get the gist of what Joe is saying, also following through on his
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
OK,
What I'm suggesting is that you forget the GUI standard as GUI standard, and
concentrate on a markup standard (SGML, HTML, XML, Display or otherwise
Postscript, PDF format, etc.)

Then the compiler vendor, would be free to take these standards and implement
them in a GUI for whatever platforms they choose to support. This implementation
would be part of the runtime supplied with the compiler.

I would avoid (like the plague) charging for this runtime (increase in compiler
charge ~ OK).
```

```
Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:

>What I'm suggesting is that you forget the GUI standard as GUI standard, and
>concentrate on a markup standard (SGML, HTML, XML, Display or otherwise
>Postscript, PDF format, etc.)

i would skip that as well. standardizing markup in a compiler is akin
to forcing vendors to have exec sql. and there is nothing worse than
mixing languages. you can have many different tools and source code
which connects to markup code, but to force it on each implementation
is bad news. sample code and calls, not standardization.

what i would like to see (in cobol) is a tool which accepts http as
source, and writes out cobol code skeletons, creation of
picture/plugin files, a search engine, and credit connection code.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
> Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

This is two in a row...  I agree.

After seeing the hoops that we have to go through to get accurate
presentation for online bills on both explorer and Netscape (let alone
opera, ...) HTML is not the standard to work with yet.  It really is a
bugger to work with unless you have support routines.

PDF is only an output strategy (a good one mind you, but proprietary).

XML has promise in that you cobol program would accept and output a
data stream without consideration for format.  The screen format sits
outside the program, not really what we are discussing here.  This is
still a fairly young standard though.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

_(thread depth: 44)_

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mlnkds848gl28is5s5sos9qn509v6emoaa@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk> <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <Ol6B4.108$l34.9414@news3.mia> <38D6F4DC.6732DF07@home.com> <V4LB4.2999$z22.234622@news2.mia> <38d79192.1080588118@news.cox-internet.com> <38D7CC51.C4459DC1@cusys.edu> <38d7df87.1100548057@news.cox-internet.com> <d0TB4.47179$wk.1867140@news1.mia> <38D86623.D72D8A57@home.com> <38D8E06A.A85D3D9C@iinet.net.au>`

```
Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:

>I have been reading this thread and I think that most people are missing the
>point about "GUI SCREEN" I-O.

>There are a couple of standards around now that can be used to map function
>onto the native GUI drivers for whatever system you are running on.

>Firstly there is "Display Postscript"
>This can be used to define the layout and content of the screen/window etc.

>Then there's "Display PDF"
>Apple has started using a variant of PDF to display all of it's windows. This
>this has the advantage that any screen or window can be captured to a PDF
>document.
>This function is purportedly being incorporated into the OS X operating system.


>Finally, there's good old HTML (the thing that started it all off).
>Currently, the only way a program can format a screen via HTML is to be invoked
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>that the COBOL program would use for HTML.



>I envision something like:

>Identification Division.
>Program-id. Blah.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>01  Program-Window-Data PIC X(150) Varying.
  
>Procedure Division.

>    Open Program-Window for I-O.
>    Read Program-Window into
>         Program-Window-Form1 When Program-Window-Path contains "PATH1"
>         Program-Window-Form2 When Program-Window-Path contains "PATH2".

>    ... (logic)
    
>    Move "Content-type: text/html" to Program-Window-Data.
>    Write Program-Window-Data.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Write Program-Window-Data.

>    ...
>    You get the drift.

>Now, whilst the above is for a HTML windowing system.
>The Window type could be changed to ADOBE-PDF or DISPLAY-POSTSCRIPT or whatever
>other markup language you think would be usefull (note: this works for producing
>reports as well).

>Then the logic (Procedure division) would need to be changed for each type of
>markup language. 

>Once we had this type of feature, we could write a generic module that could
>call a driver module based on the type of display system being used.

>Then (and this is the good bit) it would be up to the programmer to decide what
>level of dumbness the display's would have to enable the code to work with
>different markup scheme's. Any given markup scheme should be implemented
>identically across platforms (sort of like NetScape on WIN, Unix, etc).

this code looks bad to me. yes, standardization is nice in *theory*.
but in guis, you are dealing with reality. and the reality is they use
different fonts on different machines, different threading systems
(pre-emptive versus non), ect. and you can't have a section which
simply *ignores* code it doesn't like, as it does in browsers.



reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 45)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DB6ABE.FF8E584B@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk> <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <Ol6B4.108$l34.9414@news3.mia> <38D6F4DC.6732DF07@home.com> <V4LB4.2999$z22.234622@news2.mia> <38d79192.1080588118@news.cox-internet.com> <38D7CC51.C4459DC1@cusys.edu> <38d7df87.1100548057@news.cox-internet.com> <d0TB4.47179$wk.1867140@news1.mia> <38D86623.D72D8A57@home.com> <38D8E06A.A85D3D9C@iinet.net.au> <mlnkds848gl28is5s5sos9qn509v6emoaa@4ax.com>`

```

G Moore wrote:
> 
> this code looks bad to me. yes, standardization is nice in *theory*.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Fonts and presentation is/are what the Markup languages handle.
Why the program should be bothered with them is beyond me.

The program should confine itself to the details of the logic of the
problem being solved. Other things can beautify the resulting output.

Your argument is like the old days.
"What do you mean standard IO for disk datasets? Disks will change
geometry's, how can I optimize the access, etc. etc."

I think it's about time we took the GUI out of the '60s and into the
new millennium. The BEST way to do that is to form a standard that
allows for flexibility under the covers.

P.S. IBM has used a Markup Language "DTL" to help programmers design
ISPF 3270 screens for some time now.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:V4LB4.2999$z22.234622@news2.mia...
> suggested.  I have not seen any AcuCOBOL GUI code,

I could not resist, please bear over with me, but here is a quick'n dirty
sample, using frames, radio buttons, labels, entry
field, check box and push button.

All this may also be accomplished with traditional DISPLAY/ACCEPT for each
individual field.

You can use BEFORE, AFTER, EXCEPTION procedures or EVENT driven programming,
the choice is yours.

01  demo-screen.
        03  frame-1, frame, "Color", raised,
            line 6, col 9, size 15, lines 10.

        03  radio-button, "Red", line 9, color red,
            col 11 group 2, group-value 1, visible 1,
            event procedure is change-app, id RED-ID,
            using color-button-value.

        03  radio-button, "Blue", line 10.5, color blue,
            col 11 group 2, group-value 2,
            event procedure is change-app, id BLUE-ID,
            using color-button-value.

        03  radio-button, "Green", line 12, color green,
            col 11 group 2, group-value 3,
            event procedure is change-app, id GREEN-ID,
            using color-button-value.

        03  label, "Name:", col 13, line 7.
        03  label, "Address:", col 11, line 9.
        03  label, "Phone:", col 12.5, line 13.

        03  entry-field, col 18, line  7, size 20, using user-name.
        03  entry-field, col 18, line  9, size 20, using user-address-1.
        03  entry-field, col 18, line 11, size 20, using user-address-2.
        03  entry-field, col 18, line 13, size 20, using user-telephone.

        03  check-box, "Default Configuration", line 8, col 12
            using button-value.
        03  push-button, "Change Configuration",
            size 23, line 10, col 12, ok-button.

        03  push-button, "Save Changes",
            line 10, col 19, size 15, cancel-button.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000319180348.06344.00000826@nso-fu.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
In article <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02>, "DennisHarley"
<LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> writes:

>
>There are limitations to how much data can be processed on a single screen.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Exactly what makes you think that a GUI application can handle more 
data per screen?  If you are operating within the boundaries of a single 
screen view, both GUI and character are limited to how much space is
available and the 'clutter effect' of trying to get too much done in a single
response.   If you are referring to being able to scroll down a page in the
GUI presentation, it is nothing more than multiple pages slung together
with a single transmit/send/enter point.  This creates a much larger region 
to have to back pedal for corrections and relationship based field editing.
Maybe I'm on the wrong track?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 36)_

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#YiK96mk$GA.254@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <20000319180348.06344.00000826@nso-fu.aol.com>`

```

Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
news:20000319180348.06344.00000826@nso-fu.aol.com...
> In article <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02>, "DennisHarley"
> <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> writes:
>
> >
> >There are limitations to how much data can be processed on a single
screen.
> >A Customer data entry process may have 4 screens in a text based system.
A
> >GUI application can handle all the data in one shot. This should reduce
the
> >number of modules needed to process the Customer data.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> available and the 'clutter effect' of trying to get too much done in a
single
> response.  If you are referring to being able to scroll down a page in the
> GUI presentation, it is nothing more than multiple pages slung together
> with a single transmit/send/enter point.  This creates a much larger
region
> to have to back pedal for corrections and relationship based field
editing.
> Maybe I'm on the wrong track?
>
Take a look at a tabbed dialog. Try the desktop properties on windows 95,
98, NT.

A GUI presentation doesn't handle more data per screen. It handles more data
per transmission.
The screen can be less cluttered, by breaking the presentation into smaller
chunks.

It also reduces the number of transmissions, since the user can navigate
through the pages without  communication to the server.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 37)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D6B090.5E52F9D1@home.com>`
- **References:** `<ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02> <20000319180348.06344.00000826@nso-fu.aol.com> <#YiK96mk$GA.254@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```


DennisHarley wrote:
> 
> Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> chunks.

Dennis'es approach is correct - BUT - you can make a screen hold more. 
Consider an old DOS application - table of 10 by 12 values - reason - 10
columns was all I could get across the screen, and 12 lines down having
prettied it up with one of the DOS screen painters. What I put on the
screen 'forced' the design of the data file - 10 x 12 table with a
sequence key if more than 12 lines. Never tried it but I recall you
could change screen mode to 40 ?? - but if you did the 'smallness' would
soon make the user go blind !

User 'assures me' he will NEVER want more than 24 lines in Windows - so
I've created a screen(dialog) with 10 columns x 24 lines. Didn't quite
fit to show pushbuttons - so made this particular screen a 'child' of
the desktop not the applications's Master Menu. Use a systems font for
bold clear display of values, (again he ain't gonna go blind), disable
lines/columns not particular to this record; it is uncluttered
concentrating in one table on what the user wants. Nearly forgot, my
file record is now ONE (10 x 24).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards : was Screen was Structured v OO

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.13416ae82884e7d49899a8@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <Zkgx4.2615$HN.138687@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <38C92A24.E5366C07@home.com> <w6ay4.26363$wk.1373771@news1.mia> <8abc04$98$1@news.cerf.net> <8abgju$oa5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38c9d8fe.181706698@news.cox-internet.com> <Z5vy4.15103$aw3.243363@news2.mia> <8aeuqj$a0m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <38CBA1F4.1AC2CF8@iinet.net.au> <8agoil$9ne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia> <38CD224D.6A68EE85@home.com> <8aru5u$brf$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <38D1A0BD.DCD3FEE@home.com> <8asc9b$loe$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <38D2415F.B5BE44EB@cusys.edu> <MPG.133ca2ee483236e4989984@news.mersinet.co.uk> <yhLA4.43105$wk.1679781@news1.mia> <8b11lu$ah8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <MPG.133eb7784a43b1a698999e@news.mersinet.co.uk> <ejZREFak$GA.268@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
I noticed that DennisHarley as LegacyBlue@email.msn.com said...
> 
> Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote in message
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> number of modules needed to process the Customer data.

Depends on how they used 3270s.  IME far too little attention was paid to 
Model-4 displays which provide double the data area with their 43-line 
displays.  Furthermore, the full capabilities of the 3270s are similarly 
rarely exploited with multi-screen presentation.  A screenful doesn't 
have to be a single page of 24x80.
 
> Having said that, I should also point out that software marketing has
> decided to move up the food chain when trying to sell products. If the
> person they are targeting isn't smart enough to solicit the opinions of the
> system users, they deserve what they get.

Couldn't agree more.
```

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:LZ6z4.34088$wk.1485580@news1.mia...
> Sigh.  Bill, I suspect you agree with this, at least to some extent.
> But when the process becomes more important than the purpose for the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> waste of time.

This is simply WRONG.  When the revision work began (circa 1964) there was as
much publicity as possible soliciting input.  (I remember articles in
Computerworld. Presentations at the IBM and Micro Focus user groups - and
probably lots of other places.  There WAS a press release on it.)

It is true that as this revision has gotten later and LATER, there is
significantly LESS interest in adding something new to this revision.
Furthermore, as indicated in another post - even if it were the compiler
vendors determining the future of COBOL - how do you think those people get
paid?  Have YOU communicated to your COBOL "vendor of choice" what you think
is important in the upcoming Standard?  Have you even indicated that you
think it is important that they follow it?  (Acucorp is off; Dec is off,
Liant is off, the Fujitsu rep has NOTHING to do with Fujitsu)
```

```
Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:38d25bb0.739536231@news.cox-internet.com...
> Posted and mailed.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >paid?  Have YOU communicated to your COBOL "vendor of choice" what you
think
> >is important in the upcoming Standard?  Have you even indicated that you
> >think it is important that they follow it?  (Acucorp is off; Dec is off,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

First, you are correct that today, there is a "single COMPAQ" representative
on J4.  It is Don Nelson who PREVIOUSLY was the Tandem representative (so it
is that "part" of COMPAQ that is represented.)  There is a DIGITAL person on
the "observer" list. (Both DEC and DECUS - the DEC user group were on the
committee when development of this revision began.)

HP is on the committee and one of their user groups (I think) is now an
active "liaison".

Unisys is on the committee - but it is only "one half" (and I don't remember
which) of the original two COBOL vendors.

CA-Reallia was on the committee several years ago (and had a person fairly
active in the VERY early OO development).  Again, they have no current rep -
but do "observe" the activities of the committee.

The Fujitsu rep is a person from Germany (he TALKS to Siemans - but actually
came from an IBM background.)  As far as I can tell he neither talks to the
US or Japanese Fujitsu developers or planners.  I do know that when he is
asked about their current product and standard conformance, he "admits" to
not being involved with the actual Fujitsu product line.

The complete current membership list (including observers) for J4 (not WG4)
is available at:

 http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/00-0232.doc
```

```
What he is saying Joe is that the system (standards) is broke. "Your
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia...
> for this purpose.  Then the compiler vendor would be responsible
> for writing the 'driver' for the particular OS GUI API.

That would be an impossible task. One issue is to support video adapters and
monitors, a significantly different task is to deal with the various
printers. It simply does not makes sense, that a Cobol vendor (or C, Delphi,
C++ for that matter) should do this.

Cheesle
```

```
Cheesle wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> for this purpose.  Then the compiler vendor would be responsible
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>C++ for that matter) should do this.

The compiler vendor would map the COBOL GUI functions to the OS's
GUI API, not the actual hardware.  Just like they do now for I/O.
```

- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <38c02889.69848657@news.transport.com> <q0h0cs8i632g3o546c5voii59eiojptgb5@4ax.com> <38C0B894.A2E482B5@home.com> <7j43cson8697j65qdemgviu001u28gnqge@4ax.com> <38C1F245.BCE36D2B@home.com> <jjt5cskgfmu26ko4etdpk04ivmpgnk24oc@4ax.com> <8a16i4$l5k$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <1398cskr7docc5o2cg8qccagqbu2tmaep5@4ax.com> <38C531C9.5378276@home.com> <qRax4.4645$5Y2.70748@news3.mia> <Zkgx4.2615$HN.138687@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <38C66090.83A1B7DD@cusys.edu> <Hkyx4.41$lA4.1578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Vr6y4.25825$wk.1368603@news1.mia> <8abbml$t1l$1@news.cerf.net> <38CA5118.BE2A4038@zip.com.au> <8aef8t$330$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:
>Ken Foskey
>> Mostly they are already abstracted.  X-windows and Gates Windows being
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>misunderstood, it is just like it is today.

I have obviously expressed myself poorly, possibly by using the term
'API'.  What I am suggesting is built-in COBOL language support for
GIU in a similar manner to the way character based UI is being added
in the Screen Section.  This would consist of COBOL constructs, made
for the purpose, to become part of standard COBOL syntax.  I envision
something like an enhanced Screen Section and additional Procedure
Division statements and options to current statements (e.g. ACCEPT
and DISPLAY).  Once COBOL programmers learned to use this built-in
COBOL capability, they could write GUI applications without having
to learn additional GUI APIs or GUI libraries, just as they can now
learn Indexed Files and use that on any computer without having to
learn anything else.  Learn once, use anywhere, save amazing amount
of time and energy. :-)
```
