###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Procedural vs Object-Oriented programming

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C4ED90.7A4A494F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <38C05182.D62293B6@home.com> <38c1ddbe.138328246@news.cox-internet.com> <38C393B2.1EA2DC20@zip.com.au> <8a0jfk$cl4$1@news.cerf.net> <8a18m3$aid$1@news.igs.net> <38C47B8E.7A6BC4D0@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Out of curiosity does anybody know if languages other than COBOL 200x
> and Smalltalk, allow for collections/dictionaries ?

The C++ standard template libraries contain these.  These are also
staple in any OO application, it is very common to have to iterate
over a collection of objects doing something.  For example if you
expand a window you have to iterate over all the sub windows to get
them to repaint themselves.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Procedural vs Object-Oriented programming

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C54125.EBE8C196@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <38C05182.D62293B6@home.com> <38c1ddbe.138328246@news.cox-internet.com> <38C393B2.1EA2DC20@zip.com.au> <8a0jfk$cl4$1@news.cerf.net> <8a18m3$aid$1@news.igs.net> <38C47B8E.7A6BC4D0@home.com> <38C4ED90.7A4A494F@zip.com.au>`

```


Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Clarification on this one please Ken - are we comparing apples with
apples ? The only reference I have is to the C language 'Win 32
Programming' Rector & Newcomer. An extremely comprehensive book about 
windowing - but try as I might I don't see any reference in the index to
collections/dictionaries. I appreciate C++ does take the language a step
further.

From what you've said, yes any good OO language must have the ability to 
create 'queues'. But what I'm querying is does C++ provide a specific
pre-written tool. What am I asking ? - flat files - OPEN, READ, WRITE,
REWRITE, CLOSE etc. Each COBOL compiler gives us these features - we
don't need to know the internal mechanics but each compiler returns us
the same results. 

Now when it comes to Smalltalk and OO COBOL :-

	invoke SortedCollection "new" returning mySortedCollection
	invoke mySortedCollection "at" returning ls-string
	invoke MyDictionary "includesKey" using ls-key

Pre-canned classes are doing exactly the same as our file verbs. I don't
have to re-invent the wheel, something like say 500 plus methods within
the class/dictionary hierarchy do it for me.

Now when I talk about 'common classes' I don't want to inhibit the
compiler vendors, but whether it is Fujitsu, AcucCOBOL, Liant or Merant
etc. I do want them all to agree on a common terminology (method names)
that have the same meaning/effect as READ, WRITE, REWRITE etc... The
mechanics of how their version of a particular class handles it is
internal, but the common method-names give me the same result from
compiler to compiler. Further, if one particular vendor wants to enhance
collections/dictionaries with additional features - fine.

Now are you saying C++ does this - or just gives you the ability to
write code to do this ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB

> Thanks
> Ken Foskey
> http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Procedural vs Object-Oriented programming

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C64B6C.FF7E30B7@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<20000303151450.02510.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <38C05182.D62293B6@home.com> <38c1ddbe.138328246@news.cox-internet.com> <38C393B2.1EA2DC20@zip.com.au> <8a0jfk$cl4$1@news.cerf.net> <8a18m3$aid$1@news.igs.net> <38C47B8E.7A6BC4D0@home.com> <38C4ED90.7A4A494F@zip.com.au> <38C54125.EBE8C196@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Now are you saying C++ does this - or just gives you the ability to
> write code to do this ?

Standard template library (STL) is delivered with all C++ compilers. 
It is public domain source developed about 10 years ago, it does
everything you talk about.

If we are serious about a public domain the C++ STL is the place to
start looking for ideas.  Most programs will use these concepts.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Procedural vs Object-Oriented programming

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2000-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000315185254.08668.00000076@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38C64B6C.FF7E30B7@zip.com.au>`

```
Interesting that you recommend using the C++ STLs as a model.  My friends who
are currently making a VERY good living by going in to rescue C++  projects in
trouble say that the first thing they do is dump all the (and I quote them
here)  "template crap".  Then the second thing they do is dump all the object
libraries.  Third thing they do is code the applications as close to procedural
'C' as possible, and then cloak that procedural code in C++ object "wrappers"
so their clients can say they are object-oriented.

These are people who have been doing C++ for 10 years or more, and they seem to
know what they speak of.  At least, they all have a LOT more money than I do!

Sigh.


Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

## Thread: Am I wasting my time? — 14 msgs

### Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** anonymous <federalreservebostonNOfeSPAM@juno.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0aa387a0.069fd549@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <ERHp4.1858$0g.34837@news.swbell.net> <oFVp4.2503$Ez.59564@news2.mia> <MPG.1317987789ebd33f989864@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
In article <MPG.1317987789ebd33f989864@news.mersinet.co.uk>,
Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote:
>
>My first degree was in Classics (Latin, Ancient Greek and
ancient
>history) and I know several competent programmers who have
modern
>languages backgrounds.  As far as I can work it out, the ability
to use
>another language, other than your mother tongue, makes you
receptive to
>other linguistic concepts and structures and, in this respect,
>programming languages are not much different to spoken
languages.
>
>--
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The famous "spaghetti code" must originate in some part with
programmers who don't know the frame of reference that they're
operating in.  Let's take Latin as a paradigm for failure to
understand, and supose that someone writes a test question:
(Excuse my failure to write in authentic capital letters.)

Quot sunt duo plus duo? [How many are two plus two?]
(a) unus; (b) tres; (c) quattuor; (d) quinque; (e) non est supra.

And someone comes along, figures out (a) through (d) and by
asking learns that "None of the above" was offered as choice (e).
Someone says, "Geez, that's great.  I want an answer that says
"None of the following."  So "non est infra" enters the answer
pool but the test composer doesn't know what the question means,
or the answer either.  So in the next version we get

Quot sunt duo plus duo? (a) Non est supra; (b) unus; (c) tres;
(d) quinque; (e) Non est infra.

And behold, we have a question with five choices (five IF
statements) and none of them is valid.


* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

```
> programmers who don't know the frame of reference that they're
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** anonymous <federalreservebostonNOfeSPAM@juno.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<022831f2.8ceda792@usw-ex0106-047.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <ERHp4.1858$0g.34837@news.swbell.net> <oFVp4.2503$Ez.59564@news2.mia> <MPG.1317987789ebd33f989864@news.mersinet.co.uk> <0aa387a0.069fd549@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com> <38C024CF.29D7B7F6@neo.rr.com>`

```
In article <38C024CF.29D7B7F6@neo.rr.com>, Steve Thompson
<sthompson2_@neo.rr.com> wrote:
>What was your point?  Mine is, lots of words, looks highly
>educated (throwing all that Latin about) but, you didn't explain
>pasta.  Why is COBOL written in a spaghetti like fashion?  Or
>should it be better asked as, "How does it come to be this way?"
>
Lots of people don't understand programming logic.  They even
think they understand English, but when they write COBOL (and
they think they ought to be able to, because they can write
English, nicht wahr?) they make a complete botch of it because
they don't understand the problem they're dealing with.

Our Latinist doubtless understands how people can botch Latin
(see, for instance, the film _The Life of Brian_, where some fool
has to be taught the correct way to write "Romans go home" in
Latin) and likewise understands how people can botch COBOL.  I
thought he, at least, might be amused by a semi-plausible (may be
correct or may not) account of how an incorrect sequence of
operations, resulting from total ignorance, produces implausible
results.  A level of humor which, apparently, is not shared.
Regrets and apologies to you, sir.

(But I definitely recall an election four yeaers ago in which
"None of the above" seemed to be a popular watchword.)
```

```
I noticed that anonymous as federalreservebostonNOfeSPAM@juno.com.invalid 
said...
> Lots of people don't understand programming logic.  They even
> think they understand English, but when they write COBOL (and
> they think they ought to be able to, because they can write
> English, nicht wahr?) they make a complete botch of it because
> they don't understand the problem they're dealing with.

Yes, but I'm not sure I follow you.  The concept was that linguists are 
used to thinking differently in different languages.

BTW apology accepted regarding the use of lower case letters but you also 
used the letter "u" in your examples, when we classicists shudder at the 
very suggestion that such a letter should exist - it only got added 
relatively recently because some people wanted it to look like other 
languages.  Wow, back to the "C-ification" of COBOL.
 
> Our Latinist doubtless understands how people can botch Latin
> (see, for instance, the film _The Life of Brian_, where some fool
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> "None of the above" seemed to be a popular watchword.)

We have them all the time in the UK - fortunately we also have some 
political candidates for whom a vote indicates the sentiment.  The 
Monster Raving Looney Party, for instance, has even been successful at 
some local elections.
```

```
I noticed that SkippyPB as swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam said...
> On Tue, 07 Mar 2000 10:19:30 GMT, Bill Lynch
> <wblynch@worldnet.att.net> enlightened us:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Okay, where's the hidden camera? This is a Monty Python skit, isn't it?

Not at all.  The Monster Raving Loony Party is active wherever it can be, 
despite its small membership roll.  It's interesting to note that all bar 
one of its initial major policy commitments have been enacted in English 
law.  The, as yet, unsuccessful one is "free beer for all" but we live in 
hope.  So, vote MRLP - the only party that knows how to party.


> Is Lord Such still active in the Monster Raving Looney Party?

David Sutch died last year by his own hand a few months after the death 
of his mother.  ITN (http://www.itn.co.uk/Britain/brit0729/072905.htm) 
did a small article on him when he decided not to stand at the last 
general election.  There's a tribute page to him on the (un)official 
party website at http://innotts.co.uk/~jbrewer/Loony2.html

You can have a look at the original manifesto by visiting 
http://freespace.virgin.net/raving.loony/manifesto.htm - only go to the 
main page if you have a replacement optical system readily to hand.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.133ce64089ef4d8b98998f@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <ERHp4.1858$0g.34837@news.swbell.net> <oFVp4.2503$Ez.59564@news2.mia> <MPG.1317987789ebd33f989864@news.mersinet.co.uk> <0aa387a0.069fd549@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com> <38C024CF.29D7B7F6@neo.rr.com> <022831f2.8ceda792@usw-ex0106-047.remarq.com> <MPG.132d26c9781882b298993f@news.mersinet.co.uk> <38C4D79E.456EA127@worldnet.att.net> <0FBF84240594D336.16E018CCA5BAED12.830D315AC8BB7992@lp.airnews.net> <MPG.13364a22fef081b598996a@news.mersinet.co.uk> <B353392BE2E23ADA.65A2120891B2EA86.0848F2A8F20B48C7@lp.airnews.net> <38CF4C9B.BB5F42D0@worldnet.att.net>`

```
I noticed that Bill Lynch as wblynch@worldnet.att.net said...
> SkippyPB wrote:
> > 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is Lord Sutch related to Lord Buckley, by any chance?

I don't know, Bill.  It should be noted that David Sutch changed his name 
by deed poll (I.e. a full legal change) to David Lord Sutch before the 
first time he stood for election to Parliament.  He'd previously had some 
small recording success as "Screaming Lord Sutch" back in the 60s.

At general elections he would stand in direct opposition to the Prime 
Minister of the day in their own constituency, and he would stand in just 
about every by-election.  One government proposed the idea that the 
electoral deposit (every electoral has to pay a deposit to the 
authorities) should be raised to a level that would make it financially 
impossible for candidates such as him.  (Candidates who score below a 
certain percentage of the vote lose their deposit.)  This proposal was 
rejected by Parliament probably because even the major parties have been 
known to lose their deposits.

Another little anecdote was when a major bank foreclosed on him - in 
court, in his defence, he said something like "It's their own fault.  
They knew I was a loony before they lent me the money, what did they 
honestly expect?".  He was a real character and his death was a genuine 
loss to the British political scene.

Some of us like to carry on because a vote for the loonies is either a 
vote for the party that knows how to party, or it's a damning "none of 
the above" vote.  The message may be small, but at least it gets a voice.
```

```
<charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> enlightened us:

>I noticed that Bill Lynch as wblynch@worldnet.att.net said...
>> SkippyPB wrote:
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>the above" vote.  The message may be small, but at least it gets a voice.

Here in the U.S.A. we had a similar person although nowhere near as
looney as Lord Sutch.  His name was Pat Paulsen.  He was a well known
T.V. comedian and movie actor who passed away in 1997.  Pat was
approached by the Smothers Brothers with the idea of running for
 President. Pat's reply was "Why not? I can't dance - besides, the job
has a good pension plan and I'll get a lot of money when I retire."

Pat's campaign was based in comedy and he ran it using outright lies,
double talk and unfounded attacks on his challengers.   Who would have
thought this style would be the method of campaigns in the future?

There is a really nice web site about Pat and you can find his
proposed ammendments to the constitution there.  Go to:

http://www.paulsen.com

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

How come you never hear about gruntled employees?





Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

### pl. Advice on Compiler

- **From:** sminni@vsnl.com
- **Date:** 2000-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89oddg$cbe$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I was using MF-COBOL years ago on ms-dos, migrated aroung 4yrs ago.
Now I am looking again for a COBOL compiler that can offer the following
for professional level working

1. Platform Win9x/NT & Linux GUI & Character
2. Object Oriented Features
3. Free Deployment (no questions)
4. RDBMS access capability Oracle/ODBC

Can i get some advice which products are good and comply to above
So far I think have narrowed down on Fujitsu & LegacyJ mainly
because of free runtime (I do not mind high initial cost)

Sanjay Minni               nospam_sminni@vsnl.com
Minisoft Private Limited   www.minisoftindia.com
India


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

- **From:** Shawn Gordon <shawn-gordon@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C1743C.9EA72C29@home.com>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I've been messing with learning OO COBOL for a little while now, and
there are some interesting possibilities.  It's more that you can
formalize a lot of things that you used to have to make up depending on
your platform.  For example, I code on the HP 3000 primarily, and HP
while having a pretty skimpy COBOL implementation, does have a very
nifty macro facility that expands the code inline during the compile. 
You can pass parameters and all sorts of things.  I've been using these
as make shift objects for years.

Say you have a bank finance system and you want to deposit money to an
account.  You could create an object such that you could almost say
"deposit money to account" where "deposit" is an object.  Then inside
that object you would standardize all the code that has to do with
depositing the money.

You can do this with sub-programs and paragraphs, but this takes it a
step further.  OO also makes it easier to deal with Windows officially. 
There is a lot of other stuff, but that should give you a basic
understanding.

Shawn Gordon
President
TheKompany.com


Foodman wrote:
> 
> In article <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com>,
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C185C7.AFED603A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C1743C.9EA72C29@home.com>`

```


Shawn Gordon wrote:
> 
> I've been messing with learning OO COBOL for a little while now, and
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Are the already-converted only those worth addressing?

Don't know why but didn't get original message from Tony, only see it
from Shawn's message. No you don't address things only to the 'already
converted' but gurus like Yourdan earn BIG BUCKS writing a lot of crap.
Yours truly is trying to earn a very modest buck, and learning OO at the
same time.

As your interest is 'casual' - 'What is OO about' is too big a topic for
this amateur to cover. If you had made a very specific point I would
have responded as I did in the other comment that 'OO can really be
covered by the linkage section'.  

Your original question suggested to me that your enquiry 'was casual'.
Again, go back to objectz.com if your interest is just a niche beyond
'casual'. If you are browsing the bookstores on OO COBOL, ignore those
tomes full of zillions of charts and graphs. If the author hasn't put
his money where his mouth is, and illustrated his ideas with REAL COBOL
source code, that will work in a given compiler - then chuck the damn
thing. There are plenty of books covering OO in general which will quite
happily get you lost in all the theoretical crap.

What you need, and I need just as much, is real COBOL code which shows
us how OO can be applied. He hasn't released any code, but check out Don
Tees' message where he has described what he is currently designing
using Screen Section and Fujitsu - a very, very interesting approach to
using OO with COBOL - and note again, zilch about Windows !.

As regards Flexus - (and I think Bob Wolfe will confirm) it doesn't yet
address the issue of returning objects to the COBOL calling program - to
my mind an absolute must when I'm toying with collections/dictionaries.

And touching on Windows, you are obviously 'pro' Flexus, and there's
nothing wrong with that. But at the same time there are others who are
telling me just as adamantly that Visual Basic or Activex is the right
tool to use. Simple answer, I'm using the tools inclusive within the
product I bought (OO and OO GUIs). Ken Mullins, another NetExpress user,
would probably make just as strong a case for his direct calling of
APIs. The various proponents of the different products make a case that
using product 'X' is a quick/effective way to do that lousy Windows
stuff without a lot of aggro. However, that blindly accepts that Mr.
Gates and his Windows is the de facto standard.

(And for a bit of trivia, yonks ago there was another product called
Window (singular) - the Luftwaffe and RAF/USAAF respectively dropped
shredded tinsel foil over enemy territory to confound radar equipment).

Assume there were 10 operating systems with an equal share of the
market, not just Windows. Does that mean I should write software to
accommodate each O/S version, because Customer A likes Windows, B likes
Unix, C likes Linux etc. That presents a nightmare - but that nightmare
is at least partially curable using OO techniques to address the various
O/S. Given the above scenario, and only Bob can confirm, could Flexus
handle it ?

Yes - let me say it, I want OO COBOL to be 'all singing, all dancing'
and to hell with Windows or any other O/S - I want my business logic
totally controlled from COBOL, not by a casual click with a Mouse !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c2719b.176187299@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C1743C.9EA72C29@home.com> <38C185C7.AFED603A@home.com>`

```
On Sat, 04 Mar 2000 21:56:55 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:



>Assume there were 10 operating systems with an equal share of the
>market, not just Windows. Does that mean I should write software to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think the answer is a qualified Yes.  The qualification is that
different OS's with different API's have different functionality.
Some things would only be supported under certain OS's.  Presently
there are versions of sp2 for a myriad of OS's where one could take
one's standard COBOL code and run on many different platforms, without
changing the source.

Creating an object that could utilize sp2 for the user interface,
should not be that difficult.  There's nothing inherent in sp2 that
would make that difficult.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C3C944.C2BB6906@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C1743C.9EA72C29@home.com> <38C185C7.AFED603A@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Assume there were 10 operating systems with an equal share of the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> handle it ?

But I write for whichever mainframe environment my employer uses - and
no other.

You do bring up an interesting point though.  I have found that doing
conversions from one mainframe computer to another has been less and
less common over the years.  Conversions look like conversions, and
usually a redesign is a better way to go.  I have to admit that I
haven't switched from a system based upon a relational database to
another based on a relational database - that might be more practical
for a conversion. 

It has been cheaper to redesign everything and just convert the data. 
Maybe with an OO system, it would be worth while analyzing what should
be kept, or maybe you keep all of the old objects, even if you are doing
a redesign of the system.  I don't know. 

J.D. Edwards spent a fortune to move their ERP from a single platform to
a generic platform.  But they didn't create a system designed for a
single shop.  (SAP and BAAN basically did such a design - requiring all
shops to give up their competitive advantage and become the same).  I
imagine OO was a big part of such an effort (if anybody knows if this is
true or not, please pipe in).

I am more interested in deciding between buying a billing package from
vendor A with an OO design, or from vendor B with standard Cobol.  Our
mainframe programmers are already skilled in standard Cobol.  When we
buy a receivables package from vendor C, would the two OO designs
synergistically work together?  Can we build on the purchased objects,
and count on them being legally available when we switch
vendors/platforms?

PC's tend to be the bleeding edge nowadays.  Mainframe systems can't
afford to bleed - but no amount of PC designs will catch all of the
problems with enterprise systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c3c9b3.5074193@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C1743C.9EA72C29@home.com> <38C185C7.AFED603A@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

[snip]

Jimmy:

I agree 100% with your statements that comparing OO with any of our
products isn't really a proper comparision.  It's a classic "apples
and oranges" comparision.  COBOL sp2, FormPrint, Thin Client, etc.
will work just fine from a COBOL application which is OO or non-OO.

But using object oriented programming techniques doesn't imply
developing a GUI or Windowing or communicating with the Windows Print
Manager or running an Internet based Client/Server version of your
application.

>Assume there were 10 operating systems with an equal share of the
>market, not just Windows. Does that mean I should write software to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>handle it ?

Actually, yes.  Our products have always been compiler independent as
well as largely O/S independent.  We don't *choose* to support all
operating systems, but the ones we have supported in the past (some
are still supported) include:

MS-DOS, many UNIX derivatives, VAX/VMS, Open VMS, OS/2, Flex/OS,
Windows 3.0, Windows 3.1/3.11, Win95/98/NT/2000.  All of these have
been supported with our fundamental philosophy that the program source
code should NOT have to be changed, nor the screen definitions.  The
COBOL programmer simply plugs in a different CALLed sp2 module.  In
some cases the CALLed module was an .exe (MS-DOS); in others it was an
.obj (UNIX) and in others a dynamic link library (Windows).

>Yes - let me say it, I want OO COBOL to be 'all singing, all dancing'
>and to hell with Windows or any other O/S - I want my business logic
>totally controlled from COBOL, not by a casual click with a Mouse !

I couldn't agree more.  That is precisely our philosophy as well!  




Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C3F7A8.F310DDF8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C1743C.9EA72C29@home.com> <38C185C7.AFED603A@home.com> <38c3c9b3.5074193@news.epix.net>`

```


Bob Wolfe wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Let's develop that last bit some more - I said OO COBOL but truly I
meant COBOL. As you've indicated you merely load a version of SP2 per
O/S. Under ideal conditions you would prefer not to do that giving you a
saving on development/maintenance costs.

I don't know how many staff you have but all I have is moi ! So if
Chevron says they like my system but they want it for a mainframe, then
Amoco says they like it but want to run it on Unix etc..... I run into a
real dilemma.

Howard said in a separate message he converts to a particular O/S
environment. I don't think what I'm really after is impossible.
Regardless of O/S if source code is written to a common format, subject
to which COBOL standard is current, then it should be solely a matter of
taking that source through a different compiler.

Simple example of where it just can't happen. Don Tees is now writing
in  Fujitsu OO, and Thane will(is) soon be doing the same. BUT, as it
stands they can't swap code with me and NetExpress - it just wont work.
And here I'm not referring to the fact that the two compilers have taken
a different approach to OO, just the common syntax to identify classes
etc. Same applies with Cheesle and AcuCOBOL - if he's using that 'dual
feature' Screen Section Text/or GUI he can't swap code with us either.

Judson has his date routines and Michael his field conversions, I
haven't looked at the latter but I think he said they are in BASIC - no
matter - what if he writes them in COBOL. To be useful, Judson's and
Michael's routines have to be acceptable to any compiler - because each
compiler accepts a common source format.

What each compiler does is a separate issue -  but the source from COBOL
should be universal so that we can all start swapping some useful code. 
Agreed there will be differences in file handling syntax, (I'm vague on
the topic but I'm thinking of the JCL control on mainframes). I'm really
getting at the non-device aspects of the language giving us portability
of routines which currently get re-invented over and over again as you
move around the world. (I've mentioned it privately to Bill and Ken  -
but I'm not sure Ken buys into the idea).

Yep, I'm back to my theme Open Source - but to clarify that issue I've
re-christened it 'Public Domain' COBOL source code.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c417c9.25070963@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C1743C.9EA72C29@home.com> <38C185C7.AFED603A@home.com> <38c3c9b3.5074193@news.epix.net> <38C3F7A8.F310DDF8@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

[snip]

James:

>Let's develop that last bit some more - I said OO COBOL but truly I
>meant COBOL. As you've indicated you merely load a version of SP2 per
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>taking that source through a different compiler.

I think that we've managed to come close to that.  For us, a UNIX port
is literally a recompile.  It's fast and simple.

Even a port to a non-supported operating system has typically only
taken us one to two mandays, which is pretty quick.

I do agree with you that under ideal conditions, all that should be
necessary is a recompile.

>Simple example of where it just can't happen. Don Tees is now writing
>in  Fujitsu OO, and Thane will(is) soon be doing the same. BUT, as it
>stands they can't swap code with me and NetExpress - it just wont work.

Not writing Fujitsu OO code.  If they wrote non-OO source and stuck to
the ANSI standard, you should be able to compile their source in
NetExpress.

>And here I'm not referring to the fact that the two compilers have taken
>a different approach to OO, just the common syntax to identify classes
>etc. Same applies with Cheesle and AcuCOBOL - if he's using that 'dual
>feature' Screen Section Text/or GUI he can't swap code with us either.

I'm not so sure that it is the dual feature that prevents code swap
with Cheesle....I think that doing any GUI development (dual mode or
not) results in source code which is proprietary to Acucobol.

>Judson has his date routines and Michael his field conversions, I
>haven't looked at the latter but I think he said they are in BASIC - no
>matter - what if he writes them in COBOL. To be useful, Judson's and
>Michael's routines have to be acceptable to any compiler - because each
>compiler accepts a common source format.

Ooooooohhhh!!!  I love it when I read "routines have to be acceptable
to any compiler"  That is indeed our philosophy and we couldn't agree
more!

>What each compiler does is a separate issue -  but the source from COBOL
>should be universal so that we can all start swapping some useful code. 

Agreed 100% (if I could agree more, I would!)

>Agreed there will be differences in file handling syntax, (I'm vague on
>the topic but I'm thinking of the JCL control on mainframes). I'm really
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>re-christened it 'Public Domain' COBOL source code.

It does indeed make sense to avoid re-writing code......but I have
seen a marked increase in propriertary language extensions during the
past few years.  This will only serve to make your source....and
Thane's source....and Cheesle's source...and Ken's source (blah, blah,
blah), LESS compiler independent and more proprietary.

In my opinion it diminishes all of the very important reasons to have
a language standard.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C42E4C.3886C9CE@home.com>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C1743C.9EA72C29@home.com> <38C185C7.AFED603A@home.com> <38c3c9b3.5074193@news.epix.net> <38C3F7A8.F310DDF8@home.com> <38c417c9.25070963@news.epix.net>`

```


Bob Wolfe wrote:
> 
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> a language standard.

In principle you are right. But it is an area we have to be very careful
about. Reflect - if Fujitsu and Merant(M/F) hadn't jumped the gun on
implementing OO - that is, we were still awaiting the issue of a
standard, in all seriousness we would probably have to wait until 2005
or 2010 before 'official' OO versions of COBOL were available. And the
impact of that would be that COBOL would occupy a lesser role in
desktops, with the incorrect assumption that it was a dinosaur designed
for mainframes only.

It's bad enough as it is, with academics assuming COBOL is dead, and
ignoring it as a teaching topic, plus as we know from queries made to
this group, there are youngsters brought up on C++ etc. who genuinely
didn't know there was a useful beast called COBOL.

Same goes for features like Level 78 in Merant which ARE to become part
of the standard. Providing our standards have a general consensus that
something is acceptable, then I think it's good that individual compiler
vendors take a chance and introduce such features well in advance of the
official imprimatur. That alone gives feedback on whether or not the
feature is desirable/effective.

On this advanced feature thing - pity AcuCOBOL didn't push for their
'dual Screen Section' to be an acceptable part of the standard, rather
than producing something which is unique to a specific compiler. What
was already in Screen Section would have been a good base on which to
build COBOL GUIs. Not going to get too much enthusiastic support from
you is it - make of bit of a dent in SP2 :-)

Surprisingly, in another message I see Bill (the great COBOL believer)
doesn't support the idea of COBOL being the answer to everything we need
to do - further backed up in what I read last night in objectz.com where
the chief honcho at Fujitsu makes the same point. (I keep coming back to
my language analogy - if I write a book in English, I don't include
chapters written in Latin or Greek. OK so German is a better language
for philosophy than English, but the appropriate German words can be
included in an English text to convey their meaning, just as I can use
raison d'ï¿½tre or belles lettres).

In terms of OO, I've just checked my paperback 'OO An Introduction for
COBOL Programmers' - where Raymond Obin publishes his interpretation of
the intended. Time wise, his book is dated 1993 and his text says 'OO
will be considered for the next standard in - 1997 - now we are a
quarter of the way through 2000 ! (Assumption here, but if Ray published
his book in '93 then they were discussing the topic in '90 or perhaps
even EARLIER (?) - that's a hell of a time period).

Although Bill and the other stalwarts on J4/WG4 will do their damndest
for us at Newbury in May - there's still some two years hard work before
agreement on common classes, and the tricky issue of destroying objects
(the finalizer method). Therefore, it's not unrealistic to think that
our 'true' OO standard wont really achieve maturity until 2005, (seems
to me I recall that date being used by Ken in an answer to Howard's "Any
predictions ?') - a very good reason for Fujitsu and Merant to have
taken the jump on the concept.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c51b30.6942482@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C1743C.9EA72C29@home.com> <38C185C7.AFED603A@home.com> <38c3c9b3.5074193@news.epix.net> <38C3F7A8.F310DDF8@home.com> <38c417c9.25070963@news.epix.net> <38C42E4C.3886C9CE@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>for mainframes only.

True.  I'm not suggesting that the compiler companies *not* introduce
new features, but it does represent a risk for the programming public.

>It's bad enough as it is, with academics assuming COBOL is dead, and
>ignoring it as a teaching topic, plus as we know from queries made to
>this group, there are youngsters brought up on C++ etc. who genuinely
>didn't know there was a useful beast called COBOL.

Agreed.

>Same goes for features like Level 78 in Merant which ARE to become part
>of the standard. Providing our standards have a general consensus that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>feature is desirable/effective.

I do agree that the vendors are taking a chance that their innovations
will not be accepted as part of the standard, but they should be able
to modify their compiler to adapt to any new standards.  As long as
they *continue* to support both their older, non-standard extensions
in addition to the "official" new standard, then all is well.  

Dropping support for proprietary extensions when a new "alternate"
standard is introduced would place their own customer base in a very
bad position.  The programmer may have to change hundreds or even
thousands of programs if support for a proprietary extension is
dropped.

>On this advanced feature thing - pity AcuCOBOL didn't push for their
>'dual Screen Section' to be an acceptable part of the standard, rather
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>you is it - make of bit of a dent in SP2 :-)

I doubt it.  We have a code generator in sp2 which is template driven.
If a new syntactical GUI standard were introduced, we would simply
modify the template to accomodate the new ANSI standard.  One doesn't
exist, though.

We've already done this to generate HTML and CGI script for a
soon-to-be released web browser client, so I can't believe that it
would be a major problem for us to adapt.....and by the way, we would
obviously continue to support the CALL USING approach, because many
programmers prefer that approach over a screen section
approach...otherwise, we wouldn't exist.

>Surprisingly, in another message I see Bill (the great COBOL believer)
>doesn't support the idea of COBOL being the answer to everything we need
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>raison d'�tre or belles lettres).

I agree with you on this one 100%.  I have always believed that if you
want to write a program in Visual Basic, then write the whole damn
thing in VB.  Certainly the rocket scientists of the world are quite
adept at maintaining mixed language programs, but the problem is that
not everyone is a rocket scientist.

>In terms of OO, I've just checked my paperback 'OO An Introduction for
>COBOL Programmers' - where Raymond Obin publishes his interpretation of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>even EARLIER (?) - that's a hell of a time period).

I don't want to get in a discussion of how fast change should take
place...that's a volatile topic.  It does seem to take much time and I
do agree that when change is too rapid, the results can be
less-than-desireable.  Because I'm not a part of that whole process, I
do not consider myself to "have the right" to voice my opinion on the
subject.

>Although Bill and the other stalwarts on J4/WG4 will do their damndest
>for us at Newbury in May - there's still some two years hard work before
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>taken the jump on the concept.

I agree wholeheartedly.....as long as they plan to support their own
internally developed classes for a period of several years after the
approval of the common classes.  Otherwise, a lot of programmers will
be required to modify their code "under duress."


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
wrote:

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

This is one reason I have resisted the OO urge until now.  I think

don't want to have to unlearn it when the standard is approved.
Fujitsu v4 and 4.2 were not very close to the standard  - but v5 is
very close.  I think that Jimmy could use my code if NetExpress is
close to the standard.  Once we have a standard, we will be able to
share code if both our compilers comply.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C5399F.15FCF8F5@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C1743C.9EA72C29@home.com> <38C185C7.AFED603A@home.com> <38c3c9b3.5074193@news.epix.net> <38C3F7A8.F310DDF8@home.com> <38c417c9.25070963@news.epix.net> <38c52995.47823844@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> This is one reason I have resisted the OO urge until now.  I think
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> share code if both our compilers comply.

The hard part, the significant part, of OO isn't syntaxual.  If they
change the syntax, no big deal - we can change our syntax.  We can learn
now - it is just that we may wish to hold off doing real (paying) work
with it.

But one can actually do OO analysis and design without an OO
implementation, or do Java applications, and even play with a version of
OO cobol which we don't think will be our working version.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C55CFC.F3BB3519@home.com>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C1743C.9EA72C29@home.com> <38C185C7.AFED603A@home.com> <38c3c9b3.5074193@news.epix.net> <38C3F7A8.F310DDF8@home.com> <38c417c9.25070963@news.epix.net> <38c52995.47823844@news.cox-internet.com> <38C5399F.15FCF8F5@cusys.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> OO cobol which we don't think will be our working version.

OK Howard call me a heretic. I really don't see that there is a separate
avenue in COBOL for "OO analysis and design". To my mind it is the same
thing as in structured. So people illustrate OO with examples of
different animals, bits of animals, and calling methods to do different
things, plus this all gets described in diagrams, showing like a walnut,
how the kernel is the object with bits on the edges representing
different methods. Ken developed this theme in the past illustrating
with payroll, showing the difference where directors got
dividends/shares instead of a payroll cheque.

For us in COBOL the starting point has to be focused on file handling,
flat files or otherwise. If you haven't designed your system to handle
all the contingencies, then whether or not structured or OO you aren't
going to be able to do it. And just to satisfy OO, I'm not going to
scrap my traditional approach to the database to accommodate zillions of
sub records to handle permutations. Similarly is there a place for new
gimmicks like OO's CRC cards - we've been this route some decades ago.

Call it simplistic if you like, but payroll is essentially :-

	Employee File (name, address, history)
	Rates file ( to handle various payroll permutations)
	Transaction file
	Spin-off file (a) Cheques etc. (b) G/L entries (c) Statistical :-
	  how many employees in category x, age x, salary range x

OO is a methodology allied against a very firm base for data handling
that we have established in COBOL over a long period of time. It just
gives you the edge to be a little more flexible with our COBOL DBs.

I can't help but reflect back when Thane said he found the DB at his
installation SLOW. Somebody screwed up on the design - it wouldn't be
any more effective if you used structured or OO.

Is there any difference in the system I wrote (RM/COBOL 1980) and what
I'm now doing with OO - NO ! The guts of the system is still 2 key files
- Items (describing the thing that is being inspected) and Readings ( 10
x 24 table of instrument readings taken for the item being inspected).
All I have done is enhance the thing to make it more flexible for the
user, (and perhaps in a sense more importantly, more flexible for ME TO
USE with OO methodology to give the user what he wants). I'm using the
nifty tricks that OO gives me to do this. 

On reflection, I could have still used that old RM compiler, (but it
hasn't been supported by Liant now for about 3/4 years), calling Flexus
SP2 to give me pretty Windows pictures - but would have lost out on OO
flexibility.

The proof of the pudding is in the eating. You can arrive at
assumptions/conclusions in the abstract, but until you've actually coded
it.......... ?
 
Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```


Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> moving back to procedural.

Which in itself is reason enough to go slowly.  All of our existing code
and all of our existing programmers are procedural.  Heck, our purchased
code is ugly code which is obviously converted from old systems by
beginner programmers who have never had a structured programming class. 
But we keep it the way it is as much as we can to implement their
maintenance fixes.

We don't want our programmers to "have trouble moving back to
procedural".  And we don't want to buy packages written in OO by bad
programmers, when we don't have the expertise to work around their
deficiencies.

And nobody knows how hard maintenance will be in the long term in
enterprise type situations.
```

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> What each compiler does is a separate issue -  but the source from
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> into the idea).

I am a supporter of the idea.  The thing is that we all program to
interfaces.

If Merant has a particular version of a collection tailored to it's
particular compiler, fujitsu another and another was written by some
willing sole for MVS and registered on our public domain server then I
would be as happy as a pig in mud. My program would contain a
reference to the collection interface and I know it will work on any
platform.

I use the string command regularly, I don't know how it is implemented
I just know how to make it give me the results I want (most of the
time).

Public domain source is wonderful.  There may be a series of versions
doing the one job though.

By the way is there a way of telling what compiler you are running. 
Each C compiler has a magic code that tells you what it is.

MVS C = __HOS_MVS__
Borlandc = __BORLANDC__

If we have codes that are __RM_COBOL__, __NETEXPRESS__ and __FUJITSU__
we can have the variations handled in the one source member.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

```
how hard would this be to modularize?

working-storage section.
01 compiler-mf  pic 9 value 1.
01 compiler-rm  pic 9 value 2.
01 compiler-acu pic 9 value 3.

linkage section.
01 compiler-version pic 9.

procedure division using 	compiler-version
				other-data

evalute compiler-version
when compiler-rm
	perform rm-code

end-evaluate
.

placing it into classes would only move the *code* from the programmer
to the compiler maker and make it that much harder to alter.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89s3m2$b9d$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38C155BA.15EB76B9@home.com> <89rqcj$nlq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
>My interest is casual, I have no interest in actually learning it
>since I cannot imagine that I could do stuff with it which I cannot
>do now. Nor is Flexus only about windowing.
>
You must either have an extremely weak imagination or be a far better
programmer than any I have met in the last thirty years.  I take it you have
a program that passes the Turing test, another that solves the world hunger
problem, a third that ensures world peace, etc.?

OOP is an entirely different programming method than the traditional source,
compile to object, link object to executable, and execute executable
methodology.

Instead, libraries of "objects" are built, and executables are built
dynamically, on the fly, from those objects.  Programs are built through
dynamically creating objects, linking to them, and executing methods within
them. They can  also be destroyed by unlinking from them, all within a
single program. Those objects exist at the object code level, so can be
constructed in any language.

OOP is a new way of constructing programs, having nothing to do with
graphics or even I/O.  It is as fundamental a concept as the word "compiler"
or the word "interpreter".

Comparing it to SP2. SP2 is a graphics applications package used to design
GUI front ends(typically for Cobol, but SP2 could be used for any language,
in theory). The two are only related in that they both have to do with
computers.  You can use OOP methodology and SP2 both, or either. Currently,
I am using both. I am constructing SP2 objects.

Perhaps I should apologize for my snide tone at the start of this post, but
I am going to leave it and post it as it stands.  I am reminded of unit
record operators back in the 60's that refused to deal with computers
because they could not imagine doing anything with them that they could not
do with unit record equipment.  It is an attitude that has hurt Cobol as a
language for thirty years now.
```

#### ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2000-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000305183219.02212.00000244@ng-ft1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Excellent questions.  Origins of OO came from what I regard as somewhat arcane
distinctions being made between what were being referred to as "Semantic
Models" and  "Object Models".  Roger King, in a lovely,(and mericifully brief),
 article published in the late 1980s titled "My Cat Is Object-Oriented",  does
the best 6 page summary of what OO was originally striving to be.  If you can
find it, read it.

There is one major notion underlying OO.   This is that the methods for
modifying and accessing data must be imbedded in the data type definition; ie
you may not define data without also defining how you will use it.  From this
notion spring subsidiary rules,  such as that OO is more concerned with 
describing behavior than structure, and that you may not really make
distinctions between properties and methods.  And then there is inheritance...

It is an interesting game, and you can argue over just where to "bound" an
"object" at least as long as you can take to argue the scope of a module.     

My take is that what are now commonly referred to as OO techniques are pretty
well unrelated to the original OO concepts. They are more about how to program
'C' and 'C' derivatives under UNIX( or under UNIX-derived operating systems).

As to OO and COBOL, I would say that the original, classic COBOL was and always
has been OO in the sense of imbedding methods for handling data as part of the
data definition.  The MOVE  statement, when coupled with the COBOL rules for
data definition,  are, to my mind, what that sort of OO is all about.   If C++
had MOVE and decent native data typing, you could dump 50% of the code in C++
programs I've seen.  If C++  had PERFORM, you could dump another 
30%.  Of course, then C++ would look a lot like COBOL, and we can't have that,
now can we?

As to who needs what is now the common definition of OO, unfortunately we all
do.  Right now, it is where the money is.
Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?


- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
> Just what is OO?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ordinary COBOL and the FLEXUS products?

You may for instance have one code interface saying:

    Object create as ant
    Object wear shoes
or
    Object create as human
    Object is a limp
    Object wear shoes

and you do not have to tell how many shoes the particular instance needs, be
it 1, 1 pair, 2 pair or 3 pair. As the object instance itself knows this.
This you cannot do with Cobol or Flexus. Btw, why Flexus? This is a Cobol
thread isn't it? :-)

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c56baf.64748694@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net> <8a3n44$90i$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
On Tue, 7 Mar 2000 11:59:39 -0800, "Cheesle"
<cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:

>"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
>news:38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is sometimes hard to explain.  I can take an application that is
written using Sp2 and send the executables to you and you can run them
on your machine.

WITHOUT changing the source code, simply RELINKING the application I
can turn the exact same application into a Thin Client application.
It now runs on MY server, with all CPU use on my Server and all data
files on my server - with ONLY the user interface on your PC via
TCP/IP.

Soon, with only minor modifications (because browsers don't support
everything Windows does) I can do the SAME thing, but in your browser
via HTML.

Sp2 TOTALLY eliminates any user interface dependancy from your code.
Thin Client is very impressive - up to and including Printing on your
work station from a thin client application.  

In all honesty, the most impressive product in the Flexus line is Form
Print.  This Windows printing tool makes it EASY to create simple or
complex Windows print jobs involving graphics, text, pre-printed
forms, or scanned forms populated with data from a COBOL program. 

Don't get me wrong, sp2 is my favorite, but I think of Form Print as
the unsung hero.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a5usn$pql$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net> <8a3n44$90i$1@news.cerf.net> <38c56baf.64748694@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:38c56baf.64748694@news.cox-internet.com...
> WITHOUT changing the source code, simply RELINKING the application I
> can turn the exact same application into a Thin Client application.
> It now runs on MY server, with all CPU use on my Server and all data
> files on my server - with ONLY the user interface on your PC via
> TCP/IP.

That makes sense, after all, the sp2 is an "outside" application, in that
sense it would be just like if one called a DCOM function. That is still a
ui stuff though, so I haven't missed something here.

> Soon, with only minor modifications (because browsers don't support
> everything Windows does) I can do the SAME thing, but in your browser
> via HTML.

More and more portable. Impressive!

> In all honesty, the most impressive product in the Flexus line is Form
> Print.  This Windows printing tool makes it EASY to create simple or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the unsung hero.

I believe you. Especially since the issue of a graphical print in my opinion
does not belong in Cobol, or any language for that matter. What should be of
concern from development point of view is to deal with the business logic.
When it comes to the report itself, it should be left to the end user.
Many would now say that you can do that using Crystal Reports and equal.
Well, the problem with those, is that you will have to go via a time
consuming process of creating a temporary file with all the implications
that lies there in.

My former employer DI Systemer AS has their own version of sp2, which as far
as I know offers the benefits of sp2 and more. The Cobol runtime exports all
possible data fields to the print engine, and the end user designs the
report to fit their individual needs. Providing all the bells and whistles
that a graphical report engine should, sum fields, graphics, conditional
fields, whatever. It is really slick, more people should have been able to
use it.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C69380.68E9435E@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net> <8a3n44$90i$1@news.cerf.net> <38c56baf.64748694@news.cox-internet.com> <8a5usn$pql$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:
> 
> I believe you. Especially since the issue of a graphical print in my opinion
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> that lies there in.

But this is the type of thinking I want to see COBOL addressing. 
Whether it is a temporary file, a COBRA connection, Java type half
objects, some type of temporary ODBC connection, XML, HTML, or a direct
feed into Crystal Reports or Lotus 123...  

We shouldn't hard code meta data, comma for comma delimited fields,
etc.  Instead, we should have standard protocols for delivering data to
(or accepting from) our user's applications.

I suspect the solution will be to use a user data warehouse database as
an intermediary with our more secure data, creating particular databases
which the users can use as they please.  This solution may decrease the
demand for COBOL programmers more than any "replacement" language will
ever approach doing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uEpzxETi$GA.70@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net> <8a3n44$90i$1@news.cerf.net> <38c56baf.64748694@news.cox-internet.com> <8a5usn$pql$1@news.cerf.net> <38C69380.68E9435E@cusys.edu>`

```
See below,
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38C69380.68E9435E@cusys.edu...
>
> I suspect the solution will be to use a user data warehouse database as
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ever approach doing.

I believe that these are called Data Marts. But you still need programmers
to feed them.

I have delivered "one time" reports to users in the form of MS-Access
databases.
I would get a request for a report, call the user and ask them if they
wanted a report, an Excel Spreadsheet or an Access Database. They usually
pick the Access Database.
I give them a loaded database with some queries, and simple reports.

I keep picturing the User in their office until 3 in the morning building a
system.
Users are data hungry.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C6ABBC.ACAB9ED1@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net> <8a3n44$90i$1@news.cerf.net> <38c56baf.64748694@news.cox-internet.com> <8a5usn$pql$1@news.cerf.net> <38C69380.68E9435E@cusys.edu> <uEpzxETi$GA.70@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
DennisHarley wrote:
> 
> > I suspect the solution will be to use a user data warehouse database as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> to feed them.

I thought of the term shortly after my latest message.  We will need
programmers - who understand the data well.  But the tool used to feed
these data marts may very well be something other than a conventional
"language".  A narrower focus might not need to be able to do all of the
things a general purpose language needs to do, and some GUI data
modeling/manipulating tools may be more valuable.

Cobol is still the best tool for doing what it was the best tool for 30
years ago, and that's what I use it for.  I don't see C or C++ or Java
or Visual Basic or whatever taking over this function.  But it is quite
possible that the mainframe type applications which it was used for then
may be superseded by a new method of data management.  
 
> I have delivered "one time" reports to users in the form of MS-Access
> databases.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Users are data hungry.

I wonder if OO people have quick and easy ways of converting data to
MS-Access format, and what type of interface such a method would use. 
Also, could a non OO Cobol program use objects created to handle such
requirements?
```

```
I strongly doubt it. I'm still generating ascii-delimiteds with those
damned commas in between - I don't really see a quick solution and

	string myId	delimited by size
	       myComma  delimited by size
	       etc 
	       into ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c72795.5190958@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net> <8a3n44$90i$1@news.cerf.net> <38c56baf.64748694@news.cox-internet.com> <8a5usn$pql$1@news.cerf.net> <38C69380.68E9435E@cusys.edu> <uEpzxETi$GA.70@cpmsnbbsa02> <38C6ABBC.ACAB9ED1@cusys.edu>`

```
On Wed, 08 Mar 2000 12:36:28 -0700, Howard Brazee
<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>I wonder if OO people have quick and easy ways of converting data to
>MS-Access format, and what type of interface such a method would use. 
>Also, could a non OO Cobol program use objects created to handle such
>requirements?

Yes.  Non OO COBOL (That supports OO however) can call "outside"
objects, via OLE or COM or DCOM (Which are all names for the same
thing really) in the MS world, and COBRA in the non MS world.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a8k9t$l6t$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net> <8a3n44$90i$1@news.cerf.net> <38c56baf.64748694@news.cox-internet.com> <8a5usn$pql$1@news.cerf.net> <38C69380.68E9435E@cusys.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38C69380.68E9435E@cusys.edu...
> But this is the type of thinking I want to see COBOL addressing.
> Whether it is a temporary file, a COBRA connection, Java type half
> objects, some type of temporary ODBC connection, XML, HTML, or a direct
> feed into Crystal Reports or Lotus 123...

The problem I see with this, is that you either loose the Cobol business
logic, has too replicate it into the report engine or you gain a good
performance loss.

When I wrote down the early specs for the Print engine that my former
employer now has, it was focusing on:
    - Maximum speed
    - Reports should be possible to design/modify by end user
    - Keep the Cobol business logic
    - The Cobol application should have complete control of the print
process, however
      without the need to worry about low level api stuff.

The outcome is a beauty, a typical application would be like this:
    ' Load DLL
    ' List user reports, select one and load it
    ' Initialize the report
    ' Iterate through, feeding the DLL with raw Cobol data. Show progress,
whatever

If the end user does not like the layout, he/she is free to modify the
report.
The vast benefit with this approach is that the Cobol developer does not
have to think about the report at all. He/she just calls the DLL to feed the
data as he/she would write to a file.

> We shouldn't hard code meta data, comma for comma delimited fields,
> etc.  Instead, we should have standard protocols for delivering data to
> (or accepting from) our user's applications.

This is pretty much how it works, although it is of course no world wide
standard.

> I suspect the solution will be to use a user data warehouse database as
> an intermediary with our more secure data, creating particular databases
> which the users can use as they please.  This solution may decrease the
> demand for COBOL programmers more than any "replacement" language will
> ever approach doing.

This is doable today and has been for a while using ODBC, "open" databases
or ascii files.
In my opinion it is not as good as our approach.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C7E99B.A3D0D5A8@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net> <8a3n44$90i$1@news.cerf.net> <38c56baf.64748694@news.cox-internet.com> <8a5usn$pql$1@news.cerf.net> <38C69380.68E9435E@cusys.edu> <8a8k9t$l6t$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:
> 
> "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> performance loss.

If the report engine or spreadsheet is on a PC, who cares about
performance loss?  The user reads it in and does his thing without
effecting anybody else.


> When I wrote down the early specs for the Print engine that my former
> employer now has, it was focusing on:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> > (or accepting from) our user's applications.

So you're supplying a report program for the user.  I'm used to
supplying paper with words on it.  If your user can pick out the parts
of the report he wants, or if he can get your data into a spreadsheet -
I don't care whether his side of this communication is written in Cobol
or in Crystal reports.   But he shouldn't need to put in a service
request to the centralized IS shop to get a chart from a table he
already has (or worse, to hand type the data from my printed table into
his spreadsheet).
 
> This is pretty much how it works, although it is of course no world wide
> standard.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cheesle

My suspicion was for what I think will actually be prevalent - your
solution could very well work better.  At least until you have to
re-write it when you move to another platform (instead of having the
data mart vendor create the new tool for the new environment).
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8aa2st$iev$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net> <8a3n44$90i$1@news.cerf.net> <38c56baf.64748694@news.cox-internet.com> <8a5usn$pql$1@news.cerf.net> <38C69380.68E9435E@cusys.edu> <8a8k9t$l6t$1@news.cerf.net> <38C7E99B.A3D0D5A8@cusys.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38C7E99B.A3D0D5A8@cusys.edu...
> Cheesle wrote:
> If the report engine or spreadsheet is on a PC, who cares about
> performance loss?  The user reads it in and does his thing without
> effecting anybody else.

I can ensure you that end users would not like to have to invoke Excel or
something else reading a temporary file to print their invoices, payroll,
order forms, especially not for a point of sale, where the customer is
waiting on the other side of the desk.

Besides, the more links in the chain, the greater risk for error conditions.
And... If you wanted transaction management, how would you accomplish that
"printing" to a file, launching Crystal, let the user select / design a
report and so on...

No way Howard, the alternative is poor, very poor.


> So you're supplying a report program for the user.  I'm used to
> supplying paper with words on it.  If your user can pick out the parts

Important point I forgot to tell you, a complete set of reports are
delivered togethert with the application, the visual designer is delivered
with the application so customers that feel they need a different layout,
may get that, *without* having to contact the centralized IS shop.
The designer is created for end users, it is wysiwyg all the way.

> (or worse, to hand type the data from my printed table into
> his spreadsheet).

Which is precisely why this utility was made.

> My suspicion was for what I think will actually be prevalent - your
> solution could very well work better.  At least until you have to
> re-write it when you move to another platform (instead of having the
> data mart vendor create the new tool for the new environment).

This is indeed a very good point. However, the solution to this is very
close, it is a small issue to wrap this dll into a DCOM service or at least
an RPC server, and then you may actually provide the functionality on any
platform that supports RPC, as long as you have at least on computer running
any variant of 32 bit Windows. When this is said, you should also take into
consideration that as of today, Windows is the only OS with a standardized
printer API.

Cheesle
```

```
I won't be the one do do it now, but this could really lead to an
interesting philosophical discussion of  "Why it sometimes seems there is an
excess of people who want the world handed to them instead of getting off
their dead butts and working for what they want" (or, "The Entitlement
Mentality in Today's Society.")

You, sir, have managed to hit me somewhere deep within.

[hotbutton]

There is a certain regular contributor to this newsgroup who posts with
amazing consistency in response to recruiter solicitations thus:

" When posting to comp.lang.cobol please include a rate, or range of rates,
associated with the position(s) offered; doing otherwise leads many to
conclude that you are either trolling for resumes or running a blind ad to
determine rates."

Now, I am not enamored this particular response (for reasons I shan't bore
you with at this time),  but this same person also posts with amazing
consistency in response to those who post what is obviously homework:

"Please do your own homework."

..which post I enthusiastically endorse, support, laud, and would probably
vote for.

Generally I ignore those whom to me it seems would rather be be handed
answers than experience the joy of learning through work, because they'll be
history soon anyway.

[/hotbutton]

MCM
```

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:38c9d9f1.181949073@news.cox-internet.com...
> What has the acceptance of the product been like?

Honestly, I don't know the numbers as I do not work there anymore. But I
have been told that it has been widely accepted, using the default forms.
Obviously, there is no way of controling if the end users are using the
designer or not.

> targeted report writers on several occasions. With EACH I have found
> that MOST (not all) users come back and want us to write the report
> queires for them.

One important distinction here, the user does not do the query, the data set
are defined statically, it is just a matter of choosing what they want
printed, where they want it printed and how it should look like. Besides, I
am aware that the end user may choose not to do it anyway, this method does
however simplify the process anyway as the development of a report may be
moved to sales and/or tech support, rather than occupying the small resource
of Cobol developers.

Cheesle
```

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c58e97.36489760@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net> <8a3n44$90i$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
"Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:

>"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
>news:38c3cf44.6499768@news.epix.net...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>joke, right?

Absolutely!  I actually enjoy a good teasing match now and again!

>To be serious, I actually was wondering if there were something I was
>missing. I know one can use Flexus to do a dialog, to access ActiveX and
>printing. Is there something else that I don't know of?

I'm getting old and can't remember the details from the original
postings.  Yes, sp2 supports embedded Active X Controls (OCX, not
currently OLE.....but perhaps someday).  Yes, FormPrint supports
Windows Print Manager printing.

I'm not sure if you are aware of our Thin Client and Web Browser plug
ins which allow you to run your sp2 and FormPrint applications across
a TCP/IP network unchanged.  i.e. you leave the COBOL *entirely* on
the server (Win32 or UNIX server) and run the "front end" or printing
on the client machine (a Win32 machine).  It's pretty neat, mainly
because it is lightning fast.  It operates at very high speed across
the network because the only thing that gets sent back and forth
across TCP/IP is a small parameter block.

...now please don't tease me about my "shameless sales pitch" in the
previous paragraph.....I'm sure that Jimmy will do a fine job of
teasing me.

;-)





>
>Cheesle
>
>

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C4F6E8.5C1D3F59@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <nt78cs43t239kht3mhpsp0dhlse31buh0d@4ax.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
> "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> would still be written. i am sorry, but there is no advantage to oo.

When you write your mainline programs you imbed a lot of logic about
the state of various data items (humans have two feet).

In an OO system your mainline would request your object to do
something (put shoes on), the details are left to the object.



I will go back to the tired sample of graphics (not a brilliant
sample).  We first write a program that accepts a graphics object and
tell it to draw the object on the screen at a point.  We can then add
as many object types as we like to the program without ever changing
that main line of logic every again, when we add square we draw it,
when we add ellipse we draw it, when we add a cube we draw it.  The
only position outside the object itself that knows about the different
object is the thing that creates them.

Creating an object is commonly built into a factory method of the
parent object, so the mainline would call the 'graphics' object
'create' method with the menu item number.  The create method would
return an instance to a graphics object but it would actually be one
of it's children (e.g. a circle object type).  When you want to add a
new type you add the menu item and alter the object you want to
change, NOTHING ELSE CHANGES!

This principle is defined as 'Extensible without modification'.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C654E6.58A5A063@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<89r5q9$a5h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <89ve50$sno$1@news.cerf.net> <nt78cs43t239kht3mhpsp0dhlse31buh0d@4ax.com> <38C4F6E8.5C1D3F59@zip.com.au> <8a3d6o$id8$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk> <8a3n5p$91e$1@news.cerf.net> <8a4o39$1ie$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Alister Munro wrote:
> 
> Don't need to recompile. Were programmers. We want to recompile.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Alister,

I will change from graphics to dealer trading as a closer mesh to our
projects.

Explain how without changing a single line of code you can insert a
new trade type into every program in a dealer suite and have it
perform a new trade function.

When we create a new object we actually need to know what we are
creating (no surprises).  The issue with disappears other actions.

Dealer trading:

task - Create new trade:
	Figure out sort of trade and do set up
	No change here for OO, it is implicitly centralized into a
	called module though.


task - Adjust trade by $300 US dollars.

Procedural solution:

	evaluate trade-currency-type
	   when 'USD'
		perform add-USD-currency
	   when 'AUD'
		perform add-USD-AUD-currency

OO solution:
	thistrade.add-currency( 300, USD )

The interesting thing is the code is actually simpler because of OO. 
Copybooks wont help here.

Perform hit:
	Procedural average tests = (number of cases) / 2
	procedural performance of code = x

	OO performance of code = x

My code is cleaner than the Procedural code and it runs faster.

I never recompile my main program because it never changes.

If I introduce a new trade it cannot affect any existing trades
because the code they run under has not changed.  The change risk has
been reduced, the testing has potentially been reduced as well, but I
have XP test rig and it costs nothing to test everything :-}

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO who needs it and why?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
