<a id="t-1381bf5afa"></a>
## Thread: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects. — 29 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
When accessing data files using OO COBOL, how do you OO COBOL folks
organize your classes and methods?

Do you have a single Indexed File acces class with
Open/Read/Write/Rewrite/Delete/Close methods?  

How are the different FD's handled?  Do you have to have an override
method for each different file and
open/read/write/rewrite/delete/close method?

Is there any way to use different data definitions but common methods
for open/read/write/write/delete/close?

That's enough of a subject for this thread.  Looking forward to the
answers.
```

#### ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C569E5.5DD1912D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> When accessing data files using OO COBOL, how do you OO COBOL folks
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> answers.

I think you've already picked up on my file-handling source. If you
haven't, e-mail me and I'll send it again. (Just to jog your memory it
was based on Wilson Price's (objectz.com) technique - (1) an editing
program requiring access to a file/files, (2) the DB interface for the
specific file (3) A class Super DBI that the individual DBIs get methods
from and (4) the specific file containing use of all the file verbs).

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Welcome to the world of OO design. Don't assume that data is stored in a
file that can be accessed using COBOL verbs. It may be a relational
database, IMSDB database, ODBC interface, proprietary API, streams, etc. The
real trick in building a class library is thinking about likely future
modifications and doing a reasonable amount of work initially to make those
future modifications easy.

While I may have a class that deals with COBOL files it would be subclassed
to hide the mechanics of a READ (OPEN, START, READNEXT,etc.) in a get()
method, using global variables or some other method to keep track of whether
or not a file is already open. The class would also be responsible for it's
own error handling, probably making use of a common error handling class.

Karl Wagner

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com...
> When accessing data files using OO COBOL, how do you OO COBOL folks
> organize your classes and methods?
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> answers.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c57de7.69413320@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com> <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
On Tue, 07 Mar 2000 20:58:07 GMT, "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
wrote:

>Welcome to the world of OO design. Don't assume that data is stored in a
>file that can be accessed using COBOL verbs. It may be a relational
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Very good and valid points.  Let's talk about these Global variable
flags for a minute.  I can see the need.  You invoke a method to
create an object.  This method uses the "get" method described above.
This method must invoke the Open and Read methods - Where does one
keep this flag?  Not part of the object!  Or does one create a file
state object and examine it's properties before deciding if another
open is required?  When talking about a Global variable, are you
saying to keep it in the mainline routine, then reference it in your
"get" method?  Doesn't that violate encapsulation?

Fujitsu offers the ability to create an exception that is caught in
the declaratives of the invoking routine, by using the RAISE verb.
Does MicroFocus offer this?

Also, do any other COBOL vendors off OO extensions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a4k6o$n7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com> <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38c57de7.69413320@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
>
> Fujitsu offers the ability to create an exception that is caught in
> the declaratives of the invoking routine, by using the RAISE verb.
> Does MicroFocus offer this?
>

FYI,
  RAISE, RESUME, exception-objects, and exception declaratives are all part
of the draft Standard.  I don't know if NetExpress supports them yet or not -
but they will when/if MERANT provides a (future) Standard conforming
implmentation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<64ix4.125091$45.6316954@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com> <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38c57de7.69413320@news.cox-internet.com>`

```

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:38c57de7.69413320@news.cox-internet.com...
> On Tue, 07 Mar 2000 20:58:07 GMT, "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >database, IMSDB database, ODBC interface, proprietary API, streams, etc.
The
> >real trick in building a class library is thinking about likely future
> >modifications and doing a reasonable amount of work initially to make
those
> >future modifications easy.
> >
> >While I may have a class that deals with COBOL files it would be
subclassed
> >to hide the mechanics of a READ (OPEN, START, READNEXT,etc.) in a get()
> >method, using global variables or some other method to keep track of
whether
> >or not a file is already open. The class would also be responsible for
it's
> >own error handling, probably making use of a common error handling class.
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> "get" method?  Doesn't that violate encapsulation?

I used the term 'global variable' to couch it in terms that you would find
familiar. Java would call it a 'class variable' which is scoped at the class
level, rather than at the object level. A common technique is to define the
class variable then define methods to modify or query it. A method like
isOpen() would return it's value within the object. If the implementation
does not support class variables then there are other ways to accomplish the
same thing, with varying performance and coding impacts.

>
> Fujitsu offers the ability to create an exception that is caught in
> the declaratives of the invoking routine, by using the RAISE verb.
> Does MicroFocus offer this?

Other languages use throw, try and catch to implement exception handlers.
I'm sure one of the Merant OO COBOL types will provide an answer. Most of my
familiarity with OO programming comes from languages other than COBOL.
Blasphemy! I know but what can you do?

>
> Also, do any other COBOL vendors off OO extensions.

My company has a product (almost 20 years old now) that implements a
quasi-OO paradigm to assemble source code. I use that to get the benefits of
OO in COBOL without locking in to a particular vendor's implementation. It's
NOT OO but there are a lot of parallels in how it assembles source code,
including inheritance (including multiple inheritance), polymorphism and
encapsulation.

Karl Wagner
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C69B1E.8BDF788@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com> <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38c57de7.69413320@news.cox-internet.com> <64ix4.125091$45.6316954@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```


"Oscar T. Grouch" wrote:
> 
> "Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
> news:38c57de7.69413320@news.cox-internet.com...
> > On Tue, 07 Mar 2000 20:58:07 GMT, "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
> > wrote:

Karl,

Thane has raised a whole daffy of questions regarding file handling -
and has now asked me to send the source of what I'm doing. It resolves
all the questions he was asking about file handling - and even with it's
imperfections - is a working model that can be used for flat files.

You both mentioned the topic exception handling. Fujitsu and Merant both
provide it - they have to - if you invoke a missing method or make a
typo on the method-name they have to have a mechanism to report it - the
exception handling takes you to class FjBase or Base (in the case of
Merant) and you get 'Don't understand message ...."; I don't use it but
you can override the exception handling and insert your own for a given
circumstance.

Now with regard to Thane's query about file handling - and specifically
flat-files - and I'm pretty sure the technique could be adapted to
whichever DB you are using - this diagram illustrates what I have I have
designed, based on the model produced by Wilson (Will) Price of
objectz.com :-

	(1) Main App/Main Menu
	    .
	    .
	    .
	(2a)Print Program(Class)
	(2b)Edit Program
	    .
	    .
	    .
   (3)Customer DBI....inherits from ..>(4) DBI Methods...>(5)Customer
							     File
								.
								.
								.
							  (6) File 								      Errors
	

- In my case I have established handles for all files I need at (1)
- from (1) I invoke (2a) or (2b) passing (1) as a handle MyApp to either
- 2a or 2b. 2 invokes 'back' to (1) to get any file handles that it
  needs (I've 'assembled' filenames at (1) because I'm using many 
  sub-packages of data, (Oil Company # 1 Plant # 16)
- 2 invokes 3 to open file which is passed via Super (4) on to (5) where
  only one method uses all the file verbs - so file-status is retained
  in (5) - any file-status errors are passed on to the generic (6)      
saying which filename and which file-error and (6) then spits out a    
messagebox with the error. ( 6 contains all the ANSI file errors plus
  Merant extensions in Working-storage tables).

- typically 2 just says invoke MyCustomerDBI "read-next-record"; if it
  was "start-primekey" or "read-primekey" then it also passes the key.
- 3 doesn't concern itself but passes that message on to 4 - which
sets   a flag saying which verb to use - this verb flag is passed to 5 -
  which accesses the record and found/notfound - returning the          
access-result plus a copy of the record - to 4 - which means 3 has got  
it, because it is a sub-class 4.
- Note most of the donkey-work for file-handling occurs in 4, completely
  by-passing 3 - the latter serves as a 'retainer' where I can say    
from (2) invoke MyCustomerDBI "get-zipcode" or (3) also acts as a    
'retainer' to build up lists(collections/dictionaries) primarily used  
for listboxes/droplists. 
- now either the whole record is returned to 2 or only specific data
  to meet the particular query, based on the original message
passed      from 2.

Hope the above doesn't sound like mumbo jumbo - and I've used the
numbers, hoping to illustrate easier what it is doing.

So forget all about your syntactical get() set () etc... You do it in
plain English in COBOL - and use our traditional file-status to pick up
on errors.  :-)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C65D66.682459A2@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com> <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <38c57de7.69413320@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> Very good and valid points.  Let's talk about these Global variable
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> "get" method?  Doesn't that violate encapsulation?

The file object would actually store a status variable as part of it's
instance (this object not any other).  This is an optimization you may
choose to do later.  I would design it to automatically open the file
when I create the class.  Current cobol standards imply you would have
to code a 'completion' or finalize method that is explicitly called by
you program (I would like it automatically called during the object
cleanup).  This finalize method would close the file.

Global variables are very, very bad.  Everything is an object and must
be thought of as one.

By the way mapping of data to a relational database is one of the
classic problems of OO.  They are designed differently.  You
effectively totally hide the database behind an object cover and then
think about when you really read the data off the table.  Day one you
simply load everything, when you work out you are trying to read to
much you can put in lazy initialization, only read data when truly
accessed.

Interesting site, migrating from procedural to OO: 

http://www.projtech.com/pdfs/migr.pdf

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

```
wrote:


>The file object would actually store a status variable as part of it's
>instance (this object not any other).  This is an optimization you may
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Maybe I am thinking at too low a level.  I was considering a "record"
object (Possibly populated from multiple files).  Now I don't know why
it couldn't get the data from a file object.

I dislike having the files left open (especially I/O) when not
actually being updated.  My present code closes the files unless being
used.  The extra overhead of opening for each use is usally hidden by
the local machines cachine, and more than offsets the inconvenience of
corrupted files.  That will certainly impact my design.  But I suppose
that if you create a file object for each access, you really don't
need to track it's open status.

BTW: Fujitsu's finalize is as you desire with v5 at least - I don't
know if it closes open files.  Bill - does the standard mention that
all files are closed after an exit-method (I hope NOT) as was true
with Exit-Program?

>
>Interesting site, migrating from procedural to OO: 
>
>http://www.projtech.com/pdfs/migr.pdf
>

I have not had time to visit all of the sites you have mentioned yet,
but I do appreciate your mentioning them!
```

```
Simply put, would you start any application by defining what file
structure you would use.

First define your business object, just quickly I can see we have
projects, a time sheet, employees, ....

Next what do these object do?

Methods should be asking the object to do something.  Getting
information and setting information is really breaking encapsulation.

If we talk about shoes again, we can ask how many shoes this object
has and then put them on or we can just ask the object the put them on
and not care about how many, or special tricks to attach them to a
millipede.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a5kr8$gvf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com> <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
In article <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>,
  "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com> wrote:

>>The class would also be responsible for it's
>>own error handling, probably making use of a common error handling
>>class.

Although my knowledge of OO could be engraved on the head of a
pin with enough room left over for a PL/I manual, perhaps I should
shut up.

In my experience, a 'common error handling class' (routine) is a curse.
This class (subroutine) which would open, read, etc. must
be able to distinguish between application errors (no data),
locked files and records, system errors (damaged file), etc.  I have
never been able to come up with satisfactory common I/O routines which
would consider EVERY eventuality and provide MEANINGFUL error messages
to the user without making the routine complicated.

For example, the 'READNEXT' must differentiate between an
AT END on the first read versus all reads after the first.
Depending on the application, perhaps the OPEN should create
the file if it does not already exist, etc. So, I think your classes
need to consider this level of detail otherwise they are NG.

Since Thane plans on developing a simple application and presenting
its evolution to the forum, we should consider real-world requirements
rather than handing off one's responsibilities to a 'common error
handling class' and make the application complete.

Thanks

TOny DIlworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Ptx4.512$K5.20796@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com> <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8a5kr8$gvf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
A common error handler, or any class for that matter, does not necessarility
deal with EVERY eventuality in every context. It deals with the things that
are generic. If you want to concern yourself with a specific eventuality
then you override the default handler with one that makes more sense in that
specific context.

Clearly a getNext() method should return an 'at end' condition in MOST
cases. I would argue that the best way to do this is to have the data access
method 'throw' the exception and have my application code 'catch' the
exception, rather than allowing it to pass to the default error handler.

The default exception handler should probably know the difference between a
recoverable and a fatal error. I would expect the data access error routine
to provide notification methods (logging, operator 'shouts', etc.), default
methods for fatal errors (disk full, file not found, etc.) maybe some
deadlock detection and anything else that should be handled, but not coded
again and again and again and again and again and...

Karl Wagner



"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8a5kr8$gvf$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> In article <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>,
>   "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a5s2p$uo$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com> <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8a5kr8$gvf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The approach that I am taking is a mixture.  I have some common utility
routines (like a common place to store file ID's, and standardised waits for
lock routines) that can be inherited. Then I create a new object for each
file in a system.

Those are file dependent, but with standardised names for the methods.  One
thing that I have included in the standard class is a dump to a sequential
file, and a reload from a sequential file for all Isam files.  That allows
emergency patching with a text editor, and makes conversions from one
version of the software to the next much easier.

The methods that I am using are very close to what Thane laid out. In effect
open-file, close-file, start-key-name1, start-key-name-two, read, read-next,
write, etc.

I am not quite sure if this will be the way I do it a year or two from now.
Unfortunately, the learning curve on this stuff is fairly steep, and I have
a hunch that it will be the third or fourth system that gets it right.  One
thing that I do see buried in the file object is all the recovery methods.
The standard file dump and reload has also proved to be a godsend during
development.  I am thinking of adding a method that dumps to sequential,
edits with a file editor, and reloads just for program development. The
advantage of OOP is that as soon as I add it to the base method, it is
available for every file on every system.

…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C6BF97.EBC2FD0F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com> <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <8a5kr8$gvf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Foodman wrote:
> 
> In article <z3ex4.123709$45.6251485@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the user without making the routine complicated.

The following is for flat-files and a single user, but it can be
adapated to your EVERY, including locks. This is for my Clent File
(Customer File if you will). This is the only method that actually
'touches' Client records, is self-contained on file-status checks,
invoking FileErrors when they occur. I have listed only those verbs that
apply to this particular indexed file, for sequentials and relatives,
I add the appropriate verbs and also discard those not applicable  :-

It doesn't show in the source here, but my " +++ ", using a template for
a file, indicates which lines to change to fit a specific file. Given
the data structure of a new file, it can be reproduced within some
thirty minutes giving you a compiled error-free program. 

So far as your application is concerned - this is the only access to the
file. Sure, you can make logic errors in your calling programs, but
where appropriate they will show here as file-status errors.

*>-----------------------------------------------------------           
Method-ID. "access-file".                                               
*>-----------------------------------------------------------           
input-output section.                                                   
                                                                        
 set keycompress"7"                                                     
 set datacompress"1"                                                    
                                                                        
*>    NOTE :      link with cbldc001.obj                                
                                                                        
*>  Add additional info for alternate keys                              
                                                                        
copy "clients1.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==Data==.                    
                                                                        
File Section.                                                           
FD  Data-File.                                                          
01  Data-record.                                                        
copy "clients2.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==Data==.           *> +++   
                                                                        
Linkage Section.                                                        
copy "fileactn.cpy".                                                    
01 lnk-PrimeKey                          pic x(06).            *> +++   
01 lnk-result                            pic x(4) comp-5.               
                                                                        
Procedure Division Using     file-action                                
                             lnk-PrimeKey                      *> +++   
                   returning lnk-result.                                
                                                                        
*> "evaluates" below are listed in most likely frequency sequence       
*> for this particular file                                             
                                                                        
   set file-result-ok to true                                           
   initialize ws-Error-Data                                             
                                                                        
   if   dont-initialize                                                 
        continue                                                        
                                                                        
   else initialize os-record                                            
   End-if                                                               
                                                                        
   Evaluate TRUE                                                        
                                                                        
     When read-PrimeKey                                                 
                                                                        
      move lnk-PrimeKey to Data-PrimeKey                                
      Read Data-File                                                    
        Invalid key                                                     
          Set record-not-found to True                                  
        Not invalid key                                                 
          Move Data-record to os-record                                 
      End-Read                                                          
                                                                        
     When read-next                                                     
                                                                        
      Read Data-File next record                                        
        At End                                                          
          Set file-finis to True                                        
        Not at End                                                      
          Move Data-record to os-record                                 
      End-Read                                                          
                                                                        
     When start-PrimeKey                                                
                                                                        
      Move lnk-PrimeKey to Data-PrimeKey                                
      Start Data-File key >= Data-PrimeKey                              
        Invalid key                                                     
          Set file-finis to True                                        
      End-Start                                                         
                            *> DBIMethods "rewrite-record"              
                            *> uses 'set-file-object' below             
     When rewrite-record                                                
                                                                        
      move os-record to Data-record                                     
      Rewrite Data-record                                               
                                                                        
                            *> DBIMethods "write-record"                
                            *> uses "set-file-object' below             
     When write-record                                                  
                                                                        
      move os-record to Data-record                                     
      Write Data-record                                                 
                                                                        
     When delete-record                                                 
                                                                        
      if  lnk-PrimeKey = os-PrimeKey                                    
          Delete  Data-File record                                      
      End-if                                                            
                                                                        
     When read-previous                                                 
                                                                        
      Read Data-File previous record                                    
        At End                                                          
          Set file-finis to True                                        
        Not at End                                                      
          Move Data-record to os-record                                 
      End-Read                                                          
                                                                        
     When close-file         Close Data-File                            
     When open-io-file       Open I-O Data-File                         
     When open-input-file    Open input Data-File                       
     When open-output-file   Open output Data-file                      
                                                                        
                                                                        
    When other                                                          
      invoke super "invalid-action" using file-action                   
      set file-error to true                                            
                                                                        
   End-evaluate                                                         
                                                                        
   if ws-filestatus <> "00"                                             
      move os-PrimeKey  to ws-error-key        *> +++                   
      move os-Shortname to ws-error-name       *> +++                   
      invoke super "check-file-status"                                  
             using     ws-ErrorFields                                   
                       file-action                                      
                       ws-file-result                                   
             returning lnk-result                                       
                                                                        
   else move ws-file-result to lnk-result                               
   End-if                                                               
End Method "access-file".                                               
*>---------------------------------------------------------------       


Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>

```
In article <38C6BF97.EBC2FD0F@home.com>,
  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

> Method "Access-File"

Thanks for taking the time to send 'Access-File'.

However, I do think that it is a little over-simplified.
Surely, most files have multiple keys. Your method would
require starts for each of those keys, and perhaps different
types of starts. Furthermore, depending on the application,
the same file-status may have different meanings. Presumably,
these are handled in the program which calls 'access-file'.
Is it an oversight that you do not check for errors in 'write-record',
'rewrite-record', and 'delete'?

I think if your method had to consider all these other things,
it would become much larger. Anyway, does the Main program
not have to test the status anyway, to find out what the method
did?

If it isn't too much trouble, could you provide a copy of the
Main program which would show the use of 'Access-File'.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> In article <38C6BF97.EBC2FD0F@home.com>,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for taking the time to send 'Access-File'.

Believe me it isn't over-simplified. Thane, at Tony's request I'll do
some extracts to show how it pieces together - and it will also answer
your query about my "invalid file-action" bit which our friend Ken would
probably say is XP - me I call it belt and braces or perhaps more
clearly "peace-of-mind" for me; I know it's going to work and I've
trapped yet another eventuality of an error occurring in a message being
passed to the file class. 

Regarding the oversight on writing, rewriting, deleting etc... that
puzzled me a bit until I went back and looked at the source. Now here's
the problem - I've hived off the file handling as a totally separate
entity - so the method I'm showing you doesn't show the complete story.
I think the extracts I produce will. Your oversight is covered in the
''Edit Customer File' class so :-

	read record
	if found set RecordFound to true
	         set RecordNotChanged to true
		 then display it
		 (set flag-changed if user changes anything)

	if not found
		 set RecordNotFound to true
		 set Record NotChanged to true
		 start accepting new data

Now following on (in the Edit) from :-

	OK update :-

	if	recordFound
		rewrite record

	else	write record

and 	from OK to delete this ? -

	delete-no display empty-screen

	delete-yes if RecordFound
		      then delete
		      display empty-screen

Bear with me Tony, while I do those extracts. I think you see it is more
advantageous to develop something through a series of articles for
cobolreport.com rather than random bits and pieces here.

Howard - I noted you recently used the phrase 'belt and suspenders' - is
that legit Yankee talk or did you folks yet again Americanize - by
translating my  English-English 'belt and braces' ?

Jimmy
```

```
>
>	read record
>	if found set RecordFound to true
>	         set RecordNotChanged to true
>		 then display it

A not really OO Query here.

I notice you using a RecordNotChanged flag.  What causes it to get set
to RecordChanged?

Presently, when I read a record, I store a copy in a "Last Record"
area, so that I can compare before tossing the present record and warn
the user of a not saved state.  It's wasteful, but only a single
compare.  Tell me a bit about your flag - I like it better than what I
have done.
```

#### ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - File Access Objects.

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a7683$m98$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
As I see it, we start with some simple objects that can be implemented with
accept/display statements.  Things like
DISPLAY "THIS IS STATIC TEXT" AT LINE X COLUMN Y BACKGROUND-COLOUR
PALE-BLUE.."
.
Using those and basic mouse trapping, I can write a text GUI that would run
on an XT if I still had one.

 A pushbutton should take an expert about an hour.

 We then emulate those objects with SP2. Swap DLLS, and we have a gui.
Later, we can add new attributes like borders arround the buttons.  Existing
code can be converted to that as a standard. Nothing but standard Cobol is
needed, not even a screen section, and we can covert anything to GUI.

The key is that upgrading now becomes quite simple.  We can test setting new
attributes and starting to add new pictorial attributes to existing objects
on an existing screen.  Systems can make graphical attributes available to
the user as they are tested. The business aspects of the of the software
have nothing to do with the human interface, and it can be changed strictly
within the DLL.

Give us an expert at HTML, and we have another DLL. Swap DLLS and upgrade to
CGI.

Same with Isam.  Build some simple objects and standardize the interface
early.  I will propose a standard right here and now.  Object data records
should never contain comp field of any type, BCD should never be used, and
all signed numeric data should be SIGN SEPARATE LEADING. That way, every
cobol in the world can talk to every other Cobol in the world.  If everybody
in this echo agreed to use that, we would have made a good start.

Then swap a central data base DLL, or client server DLL. If I can code it as
simple open, close, start, read-next, or whatever, then you can convert the
I/O to anything you like.

Keep it simple, and the same program runs as a dos program with a mouse, a
GUI or a net application, all at the cost of renaming a DLL.

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <38c557b1.59629519@news.cox-internet.com>...
>When accessing data files using OO COBOL, how do you OO COBOL folks
>organize your classes and methods?
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>answers.
```

```
> PALE-BLUE.."

> Give us an expert at HTML, and we have another DLL. Swap DLLS and upgrade
to

To my understanding, with Acucobol the above will display on gui, character
and www. Without swapping DLLs.

The killer here would of course be to use screen section and then be able to
provide graphical controls too, leaving to the runtime to determine whether
to use the gui or character definition.

Isn't that easy enough?

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-b2959860fe"></a>
## Thread: Learning OO COBOL — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Learning OO COBOL

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c52441.46459279@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
I've just started my journey into OO COBOL.  I now understand why this
seems so hard for many to learn.  You need to know it all before you
can understand a piece.  By reading a book, you are learning
procedurally, but you really need to learn via OO!

I must comment that with v5 (beta) Fujitsu has done a good job with
the manuals, devoting 4 chapters of the user guide to OO programming
concepts and advice.

From what I have read so far, I have several questions - but I'll try
to limit them to one at a time so as not to flood the group and so
that we can discuss each one individually.  Let's start with Factory
Objects.

It appears to me that the Factory Object is nothing more than a tool
designed to manage multiple instances of objects within the Class that
the factory resides.  It is used to create and destroy instances
(objects) within a class.   I can see it as being useful for something
like creating 2 instances of an employee record so that fields from
one can be copied into another.  It also can initialize new objects as
they are created.

So, my question is how do you folks doing OO COBOL use Factory
objects?
```

#### ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<teax4.123071$45.6244205@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38c52441.46459279@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
One way factories are used is in handling input streams (parsing). Suppose
you're reading a set of name/value pairs from an input stream, a pretty
common task if you're coding a CGI, or EDI application. You can use a
factory to create objects based upon the contents of the stream, kind of
like UNSTRING on steroids. Having created the object, all the methods are
now available for that object; such as isValid(), toStorageFormat() or
anything else you dream up.

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:38c52441.46459279@news.cox-internet.com...
> I've just started my journey into OO COBOL.  I now understand why this
> seems so hard for many to learn.  You need to know it all before you
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> I've just started my journey into OO COBOL.  I now understand why this
> seems so hard for many to learn.  You need to know it all before you
> can understand a piece.  By reading a book, you are learning
> procedurally, but you really need to learn via OO!

Thane, I held off on this until I checked with Ed Arranga. He has asked
me to write a series of articles for :-

		cobolreport.com

on my personal travails with OO COBOL, what did it mean, how did I
approach it, when did I fall flat on my face and where am I now. I think
you know enough of me now that it will be plainly written, not thoughts
from Einstein - even things like 'callback' still throw me, although Ken
keeps on telling me I'm using it all the time.

You'll appreciate having scribed TYC24H that you need to give a chapter
or idea a lot of thought before you put it together. This COBOL report
approach will allow me to express my ideas and how I designed, so that
hopefully, it will be a guide or workbench on which others can build -
correcting my mistakes or approach as they go along.

That said, you'll also appreciate that CLC isn't a good place to develop
an idea in great detail - somebody asks a question and you try and give
as quick an answer as possible. But for the real nitty-gritty - then
cobolreport is a better avenue. Incidentally that's the way Dickens and
Conan Doyle did their thing, writing serially for a magazine - Nah! I'm
not one of the literati - just a comparison.

I'll do my damnedest to respond with quick answers here when I can, but
really, the specific detail I will be penning in cobolreport. Hopefully
if folks read the articles it will cause them to raise queries or
suggest improvements, or different techniques. What I've achieved so far
in OO works for me - but I make no claim it is the right or only way.

Having said that, let's touch on your queries and that timing
application you've written about :-

- Firstly, here goes the heretic - don't get too enmeshed in the
theory;   learn sufficient of it to give the coding a try; you've got a
mental 
  reference to the theory in your mind; go back check it later as 
  required

- Definitely make use of that conformance checking you have in
Fujitsu;   at the moment just a directive in Merant

- Your application. Go back to square one; how would you do it with
a     bunch of paper on a table. Now automate that process and put it
into a   COBOL database - which files would you define for structured.
OO is no 
  damned different for file handling - it merely gives you
flexibility    to do more things - covering that error checking that
you   referred    to. Figure out your files, and imperfect or not, my
file-handling       stuff I send you should get you up running quickly.
So with your   file-handling stashed away, using SP2 for Windows - that
means you've   got about 10 minutes to spend on the business logic :-)

- Query - will be interesting to see what you can do with my code in  
Fujitsu without having to severely edit it. Certainly messagebox - but  
you will be substituting using SP2

- Your question here about FACTORY :-

  I call my sub-programs from MyApp/MyMenu - so I have them listed in 
  MyApp :-

	Class-control.		MyEditProgram  is class "edtprgm"
				MyPrintProgram is class "prtprgm"
				My etc....

so, the user having made a menu selection :-

	when choice ?? invoke MyEditProgram "new" using self

too complicated to explain briefly why I am using self above - lets me
get back at MyApp to get a link to file handles.

Now when we get into the Program(Class) - MyEditProgram, and I'm leaving
chunks out below to paraphrase :-

	Class-id.		MyEditProgram
				inherits from base.
	*>-----------
	FACTORY
	*------------
	method-id. "new".
	*>--------------
	Local-storage section.
	01 ls-self			object reference.
	Linkage section.
	01 lnk-App			object reference.
	Procedure Divison using lnk-App.
	 invoke self "new" returning ls-self
	 invoke self "begin" using lnk-App

	*>---------
	OBJECT
	*>----------
        Object-Storage Section.
	01 os-App			object reference.	 
	01 os-Dialog			object reference.

	*>-----------
	method-id. "begin".
	*>------------
	Linkage section.
	01 lnk-App			object reference.
	Procedure Division using lnk-App		.
	  set os-App to lnk-App
	  invoke self "get-handles"  *> this one jumps back to MyApp
				     *> to retrieve handles to my files	
			             *> putting their handles in OS
				     *> above
	  invoke self "open-files"
	  invoke self "setup-dialog" *> which initiates os-Dialog
	  invoke os-Dialog "show"    *> now bloody Windows takes over
				     *> and we've got to wait for an	
				     *> event to  be returned 
				
	End Method "begin".
	*>---------------------

Whoa !- how do we terminate the thing or finalize. Either as a result of
a file-status error or the user clicking on Exit - I go to following
method :-

	*>-----------
	Method-id. "exit-program".
	*>-----------------
	  invoke os-Dialog "hide"
	  invoke os-Dialog "finalize-objects"
          invoke self "close-files"
	  invoke --- any other objects you have independtly created for
		 this program - NOT my standard files - I only get rid
		 of those from STOP RUN at MyApp
	  invoke self "finalize" returning self
	End Method "exit-program".
 	*>---------------------

Having done this the program gracefully returns to a display of your
MasterMenu.

On the question of closing - you might want to return a value to the
MainApp to trigger off another program, so in that case :-

MainApp - invoke Edit "new" returning os-Edit

	  invoke os-Edit "begin" using self returning ls-result
os-Edit
	*>---------------------
	  method-id."begin".
	*>---------------------
	  Linkage....
	  01 lnk-App			object reference	
	  01 lnk-result			pic x(4) comp-5.
	Procedure Divison using lnk-App returning lnk-result.
	  set os-App etc.....
	  move zeroes to lnk-result
	  .... do the rest of the stuff
	  ... the last line of "exit-program" will return us here
          move ws-result to lnk-result
	End Method "begin".
	*>--------------------

In this instance "exit-program" in os-Edit would hide the dialog and
"finalize" other than self. When MyApp receives the result it can then
"finalize" os-Edit because it has the handle.

The above is a variation on a theme - and ISN'T the only way to do it  
. 

Now for comparison, calling Merant classes :-

1. invoke SortedCollection "new" returning myCollection
2. invoke CharacterArray "settext" using .........

Note the second one doesn't make a call to FACTORY - musat check that
out to describe it.

In brief, hope the above helps - at least some. Took long enough just to
type this - so you can see the dilemma of doing quickies in CLC, and
really doesn't give me time to orderly assemble my thoughts.
	
Jimmy
```

```
On Wed, 08 Mar 2000 22:35:43 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>>- Definitely make use of that conformance checking you have in
>Fujitsu;   at the moment just a directive in Merant

After reading about it I am sorely tempted to simply declare the
handles as generic object references, but I thought better of it and
will use all the conformance checking I can get.  I would much rather
catch this at compile time than at run time - although indirect
references can't be checked until runtime.

>- Query - will be interesting to see what you can do with my code in  
>Fujitsu without having to severely edit it. Certainly messagebox - but  
>you will be substituting using SP2
>

Standard Fujitsu (Not PowerCOBOL) can call the API, but lacks a
procedure pointer.  I can't open the first window with it.  I don't
want to get into the whole message queue mess.  Making a "UI" Class
for handling the SP2 calls will be the more trivial part of the
application.
  I call my sub-programs from MyApp/MyMenu - so I have them listed in 

>
>Now when we get into the Program(Class) - MyEditProgram, and I'm leaving
>chunks out below to paraphrase :-
>

This is all very similar if not quite identical with what Fujitsu
does.  I'm encouraged. (Except for Object Storage, I can see only a
minor Syntax change with Fujitsu - it's still called Working-Storage).

>Whoa !- how do we terminate the thing or finalize. Either as a result of
>a file-status error or the user clicking on Exit - I go to following
>method :-

Fujitsu has an implied finalize along with an actual method which is
_Finalize - for some strange reason.  Setting an object reference to
null will invoke it (when it feels like it - garbage collection).

>Now for comparison, calling Merant classes :-
>
>1. invoke SortedCollection "new" returning myCollection
>2. invoke CharacterArray "settext" using .........
>

What does the invole of a SortedCollection do?  Does it simply make
the group of classes available to you dynamically?

>In brief, hope the above helps - at least some. Took long enough just to
>type this - so you can see the dilemma of doing quickies in CLC, and
>really doesn't give me time to orderly assemble my thoughts.
>

I understand and look forward to your COBOL report articles.  I'm on
an exploration now, and I think I have a firm grasp of the ship, now I
just need to learn how to trim the sails.  Next week I might decide I
am sailing off the edge of the world, but for now I am with it still.
Thanks!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C74E23.9E4BD88F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38c52441.46459279@news.cox-internet.com> <38C6D4E2.F3731B34@home.com> <38c72c9f.6480943@news.cox-internet.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 08 Mar 2000 22:35:43 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the group of classes available to you dynamically?

In effect yes. You are telling the system to build up an array. The
structured equivalent of :-

01 myTable.
   05 myRecord occurs
	"how many do you want ?" 	pic x(???).

I'm a little vague if you don't set an initial size, (assuming you
aren't adding records right now), but the compiler must set an initial
value). And you can control the size of collections "growto" to a
smaller or larger number. There are a whole daffy of collection types,
bags, orderedCollections, SortedCollections and Dictionaries - and you
have the ability to turn one into another, or extract single or 'blocks'
of records into another collection. And they can contain intrinsics,
objects and bitmaps. The essence of how they work is that the collection
has an object reference (say, I dunno I'm not into hex - OA OB 00 00)
when you look at it in Animator. Then each element in the collection
also has its own object reference. Obvious caution here that your
collection doesn't get too large, otherwise you are going to blow
memory. Some of the collections have an index, others don't.

Example of dictionary - from RM data files I read in old 'Systems
records" having built a dictionary of what I've already got in N/E I can
then test the dictionary to compare descriptions - then allocate records
in the N/E system as appropriate.

No maximum guidelines - but I have created a collection with 950 entries
so - 951 object references + 950 x 40 characters. It worked fine, but I
got nervous about this, goes back to only having 64K with the 'Shack
Model II - I've done away with that collection by generating the
descriptions from a set of mnemonics. My prime use is for
Listboxes/DropLists.

You should hear young Simon Hart in Norfolk enthuse. He uses them for
all sorts of things; I know Michael M. likes using relative files as a
temporary - Simon builds up a collection in memory. And of course the
buzz-word I've read in CLC is 'populate' the collection, but I suppose
it's expressive - I don't like it, always makes me think of 'copulate' -
not that there's anything wrong with the latter word in the right
circumstances :-)

You'll get a better idea of collections when I send that source. Did I
refer you to Adele Goldberg's Smalltalk80 for collection examples ?

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C659E1.A3B87059@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38c52441.46459279@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> It appears to me that the Factory Object is nothing more than a tool
> designed to manage multiple instances of objects within the Class
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> new objects as they are created.

I have tried to answer this once before.

Simply put a factory method can be called BEFORE the object actually
exists.  Most methods rely on the object actually existing and the
data is available and primed with reasonable values.

A classic example for a factory would be incrementing the customer
number counter on a new customer.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C73F6A.D5698B59@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38c52441.46459279@news.cox-internet.com> <38C659E1.A3B87059@zip.com.au> <38c72896.5447499@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:

> 1.  Can you explain the SELF and SUPER object references when used
> instead of object Handles?

When you first define your application you define the interface not
how it does it.  If you want to store and retrieve data do that, today
you might buffer a sequential file into memory, tomorrow Oracle
database.

When you inherit from a parent you get a set of methods for that
parent automatically.  You can make the choice to use all these
methods except one.  When you use and instance of a child object you
call the method on the child, it may well be serviced by the parent
method.

Now if I define my basic trade and set up a method that does something
(can't think of an example).  You want the child to perform additional
steps in that method but also the original parent code.  You define
SUPER method in the child objects method and do the parent one as
well.

Note:  there is a big distinction between inheritance and using an
object for reuse.

> Unrelated - which OO COBOL are you using Ken?

The free one... Fujitsu.  I ONLY use PC cobol to support this news
group so financing a major upgrade is out of the question.

I did request a demo version of net express to work with Jimmy, I
suppose I was too honest, it was not forthcoming.  If a friendly
vendor wants to facilitate a more recent version for free I promise a
sales pitch regularly :-}

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c7a1da.36495943@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<38c52441.46459279@news.cox-internet.com> <38C659E1.A3B87059@zip.com.au> <38c72896.5447499@news.cox-internet.com> <38C73F6A.D5698B59@zip.com.au>`

```
Posted and mailed.

On Thu, 09 Mar 2000 17:06:34 +1100, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
wrote:

>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
>> 1.  Can you explain the SELF and SUPER object references when used
>> instead of object Handles?

Your explanation snipped - I'm going to regurgitate it and you tell me
if I got it right.

SUPER - Use within a child (sub) class to execute a method in the
parent - one already HAS an object created, so one does not need an
"object" handle.

SELF - Use within a child (sub) class to execute another method in
that sub class, - again one already HAS an object created so one does
not need an "object" handle.

Close?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C7E4AF.CB08DED2@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38c52441.46459279@news.cox-internet.com> <38C659E1.A3B87059@zip.com.au> <38c72896.5447499@news.cox-internet.com> <38C73F6A.D5698B59@zip.com.au> <38c7a1da.36495943@news.cox-internet.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> Posted and mailed.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Close?

Real close. 

SUPER - Except self can also be used within super to get at methods
within super. As an example - in the M/F classes there are methods that
they call "PRIVATE". So if I invoke from MyProgram to one of their
classes, and they are all built into an upwards hierarchy, then the
method I invoke will jump to the super, which may contain references to
do something within itself - one of those 'Private' methods - and they
DO STRESS - don't monkey around trying to call these 'PRIVATE' methods
directly.

Agreed you don't need a handle to Super (Normally - but see below) -
you've got that when you call(instatiate) the sub

SUB - Just to clarify - 'cos I'm not quite sure exactly which point you
are making - by invoking MySubClass "new" you ARE establishing a handle
or object reference - is that as you understood it.

SUPER 'ABNORMAL' - Nothing abnormal about it - but just to express the
difference. Now in playing around with date functions, incomplete, but
I've written :-

	- DatesTrigger - structured (kick-starts application) - and I
			 used it to make 'date-calls'
	- DatesSuper   - which contains all the date functions and
			 formatting dates for display

then I have a series of subs (all inheriting from DatesSuper - with
data  :-

	- DatesEnglish  - these contain the language wording for
	- DatesFrench		days and months
	- DatesGerman etc...

Now typically the trigger or calling program would :-

	invoke DatesFrench "new" returning os-DatesFrench
	then you pass messages to os-DatesFrench - but the 		
	donkey-work is going to done in DatesSuper and let the sub 
	get the day and month name translation

So out of curiosity I did :-

	from DatesTrigger :-

	invoke DatesSuper "new" returning os-DatesSuper - then
	invoke os-DatesSuper "get-date21" returning ls-Date21

So your normal route is call the sub (establishing a handle(object
reference) to that) - BUT - you can call the super direct. The above
illustrates that you can do it, but it really is unnecessary.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C7A8AE.1973A603@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38c52441.46459279@news.cox-internet.com> <38C659E1.A3B87059@zip.com.au> <38c72896.5447499@news.cox-internet.com> <38C73F6A.D5698B59@zip.com.au> <38c7a1da.36495943@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> >Thane Hubbell wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Close?

Cupee doll for this one.  How long before you are explaining stuff to
me again.  The super and self are object object handles though.

When you create a child class you also create the parent class that
you inherited from.  By using 'super' you are accessing the object
that is an instance of the parent class,  you can (obviously) only
access parent data items.

As someone else described, if you execute a method and your child has
no explicit one of it's own then the parent object's method is
automatically used (this is recursive).  When I ask an instance of my
Groper class to breath, and it has no method breath, the fish class
method is used and the gills will be opened.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: Gap analysis - (off topic post)

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a40oe$ktr$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<20000307074224.22009.00000136@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```
Nice to see your signature again and I am wish for you, that you get the
job.

As many other good men (no women) has stated, the gap analysis (in our
world) shows the difference between two software products.

Normally you will make a list of all the functions that matters to your
company (add customer, change customer, remove customer, receive orders from
internet etc. etc). You can even put values on the list (10 is extreemly
important, 1 is 'nice to have').

Then you look at your options using your list as a 'shopping list'. After
that you eliminate equals. Now you have the 'gap' left. Now sort the list in
descending priority, and it is normally obvious, what you should give a
closer look.

Sales persons are using the same method to control their presentation. Have
you ever seen a presentation with focus at the week spots - I'll bet that
you have not. However, looking at a presentation, you will often see much
time spent on nice things, that the competitor cannot do. The reason is a
gap analysis with coustomer values multiplied with a 'Us / them' -value.

Well that's about it - good luck

Ib
RoadRaat skrev i meddelelsen
<20000307074224.22009.00000136@ng-cd1.aol.com>...
>This doesn't pertain to COBOL.  I'm sorry about the off-topic post.  I used
to
>post frequently here when I was doing mainframe work; there were a lot of
>people here who were very encouraging to me when I was in school.
>
>What is gap analysis?  I'm being considered for a job that has been
described
>by the employer as "grunt work".  That's fine by me.  I need a first job.
I
>think I interviewed fine.  I took copious notes about the job he described,
but
>I was little timid about coming out and asking directly about this term,
gap
>analysis.  The project involves migrating a number of legacy processes to a
>client/server architecture.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>RoadRaat
```

```
> that you eliminate equals. Now you have the 'gap' left. Now sort the list
in
> descending priority, and it is normally obvious, what you should give a

No offense intended of course, but isn't this all just "common sense"
blended with some sort of cookbook/worksheet methodology ?  [I will admit
that one could make this argument for probably 90% of what is taught &
performed in the name of engineering/science --- the remaining 10% is really
the essence]

Not trying to minimize gap analysis or its practitioners, but it seems on
the surface as though it is just rewarmed "rational decision making" like
taught in old-fashioned engineering economics class (e.g., identify
alternatives, assess costs, etc.) wrapped in a shiny new bag.

Either Ib was oversimplifying in the interest of clarity & terseness, and
omitted key differentiator(s) vs. other techniques, or there is not much
steak to go with all the sizzle :-(

Just curious...not trying to be argumentative...but rather, interested if
there is really something new & different & ultimately beneficial to the
work at hand (as opposed to trainers, authors, HR people, etc. :-) with
resepct to skill in gap analysis.
```

```
I agree with your message. Although Ib was courteous in providing the
information in his reply - which was a genuine response to a genuine
query from RoadRaat - ironically he and I just recently both posted
questions about 'this modern crap'. 

Unfortunately it's a fact of our 'modern' world - everybody is coining
new phrases, parenting-skills (I wonder what Adam and Eve did without
attending that course), all that self-esteem stuff starting with
Carnegie, the Pentagon dreaming up phrases to cover the Gulf War - we
are going to ..... (can't remember the cute phrase - was it
'interdiction' ?) but in the vernacular they meant "We are gonna bomb
the s.... out of Iraq", and as I've written previously, the WW2 word
'blitz-krieg' described adequately what they intended.

Agreed people can make an argument for new methodology, co-joining some
very common-sense ideas - but please folks - don't create word-hurdles -
put it in plain English so that we don't have to reference an EDP
dictionary for a clue as to what is being talked about.

And I really think Michael(John) Mattias failed us miserably on this
one. He's usually quick to jump in with one-liners, so I'll do it in his
style :-

	Gap analysis ?

	Now would that be an appointment with a dentist ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

Its not a new coinage.  Nor is it a methodology.  Its just a simple title of
a fairly frequently done task.   I'm not even sure if its a computing
phrase.  I can't remember when I first heard it but it must be at least 20
years ago.

If I was producing a list of tasks to be done I would certainly prefer to
use:

Produce a gap analysis report.

Instead of:

Analyse the difference between the old software and new software.  Find
where the new software does not meet user requirements/current processing.
Produce a list of these mismatches in order of importance.  Identify where
the mismatch can be met by different methods .....

I can give this explanation if somebody comes and asks 'what's a gap
analysis?".  And if they already know, I've saved a few trees or maybe some
bandwidth or maybe some disk space and certainly some time. <G>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Gap analysis - (off topic post)

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a6q67$rh5$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<20000307074224.22009.00000136@ng-cd1.aol.com> <8a40oe$ktr$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8a63a8$la59@kcweb01.netnews.att.com>`

```
If you read my statement about Gap-analysis as an anouncement for an
exciting new animal in the business, you are wrong. As I stated elsewhere,
elderly people like me would love re-use, but why are they renaming their
pets all the time.

When I first saw a Gap-analysis in 1982, I found it interesting - though it
was called an Argument-analysis. The needs for a true compare of
EDP-services made that kind of work very popular. The difficulties we found
were that it was almost impossible to find equal terms for evaluating
products.

I am using my own dialect of the analysis now. It's shorter, It's simple,
not only rational and it is based upon my very own 'good-enough-principle'.

When I suddenly saw the Gap-analysis introduced as a new born baby, I looked
into it. Still, I cannot tell the diference between our work in the eighties
and what engineers are taught nowadays, except for a few improvements we
also had to make back then.

Yes, I was trying to give a very simlifyed explanation and I added a few
improvements used in real life. Yes, I agree - I will even raise the bet.
97% is common sence. The problem with this stuff is that values are
impossible to decide upon from a rational point of view.

Try to use it at home. Compare 2 food-processors. Find 25 focus points to
evaluate the quality. You can use spread sheets and whatever - You will buy
the red one, and that's life.

I do think that the Gap-analysis is worth something. The process itself -
finding out what to compare - will improve your product knowledge, it will
give you a good background - before you buy the red one.

Regards
Ib
Maurice Baker skrev i meddelelsen <8a63a8$la59@kcweb01.netnews.att.com>...
```

<a id="t-82bfd215eb"></a>
## Thread: Question about Spool32 — 2 msgs

### Question about Spool32

- **From:** "Roberto Bongini" <futura.softw@mclink.it>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a31e3$rdp$1@news.mclink.it>`

```
Hi,
I use NetExpress 2.0 and I have a problem about a program that in Animate
run correctly with the standard call "PC_PRINTER_xxx". When I build my EXE
the program print only 4 page of 52.

Why?
Is possible that the program is fast and the call "PC_PRINTER_CLOSE" stop
the spool, and in the animate (with F11) is very slow that the spool can
complete the job?

Anybody know the API, and relative DLL, to retrieve the status of printer
spooler of Win9x, like the jobs on the queue or the print is busy etc.?


Thanks in advance.
Roberto.

FV
```

#### ↳ Re: Question about Spool32

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C578F3.DAE405CA@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8a31e3$rdp$1@news.mclink.it>`

```


Roberto Bongini wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I only use PC_PRINT for all my printing but with N/E V3.0 - so there
possibly could have been some subtle fixes between 2.0 and 3.0. As a
suggestion :-

- Can you trap on the Animator as you are about to do a page-change,
  where you get the line-count from PC_PRINT and see if it follows  
through with page 5

- regarding printer status - I check the status within PC_PRINT every  
time I throw a print-line at it - just as I check file-status for
  every flat-file access.

- another thought - do you have the latest "fixed" version  
(CBLPRINT.DLL, I think), when you are building your EXEs - if you have
  a Redistribution directory it must contain the 'fixes' not the  
originals from the CD

The next two are dumb, but it happens :-

In your print routines have you got your IF END-IFs or in-line PERFORM,
END-PERFORM paired off correctly ? Coupled with PERFORM do you use EXIT
PERFORM. Not doing that on some occasions has left me a bit quizzical
until I spotted it. I'm assuming you aren't using OO - but if you are
watch out for incorrect use of EXIT METHOD, e.g :-

	*>--------------
	Method-id. "print-something".
	*>--------------------

	 evaluate true
	  when 	a-condition perform A-record
	  when  b-condition perform B-record
	 End-evaluate

	 invoke self "print-detail-line".

	 Exit Method.		*> this is the one you DO need

	A-record.
	  move x ....
	  move ...
	  .

 	B-Record.
	  move x ....
	  move ...
	  .

	Exit Method   *> <<< delete this sucker - otherwise you'll exit
		      *> the first time you perform B-Record without 			      *> a
chance of invoking "print-detail-line"

	End Method "print-something".	
	*>-----------------------------------------------------------	

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: Time sensitive programming? — 1 this month / 5 total

_Thread started [2000-02](2000-02.md#t-7b994b4efb)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Time sensitive programming?

- **From:** "David Olson" <dqolson@fastdial.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sca7oef6bi722@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<89a9od$9pu@netaxs.com> <20000227004633.06851.00000196@nso-fb.aol.com>`

```
> The timing issues are related to the amount of time it takes a document
> to move from the 'read' location to a 'commit point'.  The program must
have
> a pocket designation back to the device before the document reaches
> the 'commit point' or SSSHHHRREEEEDDDD!  In the older MICR Readers
> that used metal sleeves, if the document got to the commit point before
the
> 'pocket select' decision, checks would be cut in half (length ways).
Later
> machines would dump everything to the Reject pocket while still other
> machines would sling the checks all the way to the last pocket on the
machine.
>
>

Even more "interesting" was a similar problem with GE check sorter-readers.
When they didn't get a command fast enough, they did a "bogie reset" ...in
other words took all the feed rollers up from the document path. The result
was a literal blizzard of checks:) The problem was, which three of them had
already been read when the storm started:) And before you say solving that
problem is easy, this was before the days when check numbers were routinely
encoded on the checks:)

Ahhhh...the memories are fading....
```

---

#### ↳ Re: Has anyone written symbolic substitution subprogram?

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sca1ile2bi7147@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8a1hsg$fm7$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <20000306202845.03148.00000703@ng-fo1.aol.com>`

```
The DMS Reengineering toolkit
(http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.html)
is designed to read/parse arbitrary languages, and enable custom
analyses/modifications
to those languages.    It could be used for this purpose.     We have a
draft JCL reader implemented.

It doesn't "slide text" around.   Rather, it parses the language text to
"syntax trees", a compiler
representation, carries out the changes on the syntax trees (this means it
isnt confused by
spacing, gaps, comments, newlines, or any other low-level "text" details, so
the changes are
very reliable), and reverses the parsing operation to regenerate source from
the text.

It would be especially good if you have complex substitutions to make.
```

#### ↳ Re: Has anyone written symbolic substitution subprogram?

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uabvMdIi$GA.264@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<8a1hsg$fm7$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <20000306202845.03148.00000703@ng-fo1.aol.com>`

```

I once did a COBOL symbolic substitution program for JCL & PROCs.
I also have rexx macros which perform substitution in source code models, as
well as macros to convert PROCs to JCL.

I would recommend getting away from the &something in the free form input.
You might have to handle &some, and &something.

You can use a special delimiter, like ~ ? " etc, to enclose your
substitution variable. (~rundate~)
This makes parsing the input easier. You have the same delimiter to start
and end the variable.

You could also allow overriding the default delimiter.
$DELIMITER = @

SORT FIELDS=COPY
OUTFIL INCLUDE=(8,10,CH,EQ,C'@RUNDATE@'),FNAMES=FILE1
OUTFIL SAVE,FNAMES=FILE2


Theo DP <theodp@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000306202845.03148.00000703@ng-fo1.aol.com...
> >Subject: Has anyone written symbolic substitution subprogram?
> >From: jasman@theoffice.net
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >I'm working on a program that will read a set of free form control cards
that
> >will contain symbolics that look like those found in MVS JCL. The control
> >cards
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Before I spend the time to create a routine to replace &RUNDATE. with
values
> >and
> >slide the text to the left if needed, has anyone already written the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >and its value (or a table of symbolics and values but I won't be greedy)
and
> >have the routine return the record with the replacement complete.
> >
> >I have a main program that will read control cards and using the PARM
values
> >will perform the substitution, but because I am deriving the values by a
> >series
> >of complex calculations this is not a workable solution. I'll be glad to
> >share
> >that BAL code with anyone that wants a copy (great for having generic
control
> >cards that get filled in via JCL parm values).
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> manual...
```

<a id="t-f5fa25f94e"></a>
## Thread: Learning OO COBOL - A project example — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Learning OO COBOL - A project example

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c57bfc.68921984@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Corresponding with Tony, I think we came up with an idea.  He
suggested a time clock application.  I have need to track my time on
multiply projects - as do others in my department.  I think a network
based Time Tracking application would be an interesting, and simple
enough project.  

Initial specs - Choose Employee, Chose Project, Clock In and Out.
Some allowance for adjustment of time based on late entry should be
provided.  Maint to Add/Change/Delete employees and projects should be
included, as should some reporting - up to and including export into a
CSV for inclusion in a spreadsheet.  It's getting bigger already.

So, OO GURUs - Given the above specs, (for lack of a better word) how
would you structure the classes and methods for an OO implementation.

For now, ignore the intracies of the UI.  I will take care of that
with SP2 - but the class that handles that should be isolated from the
rest of the application, allowing any interface to be employed, from
Screen Section to Native API calls.
```

### Re: CICS

- **From:** charlie.smith@sdpsbbs.sdps-bbs.com
- **Date:** 2000-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<952495674.1@sdpsbbs.sdps-bbs.com>`

```
Hello Kupendra!

Mar 07 13:34 00, Kupendra Shivapuram wrote to All:

 KS> I work for Peerlogic and we have a full functional CICS engine 
 KS> running on UNIX
 KS> and Windows NT. For more information about CICS on open systems 
 KS> and other
 KS> products visit www.peerlogic.com.

 KS> Kupendra Shivapuram

I can provide a little testimonial on the UNIX version of Unikix.

The company I work for has been installing the Unikix product at our
 client sites for over 6 years now.  In the early days, most things
 worked quite well but there were problems.  The UNIX version of
 UniKix has matured very well.

Now I can't quite say the same thing for the NT version.  I was just
 brought to help finish up our first NT install and from what I've
 seen so far, the NT version is not quite ready for prime time.  It
 is functional and most everything that I have used appears to work
 properly.  Maybe it's just the transition from the (stable) UNIX
 world to the (finicky) NT world.

One area that is causing us tremendous grief is the shell environment
 itself.  We have developed an entire suite of tools (via UNIX
 scripts mostly) to control and maintain the Unikix regions.  Our
 tools ported over to NT after extensive modifications - that was to
 be expected.

One item we're trying to understand better is the kxloadenv program
 that is used when starting a shell.  What exactly does this program
 do?  Does it only set the environment variables in the .env file for
 a region.  Or does it also perform other functions?

Thanks

Charlie Smith




Regards,
Charlie
```

```
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…

It was created such that from a Windows NT desktop an operator can launch
a UniKix region. It loads the environment variables from the file,
specified as an arugment to kxloadenv and then launches unikixmain. This
way you can have different icons for different regions and the opeartor
can bring up the required region without knowing the pathnames, commands
etc.

Kupendra


>

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
```
