<a id="t-153301bad9"></a>
## Thread: OO: automated editors — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO: automated editors

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C7A59D.31A7D2FF@zip.com.au>`

```
This is all about editors and how to make them really smart by
understanding the fundamental nature of the language. 

I described the Small talk editor that can automatically pull out
logic into a new method.  Jimmy has requested a sample.  Here is some
code code from my parser.

/*****************************************************************
*         The input-output section  basically the heading and
*         comments...
******************************************************************
       F000-PROCESS-IN-OUT-SECTION.

           PERFORM U000-READ-TOKEN
           EVALUATE TRUE
      *      We have another section
             WHEN NOT INPUT-OUTPUT-SECTION
                 CONTINUE

             WHEN TOKEN-EJECT ( ACTIVE-TOKEN )
             WHEN TOKEN-SKIP1 ( ACTIVE-TOKEN )
             WHEN TOKEN-SKIP2 ( ACTIVE-TOKEN )
             WHEN TOKEN-SKIP3 ( ACTIVE-TOKEN )
                 CONTINUE

             WHEN TOKEN-COMMENT ( ACTIVE-TOKEN )
                 CONTINUE

             WHEN OTHER
                 DISPLAY 'Parsenv: in input-output '
                         TOKEN( ACTIVE-TOKEN ) ' : '
                         TOKEN-WORD (1:TOKEN-WORD-LEN)
                        ' is not something I understand'
                 ADD   1             TO TOKEN-ERR-CNT

           END-EVALUATE
***************************

I have decided that the error handling routine is better handled by a
separate method.  In the small talk editor I highlight the display and
the add 1 and press a move to new method button.

The editor requests a new name (say error-handling) and I press enter.
It inserts a method call in place of the old code and creates a new
method amending the interface definition (the prototype of each call
for the method for compile time check - read the new standard or a
good book).

The editor knows that token and active token are part of the object
(say).  No need to pass then in the method call.  The word length is
transitory (part of this method not part of the object storage) so
this would be passed to the new method (using clause) the interface
has the word length defined in the interface and the call statement
adds the word length as a parameter.

All this is achieved with very few keys strokes and is integrated
straight into the editor.  It was originally built outside the editor
but they found that very few people used it.  Once it was in the
editor then everyone used it.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: automated editors

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<scfcsna2bi727@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<38C7A59D.31A7D2FF@zip.com.au>`

```
Right.    A good "structure" editor can move language pieces without making
syntax mistakes,
because it knows the language syntax.   To go beyond that, to move code
without making
semantic mistakes, the editor must know the semantics of the language too,
as the Smalltalk
refactoring editor seems.

Most interesting changes to programs can be considered as changes that
either preserve semantics
(usually optimizations) or modify semantics (change of functionality).
You can carry this out
with editors if the changes are small.    If the changes are big or
pervasive, you really want a
tool.

One tool designed for massive changes, that knows COBOL syntax and some
degree of COBOL semantics,
is the DMS Reengineering Toolkit
(http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.html).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: automated editors

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C8F774.C2F26C9@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38C7A59D.31A7D2FF@zip.com.au> <scfcsna2bi727@corp.supernews.com>`

```
> One tool designed for massive changes

I love it when a thread is hijacked by a vendor for their own
purposes. Am I the only one that thinks this is rude.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

```

Do you not want to know what is available to you?  You're obviously an
experienced COBOL professional and may know everything about COBOL and
everyrthing about every company related to the COBOL industry, but there are
plenty new into the business who like the chance to learn new things - and
we need them to keep on coming.

I've been in the business for getting on for 20 years, but I like to learn
new things from which ever source they come.  I thought the comments were
thought provoking not sales glitz.

I don't participate much on the group (even though I'd like to share views
sometimes) because I'm from a vendor and thought I'd get this sort of rude
reply.

Why is it OK to keep seeing references to Flexus?  Maybe Bob's a c.l.c.
buddy.  I've got nothing against his comments and long may he continue.

Gerald Moull
```

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> This is all about editors and how to make them really smart by
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Thanks for the example Ken - and I was intending to get around to
replying. Merant provides the same sort of features - create a class
create a method. This is a template they generate for a class, (having
asked you for options) :-

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      *> Class description
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       class-id. newClass data is protected
                 inherits from Base.

       object section.
       class-control.
           newClass is class "newclass"
           Base is class "base"
      *>---USER-CODE. Add any additional class names needed below.

           .

      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       working-storage section. *> Definition of global data
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------

      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       class-object.   *> Definition of class data and methods
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       object-storage section.

      *> OCWIZARD - start standard class methods
      *> OCWIZARD - end standard class methods

       end class-object.
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       object.         *> Definition of instance data and methods
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
       object-storage section.

       end object.
       end class newClass.

---------------------------------------------------------------------

Only problem with above it isn't 'my programming style'. Now if they
could allow me to include my own default template(s) - then generate ??
- maybe. Not worth Merant's time on a 'maybe' is it. Truth is, and
somebody else made similar comment elsewhere, so much of COBOL coding is
grab a copy of something else, in 'your style' copy it deleting the bits
you don't want.

Missed it first read through - are you coding in Smalltalk and have you
dabbled with collections - or are you just using a Smalltalk generic
editor ?

Jimmy
```

```
You missed the point.  Any editor can do templates (hell my copy of vi
does this).  It extracts highlighted code out into a called routine
automatically.  It drags the necessary variables across the whole bit.

This really is a tough call for the editor to do.  It must 'know' the
language.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO: automated editors

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 2000-03-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bnfit$9eo$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38C7A59D.31A7D2FF@zip.com.au> <38DD542F.C09052FB@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
[Snip Net Express wizard output]
>Only problem with above it isn't 'my programming style'. Now if they
>could allow me to include my own default template(s) - then generate ??
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>you don't want.

Jimmy,
Although undocumented (and therefore not something support are likely to 
want queries about :-), the class wizard works off a template file 
(class.wiz) in your \Base\Bin directory. Names generated by the wizard are 
specified between '%' markers, and lines beginning "*> OCWIZARD" allow the 
wizard more specific access to add code (so don't delete them!). 

You should be able to *reasonably* safely edit the template to your style.

Be warned, though, that these template files *are* likely to change 
a bit between product versions.

Steve.
```

```
    I'm starting now with OO Cobol in MVS and before breaking my head
with the issue I would like to know your points of view about this.
    In my shop we are supposed to have all the aplications well
structured.
    Of course we change from 'lineal programming' to 'structured
programming' becouse the advantages looked very clear, but
do you feel the same with OO ?, do you think we should change, or even
try, change to OO ?

Regards.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a8jco$kir$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es>`

```
"acevedo" <jdomingu@personal.unicaja.es> wrote in message
news:38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es...

> I'm more confused now, I know nothing about Acucobol, SP2,.... PLEASE I

Acucobol is a Cobol environment and SP2 is among other things a user dialog
mechanism, I can't see that none of these explicitely have something in the
context of procedural vs OO discussion, other than that Acucobol does not
support OO Cobol syntax and SP2 is a third party add on tool for any
language which isn't supporting OO other than being callable from any OO
language that supports linking in the libraries.

> don't want to begin a war, I only want to clarify my (little) ideas about
> OO and if it's worth, Has anybody schemed the 'pros and cons' between
> Structured and OO?

This really is a difficult subject, much because understanding benefits and
disadvantages varies vast with the particular task, and the degreee of
benefit for the one or another will also vary depending on the experience of
those who implement. This is not only restricted to knowledge of OO vs
procedural, but also to the business logic one is to implement.

Software design and planning is of course important anyway, but it is my
experience that an OO suffers more from poor design and planning than do
procedural.

I really don't know how to express a short rule for when to use OO and not.
Sorry.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c7f2f6.57263269@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es>`

```
On Thu, 09 Mar 2000 14:15:49 +0100, acevedo
<jdomingu@personal.unicaja.es> wrote:

>don't want to begin a war, I only want to clarify my (little) ideas about
>OO and if it's worth, Has anybody schemed the 'pros and cons' between
>Structured and OO?
>

I don't think you will find much of an arguement against sttuctured
code.  OOP is it's simplest form is formalized structure.  COBOL has
extensions (soon to be part of the language) to enhance our ability to
adopt this formalized structure.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ammm201v5m@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es> <38c7f2f6.57263269@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Choose the tool that best gets the job done.  I hate it when people use 
pliers as wrenches.  Eventually the bolt head gets rounded off and the bolt
is stuck.

I would say that most business tasks and logic can be handled with
structured, procedural processing and as such COBOL II is quite often the
better tool to attack the job with, not OO.

Programs are simply data and instructions.  Getting the computer to do what
you want it to do, to be smart, to be reliable, to be accurate and fast, is
the real goal and the connection between structured and OO isn't really that
big a deal now, is it?  Who cares really.  I don't.  I've programmed in a
bunch of different languages and the end result should always be the same.
Good quality software.  A language is not a crutch, it is not an excuse for
writing sloppy code.  Test your code.  Always test and then test again.

My company has a computer at work that is used to move a huge robot crane
arm to select skids of product for workers to unload.  It travels back and
forth on rails, goes up and down, and lifts the product out.  It does the
work of 20 men at least and people don't have to load the product in the
cold freezer ( it's a frozen food warehouse) .  They finally took out the
original computer.  It was a mid or late 70s computer with the printer built
in on top.  Kinda like a beige low-rider.  It ran a program coded in
FORTRAN, our company coded back in that time period.  My point is... The
computer is doing usable work and is reliable.  It has been cost effective
enough to run it just like it is for 25 years.

Jeff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38CFA0E2.DB2897E1@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es> <38c7f2f6.57263269@news.cox-internet.com> <8ammm201v5m@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> Choose the tool that best gets the job done.  I hate it when people use
> pliers as wrenches.  Eventually the bolt head gets rounded off and the bolt
> is stuck.

True.  But remember that the tool that best gets the job done is the one
that best gets the job done by the people using that tool.

If you're a contractor writing a program for a customer, you shouldn't
write it in a language which your customer doesn't have the expertise to
maintain - unless the advantage of your language is so overwhelming that
it is worth the cost to hire and train for this skill.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38cfce75.572284122@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es> <38c7f2f6.57263269@news.cox-internet.com> <8ammm201v5m@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
On Tue, 14 Mar 2000 19:48:53 -0500, "Jeff Baynard"
<union27@macconnect.com> wrote:

>
>Programs are simply data and instructions.  Getting the computer to do what
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>writing sloppy code.  Test your code.  Always test and then test again.

I resisted the OO urge for far too long.  My coding style is
structured and modular - but is that enough?  I've taken to giving my
existing systems an OO Analysis - what could I have done if this was
OO?  How would this have worked better (or worse!) with OO?  What I've
found is that I could have saved myself a ton of work with the reuse
OO gives me.  Don't get me wrong - I reuse code all the time.  I have
standard paragraphs that perform functions that I can use as copy
members or pasted into the code.  Things that were  of medium
complexity become simple with OO, and things that were simple become
trivial.

For a GOOD explanation of the benefits of OO, in plain language, using
common COBOL practices as examples, read Chapter 27 of COBOL
Unleashed.  It's a very good, plain language explanation.

Let me give you a real world example I recently found.  I am
converting a billing system from flat files to database.  One feature
of this system is the ability to "sponsor" another company and pay
their charges.  When the invoice is generated for teh sponsoring
company, the customer information from the sponsoree (name, address
etc) is printed on the invoice.  In the original version there is a
separate flat file that has just that information in it, keyed by the
customer number.  So a separate read is performed and the information
is moved to the invoice.  The "master" customer file record, of the
sponsor, remains untouched.  Going to the database, we don't have the
separate tables.  So now, we populate the record from one entry, then
save this data to a temp area, read again for the sponsoree, then move
the address info, then move our saved sponsor fields back to the
active fields.  An alternative is to write a second subprogram doing a
select on the database to get the sponsoree information we need. 

If this program were OO, I could have separate "customer" objects and
reference the one I want, having multiple copies of it, as objects,
active at one time.  

Only a single access program is needed, and no swapping of fields or
any of the other gyrations are necessary.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D0E5C3.AC18F131@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es> <38c7f2f6.57263269@news.cox-internet.com> <8ammm201v5m@enews3.newsguy.com> <38cfce75.572284122@news.cox-internet.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> If this program were OO, I could have separate "customer" objects and
> reference the one I want, having multiple copies of it, as objects,
> active at one time.
>
But, If you are backing your objects with a relational database,
you have to have some way of instantiating them with the data from the
database.
This takes extra work, but is probably worthwhile if you can abstract
the DB read.

It becomes a bit more of a problem when you want to write or update data
in an object.
Now, when you instantiate, you have to get the DB data for update and
ensure you obtain locks on the necessary rows to prevent others from
accessing the data.

This can be done, probably by a method that reloads the objects
variables locking the DB rows as it goes.

Now once you have done your updates, you have to COMMIT the changes.
After a bit of thought, you find that you need a whole CLASS to manage
the DB access.
Doing such things as remembering which objects are instantiated from DB
data, which objects have been converted from memory access to DB via a
"read" method, which objects have hold locks via a "read-for-update"
method, which objects have had data updated, which objects belong to a
transaction, how to commit multiple objects based on a transaction, ....

The list goes on and on and on.
However, If you spend the time to add the classes for DB access, or buy
an equivalent set of classes, you will find that OO DB access is
relatively easy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D15050.FA9A1AEE@home.com>`

```


Joseph Katnic wrote:
> 
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> relatively easy.

Joe, as I read the above I thought,  "He's going to finish up trashing
the OO concept". Quite the reverse. In a nutshell with your final
paragraph you give the very essence of OO flexibility - coupled with
that very important thing, before writing a line of code, think through
and plan the design, then you create and write your 'support' classes
which make the bread-and-butter stuff zing.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D24E4B.123A2D28@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es> <38c7f2f6.57263269@news.cox-internet.com> <8ammm201v5m@enews3.newsguy.com> <38cfce75.572284122@news.cox-internet.com> <38D0E5C3.AC18F131@iinet.net.au> <38D15050.FA9A1AEE@home.com>`

```

"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Joe, as I read the above I thought,  "He's going to finish up trashing
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
That's true,
and in case you missed it, what I was describing was changing the BASE
objects and classes to DIRECTLY support DB or persistent data.
There are quite a few schemes around to do this.
The advantage is that you essentially signal a LOAD method with the
CREATE method to load the data.
To save the data with existing objects under this scheme you simply
invoke the SAVE method which may only write out changes to the objects
variables.
And finally to save a new object you invoke the "change to persistent"
method and probably pass parms of DB name, Table name, etc.
Furthermore, all your existing code works as it always has.
Neat hey!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38CF86C9.F539F900@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es> <38c7f2f6.57263269@news.cox-internet.com> <8ammm201v5m@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> I would say that most business tasks and logic can be handled with
> structured, procedural processing and as such COBOL II is quite often
> the better tool to attack the job with, not OO.

I just wonder how many of these answers are:

I don't know how to best implement this so it is better to stick with
the way we are.

I can give you the exact reverse.  I have seen guru's say that they
would never again implement a procedural program, it is simply better
and faster in OO.  (I personally do not proclaim this, my quick and
dirties are straight sequential programs)

If you have not written a sizeable suite in OO then you are not
qualified to make these sorts of pronouncements.  You could not write
a program in C because you have not had practice and a C programmer
could not write cobol.  Once you or they make the jump then it becomes
second nature.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

```
the one thing java has that helps the oo paradigm is native calls.
this practically tells a person *which* code they have to look out
for. since oo will be having some switch which can set a variable, is
it possible to have the following syntax?

copy code-and-data replacing some-clause with some-other-clause
	[DEPENDING ON COMPILER-TYPE]

where compiler-type is a pic x(80) variable

and

DISPLAY LOCAL-COMPILER

which displays the compiler type return value for the local compiler.
I used to think about displaying all other sorts of information (gui
objects available, ect) but basically, if you have a correct compiler,
either it will compile with correct syntax, or it won't. but you
wouldn't want to copy in a non-existant native routine.

>Jeff Baynard wrote:

i would probably write it modular instead of oo anyways. they may look
different, by just an inch, but i don't see any functional differences
(one person brought up having a hash table for oo objects, and i think
i countered by saying the correct procedural construct was a table of
functions, not an evaluate.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: fwd to j4? comments?Re: Structured Vs OO

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D363A2.1395D246@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es> <38c7f2f6.57263269@news.cox-internet.com> <8ammm201v5m@enews3.newsguy.com> <38CF86C9.F539F900@zip.com.au> <lse1ds0ts54aijdk3efnsqnun5bg7lbs3v@4ax.com> <38D22407.83392A6C@zip.com.au> <MPG.133cdf59f9411b6b98998b@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
Charles F Hankel wrote:
> 
> I noticed that Ken Foskey as waratah@zip.com.au said...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> modular in the way that I was writing modules twenty-five years ago.

OK for the uninitiated.

McConnell points out that modular is about grouping functionality
together.  When you group functionality together you often need to
perform multiple external calls to the 'module'.  This is achieved a
couple of ways:

1.  have one call with a flag that switches the function.  This is
typically, open, read, write and close type functions.  This is
coupling and considered bad.

2.  Have multiple calls that reference a common batch of data.  Sounds
like an object doesn't it.

As far as I can work out there is no other method of doing this in
Cobol.  In C you can have data that goes across a number of modules
but is protected from modification by other programs (Static globals
in a source module).

Before I read up on this I considered a module where a call ends up. 
I realized after reading this that it was a bit broader than this, it
covers encapsulation as well.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es> <38c7f2f6.57263269@news.cox-internet.com> <8ammm201v5m@enews3.newsguy.com> <38CF86C9.F539F900@zip.com.au> <lse1ds0ts54aijdk3efnsqnun5bg7lbs3v@4ax.com> <38D22407.83392A6C@zip.com.au> <MPG.133cdf59f9411b6b98998b@news.mersinet.co.uk> <38D363A2.1395D246@zip.com.au> <eAInTJOk$GA.245@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
DennisHarley wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> within it for functions.

True - OO isn't really something new. Sometime since I played around
with nesting, but I'm thinking back to the problems of what you nest
together, how you call them, cancel them etc.

OO parallels this - BUT - you are addressing one method in a
program(class), not the whole class. Something I rarely do, but if I use
the Animator with the option, "animate both Base and GUI classes" -
i.e. not just what I have coded - you can literally go dizzy trying to
watch the flow from one method(class) to another method(class), and
repetitively in some instances, like when handling arrays. I've never
taken a count - but as a simple example, if I'm dabbling with an
entryfield, it may well call upon as many as a dozen individual support
classes to support what I'm doing - some methods may be one/two lines of
code, others a 20-line 'paragraph'. 

I'm not saying you can't - but I think it would be fairly difficult with
structured to compete with this concept.

Jimmy
```

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:38D401C8.C63B8A6B@home.com...
>
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> > You could have a null main module with the global data , and programs
nested
> > within it for functions.
>
> True - OO isn't really something new. Sometime since I played around
> with nesting, but I'm thinking back to the problems of what you nest
> together, how you call them, cancel them etc.

Calling them isn't that difficult. Getting the proper structure is
important, otherwise the mainframe COBOL Optimizer repeats code
unnecessarily.
Since they are compiled with the main program, and constitute a single
run-time module, I have never tried to cancel them.

> OO parallels this - BUT - you are addressing one method in a
> program(class), not the whole class. Something I rarely do, but if I use
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I won't disagree with you here.

There are 2 main reasons I push nested programs.

1) I feel that they are a stepping stone to OO for us mainframe Procedural
people. SOME of the concepts of OO are less unfamiliar to someone if they
have been using nested programs. While I cannot, at this time, start coding
OO on my own, I can and have used nested program in projects. The reception
has been mixed, some people can't deal with it, while others like it.

2) It allows for modular design. I don't have to fight about run time
degradation. Since there is no external module, there is no Dynamic Load
argument, or Static Link argument. There is a recompile argument, but I can
usually overcome that by pointing out the benefits (code reuse, model
programs, and it's easier to change a copy book and recompile, than it is to
maintain the same logic (using different styles) interleaved within
programs).
```

```


Joseph Katnic wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 400-500 OO object methods?

Agreed - but get back to basics. Computing, (in COBOL-land) is primarily
automating of human functions. There are still people around who have
trouble organizing a paper filing-system. Just as the natural sciences
group animals, birds etc. you've got to hit the right structure. Same
applies to OO classes and the methods they contain. So I have
DateandTime - somebody might have a need to break that down into
DateClass and TimeClass to zero in on specifics; whether their approach
or my approach - doesn't matter a jot.

Similarly, just read a recent message (Answerlab) where somebody is
having trouble with flat files - 105 CHARACTER PRIMEKY - which can
contain spaces in sub-fields and also has pic x(07) occurs 13. What the
hell are they doing ! Without knowing the specifics, I can only go with
my gut reaction.

A WWII tale told to me: An RAF flight sergeant takes over an orderly
room from a pretty WRAF sergeant. "Where's the filing system ?", he
says. "Here", she replies pointing to two HUGE stacks of paper on her
desk, one on the right, the other on the left. "That's the IN-mail and
that's the OUT-mail". Nothing changes for some folks.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: fwd to j4? comments?Re: Structured Vs OO

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D4A330.9FECD74F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es> <38c7f2f6.57263269@news.cox-internet.com> <8ammm201v5m@enews3.newsguy.com> <38CF86C9.F539F900@zip.com.au> <lse1ds0ts54aijdk3efnsqnun5bg7lbs3v@4ax.com> <38D22407.83392A6C@zip.com.au> <MPG.133cdf59f9411b6b98998b@news.mersinet.co.uk> <38D363A2.1395D246@zip.com.au> <eAInTJOk$GA.245@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
DennisHarley wrote:
> 
> I think that nested programs would fit into this model nicely.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> nested within it for functions.

This is close (It nicely parallel's Mr Klein's thoughts as well).

1. It passes the data between the modules magically (behind the
scenes).  All you need to replace the information and algorithms is a
drop in.  (preserve the interface).

OK what is the difference:  Protection.  If you happen to hit on the
same external name strange results can occur in your program.  This
will happen when you begin mixing these modules from different
sources. This is the goal isn't it.

Bill also mentioned calling with values.  YOu can externalize it and
pass around structures.  The problem again is protection, the user
program can see the data elements and potentially mess with them, then
you replace your algorithm and oops some programs do not work.

It can be done with some routines (one call one module), it is just
sometimes we need the multiple calls to access the one 'module'.

You are right nested programs is a nice slow entry into some OO
concepts.  Probably the way of choice for us mainframers with slow
bureaucracies.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

```
>

Ken,
  I may be missing something but how does this differ from COBOL CALL BY
REFERENCE versus CALL BY CONTENT?

When I first read your note, I thought you were talking about something
similar to COBOL "external" data - but that may be modified by every program.
On the other hand, if you do use the two types of CALL BY statements, I think
it does what you are talking about.  (And the same program may be called by
different calling programs which pass the parameters in different ways -
"unknown" to the called program.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: fwd to j4? comments?Re: Structured Vs OO

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.133eb1b160d7def398999c@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es> <38c7f2f6.57263269@news.cox-internet.com> <8ammm201v5m@enews3.newsguy.com> <38CF86C9.F539F900@zip.com.au> <lse1ds0ts54aijdk3efnsqnun5bg7lbs3v@4ax.com> <38D22407.83392A6C@zip.com.au> <MPG.133cdf59f9411b6b98998b@news.mersinet.co.uk> <38D363A2.1395D246@zip.com.au>`

```
I noticed that Ken Foskey as waratah@zip.com.au said...
> Charles F Hankel wrote:
> > 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> OK for the uninitiated.

I think the main difficulty for OO is the way that it has been pushed and 
the direction it came from.  It's a good enough methodology to work with 
and achieve practical results.  I'm vaguely persuaded about it but really 
have only used it under supervision and not particularly good supervision 
at that.

What turned me off at first was the references by people like Grady Booch 
to inaccurate representations of the way things worked in a commercial 
environment where we have reused code for decades - either by simple 
things such as in-house utility routines such as date manipulations or by 
having common modules for I-O or whatever.  I resent it when people like 
that take one small area of IT and expound that "this was the way 
everyone did it".  It doesn't make for a good basis of understanding when 
the proponent is telling you lies (maybe unintentional ones) to suit his 
arguments.

I like that Code Complete book - it doesn't provide all the answers and 
it has one or two that I disagree with but at least Steve McConnell says 
in the preface that he bears the responsibility for errors because the 
idea of writing a book is to make choices and take responsibility for 
them even if others disagree with those choices.  IMO it's one of the 
better books around.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C84AE8.DF2866BA@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <38C7A404.DFC27A2B@personal.unicaja.es>`

```


acevedo wrote:
> 
> After sending this message I've seen the post 'OO who needs it and why?'
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Regards.

Well, having re-read your original message I sympathize for you. We
really have led you up the garden path without any answers. So a feeble
attempt to address your query.

Now I'll cast my mind back a quarter of a century ago, that's last
century - doesn't that sound real neat :-). I reflected on your dilemma
back to when I was a systems manager. I look back at those systems and I
really have a hard time trying to justify OO COBOL if it had then been
available. But remember, then we were key-punch, number-crunch and
print. No fancy terminals and the two VDUs we had - my team of
programmers were queuing up to get on the damned things. 

So the systems environment was entirely different. We never got into
inventory control (retail) which was our perceived objective because we
were always maintaining lousy accounting systems we had inherited.  With
the lack of screens, cheap local printers, decent rapid communications
links, I doubt even with inventory control we would have seen a
justification for going OO.

Mainframe or otherwise the game has moved forward and systems are
light-years more inter-active whether mainframe, minis, multi-user or
the little PC. Now my question is, is your shop seriously looking at OO
as an alternative, or are you the guinea pig who has been given the
task, and it's your head that rolls if it fails.

Suggestion - you went from 'lineal' as you call it to 'structured'. Now
assuming you have a number of applications, there has to be one that is
a real PIG - each time it comes up for maintenance one of your
maintenance guys groans - the reason - it was originally badly designed
(systems analysis) or the original programmers did a p...-poor job.

As an objective, (and this is based on the assumption that you can live
with this particular 'pig' a while longer - that is, it will still keep
going while you research), do a fresh analysis on this baby - what was
wrong with it historically, why did it go wrong, how can it be improved,
what new language/technical features will allow you to enhance the
running of a model application.

No mention of OO so far - pure systems analysis and consider it in
programming terms as what you would do with structured. Now does this
particular baby have, even with a redesigned system, complexities that
can give you heartburn. Now read some of the OO theory - do you get a
glimmer that it could possibly alleviate some of these problems.

If your answer is a DEFINITELY NO - no problem - stick with structured.

If your analysis suggests, well maybe, here and there....., even without
a deep understanding of OO - then possibly you should give it a shot.
Others may disagree but I see no difference in analysis of a system for
either structured or OO. The system IS THE SYSTEM the language is COBOL
and additional methodology added to COBOL is the OO.

Don't get steeped into too much OO theory - get a reasonable grasp of
the concepts - AND VERY IMPORTANT - YOU GET SOLID AGREEMENT FROM
MANAGEMENT THAT THIS IS A TRIAL PROJECT, WHICH THEY MUST ACCEPT, MAY
FAIL.

Then you do your damnedest to see if you can get it to work - again
remembering there is absolutely no difference as to how you should
formulate your data files for either Structured or OO. (If there have
been miserable failures with OO, in any language, they screwed up on
file design - getting too bloody clever in accommodating OO buzz-word
technology).

You have to get management to buy into this new technology bit - that it
is a new concept, you want to give it a try, because it appears to offer
prospective benefits - but at the same time they have to accept your
caveat that it may fail. Don't compromise on this one - if they get iffy
- call the damned thing off!

If as the project goes along you do start getting the feeling it is
going belly-up then call it quits. Humbly declare, we are beaten. That's
better than dragging the nightmare out to an inconclusive solution. 

And we are talking one research project - the others are still
chug-a-lugging along in structured happily doing their own thing. One
last thought - DON'T GO FOR THE BIG BANG approach and try and switch
everything at once. Get a feel for the methodology and other systems can
gracefully be switched at a later date if OO becomes your personal
Epiphany.

Hope that's a little closer to your original question.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Hi James,

This is as good a place to butt in again as any.

In line, top down, bottom up.... imbedded, real time,
all buzz words with different meaning to different people.

I'm so slooooow, I'm trying to understand what the difference is between:

Perform

Call using

OO words to start something.

I can say I understand that Perform is a go to, and return, but remove the
return so that someone can fall through.  It's a hold over to the old
times.

I can understand Call using is a "transfer of control to the called
"object" with certain values to control the called module.  Returning is
not mandatory from what I believe, but under control of the program to call
further or return or quit.

Now OO if I can remember rightly does something
like select a factory
manufacture an object
run the object which can be unique or get some of it's logic elsewhere.

Your short illustrations do a lot to help someone,
but I fear that the 4th of July will come and go, and
I'll still be on an early page trying to figure out what the hay you are
saying.

I'll wait a little longer before I gripe again.

Warren Simmons
```

```
Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
Hello Warren old friend.

I'm getting the buzz 'from She Who Must Be Obeyed' it is 23:52. So
quickly :-

	Structured			OO

	from Menu call progx with	invoke prog x "new"
	     params			   with params

Programx :

	Linkage x y z			
	MainLine			Factory
					method-id "new"
					Linkage x y z
					invoke super "new" retuning "me"
					invoke me "begin" using x y z

	Do something with xyz		Object
	perform para-a			method-id "begin"
	perform para-b			Do something with xyz
	perform para-c			invoke "method-a" using x
					invoke "method-b" using y
					invoke "method-c" using z


	Para-C.				method-id "method-c"
	 call ABC using x		invoke ABC "new" using x
		  returing w			returning w

Slight variation on above - could have already established a handle to
ABC because we are doing many calls - so we initially we would have in
"begin" inovke ABC "new" returning os-ABC. I'm extremely vague about
entry points but I think they are parallel

- then our invoke becomes invoke os-ABC "do-this-specific-method" using
x returning w

Gotta go - SHE IS MOANING NOW - hope that illustrates it a bit. Same
McCoy but overall greater flexibility.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C8FAC3.F283225@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es>`

```
acevedo wrote:
> 
> Hi all.
> 
>  I'm starting now with OO Cobol in MVS and before breaking my head
> with the issue I would like to know your points of view about this.

Yes do it, but do not do it alone.  If you cannot convince your
partners to convert then you leave a legacy of code they cannot
understand.

> In my shop we are supposed to have all the aplications well
> structured.

In all shops....   We left that argument behind about 30 years ago.

>  Of course we change from 'lineal programming' to 'structured
>  programming' becouse the advantages looked very clear, but do you
> feel the same with OO ?, do you think we should change, or even
> try, change to OO ?

By linear I assume you mean what I call monolithic.  One program with
working storage section and code with no outside interference.  Maybe
some copybooks for the data.

By structured I assume you mean break down some of those monsters and
get some reuse into place.  Centralizing maintenance is a great idea.

You may have heard of a concept of modular programming.  It's
definition is flexible.  I read Steve McConnell and he highlighted
that modules are different functions that operate together.  For
example your body is a module that functions well (I hope) it has a
hand function and two feet function that do the walking task.

OO gives us the ability to generalize this a little better than
average procedural code.  For example the description above is a data
driven analysis, you have two feet I can see them.  An OO
implementation would be a functional analysis I want you to move, if
you are wheelchair bound - you roll, if you have feet - you walk but I
truly do not care how you achieve your goal.

I would recommend that you use OO in programming but you must have buy
in from everyone around you.

It really is worthwhile understanding some of the more general stuff
on programming first.  Check out rapid development by Steve McConnell,
this is an excellent source.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b7p2a$4l3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es>`

```
In article <38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es>,
acevedo <jdomingu@personal.unicaja.es> wrote:
> Hi all.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have two points:

1) Benefits

The real benefits from OO are supposed to come from maintenance rather
than development.  Most of the replies so far seem to be about
development.

Is there anyone out there with experience in maintaining OO code?  How
much easier is it?

2) OO Concepts

To my mind there are two OOs � one is the additions to COBOL to make OO
COBOL and the other is the general concepts.  OO is often described as
a way o thinking,

As a transition, you might try using OO techniques with classic COBOL
code.

For example:

It is normal to use a code in transaction files to identify the type of
transaction.  Suppose that instead of a transaction code you put in the
name of a module that processes that type of transaction.  This gives
you encapsulation of data and code.

If you use standard extensions of the module name as entry points to
perform different functions, you have effectively created a class and
methods.  For example, a module name + ED becomes the entry point to
edit the transaction, + AP to apply it to master file etc.

Once you understand OO concepts, then making use of them is no more
difficult then using to use structured code or modular programming.

Breaking the job into two steps � first learning and applying OO
concepts then learning to use OO COBOL -should make the transition much
easier.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D784D2.4AB54FA@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <8b7p2a$4l3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Milne wrote:
> 
> I have two points:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> much easier is it?

Especially in large systems.  That is the theory anyway.  I cannot find
anecdotal evidence to support this claim in an enterprise sized
environment.  Of course, OO requires a discipline, which if it were
applied to a structured code environment, would beat unstructured every
time - but I want to see a comparison similar enterprises with similar
discipline between OO and structured.   Maybe it doesn't exist.  If so,
why not?  Did the OO experiment not get fully implemented?  Why not?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D7B26F.1FB10CA@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <8b7p2a$4l3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38D784D2.4AB54FA@cusys.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Ed Milne wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> why not?  Did the OO experiment not get fully implemented?  Why not?

I agree maintenance, as you have oft repeated, is a very important issue
if you switch from structured to OO - but for my money the main
advantage of OO is the flexibility offered with reusability.

How will we ever prove your point about maintenance - not until such
time that you have the same facilities on a mainframe that we currently
have in Fujistu and NetExpress to dabble with OO. Until such time as you
mainframers can pick up the gauntlet to parallel an application
structured v OO - we will never know.

(And on another point you made elsewhere, I'm with you - we don't want a
void growing between mainframe and PC versions of COBOL).

Jimmy
```

```
>
> What is to wait for.  cobol 390 has OO stuff in today.
>

Management approval.

If anyone in an OS/390 shop goes off on their own, and starts using OO, they
may go from current to former rather quickly.

There is no maintenance benefit, if there is nobody who can maintain the
code.

This has to be a group decision, not an individual decision.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Vs OO

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

```
I believe that IBM has some reference sites for shops that are ALREADY using
the OO features of IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM.  If you want me to, I can
pursue this for you - or you can contact your local IBM rep/office for such
information.
```

```
for such

Thank you for the information.

My previous posting was too general.
I usually work in environments where there may be 30-40 programmers
working on a major application.
I may be 3-4 levels of management away from the manager of the major
application, and there are others at his/her level handling other
major applications.
Programmers are moved around within the major application depending on
the change/enhancement requests.
If one programmer would take it upon themself to implement OO, they
would be in big trouble.
In these environments there has to be management approval and support.

I have worked in small shops where I had much more flexibility. The
entire IT staff could meet in a broom closet.

> --
> Bill Klein
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

using OO,

maintain the
> > code.
```

```
No it wasn't - it was understood as written. Your argument is perfectly
sound. I'll use old titles that I know - If a large installation is
going to consider OO then it has to be a germ of an idea from the DPM,
Systems Manager or the Chief Programmer - in all probability, depending
upon the structure, the idea should flow from the last to the first. Now
I don't know where Howard is placed in his organization - but he has
studied OO - If he thought it merited 'a go', nothing to stop him
passing the idea up the organizational tree ?? - assuming he's not
dealing with boneheads).

isolated application or module that you can run in parallel. If I was
still a System Manager, (with no true day-to-day involvement with
programming - my major time devoted to systems design and those bloody
politics - I would blanch if the Chief Programmer suggested the big bang
approach; if he suggested to me it was worth a try, with potential
advantages, then no doubt, with some cynicism - I'd be prepared to give
it a trial shot).

BTW - I'm in the broom closet.

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<38C79837.21E99D55@personal.unicaja.es> <8b7p2a$4l3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38D784D2.4AB54FA@cusys.edu> <38D7B26F.1FB10CA@home.com> <38D9E2F8.CFAE3AD4@zip.com.au> <O5C0wFOl$GA.161@cpmsnbbsa03> <38DB64A8.15F60FD1@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> > There is no maintenance benefit, if there is nobody who can maintain
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> would consider not using it.

Never worked in a shop where it was used significantly.  (I worked in a
VAX shop where they used an equivalent COBOL).  Management never wanted
to go through the costs of changing standards, migrating, & testing for
the advantages of COBOL II.  My current employer is now a COBOL for MVS
shop - because of Y2K issues.  But they didn't want to spend the money. 
I persuaded them to have a one day class showing the features of the new
compiler to all of the programmers, but I don't think many people use
many of the features (especially since our main vendor's code looks like
circa 1980 era style with old VSAM files converted to database).

I wonder if many shops will back into OO COBOL after they have Java
interfaces etc.
```

<a id="t-207e5143a1"></a>
## Thread: At Home Alone — 34 this month / 36 total

### At Home Alone

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi:

Having worked for myself at home in the kitchen since 1982, I am
obviously out of touch with the real world (Thank God).

Reading the various posts herein, I get the impression that the
world of DP is still just a big hideous mess. Naturally, if one is
dealing with one's legacy, it probably is.

Reading the various OO stuff, It seems that everyone has to be
able to deal with all sorts of other stuff; like Java, C++, Oracle,
etc, ad nauseum. Surely, that cannot be good from any perspective.

Did OO arise becouse of this hideousness? Is OO an attempt to cope
with the Babelia and to allow people to use a mish-mosh of 'languages'?

Do people actually design NEW systems using a
conglomeration of stuff and if so why do they not confine themselves
to one language - COBOL? Would one design a NEW system providing
an interface to Access? Someone suggested that users ought to create
their own reports, is that why one would include Access in a NEW system?
Isn't that abdicating our responsiblity as System DESIGNERS and
programmers?

Would a NEW system use batch processes?

Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: At Home Alone

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C7B425.9C981A45@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> Do people actually design NEW systems using a
> conglomeration of stuff and if so why do they not confine themselves
> to one language - COBOL? 

It depends on how you define "system".  I work for an enterprise which
has several purchased components from different vendors which have to
work together.  (we have some standards from some vendors, others for
our home grown stuff, etc).  It would be nice to be able to consider the
best products without limiting them to a particular language.

Furthermore, we have distributed needs including TCP/IP, sockets, access
to other system's databases, cash machines on site, HTML, etc.  We have
no choice but to interface with them.  Maybe OO is the best way of doing
this.  But certainly it would be limiting to require this to all be in
Cobol.

> Would one design a NEW system providing
> an interface to Access? Someone suggested that users ought to create
> their own reports, is that why one would include Access in a NEW system?
> Isn't that abdicating our responsiblity as System DESIGNERS and
> programmers?

Not if this is what the users need and want.  If a user wants to play
with our figures on his spreadsheet and look the data in a different way
today than yesterday - he should get the data in a format allowing him
to do what he wants.
 
> Would a NEW system use batch processes?

Certainly.  Why not?
```

#### ↳ Re: At Home Alone

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CSOx4.7400$K5.125009@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hey Tony,

I think the rate of change has accelerated to the point where we're all
thinking about tossing in the towel. Relational databases are definitely a
major part of new systems, including but not limited to MS Access.
Volumetrics, dollars, and whatever article some PHM (pointy-haired manager)
last read, pay a big role in deciding which relational database to use

Flexibility is key for new systems. It has to be possible to unbolt a user
interface and replace it with another, or possibly a non-human interface.
There are already many examples where the same back-end process is handling
HTML, GUI, green-screen, touch-tone phones, EDI, and other clients.

As for batch, I'd say it has a diminished role in modern systems. The need
for batch processing is largely driven by performance limitations. Hardware
advances make it possible to deal with data NOW as opposed to waiting until
off-peak hours. Batch processing is being reduced to queuing transactions to
be handled by the primary (on-line?) system which is available 24/7.

I think most large-scale systems are based around transaction processing,
which is nothing new but is finding broader acceptance.  A transaction
represents a well defined unit of work so it can be created and processed in
a multitude of ways.  A well-designed transaction will take care of all the
little details that are related either directly or by invoking other
transactions, so many of the batch jobs that existed to synchronize data are
not required any longer.

Just my $.02.

Karl Wagner


"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi:
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: At Home Alone

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38c7f1d9.56978053@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Thu, 09 Mar 2000 12:53:36 GMT, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:


>Reading the various posts herein, I get the impression that the
>world of DP is still just a big hideous mess. Naturally, if one is
>dealing with one's legacy, it probably is.
>

Uh, I can't disagree with tihs.

>Reading the various OO stuff, It seems that everyone has to be
>able to deal with all sorts of other stuff; like Java, C++, Oracle,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmers?

Sure, we still write batch processes.  Personally I would love to stay
with COBOL and use nothing else (Except maybe COBOL access to a
relational or Object database).  However, in the world we live, where
other technologies filled the seeming void that COBOL could not, other
things crept in.  We have to maintain them.  Long gone are the days of
a programmer with a single dicipline.  

>
>Would a NEW system use batch processes?
```

#### ↳ Re: At Home Alone

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#Ko4mtgi$GA.250@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> dealing with one's legacy, it probably is.

It depends on the management, some shops are well run, others are not.
Sometimes you can improve software, other times the management is so afraid
of changes that you can only make minimal changes to a program, and only to
implement new functionality. (If it ain't broke don't fix it).

> Reading the various OO stuff, It seems that everyone has to be
> able to deal with all sorts of other stuff; like Java, C++, Oracle,
> etc, ad nauseum. Surely, that cannot be good from any perspective.

You have to pick the right tool to do the job.
Often you work in concert with others who have a different disipline.
>
> Did OO arise becouse of this hideousness? Is OO an attempt to cope
> with the Babelia and to allow people to use a mish-mosh of 'languages'?

OO does allow interfacing of a variety of languages within an application,
but I doubt that this is why it is used. It is allegedly (there are still
doubters among us) a better methodolgy, because, among other things, it
allows for code reuse in a formalized manner.

I often see the same logic interleaved in many programs.
OO may be the answer to preventing continual reinvention of the wheel
(mine's oval).
But, you have to have tight control of an application by aware management to
reap the benefits.

>
> Do people actually design NEW systems using a
> conglomeration of stuff and if so why do they not confine themselves
> to one language - COBOL?

For the most part you want to use as few languages as possible.
I prefer to work exclusively in COBOL.
But, I find that other people use tools like EZtrieve, DYL280, etc. when
they should use COBOL.
Most shops have a standard that these tools should only be used for reports,
but I have never seen this standard enforced.

> Would one design a NEW system providing
> an interface to Access? Someone suggested that users ought to create
> their own reports, is that why one would include Access in a NEW system?

It depends on how you define report.
If I give the user the data they want in a format that is much more
flexible, why not do it?
If you give them a hard copy report, they will be entering the data into
Excel, or Access anyway.
All I do is make their job easier.
They can take the data and print or summarize any way they want, without
having to go thru channels. "Can I get this report in a different sequence?"
"Sure, it will take 2 days, and I will have someone available in 2 weeks".

Most of the operational processing is done in COBOL.

> Isn't that abdicating our responsiblity as System DESIGNERS and
> programmers?

We still design, and program the guts of the system.
We also design, and program the reporting. The reports are in a different
format.

> Would a NEW system use batch processes?

Sure. In large volume shops there is no alternative.
You still don't want to randomly access the customer master 10,000,000 times
during peak business hours.
This may change in the future as machines get more powerful.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: At Home Alone

- **From:** William Reed <adamreed@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C8B5D7.DFDE141E@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#Ko4mtgi$GA.250@cpmsnbbsa05>`

```
I have been in the mainframe business for over 20 years and I have seen many
things change, but there are a few things that have not changed:

1.  Programmers do not care about computers when they are away from the office.
Reason:  they say that they get enough of computers at workï¿½why sit in front of
a computer at home?  Once away from the office, they would rather be looking at
a beer in their hand than a monitor in their face.

2.  Programmers are egotisticalï¿½they do not like to have others looking over
their shoulders at their code, or make suggestions about how some code could be
optimized to take less space, run faster, or ï¿½look prettierï¿½.  They think that
they know all there is to know about their job, and nothing anyone else says can
convince them otherwise.  They think that their coding skills are superior,
naming conventions included.  I once saw a program that used an 88 level named
NO-MORE-RECORDS, and the way the programmer checked for end-of-file was ï¿½IF NOT
NO-MORE-RECORDSï¿½!

3.  Programmers are lazy.  They would rather bang out a program than spend extra
time adding comments.  They think that they have spent too much time on the
program to forget what the program does six months from now, and besides, COBOL
is self-documenting(yeah, right).  They also think that once the program is
written, it will continue to function perfectly, and will not need any changes
to it in the future.  They do not think that there will be any data going
through the program that will cause it to crash(non-numeric data will NOT get
through, and NOTHING will cause it to try to divide by zero).   And once a
program fails(usually at 2 a.m.), they resort to ï¿½quick and dirtyï¿½ programs to
fix the problem, which usually become a permanent part of the system.

4.  Programmers are ï¿½one-languageï¿½ coders.  Once they have ï¿½masteredï¿½ one
language, that is their language of choice.  They have the ï¿½hammerï¿½ mentalityï¿½to
them, every problem looks like a nail that can be driven by their language.

If none of these descriptions fit you, then congratulationsï¿½you are among the 5
percent of programmers who care about programming!  If you go to this newsgroup
regularly, then you may already be a programmer who cares!  After 20 years of
working with programmers, I can count on one hand the number of professional
programmers who do programming after working 8 to 10 hours a day at it.  Most of
the people I know who do programming at home are either engineers or
accountants, or people who want to create their own game.

I have seen all kinds of spaghetti codeï¿½ALTER/GO TO in COBOL, variable labels in
PL/I, and GOTOï¿½DEPENDING ON in BASIC.  I have seen ï¿½cuteï¿½ coding such as GO TO
FISHING.  I have seen COBOL programs with missing periods, extra periods, or
periods past column 71.  I have seen programs with 2 or more statements on every
line.  Most of the programs I have seen have GO TO/PERFORM ratios of 20 to 1 or
better.

Most of the programmers I have met havenï¿½t a clue as to what structured
programming is, and wouldnï¿½t recognize one if it bit them!  Some donï¿½t care,
because ï¿½itï¿½s too complicatedï¿½, and they donï¿½t see anything wrong with using GO
Tos.  Others think that they are writing structured programs, because they write
the programs ï¿½from the top-downï¿½.  They start at the top and go to the bottom.
I have seen an 8-page IF statement, which the programmer considered to be a
ï¿½structuredï¿½ program because it did not contain GO TO statements in it.

If you think that OO programming will eliminate the old spaghetti code of COBOL
programs, hang on to your coding pads!  Misuse of OO programming will create
ï¿½spaghetti-OOs!

My predictionï¿½in 5 years or less, a majority of these OO programs will need to
major maintenance.  (If you think that these OO programs are so well designed
that they will not need maintenance, then you are either naive, or you are a
super-programmer with extraordinary insight as to the future needs of an
organization).  Then, we will see the extent of ï¿½reuseï¿½ or extensibility of
these modules.  My guess will be that there will be so much overriding code to a
class, that the overrides will be the defacto program.  Either that, or the
program will be scrapped, and rewritten from scratch.  Then, the reusable
programs will instead be the disposable programs, just like disposable diapers.

I have explained many COBOL programs to users and showed them the statements in
the program that accomplished a partucular task.  How many OO programs in your
shop could you explain to users?  After all, it is ultimately the users you
support who know how programs should work.  Many times what they wanted is not
what they SAID they wanted.

If this seems a bit cynical and jaded, consider thisï¿½how many programmers that
you are working with have a commanding grasp of OO?  If you had a peculiar
exception, how many people in your shop could you go to for the answer?  How
many others would know what to do in the case of a program exceptionï¿½and how
long would it take them to fix itï¿½at 10 a.m.? How about at 2 a.m.?  How many
programmers in your company know 2 or more languages?  And how many non-COBOL
programmers in your company have more than 5 years of experience?

Bill Reed
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: At Home Alone

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C8D89C.DB9FEF2E@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#Ko4mtgi$GA.250@cpmsnbbsa05> <38C8B5D7.DFDE141E@bellsouth.net>`

```


William Reed wrote:
> 
> I have been in the mainframe business for over 20 years and I have seen many
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Reason:  blahm blah, blah   ........ and considerably more blah

Thus spaketh the sage. He's been there, seen it, done it. And as he is
aware of all the circumstances, would we be foolish in assuming he is in
his special 5% that knows what it is all about ?

Tell you what, I have an idea. Let's get a supply of cardboard and we'll
get it cut into oblongs, ooh about 4 x 8. We'll make it easy for people,
we'll even print columns with numbers on them. Then we give them a blunt
pair of scissors that they poke holes into the cardboard. Now here's the
tricky bit, we want some sort of machine, any one got an old Ford-T
engine we could utilize - I'm a bit vague on this, but we want to shove
these cards through something that will look for the holes - we could do
all sorts of wonderful things. Not sure what, but we could.

Doesn't appeal - well being non mechanical it doesn't seem a great idea
to me either. Tell you what, we'll keep those cards and put them in big
boxes. Let's say we still allow people to poke holes. Hey, great idea -
we don't need that machine we can poke a knitting needle through looking
for a pattern of holes and extract physically the cards that are still
sitting on the knitting needle.

Well it was just an idea. OK, so let's think of something the military
might use. We could go real technicolor on this one. What was that
outfit called ? Oh yes, Remington Rand and they produced these metal
boxes with trays of hinged cardboard cards. Real neat stuff for
personnel records or inventory. Here's where the colour comes in. You
really don't want to poke around with knitting needles - so lets get
some small slips of transparent plastic, in different colours of course
- now we can use a colour for the blokes what live in married quarters,
those that live out in a hired apartment, those on leave(vacation). Gawd
the permutations are endless. My ! Even the local civvy padre when he
comes in for his money - we can tell him how many CofE(Protestant), RCs
and OD(Other Denominations) personnel there are. So you're Jewish -
sorry we've run out of colours so you go in with the miscellaneous lot
OD(Other Denominations). I sympathize, but with a shortage of colours -
honestly what else can we do ?

I knew we should have kept one of those things as a museum piece. Addo-X
made in Sweden. You know what, rather like a modern telephone keypad it
had a bank of numbers - the marvellous thing was it could add, subtract,
multiply, divide - nah - nothing real high-tech like square roots - but
you know, it would print out sums - bloody marvellous. Very impressive
when we put on a display at the Farnborough Air Show - pity, but the
King didn't come over and take a peek at his boys in blue.

We can really progress if we look backwards. Now let me see, from school
history lessons. That was it, the Industrial Revolution. Seems this guy
got real uppity about folks like George Stepehnson and what their damned
machines were doing to create satanic mills. So for a while there he
started a mini revolution. He copped it eventually, they must have
shoved him in a gaol or something. Now what was the name. John LUDD I
think. Sound familiar ?

Yours trooly,

Definitely not a sage - but most certainly old enough to be one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: At Home Alone

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** William Reed <adamreed@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38C9580E.C2BE9FFE@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#Ko4mtgi$GA.250@cpmsnbbsa05> <38C8B5D7.DFDE141E@bellsouth.net> <38C8D89C.DB9FEF2E@home.com>`

```
What in the world are you talking about?  Cardboard?  Scissors?  Knitting needles?
Colors?  How are they related to modern computing?  Do they have C++ code written on
them?

I never said I was a sage, and Iï¿½m certainly not a Luddite!  Not unless you consider
someone who knows PL/I, COBOL, BASIC(including VB), GPSS, Easytrieve, Excel
macros(VBA), CICS, Foxpro, C, C++, and a smattering of JAVA to be a Luddite!

What Iï¿½m saying is that any of these languages, OO or otherwise, have the capability
to be useful, and the capability for misuse in the hands of someone who doesnï¿½t
understand the language.  But it takes more than knowing the language in order to be
productive.    One also needs to think in a logical manner and know the underlying
systemsï¿½and no amount of OO inheritance, polymorphism, or encapsulation can fix flaws
in logic.

I know a lot more now than when I got out of college, but I know less than I need to
know for other things.  Learning is a lifelong processï¿½and Iï¿½m not done yet.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: At Home Alone

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FGey4.814$34.15343@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#Ko4mtgi$GA.250@cpmsnbbsa05> <38C8B5D7.DFDE141E@bellsouth.net> <38C8D89C.DB9FEF2E@home.com> <38C9580E.C2BE9FFE@bellsouth.net>`

```

William Reed <adamreed@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:38C9580E.C2BE9FFE@bellsouth.net...
> What in the world are you talking about?  Cardboard?  Scissors?
Knitting needles?
> Colors?  How are they related to modern computing?  Do they have C++
code written on
> them?

They were the precursors to modern computing. Many of the techniques
we use today were developed using cards with holes around the edges
(which came before cards with holes in the interior of the card). The
use of different colored cards for different classes of data, for
example.
```

```
Jimmy
```

```
Bill,

I have only one misgiving about your little ;-) spiel. Clients are not programmers.
If I were asked to show a client a piece of code that applied to given piece of
business functionality, I would not expect that person to understand (truly).
Therefor, why do it?

Mark L. Bartlett
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: At Home Alone

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%Aay4.26490$wk.1375105@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<8a86sg$dab$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <#Ko4mtgi$GA.250@cpmsnbbsa05> <38C8B5D7.DFDE141E@bellsouth.net> <38C90098.28CF5807@sound.net>`

```
Mark L. Bartlett wrote:
>
>I have only one misgiving about your little ;-) spiel. Clients are not programmers.
>If I were asked to show a client a piece of code that applied to given piece of
>business functionality, I would not expect that person to understand (truly).
>Therefor, why do it?

It depends on the situation.  When writing a particularly complex
calculation for an accountant, if the code involves lots of money,
then it is a very good idea that you and the accountant are in
agreement that the calculation is correct.  Of course, if your own
understanding of the Federal tax code, for example, is complete, then
you are in a much better position. ;-)  This would not be necessary
if all users could articulate their requirements precisely.  However,
it is sometimes necessary to program by iteration; you program what
the user says they want, then modify it until it does what they
actually want. ;-)  I have found it useful to go over such code with
the user from time to time.  At least, if you get a sign off from the
user, then the liability is shared.  If you get a sign off on a C++
routine from a user, then you have a truly extraordinary user, or the
user is drunk. :-)
```
