Cobol/Oracle conversion.

Is anyone familiar with the "ICONS" conversion tool?
"The ICONS conversion tool will convert approximately 80% of the code
from the following input:  BTG�s (Back-up Transfer Generator for the
data base structure), COBOL programs, IDEAL Panels, Reports, and
Programs, batch COBOL Programs, JCL, PARMLIBS, PROCLIBS, COPYLIBS,
etc.."

Is anyone familiar with "Pro Cobol"?
"Once converted, the code will consist of the following:  Batch COBOL
programs that access subroutines written in Pro Cobol (COBOL that
includes SQL statements to access Oracle), CICS COBOL programs (to
replace previous IDEAL programs), Basic Map Support (BMS) and copylibs
(to replace the IDEAL panels) and an Oracle DBMS instead of Datacom."

Any information will be greatly appreciated, I'm in a bit of a vacuum
here where sometimes information seems to be on a need to know basis.

Thanks,
	Bill Thompson
```

## Thread: MF COBOL and serial printers — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL and serial printers

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ar7kq$ttl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi:

Printing problems with MF COBOL on serial ports. My application software
prints on LPT and COM ports. The serial printers are dot-matrix,
roll-paper receipt printers.

Such printers require a MODE:COMx,9600,n,8,1,P command to be in
the autoexec.  If the MODE command is not present and you send
data to COM1, the data will not print. Instead, all you get is
question marks and nothing else. After putting the MODE command
in, the question marks go away and the data will print - usually!

Sometimes, like now, the MODE command doesn't seem to work. The
MODE command is in the AUTOEXEC, but I still get question marks.
Whether one copies a file from the DOS prompt to the printer,
or you print via the COBOL program, all you get is ????????????
marks.

In such cases, one can go into control panel and print a test
page on the appropriate printer and then the question marks
go away. This is a pain in the neck for the customer to have
to go through this ritual every day, to say the least.

The plot thickens - I have a customer who, after putting the
MODE command in and then executing my application gets
RT error 004 (Invalid File Name) when my application attempts
to open a file. It seems that the application doesn't acquire
the file name at all (since the application displays an error
message including the file status and the file name and the
file name is spaces).  He removes the MODE command and he
doesn't get the RT004 error, but he gets the question marks
because the MODE command isn't there?!?!?!?!?

How can a MODE command produce an RT004 error?

Has anyone had similar problems and how did you fix it?

Does anyone know how to TOTALLY BYPASS Windows and print
directly to the COM ports?

Has anyone used the FLEXUS formprint thing to print line-by-line
to a serial port (I couldn't get it to work.)

Does anyone know of any solution to this very annoying problem?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL and serial printers

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38d14200.667413613@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8ar7kq$ttl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Thu, 16 Mar 2000 18:03:18 GMT, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:


>Has anyone used the FLEXUS formprint thing to print line-by-line
>to a serial port (I couldn't get it to work.)

I have.  There is the way I did it, and also a method that Flexus
offers - ask them for the "line printer" code example.

I set the printer up as a "Generic Text Printer" in windows and use
Form print with a one print line form calling the print function after
each line, and that works for me.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL and serial printers

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D17300.4FE6055@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8ar7kq$ttl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Foodman wrote:
> 
> Hi:
> 
> Printing problems with MF COBOL on serial ports.

Tony I wouldn't know if this would help resolve it for you. But as you
have M/F - have you tried using PC_PRINT - at least the latter can pick
up the printer you are addressing with no cross-reference in autoexec.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL and serial printers

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8as8in$le3$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8ar7kq$ttl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote in message <8ar7kq$ttl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...

>Does anyone know how to TOTALLY BYPASS Windows and print
>directly to the COM ports?
>

Get the MarshalSoft serial I/O DLL, and program the com port yourself.  I
have not done that with MF, but I have done it with Fujitsu, and it is not
very difficult.  The mode command is only setting up the UART.  If that is
the only problem, and I suspect that it is, then you should be able to
initialise the port correctly with your own code, then open it output using
normal Cobol.
```

## Thread: Transition from Mainframe COBOL to Merant Object COBOL — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Transition from Mainframe COBOL to Merant Object COBOL

- **From:** Art <manwae@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8atgft$hrp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Anyone out there familiar with migrating an existing mainframe COBOL
application calling IMS to a Merant COBOL app on UNIX accesssing a DB2
or Oracle Database on another box.  Also any particular issues or
pitfalls that need to be avoided.  We are in the process of evaluating
whether to migrate to C/C++ on unix or to stay with COBOL.  Thanks much.
         ART
```

#### ↳ Re: Transition from Mainframe COBOL to Merant Object COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8auk0s$41r$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8atgft$hrp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Are you using IMS for "DB" or "DC"?  (not their current name, but boils down
to are you using it as a database or a transaction/screen/io system - or
both)?

The answer to pitfalls on migration will vary depending on that.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Transition from Mainframe COBOL to Merant Object COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D318D7.E7770F33@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8atgft$hrp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8auk0s$41r$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Are you using IMS for "DB" or "DC"?  (not their current name, but boils down
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The answer to pitfalls on migration will vary depending on that.

A bit of amplification on what Bill has written - up-front yes I've done
my time as a systems manager with mainframes - but as a programmer -
only dinky-doo desktops.

Evaluate, evaluate. Scale of system doesn't matter, if I design A/P for
a desktop user it is pretty close to what you would do for a mainframe -
but you give yours major refinements - totally unnecessary for the small
user - but imperative for big users.

Whatever you do - don't take the big bang approach - use if possible one
self-contained module as a test-bed so that you can continue to run the
'real' application while testing the OO version and fine tuning it. Use
a common sense approach - confirm in your own minds that OO really can
do something for you. You have the necessary bread ($) - check around -
is there anybody who can talk to you as a group about OO COBOL - and not
go all prima donna on you about how MAH-VELL-USS it is - but talk in
practical terms so you can make a judgement before ever trying it.

May not be what you're after - don't know the man, having only spoken to
him briefly over the 'phone some two years ago - but talk to Will
(Wilson) Price at objectz.com/cobolreport.com; he's spent many years as
a lecturer and written one of the few practical books about OO COBOL -
he might be interested in talking to you as a group.

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Transition from Mainframe COBOL to Merant Object COBOL

- **From:** Art <manwae@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
We use IMS for both the DB and DC aspects.  However in the application I
am looking at the there is no DC aspect.  The application is a
background reporting app.  We have COBOL programmers that maintaint the
existing application.  We are looking at rehosting the data on a UNIX
box and the group wants to figure out if they should make the break to
C/C++ for this or go to Object COBOL.  I would not say that the group is
experienced in OO methods and the first attempt if they write the Object
COBOL will likely be more a port than a reenginering.
                            ART

#### ↳ Re: Transition from Mainframe COBOL to Merant Object COBOL

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38d26083.740771927@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8atgft$hrp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Fri, 17 Mar 2000 14:46:23 GMT, Art <manwae@my-deja.com> wrote:

>Anyone out there familiar with migrating an existing mainframe COBOL
>application calling IMS to a Merant COBOL app on UNIX accesssing a DB2
>or Oracle Database on another box.  Also any particular issues or
>pitfalls that need to be avoided.  We are in the process of evaluating
>whether to migrate to C/C++ on unix or to stay with COBOL.  Thanks much.

I think you want to go with Mainframe Express.  The advantage?
Virtually all of your code stays the same.  The disadvantage?  The C++
Weenie doesn't get his way. The next advantage?  The project suceeds.
I've seen many attempted ports from COBOL to C++ fail - sometimes
taking the company with them.
```

## Thread: Mainframe Web — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Mainframe Web

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D23FBD.197B2276@cusys.edu>`

```
I see that there are some companies which are using the web to offer
applications support for small companies.  The small companies would
typically need to buy something like a Sun and spend between a couple
hundred thousand and a million setting up their computer needs.

These companies are using the old EDS model to put their applications
off site - but are using the Web to make them available to the small
companies as an expense.

I am not familiar with these products, but I am wondering how well Cobol
and mainframes fit into this model.  

What do you think and what do you know?
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe Web

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oPzA4.4412$ru1.437@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<38D23FBD.197B2276@cusys.edu>`

```
For several years now IBM has had a Java Server for CICS (which handles
a large portion of the World's BIG Databases).  This allows front-end PC
apps to access the BIG IRON database of (almost) any type.
All that is needed to be bought for the PCs is an TN3270 or IRMA
(Dec IRMA or Attachmate EXTRA) license (which BTW the IBM shop
probaly has)

So that means that for the Price of Powerbuilder or Delphi
or (yech) VB / IE3 /Netscape 3 or later (no they're not free, you
pay for them in other wys) and some additional emulator Licenses they
can access the big corporate databases.

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38D23FBD.197B2276@cusys.edu...
> I see that there are some companies which are using the web to offer
> applications support for small companies.  The small companies would
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> What do you think and what do you know?
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe Web

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PhsA4.30419$Hq3.660401@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38D23FBD.197B2276@cusys.edu>`

```
IBM has an HTTP server that runs on big iron
 http://www-4.ibm.com/software/webservers/httpservers/ ). The CGI interface
supports COBOL applications.

They also have an application server (WebSphere) that allows you to write
Java applications and encapsulate COBOL processes using something called
Enterprise Java Beans (EJB).

A more common model is to move the web hosting to a different box (or
cluster of boxes depending on the scale) and use something like TXSeries
(CICS) or MQSeries to co-ordinate between the boxes. Security plays a big
role in deciding to expose your mainframe to the world at large. Granted
mainframe security is as robust as it gets.

Karl Wagner

P.S. I'm doing a COBOL CGI application at the moment for an on-line ordering
system (WANG back-end) It's taken about 3 days to cobble together a working
prototype, including designing the data model. It'll take a lot longer to
turn it into a production-ready system.

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38D23FBD.197B2276@cusys.edu...

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-61e6f5de51"></a>
## Thread: MF NetExpress vs Java — 3 msgs

### MF NetExpress vs Java

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000317075200.01697.00003143@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

The company I work for sells a point of sale system developed with NetExpress.
One of the products that we sell against has a product that is written in Java.
They advertise their product as a 64-bit system. My question is that they run
under Windows 98 just as we do with NetExpress. I thought Windows 98 was a
32bit OS. I am confused how Java can claim to be a 64-bit system ? Could anyone
comment ?

Thanks,
Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: MF NetExpress vs Java

- **From:** SimonLElliott <"Simon.L.Elliott[Delete.Me]"@CapGemini.Co.UK>
- **Date:** 2000-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D24E3F.11700B6D@CapGemini.Co.UK>`
- **References:** `<20000317075200.01697.00003143@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Bob7536 wrote:

>  I thought Windows 98 was a 32bit OS. I am confused how Java can claim to be a
> 64-bit system ? Could anyone comment ?

I'm no JAVA expert, but it's my understanding that JAVA is an interpreted language.
That's to say it's only when the source code is presented to the interpreter of
whichever environment under which the program is run that the source is converted
into a machine readable format.

This means that if you're running a JAVA program on a 64 bit machine, using a 64
bit interpreter then you end up with a 64 bit program. Use a 64 bit machine and a
32 bit interpreter then you'll end up with 32 bit object code on a 64 bit machine.

Presumably there are compiled versions of JAVA out there and conceivably you could
have a little fun trying to run 64 bit code on a 32 bit machine?

Mind you I could be wrong.

Simon Elliott
TGL TSG CICS/DB Support
Cap Gemini Southbank
London
Tel. (44) 0171 917 4255

Any opinions expressed are not necessarily those of my employer.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF NetExpress vs Java

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8atjk5$70q$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<20000317075200.01697.00003143@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
"Bob7536" <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000317075200.01697.00003143@ng-bg1.aol.com...
> The company I work for sells a point of sale system developed with
NetExpress.
> One of the products that we sell against has a product that is written in
Java.
> They advertise their product as a 64-bit system. My question is that they
run
> under Windows 98 just as we do with NetExpress. I thought Windows 98 was a
> 32bit OS. I am confused how Java can claim to be a 64-bit system ? Could
anyone
> comment ?

Java is an interpreted language, as is, for instance Acucobol. This means
that it is the particular runtime that defines whether the application is 8,
16, 32 or 64 bit.

You certainly have noticed the programming contest in this NG. Once I have
developed that one, it may be run using Acucobol 32bit Windows runtime, and
I can claim it to be 32 bit. I could also choose to execute it using
Acucobol 16bit Windows runtime, then it would be 16 bit. I don't know for
sure, but I suspect there might be a 64 bit Acucobol runtime on some Unix.
If I then executed this application there, it would be a 64 bit application.

But if your competitor is executing on Windows 98, their application will be
32bit as much as yours, because the Java interpreter on that computer may
not be more than 32bit capable.

When all this is said, I am most positive your application will outperform
the Java competitor, no matter whether they claim to be 64bit or not. Java
is well known for poor performance. So, so much for the benefit of 64bit
there. When I now have compared Acucobol and Java in this context, I would
also like to say that it is my experience that Acucobol outperformes Java as
well.

From my personal experience, I wouldn't even dream of using Java as a point
of sale application. POS systems demands a high uptime, fast execution and
the claim for portability is nonsense as a POS system has dependency on
hardware that makes it hard to port anyway.

Cheesle
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

In article <hzYA4.1109$8R4.100766@news.uswest.net>, "bfagan"
<bfagan@primenet.com> writes:

>
>Does anyone know anything about Fujitsu Cobol. I would appreciate any
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Thane and some others indicated that the free version 3 compiler comes 
with "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours" and "COBOL Unleashed".
Version 3 can also be downloaded off the web at www.adtools.com -or-
as part of ETK's development platform at www.etk.com.
There is also a book out "Mastering COBOL" by Carol Baroudi (I think)
that has the version 4 compiler included.

I have used the Fujitsu COBOL compiler for batch type programs over 
the last 3 years and found it to be an excellent tool.  The majoritiy of 
my work with the compiler is for off-loading development from the mainframe
and ocassionally having to debug a program.   The ancillary tools (Data
Convertor,
Layout Definition, ...) have been very helpful in translating files from EBCDIC
to ASCII for testing of mainframe files on the PC.

Hope that this info gets you on your way.
```

## Thread: another j4 idea-comments welcome — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### another j4 idea-comments welcome

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jjo4ds0oi757t5uaepq0v2079j0l49sd6i@4ax.com>`

```
Recently, I was lookig at how you use instrinsic functions....
you have a table of a fixed size, and you execute the function.

this is nice and all, but suppose you have a disk file, and you want
to run an intrinsic function on that file. you have to determine the
number of records, compile to that spec, and then reload in the
records again. solution?


      *table-indexed example
       01 ws.
           02 wa-data-array occurs 10000 times
                   indexed by index-by-clause


       procedure division.
       perform open-file
       perform read-records varying index-by-clause from 1 by 1
               until eof-switch-found-in-read-records

       COMPUTE wa-min = FUNCTION MIN (wa-data-array (index-by-clause))

       perform close-file


you see, the wa-data-array uses an index-by-clause in the function

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: another j4 idea-comments welcome

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b0q2q$ubc$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<jjo4ds0oi757t5uaepq0v2079j0l49sd6i@4ax.com>`

```
Gary,
  Do you know about the ALL "subscript" that is part of the '89 Intrinsic
Function amendment (and supported by all current vendors that I know of - as
it is Standard)?

COMPUTE wa-min = FUNCTION MIN (wa-data-array (ALL))

   works today.


Similarly,

 Compute which-is-smallest = Function Ord-Min (wa-data-array (all))

will tell you which element is the smallest.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: another j4 idea-comments welcome

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b3n4b$f9o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<jjo4ds0oi757t5uaepq0v2079j0l49sd6i@4ax.com> <8b0q2q$ubc$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <nmr7dsg8qgqrnl9br09unf0jgnlgbmn1cn@4ax.com>`

```
Geoff (sorry about wrong name before - and not really understanding your
point),

Earlier versions of the  revision included what was called "dynamic tables".
This was a variation on OCCURS DEPENDING ON that would "magically" grow at
run-time (based on memory available and a few other things).  In fact
SIGNIFICANT time was spent on trying to get a "good definition" of this
facility.  It underwent (at least) 3 distinct re-writes.  UNFORTUNATELY, none
of the "designs" really worked (with other features of COBOL - such as
INITIALIZE and VALIDATE) - and every time that someone would try and come up
with a "solution" to the previous design, it would either get more
complicated, cause more new problems, or both.

If this is a feature that you would still like the committee to try and
design (for a future revision), I would think that it is still a good idea to
communicate this to J4 and/or WG4.
```

- **From:** "RUSSELL STYLES" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b6rk3$1k3$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
    Maybe J4 could reduce their work load and speed things up by delegating
some decisions to the compiler writers.  Ie, send all of the compiler writer
groups
requests (with a time frame) for their ideas on some of the new stuff, like
dynamic tables.

    If none of the writers (IBM and MF and ??????) respond with a proposal
or a beta version,
consider the idea dead.  A good many of the really good ideas already work
anyway.

    This idea sounds oddly like "ODOSLIDE" on MF cobol with an occurs
depending on, in
the linkage section.  I still do not understand why INITIALIZE does not
work.  It could, easily.
At the very least, issue a compiler warning.

    Making the table automatic would be easier, of course.  And performing
an automatic expand
of a LARGE table could consume a lot of memory (unless you manage to allow a
multi region
(non-contiguous) table somehow).

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Cobol help, for a learner

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-18T00:00:00

```
Josh,

Part of learning COBOL is struggling with problems, and solving them
That is what we do day-to-day.

Being given the answer won't help you, and you won't find anyone giving
answers to broad problems in this newsgroup.

If you have tried something, and it doesn't seem to work, you can ask here,
and someone will put you on the right track.

Now, what solutions have you come up with?

josh <jac_rb@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:zsJA4.51245$3b6.209906@ozemail.com.au...
> the below data is the file I want to read in.
> but after the date and scode are read in, a transaction code is next and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I have tried a few things but im not very experienced with Cobol so I
would
> appreciate any help that you could give me.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol help, for a learner

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b2hqt$ekq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<zsJA4.51245$3b6.209906@ozemail.com.au> <OG0n#OOk$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
In article <OG0n#OOk$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa03>,
> josh <jac_rb@yahoo.com> wrote in message
> news:zsJA4.51245$3b6.209906@ozemail.com.au...
> > the below data is the file I want to read in.
> > but after the date and scode are read in, a transaction code is
next and
> > depending on that code the following data types are different.
> > How do I make the program read in the right information for each
different
> > trans code?
> > I have tried a few things but im not very experienced with Cobol so
I
> would
> > appreciate any help that you could give me.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > 09032000CNSR54321

Hi:

A lot of people in this forum don't like answering questions like
this because they think that you want them to do your homework for you.

You have to define multiple record types in your file. Each record
description defines the fields according to the type. When you read the
file, since you probably wouldn't know what record was coming next, you
have to check the record code and use the record description appropriate
to the record just read.

Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.133ce2ef7664428598998d@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
I noticed that Jerry Wu as cormwu@ntsource.com said...
> http://www.computerjobs.com
> http://www.dice.com
> http://www.careerpath.com
> 
> anything else?

It depends on where you want to operate.  For us in the UK, the one that 
stands out like the balls on a greyhound is http://www.jobserve.com 
although there are others.  It doesn't only cover the UK but also 
includes 
stuff overseas though, I suspect, maybe not in as much quantity as more 
appropriate country-specific sites.

I have set up my keyword profile with Jobserve and it e-mails me every 
day with opportunities that fit those keywords - today, for instance, the 
mail listed 39 new contract opportunities.
```

<a id="t-4fb707802b"></a>
## Thread: Cobol help, for a learner — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Cobol help, for a learner

- **From:** tim timmins <tim.timmins@bcs.org.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D3968E.F292562@bcs.org.uk>`
- **References:** `<zsJA4.51245$3b6.209906@ozemail.com.au>`

```
You could define different record layouts for each different record type and
then choose which record you want to use by testing the record type, or you
could use the redefines clause to change the way various fields in a single
record layout can be accessed:

e.g.

Either

01 Rec1.
  03 date1 pic x(8).
  03 scode1 pic x(3).
  03 trancode1 pic x(1).
       88 is-rec1  value 'A'.
   03 rec1-fld1 pic x(12).
   03 rec1-fld2 pic x(10).

01 Rec2.
  03 date2 pic x(8).
  03 scode2 pic x(3).
  03 trancode2 pic x(1).
       88 is-rec2  value 'C'.
   03 rec2-fld1 pic x(3).
   03 rec2-fld2 pic x(33).
... etc ...

or

01 Rec.
  03 date pic x(8).
  03 scode pic x(3).
  03 trancode pic x(1).
       88 is-rec1  value 'A'.
       88 is-rec2  value 'C'.
  03 rec1-flds.
       05 rec1-fld1 pic x(12).
       05 rec1-fld2 pic x(10).
   03 rec2-flds redefines rec1-flds.
       05 rec2-fld1 pic x(3).
       05 rec2-fld2 pic x(33).

... etc ...

josh wrote:

> the below data is the file I want to read in.
> but after the date and scode are read in, a transaction code is next and
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 09032000CNSR54321
```

<a id="t-43cc603de9"></a>
## Thread: Are you Event-Driven? — 18 msgs

### Are you Event-Driven?

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b579q$8ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi:

I was wondering (again) and wonder how many people writing
new COBOL applications for Windows use the Windows 'style',
i.e., event-driven, menu bars, listboxes, mouse-oriented,
radio buttons, etc.?

Personally, I find the whole Windows, mouse-oriented thing very
annoying and klutzy for business applications. The supreme
example of klutziness is the use of one of those listbox things
for the entry of the State. Nineteen clicks to replace two
keystrokes is the ultimate dim thing to do.

So, I never use any of those Windows features except pushbuttons
and those rarely. My programs are not event-driven and they
lead the user by the nose making them enter what I want them
to enter when I want them to enter it.  Unfortunately, non-computer
users like my customers perceive my stuff to be DOS-like and that
makes it hard to sell. In actuality, the software is a marvel
of simplicity (hey, if you don't blow your own trumpet, ain't
nobody gonna blow it for you). People expect things to be like
everything else even if everything else doesn't make sense.

So, the question is: how many of you develop things the Windows
way versus some other way?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

>Personally, I find the whole Windows, mouse-oriented thing very
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

In most list box implementations you can press the T for Texas, and it
will bring up Tennessee, then press T again and Texas is available.
There's a ton of other shortcuts built in that are sometimes not
understood/used by the average Windows user.
```

Yes.  I would have preferred an implementation where I would type
letters until I got a unique value.  The other day, I entered U about 8
times before I got United States of America.

The first thing I learned with Windows though is Ctl-C, CTL-V, CTL-A, &
CTL-X.  Those keys save me lots of time and work, even while using a
3270 emulator which doesn't list these in its Edit drop down.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Are you Event-Driven?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b96oa01d4g@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8b579q$8ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38d659d2.1000767486@news.cox-internet.com> <ujLB4.3036$z22.235215@news2.mia> <8b8cd4$1v5$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I suppose the basic premise of Windows is to get more people to use 
computers and as such succeeds quite well thank you.  It is certainly not
the best, not that great at all really, but people can get the handle of it.

Think about this.  If say, only 15% of the people on the earth had internet
access right now, how much money could you make by making something that
allowed the other 85% to use the internet and have access to a computer.
alot of damned money.

Windows is all about VOLUME.  ALOT of people can use the system so, alot of
people buy it.  Microsoft just happened to be in the right place at the
right time and attack the best markets.  Alot of business smarts but a
fairly lackluster product IMHO.

So, that said, Windows is here and if you want to be a good programmer, you
will have to learn it fairly soon.  It feels quite gratifying programming in
a Windows environment but the code is so fragile.  I still enjoy slinging
snippets of code from COBOL program to program.  Nothing like a good text
editor.

That was some good grass.

Jeff

----------

#### ↳ Re: Are you Event-Driven?

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2c833740.f11fc7c1@usw-ex0101-007.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8b579q$8ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:

>I was wondering (again) and wonder how many people writing
>new COBOL applications for Windows use the Windows 'style',
>i.e., event-driven, menu bars, listboxes, mouse-oriented,
>radio buttons, etc.?

I have a business application that I wrote back in '86 that has
been converted from DOS to Windows. One of the 'traumas' my
clients experienced was mouse vs. keyboard. Since the release a
year and a half ago, both my clients and I have learned a lot
about using the software without a mouse. Today, with some
exceptions, the Windows business application can be used without
a mouse.

I found three stages my clients went through: trauma, recovery
and acceptance. I have found, training on new clients is easier
using list boxes, selection boxes, radio buttons, push buttons,
etc.

Today, my phone calls are less and if I were to tell my clients
we are moving back to a DOS application, a revolt would occur.

Regards,
Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Are you Event-Driven?

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bae1h$lg3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8b579q$8ir$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <2c833740.f11fc7c1@usw-ex0101-007.remarq.com>`

```
In article <2c833740.f11fc7c1@usw-ex0101-007.remarq.com>,
Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid> wrote:
> Today, my phone calls are less and if I were to tell my clients
> we are moving back to a DOS application, a revolt would occur.


Hi:

Whether a system was designed for DOS or Windows, should be
irrelevant. A system should be designed to be supremely
efficient in terms of usage (number of keystrokes/clicks required
to perform a given function). It should also be completely
self-explanatory and require no external documentation.

The only difference between a DOS version and a Windows version
of such software would be the appearance of the screen. Using
Windows it is easy to make 'prettier' screens than DOS. DOS
screens are universally perceived to be dull and boring.

The very best-designed system would be operationally identical
without regard to the OS.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-60831c8394"></a>
## Thread: How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not? — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not?

- **From:** zsl263@263.net
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b49uc$kn7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi all,
  How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not?

Victor Zhang


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not?

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0kgcdsck8kmp5dqr3qs81vfgjhgkemqtuv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8b49uc$kn7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Mon, 20 Mar 2000 04:37:34 GMT, zsl263@263.net wrote:

>Hi all,
>  How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not?
>
Victor,

if you used COBOL II or COBOL for OS/390 and VM, and used the compile time option RENT,
then your COBOL program is reentrant.

If you want to know if any old COBOL module you found in a library is reentrant - nothing
I can remember.  But, of course, you can always let the hardware figure it out.  If it is
a batch program, coax your sysprog to place it into LPA (Unprobable, though, that you will
succeed).  For a CICS program, link the program with the RENT attribute, specify
RENTPGM=PROTECT in the SIT of your test CICS, and off you go.  If the program is not
reentrant, it will abend on you
```

#### ↳ Re: How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8JrB4.45032$wk.1775325@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<8b49uc$kn7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
zsl263@263.net wrote:
>Hi all,
>  How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not?

It depends.  On the Unisys A Series computers, all code is reentrant,
COBOL or otherwise.  There is no assembler compiler, and the system
architecture and compilers are designed to make it so.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bbgek$kmh$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8b49uc$kn7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZfzB4.95$ce5.311@news4.mia> <8b6kkn$b9m$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38D75F26.713275E2@zip.com.au> <8b9udf$4lu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
RENT is a compiler option.  It may be specified in source code via the
      CBL
          or
      PROCESS

control card which is placed BEFORE the ID division header.
```

> option in identification division.There is an option for compiler.

|

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b8iul$32f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8b49uc$kn7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZfzB4.95$ce5.311@news4.mia> <8b6kkn$b9m$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Given the IBM mainframe nature - and release of your product, then the answer
is (as others have stated),

COMPILE and LINK-EDIT with  the RENT compiler and link-edit options and your
code is "guaranteed" to be RENT.

If you want to check the link-edit attribute of an existing module, you can
use a number of tools (already mentioned in the thread).  HOWEVER, as also
stated elsewhere, you can link-edit a module as RENT even if the code is not
correctly coded to be rent (e.g. an OS/VS COBOL module or an IBM COBOL for
OS/390 module compiled with NORENT). Therefore, the link-edit attribute only
tells what the linkage editor (or binder) was told - not how the code
actually exists.

Does this help?
```

#### ↳ Re: How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not?

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <djacot@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b56mt$863$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8b49uc$kn7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8b49uc$kn7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
zsl263@263.net wrote:
> Hi all,
> How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not?
>
> Victor Zhang
>

A (very old) OS/VS COBOL program is not reentrant. A newer one is
reentrant if:
- It is compiled with the option RENT
- It is linked with the option RENT
- It only calls subprograms that are reentrant
You might want to analize your COBOL loadmodules using FILE321 from CBT
tape:
http://www.cbttape.org/cbtdowns.htm
1. Download the file
2. unzip it
3. transfer it to the OS/390 mainframe (binary!)
4. TSO RECEIVE INDATASET(xxxxxxx) where xxxxxxx is the name of the
transferred file.
5. press ENTER
6. Assemble and link the program
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to know if a COBOL program is reentrant or not?

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asfcdssle055be6mgjracj51fngo0f3inv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8b49uc$kn7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8b56mt$863$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Mon, 20 Mar 2000 12:48:33 GMT, Daniel Jacot <djacot@my-deja.com> wrote:

>In article <8b49uc$kn7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
>zsl263@263.net wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>- It is linked with the option RENT
<nitpick>
the Linkage Editor Parm RENT has nothing to do with the program bein reentrant or not, it
just sets the a (more or less) informative flag.

If, e.g., IEFBR14 is not linked with this attribute, it still IS a reentrant program.  On
the other hand, if a program is not coded reentrantly, no LINK parameter will make it
reentrant
</nitpick>
```

<a id="t-476fed2cd1"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress Dialog System and Setting Tab order — 5 msgs

### NetExpress Dialog System and Setting Tab order

- **From:** Aaron Cardon <aaron@amnutrition.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D6BA96.A9AF7F43@amnutrition.com>`

```
Does anyone know how to set the tab order in the NetExpress 3.0 Dialog
System?  I need to set a sequential tab order for all of my entry fields
and cannot find where to set the tab order.

Any help is greatly appreciated!

Thank You,
Aaron Cardon
American Nutrition Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Dialog System and Setting Tab order

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38d6c45f_4@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<38D6BA96.A9AF7F43@amnutrition.com>`

```
Aaron,

It's kind of cheesey the way they did this, but this is how it works.

1) select the Form / Screenset that you want to re-order controls on.
2) select "Edit" menu-item, then "Controls" sub-item.  This will show
the "Controls List" dialog.
3) click on the item in the list that you want to re-order, then press
the "Select" button.  This selects the item and then enables the
"Insert" button.
4) click on the item in the list that you want to insert the previously
selected item before, then click "Insert".
5) repeat steps 1-4 for every control that you want to re-order.

This is how you re-order controls in Dialog System.  I am using Dialog
System v3.1.00 in the above steps.  Good luck...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Dialog System and Setting Tab order

- **From:** Aaron Cardon <aaron@amnutrition.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D7A07E.EBEDC8A6@amnutrition.com>`

<a id="t-1548097b56"></a>
## Thread: keystroke speeds DOS app — 7 msgs

### keystroke speeds DOS app

- **From:** "Brian Butterfield" <brianb@micro-tech.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AiCB4.45781$wk.1822239@news1.mia>`

```
OK...this is a weird one...

I am running a COBOL database application on an MS-DOS runtime executing on
Win 95/98 clients from a NT 4.0 server.  This app was migrated from a
Netware 3.12 server.  After migration to NT it was found that the app was
running very slowly.  A standard export routine would take up to 10 minutes
to run.  Quite by accident it was discovered that if you ran the application
windowed instead of full screen, the processing time decreased
dramatically..1 minute 30 seconds.  As I was trying to troubleshoot this, I
was toggling between full screen DOS and windowed by hitting Alt+Enter.
That's when I discovered that as soon as I pressed the Alt key, the export
process would take off like a rocket, even in full screen mode.  I tested
several keys and no matter which key on the keyboard is pressed & held down,
the processing takes off.  If you release the key, it slows back down.
While holding a key down throughout the export process in full screen, it
processed in 30 seconds!   I'm assuming that this is some sort of interrupt
issue, but am at a loss to fully explain it or correct it.  Any help that
anyone could give be would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks,
Brian Butterfield
```

#### ↳ Re: keystroke speeds DOS app

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38d79402.1081211323@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<AiCB4.45781$wk.1822239@news1.mia>`

```
On Mon, 20 Mar 2000 22:48:09 -0500, "Brian Butterfield"
<brianb@micro-tech.com> wrote:

>OK...this is a weird one...
>

Windows NT attemptes "pre-emptive" multi-tasking.  That is, it gives
your application some CPU then moves on.  Pressing SHIFT or any key,
tells Windows your application needs attention - and it gets it.
That's why it's faster.  You can try fiddling with the idle settings
for the dos prompt to speed it up.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: keystroke speeds DOS app

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rnQB4.46976$wk.1862943@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<AiCB4.45781$wk.1822239@news1.mia> <38d79402.1081211323@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>Brian Butterfield wrote:
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> for the dos prompt to speed it up.

Thanks, that's an excellent answer, I'll remember that. :-)  But in
this particular case, the application is running on the client PC
under Win98.  It appears that is where the key is being pressed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: keystroke speeds DOS app

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38d7dd84.1100032891@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<AiCB4.45781$wk.1822239@news1.mia> <38d79402.1081211323@news.cox-internet.com> <rnQB4.46976$wk.1862943@news1.mia>`

```
On Tue, 21 Mar 2000 13:48:48 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>>Brian Butterfield wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>under Win98.  It appears that is where the key is being pressed.

Same song, different verse.  The 95/98 version shares address space
with all DOS and 16 bit applications.  It may be more impacted than NT
is.  Adjusting the settings, as another reader suggested, will help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: keystroke speeds DOS app

- **From:** "RUSSELL STYLES" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b9r00$q8c$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<AiCB4.45781$wk.1822239@news1.mia> <38d79402.1081211323@news.cox-internet.com> <rnQB4.46976$wk.1862943@news1.mia>`

```
    This particular application probably does a lot of whatever it is
that windows looks for when trying to decide when an application
is wasting time, and when it is working.  In this case, when it is
running slow, windows has decided that (with current properties)
it is wasting time.

    Holding a shift key down probably changes the internal code path,
causing windows to react differently.

    Many DOS editors will cause cpu% to stay at 100% all of the time,
even when nothing is happening.  This is an aspect of the same thing.




Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:rnQB4.46976$wk.1862943@news1.mia...
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
> >Brian Butterfield wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: keystroke speeds DOS app

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avgedss3qnmp5egliae00md4gp5i9am9o0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<AiCB4.45781$wk.1822239@news1.mia>`

```
On Mon, 20 Mar 2000 22:48:09 -0500 "Brian Butterfield" <brianb@micro-tech.com>
wrote:

:>I am running a COBOL database application on an MS-DOS runtime executing on
:>Win 95/98 clients from a NT 4.0 server.  This app was migrated from a
:>Netware 3.12 server.  After migration to NT it was found that the app was
:>running very slowly.  A standard export routine would take up to 10 minutes
:>to run.  Quite by accident it was discovered that if you ran the application
:>windowed instead of full screen, the processing time decreased
:>dramatically..1 minute 30 seconds.  As I was trying to troubleshoot this, I
:>was toggling between full screen DOS and windowed by hitting Alt+Enter.
:>That's when I discovered that as soon as I pressed the Alt key, the export
:>process would take off like a rocket, even in full screen mode.  I tested
:>several keys and no matter which key on the keyboard is pressed & held down,
:>the processing takes off.  If you release the key, it slows back down.
:>While holding a key down throughout the export process in full screen, it
:>processed in 30 seconds!   I'm assuming that this is some sort of interrupt
:>issue, but am at a loss to fully explain it or correct it.  Any help that
:>anyone could give be would be greatly appreciated.

Open properties in that does window.

Under the Misc tab is Idle Sensitivity.

That controls how much CPU is allocated to an apparent idle session, i.e., a
session which is not receiving keystrokes.

You can alter the properties of your .exe or .bat, however you run it.
```

#### ↳ Re: keystroke speeds DOS app

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p_TB4.135$ce5.245@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<AiCB4.45781$wk.1822239@news1.mia>`

```
It is not the Keystroke but rather the following:

When it runs full screen (but you don't have that screen visible)
it is running as a 'background task' so that WIndows can
provide quick response to the 'active' window.

One you 'window' the app, it gets a bigger time slice.
Once you switch to it, or if you only have it and Desktop running,
once you press 'ALT', the system knows where you are going,
so then it boosts the priority of the App.


Brian Butterfield <brianb@micro-tech.com> wrote in message
news:AiCB4.45781$wk.1822239@news1.mia...
> OK...this is a weird one...
>
> I am running a COBOL database application on an MS-DOS runtime executing
on
> Win 95/98 clients from a NT 4.0 server.  This app was migrated from a

minutes
> to run.  Quite by accident it was discovered that if you ran the
application
> windowed instead of full screen, the processing time decreased
> dramatically..1 minute 30 seconds.  As I was trying to troubleshoot this,
I
> was toggling between full screen DOS and windowed by hitting Alt+Enter.

Hi:

COBOL has been around since the sixties. If you are interested
in becoming a BUSINESS APPLICATION programmer, then you must
learn COBOL. There is no other language which does business stuff
so well.  It would take too long to go into strength/weaknesses.

Thanks

TOny DIlworth

```

#### ↳ Re: Strenghts and/or weaknesses of COBOL

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0rLB4.3056$z22.235462@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<5HEB4.8853$9M1.625317@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
L Dixson wrote:
>
> Hi, I am a college student looking for some information on the strenghts
> and/or weaknesses of Cobol programming language.  If some does have any
> information please email me. Thanks

*Very* briefly:

Strengths:
    Simple to learn and program, ergo less error prone
    Easiest language to maintain over time
    Well written COBOL is the clearest language to read
    Efficient in time and resources
    Best language for writing business applications
    Extremely stable over 40 years

Weaknesses:
    Somewhat verbose
    Not well suited for system level programming (compilers, OS, etc.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strenghts and/or weaknesses of COBOL

- **From:** "Stephen Thomas" <talkingcats@gobble.clara.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MEbC4.2372$Eq.42570@nnrp4.clara.net>`
- **References:** `<5HEB4.8853$9M1.625317@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <0rLB4.3056$z22.235462@news2.mia> <8bbg3m$ug7$1@ins20.netins.net>`

```
Floyd Johnson wrote in message <8bbg3m$ug7$1@ins20.netins.net>...
>Judson McClendon (judmc@bellsouth.net) wrote:
>: Weaknesses:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Talking about using COBOL for systems style programming.  Perhaps I could
relate an experience of mine.

A few years ago, I was lead-developer on a project that implemented a
real-time customer information system in COBOL.  It provided train running
information to about twenty stations in the Welsh valleys.  I wrote the
kernel of the system: lots of pointer manipulation.  I found no problems
using COBOL in this way; in fact, I found it preferable to C - and I've
written quite a few lines of C in my time !

And why did we use COBOL?    Portability.    V1 of the system was written on
DOS using the MF multi-threading environment on DOS.  It worked, amazingly.
But only for small stations, with a couple of train info displays.  It ran
out of stack space really fast.  V2 was ported to OS/2, and that was OK.  V3
was ported to AIX and that was where it started to work really well :)

Stephen Thomas
talkingcats@gobble.clara.co.uk ('gobble' = spamtrap)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strenghts and/or weaknesses of COBOL

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uhpC4.6261$z22.349723@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<5HEB4.8853$9M1.625317@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <0rLB4.3056$z22.235462@news2.mia> <8bbg3m$ug7$1@ins20.netins.net>`

```
Floyd Johnson wrote
> Judson McClendon (judmc@bellsouth.net) wrote:
> : Weaknesses:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> routines using COBOL.  He won a laptop for his effort.

Yes, I have written graphics routines in COBOL, building all of them
starting with the most basic elements, plot dot, draw line, draw
character, etc.  I have also written a compiler and p-code interpreter
in COBOL as part of a general file conversion utility.  Both times I
was constrained to use COBOL because it was the only language available
or acceptable to my clients.  Both times it would have been easier and
more efficient, for me and the computer, if a more suitable language
had been available.  I didn't win a laptop, but I was paid $25,000 for
the program that used the graphics routines, and I made about $100,000
selling the utility program, which is still in use after 20 years. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Strenghts and/or weaknesses of COBOL

- **From:** "Steven O. Ellis" <soellis@soltec.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D82836.C446378@soltec.net>`
- **References:** `<5HEB4.8853$9M1.625317@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
L Dixson wrote:

> Hi, I am a college student looking for some information on the strenghts
> and/or weaknesses of Cobol programming language.  If some does have any
> information please email me. Thanks

COBOL excels at defining and handling business data.  Huge amounts of data
can be defined and stored in easily understandable and accessible formats.

David Shelby Kirk has a lot to say about it:
http://www.objectz.com/cobolreport/archives/cw_shelby.htm

And you may want to check out other sources at:
http://www.CobolWorld.com/
```

http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/download.htm

And get sockets.zip.  MF examples are included, including how to
dynamically call the routines (no link with the lib required).  The
examples also cover Fujitsu COBOL.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Netexpress call entry point in wsock32.dll

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38d8ead4_2@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<8b7sjs$72c$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38d7a20b_2@news1.prserv.net> <8b9up7$4vs$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Dirk,

Yeah, I just hurriedly ripped out some code for you - sorry about that.
Just out of curiousity, you are not trying to write something that
connects to MVS are you?  If so, you might want to take a look at our
web-site and the "MVS Direct API" - just a thought...

Anyway, here are the following structures that you requested:

working-storage items:
       01  ws-msg-table.
           03  ws-msg occurs 30 times.
               05  filler              pic x(100) value spaces.


       linkage section.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      * This is the Linkage Section for GetHostByName call:            *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  temp-addr.
           05  pointer-to-names        pointer.
           05  pointer-to-alias        pointer.
           05  address-type            pic 9(4) comp-5.
           05  address-length          pic 9(4) comp-5.
           05  pointer-to-address      pointer.
       01  host-addr-pointer           pointer.
       01  host-addr.
           05  host-inet-addr          pic 9(9) comp-5.
           05  host-name-ret           pic x(4).
       01  inet-buffer                 pic x(25).
```

### Running a msdos batch program

- **From:** "J.V." <jv@pandora.be>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jKMB4.8017$ds6.28951@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Hello dear friends,

I am looking for a COBOL command to run an msdos batch program.
I use ca-realia 3. Does somebody know how I can do this?

Thank you,
John Varhard
```

#### ↳ Re: Running a msdos batch program

- **From:** "Joe Hunter" <pcs@usaor.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sdfcu8uglul157@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<jKMB4.8017$ds6.28951@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```

J.V. <jv@pandora.be> wrote in message
news:jKMB4.8017$ds6.28951@afrodite.telenet-ops.be...
> Hello dear friends,
>
> I am looking for a COBOL command to run an msdos batch program.
> I use ca-realia 3. Does somebody know how I can do this?


77  DOS-COMMAND   PIC X(121).

MOVE LOW-VALUES TO DOS-COMMAND.
STRING "XCOPY etc" DELIMITED BY SIZE
       INTO DOS-COMMAND.
CALL "DOS_EXEC-COMMAND" USING DOS-COMMAND.

Joe Hunter
pcs@usaor.net
```

## Thread: ACUODBC using VB or Delphi? — 2 this month / 3 total

### ACUODBC using VB or Delphi?

- **From:** bytor99999@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8b8p63$tkq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We are currently testing ACUODBC and wanted to know what front-end
application can access the data the quickest. Here is the scenario.
Vision Version 3 files residing in a UNIX Box, VB, Delphi, Oracle
Forms, or other Windows 98 based application front-end. Which accesses
the data the quickest? What are some of the pitfalls?

Thank you for your help

Mark


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: ACUODBC using VB or Delphi?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bb3do$gdq$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<8b8p63$tkq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<bytor99999@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8b8p63$tkq$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> We are currently testing ACUODBC and wanted to know what front-end
> application can access the data the quickest. Here is the scenario.
> Vision Version 3 files residing in a UNIX Box, VB, Delphi, Oracle
> Forms, or other Windows 98 based application front-end. Which accesses
> the data the quickest? What are some of the pitfalls?

Unless there should be some concern about it, a frontend in Acucobol will no
doubt be the fastest as it can access the data natively (that is, access
Acuserver directly).

Other than that, I personally have a preference for Delphi. I have been
using Delphi a lot together with Acucorp products, and never with much
hassle.

Cheesle
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8b8shq$df$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We are a NetExpress developer that uses Btrieve to store our data.  I
call the Btrieve API's via DLL's, and not the _BTRV that is supported
by Merant.  I'd like to start using their ActiveX controls, but I'd
like to hear from someone who's been there first.  Thanks.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress and Btrieve

- **From:** gil_nelson2@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8baoo8$1ep$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8b8shq$df$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I'm still using the activex controls from Smithware which Pervasive
purchased.  I've used them from the beginning and like them.  There are
a few quirks you have to work around but those may have been fixed in
later versions.  I would go that route before ever going SQL ODBC.  I
can crank out an application a lot quicker and I have more control of
the data.

Gil

## Thread: QAHiperstation for Stress Testing — 1 msgs

### QAHiperstation for Stress Testing

- **From:** "Greg" <gcashen@citlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AwTB4.907$RW1.105305@news-west.eli.net>`

```
Does anyone have experience with QAHiperstation, especially as it relates
to
 Stress Testing using the Unattended Replay Mode?

 I would like to know:

 1) if it is possible to run scripts off of input files so that scripts do
 not have to be copied in order for the same script to run on different
 inputs or field values, i.e. if I want to run the same script on 100 social
 security #'s, do I have to copy the script 100 times with the SSN different
 in each one, or can I tell Hiperstation to use a file to look them up using
 the same script?

 2) if I have to refresh the background data after update scripts run so
that
 Hiperstation does not get a compare mismatch, or if there is a setting in
 the configuration that will prevent this.

 3) if you have any addditonal advice that would benefit a new user, it is
 appreciated. Were you satisfied with the performance of this tool? Pros &
 Cons?

 Thank you,

 Greg Cashen
 Systems Analyst
 Deloitte Consulting
```

<a id="t-87cf4cb4d5"></a>
## Thread: Mouse in MF 3.4 — 2 msgs

### Mouse in MF 3.4

- **From:** "pmjones" <pmjones@netcomuk.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bb6qe$oq6$1@taliesin2.netcom.net.uk>`

```
One for the old MF hands ...

I've gone back in time to work on a character based "DOS box" application.
This application has no mouse control. I dug into my old examples, and
enabled the mouse via x"AF", so that (a) clicking within an ACCEPT "form"
will cause the cursor to move there (b) return LEFT mouse click as a value
when getting a keyboard char via x"AF"/27.

So far so good
- I have mouse control within a form
- I can detect a mouse when waiting for a key (cursor movement, eg PgUp, Rt
Arrow)
-- after the second I can read the word under the cursor and mimic the
desired key press

Yes, there is a question .....

Can any right mouse click be detected?

It would be a nice extra to
- have right click terminate an accept (and popup a context menu)
- be detected on getting a keyboard character

I don't want to use the CBL_ mouse routines, as this would be a huge
exercise. The application is about to get either an html or dialog front
end - this is a short term add-on, as I declared mouse control to be a
doddle given the way the application is written.

Any info appreciated !

Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: Mouse in MF 3.4

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38D92EFB.161A2A1F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8bb6qe$oq6$1@taliesin2.netcom.net.uk>`

```


pmjones wrote:
> 
> One for the old MF hands ...
> 
Old, but comparatively 'young' when it comes to using M/F. You possibly
have the documentation I'm referring to - but I did post some M/F
on-line help text a while back - check it out on deja.com. Whether it
contains a feature to test the double-click I don't recall - still you
don't want to use the CBL_MOUSE routines - you are difficult to please
aren't you :-)

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-6b822c98f3"></a>
## Thread: writing code - I'm stuck- anyone help? — 28 this month / 46 total

### writing code - I'm stuck- anyone help?

- **From:** Craig <fnsrvc@pacbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DA4E6B.C5E5A19@pacbell.net>`

```
I am stuck on a Junior College programming class assigment. We only meet
once
a week and my teacher is not around and classmates can't help. Below is
my source code. I am using Microsoft 3.0 cobol compiler in Dos. I can't
seem to get the
WRITE statement to work. Have been reading the Stern book, but it's no
help.
Anyone have ideas?  My e-mail is fnsrvc@pacbell.net

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. PROJECT2.
       DATE-COMPILED.

      ****************************************************************
      *                                                              *
      *  Author:                             Craig                   *
      *  Student ID:                                                 *
      *  Course:                             CS11 COBOL Programming  *
      *  Project Number:                     2                       *
      *  Project due date:                   February 25, 2000       *
      *  Source file:                        PROJECT2.CBL            *
      *                                                              *
      *                                                              *
      *  This software creates an output list from a master          *
      *  payroll list.                                               *
      *                                                              *
      ****************************************************************


       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

       FILE-CONTROL.

           SELECT PAYROLL-MASTER-IN
               ASSIGN TO  'A:\Project2.dat'
                    ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

           SELECT PAYROLL-RECORD-OUT
               ASSIGN TO  'A:\Project2.out'
                    ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.



       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.

       FD   PAYROLL-MASTER-IN.




       01   PAYROLL-MASTER-REC.
            05  EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-IN     PIC X(5).
            05  EMPLOYEE-NAME-IN       PIC X(20).

            05  LOCATION-CODE.
                10   TERRITORY-NUMBER-IN   PIC X(2).
                10   OFFICE-NUMBER-IN      PIC X(2).

            05  ANNUAL-SALARY-IN            PIC X(6).
            05  SOCIAL-SECURITY-NUMBER-IN   PIC X(9).
            05  DEPENDENTS-NUMBER-IN        PIC X(2).
            05  JOB-CLASS-CODE-IN  PIC X(2).
            05  UNUSED                      PIC X(32).

       FD   PAYROLL-RECORD-OUT.



       01   PAYROLL-RECORD-REC.
            05  EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-OUT      PIC X(5).
            05  EMPLOYEE-NAME-OUT        PIC X(20).

            05  LOCATION-CODE-OUT.
                10   TERRITORY-NUMBER-OUT       PIC X(2).
                10   OFFICE-NUMBER-OUT          PIC X(2).

            05  ANNUAL-SALARY-OUT               PIC X(6).
            05  SOCIAL-SECURITY-NUMBER-OUT      PIC X(9).
            05  DEPENDENTS-NUMBER-OUT           PIC X(2).
            05  JOB-CLASS-CODE-OUT     PIC X(2).
            05  UNUSED                          PIC X(32).


       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       100-MAIN-MODULE.
           OPEN INPUT  PAYROLL-MASTER-IN
           OPEN OUTPUT PAYROLL-RECORD-OUT

           READ PAYROLL-MASTER-IN
           MOVE EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-IN TO EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-OUT
           MOVE EMPLOYEE-NAME-IN TO EMPLOYEE-NAME-OUT
           MOVE TERRITORY-NUMBER-IN TO TERRITORY-NUMBER-OUT
           MOVE OFFICE-NUMBER-IN TO OFFICE-NUMBER-OUT
           MOVE ANNUAL-SALARY-IN TO ANNUAL-SALARY-OUT
           MOVE SOCIAL-SECURITY-NUMBER-IN TO SOCIAL-SECURITY-NUMBER-OUT
           MOVE DEPENDENTS-NUMBER-IN TO DEPENDENTS-NUMBER-OUT
           MOVE JOB-CLASS-CODE-IN TO JOB-CLASS-CODE-OUT

           WRITE PAYROLL-REC-REC.

           CLOSE PAYROLL-MASTER-IN
           CLOSE PAYROLL-RECORD-OUT
           STOP RUN.
```

#### ↳ Re: writing code - I'm stuck- anyone help?

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0207204e.945d5dc9@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<38DA4E6B.C5E5A19@pacbell.net>`

```
In article <38DA4E6B.C5E5A19@pacbell.net>, Craig
<fnsrvc@pacbell.net> wrote:
>
>
>       01   PAYROLL-RECORD-REC.
>            05  EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-OUT      PIC X(5).
>            05  EMPLOYEE-NAME-OUT        PIC X(20).

<snip>

>
>           WRITE PAYROLL-REC-REC.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>           STOP RUN.

You must write the record name, e.g. PAYROLL-RECORD-REC. You
should have received a compile error, invalid data/record name,
since the data name PAYROLL-REC-REC doesn't exist.

I used Microsoft COBOL about 6 years ago, had a lot of fun with
it, but compared with todays COBOL, it is an antique. BTW, Micro
Focus (Merant) wrote the COBOL compiler for Microsoft.

Regards,

Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

- **References:** `<38DA4E6B.C5E5A19@pacbell.net>`

```

WRITE PAYROLL-RECORD-REC instead of WRITE PAYROLL-REC-REC should do it.  
The record name must be specified for the WRITE statement and you're not 
using the coded name.

> I am stuck on a Junior College programming class assigment. We only meet

1) It looks like you are only going to process 1 input record.

2) change you WRITE Statement:

to   WRITE PAYROLL-RECORD-REC.

Good luck!!

#### ↳ Re: writing code - I'm stuck- anyone help?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DA89B2.B747A394@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38DA4E6B.C5E5A19@pacbell.net>`

```


Craig wrote:
> 
> I am stuck on a Junior College programming class assigment. We only meet
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone have ideas?  My e-mail is fnsrvc@pacbell.net

As somebody said, you are only actioning one record. I'll put it in my
own words - are you familiar with setting flags (Level 88s) ? :-

	perform OPEN-FILES
	perform PROCESS-INPUT
	perform CLOSE-FILES
	STOP RUN
	.

	PROCESS-INPUT.

	 set InputNotFinished to true
	 perform READ-NEXT-INPUT

	 if InputNotFinished
	    perform until InputFinished

	 	move.........
	        write OUT-RECORD
	        perform READ-NEXT-INPUT

	    End-perform

	 End-if
	.
 
	READ-NEXT-INPUT.

	*> Read NEXT CAN be used with sequential and indexed -
	*> BUT - check what your compiler allows - You're using M/F
	*> DOS V3 so you are OK with this syntax

	  Read NEXT Input-record
	     at end set InputFinished to true
	  End-read
	.


Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

We just found a construct that's a hoot. Change your PROCESS-INPUT
paragraph to:

PROCESS-INPUT.
     PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT
          READ INPUT-FILE AT END
               EXIT PERFORM END-READ
          MOVE.....
          MOVE.......
          WRITE ........
           END-PERFORM.
END-READ
          END-PERFORM.

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>

Sorry. Correct code is:

PROCESS-INPUT.
     PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT
            READ INPUT-FILE
                AT END EXIT PERFORM END-READ
       MOVE.....
       MOVE.......
       WRITE ........
       END-PERFORM.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: writing code - I'm stuck- anyone help?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bopoj$vkl$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38DA4E6B.C5E5A19@pacbell.net> <38DA89B2.B747A394@home.com> <WwRC4.753$Id7.11725@news.swbell.net> <5a8ods4mu3e264l0ocjefonqrkn4j38da4@4ax.com> <7%3D4.609$ve.22132@news.swbell.net> <80jtds07o5vk49huieca1bc08mb6b0esd5@4ax.com>`

```
As I recall, you are running IBM mainframe COBOL that doesn't (yet) support
the EXIT PARAGRAPH/PERFORM/SECTION syntax (included in the next Standard).
Once you get used to a COBOL with this facility, I think you will find that
MANY "switches" are no longer needed (and code is less error-prone).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: writing code - I'm stuck- anyone help?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.134abee919d9b39a9899fb@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38DA4E6B.C5E5A19@pacbell.net> <38DA89B2.B747A394@home.com> <WwRC4.753$Id7.11725@news.swbell.net> <5a8ods4mu3e264l0ocjefonqrkn4j38da4@4ax.com> <7%3D4.609$ve.22132@news.swbell.net> <80jtds07o5vk49huieca1bc08mb6b0esd5@4ax.com> <8bopoj$vkl$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I noticed that William M. Klein as wmklein@nospam.netcom.com said...
> As I recall, you are running IBM mainframe COBOL that doesn't (yet) support
> the EXIT PARAGRAPH/PERFORM/SECTION syntax (included in the next Standard).
> Once you get used to a COBOL with this facility, I think you will find that
> MANY "switches" are no longer needed (and code is less error-prone).

I think you'll also have to get used to more incomprehensible code too.  
To me, the great thing about COBOL was the discipline that it imbued in 
its programmers and every step further away from the English language 
basis is an encouragement for yet more abstruse coding.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: writing code - I'm stuck- anyone help?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8br560$j12$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38DA4E6B.C5E5A19@pacbell.net> <38DA89B2.B747A394@home.com> <WwRC4.753$Id7.11725@news.swbell.net> <5a8ods4mu3e264l0ocjefonqrkn4j38da4@4ax.com> <7%3D4.609$ve.22132@news.swbell.net> <80jtds07o5vk49huieca1bc08mb6b0esd5@4ax.com> <8bopoj$vkl$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.134abee919d9b39a9899fb@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
Charles,
  I haven't replied to most of your current posts - but PLEASE tell me what
is more obtuse about

   Read Whatever
            At End
                 Exit Paragraph
           Not At End
                 Perform Whatever
    End-Read
      .

compared to

   Read Whatever
            At End
                 Move "Y" to EOF-Switch
                    .
  If EOF-Switch = "N"
                 Perform Whatever
                    .


(Obviously, - as pointed out in another response - scope terminators would
allow you to nest the READ statement in another conditional - and do a
"common action" regardless of EOF status - which "ye olde" COBOL would not
allow.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: writing code - I'm stuck- anyone help?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.freedombird.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.134b66c5d0b69e42989a16@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38DA4E6B.C5E5A19@pacbell.net> <38DA89B2.B747A394@home.com> <WwRC4.753$Id7.11725@news.swbell.net> <5a8ods4mu3e264l0ocjefonqrkn4j38da4@4ax.com> <7%3D4.609$ve.22132@news.swbell.net> <80jtds07o5vk49huieca1bc08mb6b0esd5@4ax.com> <8bopoj$vkl$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.134abee919d9b39a9899fb@news.mersinet.co.uk> <8br560$j12$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I noticed that William M. Klein as wmklein@nospam.netcom.com said...
> Charles,
>   I haven't replied to most of your current posts - but PLEASE tell me what
> is more obtuse about

Not obtuse, abstruse.  Whatever
 
>    Read Whatever
>             At End
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     End-Read

Unfortunately, Bill, in work situations I haven't seen anything as 
restrained as this.  They tend to contain anywhere between ten and five 
hundred lines between the READ and the END-READ statements and, more 
often than not, we can't get proper printed listings to help use scan an 
area of code longer than 24 lines.  When code goes to these lengths, the 
indentation always seems to go to pot as well.

This is why I keep banging on about short routines - if I can see it all 
on one screen, it makes debugging a hell of a lot easier than patching 
10 screen prints together with sticky tape just to see the story.

> compared to
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> allow.)

Taking your example, my version would be as follows

           read whatever
               at end
                   move "y" to EOF-switch.
           if eof-switch = "N"
               perform whatever.

Not that I would necessarily do it exactly like this but that's getting 
into the realms of unnecessary pedantry.  As you can see, I use the "." 
scope terminator.

Getting slightly more towards a prose style, one could use:

           Read the-file, at end move "y" to eof-switch.
           If eof-switch = "N", perform the-action.

That's legal code too, even though I had to reword it slightly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: style suggestions? ReRe. bad day at work

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c0pqv02gp@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<38DA4E6B.C5E5A19@pacbell.net> <38DA89B2.B747A394@home.com> <WwRC4.753$Id7.11725@news.swbell.net> <5a8ods4mu3e264l0ocjefonqrkn4j38da4@4ax.com> <7%3D4.609$ve.22132@news.swbell.net> <80jtds07o5vk49huieca1bc08mb6b0esd5@4ax.com> <8bopoj$vkl$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.134abee919d9b39a9899fb@news.mersinet.co.uk> <8br560$j12$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.134b66c5d0b69e42989a16@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
I like this construct:

B000-MAIN-LOOPER.

READ INPUT-FILE INTO MY-FILE-RECORD
   AT END
      SET INPUT-EOF   TO TRUE
END-READ.

PERFORM UNTIL INPUT-EOF

   IF MY-FILE-RECORD-TYPE IS = 'Y'
      PERFORM B000-PROCESS-RECORD   THRU B000-EXIT
   END-IF

   READ INPUT-FILE INTO MY-FILE-RECORD
      AT END
         SET INPUT-EOF   TO TRUE
   END-READ

END-PERFORM.

B000-EXIT.  EXIT.
     EJECT

------------------------
as an alternative, though, go to is better than a switch like this.  Using
let's say an OLDER style with GO TO intact and no scope terminators, it does
the same thing but is smaller and seems just as readable for small loops:

B000-MAIN-LOOPER.
   READ INPUT-FILE INTO MY-FILE-RECORD
      AT END  GO TO B000-EXIT.
   IF MY-FILE-RECORD-TYPE IS = 'Y'
      PERFORM B000-PROCESS-RECORD   THRU B000-EXIT.
   GO TO B000-MAIN-LOOPER.
B000-EXIT.

Really, I just had a shitty day at work, doing bullshit and not being able
to have fun developing.  So I'm really just looking to start some shit
talking.  And I do prefer the first method though


Jeff


----------

Hankel <charles@hankel.freedombird.net> wrote:

…[60 more quoted lines elided]…

```
To tell the truth, I like neither.  I would write it like so:

mainline.
    open input input-file.
    read input-file at end
        move "y" to end-flag
        perform special-empty-file-routine.
    perform main-loop
        until end-flag is equal to "y".
    close input-file.
    goback.

main-loop.
    perform whatever-on-the-record.
    read input-file at end
        move "y" to end-flag.

```
While we are at it.

Mainline.
     Perform open-files.
     if no-error-flag
        set eof-flag to false (or other similar construct)
        perform process-file until eof-flag or fatal-error-flag
     end-if.

     if not OPEN-error-flag  ---- This one I normally put
        Perform close-files  ---- on the close-files paragraph.
     end-if.
     goback.

process-file.
     read input-file
       at end
          set eof-flag to true.
     if not eof-flag
        perform process input-record
     end-if.

Open-files.
     open input input-file.
     if input-file file-status not ï¿½ "normal error"
        set open-error-flag to true
     end-if.


FF
