###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bqu4j$gc4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8bible$c0i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8bj2c8$j6g$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8blpit$rs3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38df8f4a.1603834955@news.cox-internet.com> <8bobd7$k1o$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38e0dafe.1688763946@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
In article <38e0dafe.1688763946@news.cox-internet.com>,
thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
>
> It's a maintenance issue. With one shared area of WS you are free to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi (feeling dense):

Sorry, I didn't know you were talking about sharing source code.

I have 'one shared area of WS' which I have used since the sixties.
It contains common things required in every program, like subscripts,
date and time fields, page counts, commonly-used edited fields, work
areas for standard subroutines, etc. It is automatically included in
every program ever written by me and my cohorts (when I had any).

Certainly, going in willy-nilly and changing something in that copy
could have unkown consequences elsewhere when those elsewheres were
recompiled. However, it is forbidden to make changes to that copy for
any reason without permission from Authority.

To avoid the situation you describe, without resorting to OO, would
be to physically insert the standard copy into your NEW program when
first written which would allow you to change stuff as required (but it
shouldn't be required) in that program's 'private' copy. The master
copy is never changed and never COPIED at compile-time. Sure, there
may be disadvantages to this, but I can't think of any which don't
have simple solutions.


Based on what I have read and failed to comprehend about OO so far,
and given the desire to solve the problem you describe, that is what
I would do.


Thanks

Tony Dilworth




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E12486.D9373F10@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8bible$c0i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8bj2c8$j6g$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8blpit$rs3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38df8f4a.1603834955@news.cox-internet.com> <8bobd7$k1o$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38e0dafe.1688763946@news.cox-internet.com> <8bqu4j$gc4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38E11323.4C46F42D@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Put it this way - OO is not a religion. So far as COBOL is concerned it
> does add some new features, (agreed, they are paralleled in structured).
> My own thoughts are that it offers some additiional flexibility.

I am wondering though what the net effect will be of this migration of
Cobol towards the OO standards.  Will significant amounts of shops
decide Cobol is the best tool because it has these features? 

If they are not now using Cobol, will it now be a consideration?

If they have trained Cobol programmers, will they be more likely to pick
OO Cobol over a different, more "contemporary" language?

I suppose it depends on how they perceive the labor market to be more
than anything else.  I don't see that there is a significant advantage
in having old Cobol programmers going to OO Cobol vs Java - the learning
curve won't be that different - and with turnover it may be easier to
find new Java programmers.

Do they care which language actually does a better job?  Probably -
especially which one is easier to maintain.  But do they have the facts
to make this determination and to put a value on it?  I doubt it.

Does this change process make decision makers think Cobol is vibrant and
current, or that it is desperately playing catch-up?

Or does it make any difference whatsoever as companies go more towards
turnkey solutions and datamart tools?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E17C63.7761B66D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8bible$c0i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8bj2c8$j6g$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8blpit$rs3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38df8f4a.1603834955@news.cox-internet.com> <8bobd7$k1o$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38e0dafe.1688763946@news.cox-internet.com> <8bqu4j$gc4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38E11323.4C46F42D@home.com> <38E12486.D9373F10@cusys.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> turnkey solutions and datamart tools?

Two issues Howard :

(1) COBOL is always playing catch-up. Don't want to labour it, but until
the Standards procedure is faster ???? Meanwhile people are looking for
tools to talk to - Windows. That's what the 'newer' languages responded
to. So we've lost a lot of potential COBOL developers - and no I don't
think they will dramatically switch back to COBOL even with OO type
enhancements.

(2) DataMarts - I'm speaking now as a 'systems' man not a programmer. I
just don't buy into the DataMart thing - small users yes, where they are
prepared to take 'generics' - but the Walmarts, Safeways etc. who have
to have aggressive marketing - jut doesn't cut it.

Give you a simple example about 'customized' - back in my Department
store days. New Food A/P system - I proposed the system should allow you
to enter data as received and THEN - per vendor record, you put how many
weeks to cycle it before we paid. One large dairy company we bought only
yogurt from and paid them every week - BUT - we paid them some 20 weeks
LATER after the original product had been sold 19 weeks EARLIER. The
accountants blanched at the suggestion - the Food Director was thrilled
- real nice on our cash flow.

You just aren't going to get that type of cut and thrust from Datamarts.
(Was it ethical - yes - if the vendor was too bloody stupid not to have
a better control on his A/R).

BTW - The dairy company wasn't Unigate that I had moved from - they
latched on straight away  - sent us invoices attached to a WEEKLY
statement - pay up or else :-). On a visit I made subsequently they
grinned and told me they knew who had introduced that particular 'twist'
!

Jimmy
```

```
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>I am wondering though what the net effect will be of this migration of
>Cobol towards the OO standards.  Will significant amounts of shops
>decide Cobol is the best tool because it has these features? 

the net effect will be the use of linkage, local , thread storage
sections, calls (i don't think many people will use method syntax
anytime soon (i know i won't)), and evaluate compiler-version.

local storage is good, as is linkage section, and might eventually
drive out the working-storage section (local would store the variables
per thread, and linkage would be called globally).

strangely enough, i have never used 88, 77, or 66 level variables, so
i also don't see a future in the set-variable oo clause (and i hope j4
doesn't put it in).

if a linkage call by content or value has multiple (prepare for oo
buzzword) instances of data, i don't see the point of local storage
period.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E0B4A4.CFB076B7@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8bible$c0i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8bj2c8$j6g$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8blpit$rs3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38df8f4a.1603834955@news.cox-internet.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> THIS is what OO can help cure, via data hiding and common methods to
> access this data.
However, one of my pet peeves about OO, is that most OO languages force you
to set the hidden data by invoking methods. This is not a problem in itself,
but when you have an object with 50 data elements and you need 50 methods to
set the data and 50 methods to retrieve the data, then this is clearly getting
out of hand.

I'm not totally up to date with the COBOL OO language spec, but if there was a
way to do the equivalent of:

	Call Set_Private_Data Using
		Data_element1
		...
		Data_element50

Then this would be a good thing.

By the way, there is nothing to stop you from doing data verification calls
from the Set_Private_Data, just as you can put the verification into each of
the 50 set data methods.
```

```
<josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:

COBOL has the advantage of seeing the mistakes and poor

simple MOVE.  It's painless.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working-storage vs. local-storage

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E1FE1C.A1068793@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8bible$c0i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8bj2c8$j6g$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8blpit$rs3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38df8f4a.1603834955@news.cox-internet.com> <38E0B4A4.CFB076B7@iinet.net.au>`

```
Joseph Katnic wrote:
> 
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> into each of the 50 set data methods.

Firstly:  What is to stop a single method returning a structure should
you want that.  One call all the data in it.

Second:  fifty elements appears very high you would have to convince
me that they were directly related.  Object my contain other objects
and so on.  You may have 50 in theory but each are smaller.

Third:  Get and Set are BAD!!!!

The basic concept is that object work on themselves and do not expose
their internal data.  If we look at the fraction example (sorry Thane)
when the values are accessed the three values are displayed in a
sequence.  This would be better built as a string function that
returns the fraction for display (one function three internal
values).  The setter would typically be the reverse, a string input
that is interpreted by the best logic you have.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<8bhkat$621$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8bible$c0i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8bj2c8$j6g$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <8blpit$rs3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38df8f4a.1603834955@news.cox-internet.com> <38E0B4A4.CFB076B7@iinet.net.au> <38E1FE1C.A1068793@zip.com.au> <8bubup$t00$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:
> 
> God Ken, you can get carried away. <G>.  Just tell him to use a record
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Don,

I think this is where you have to make a distinction between what is
practical/desirable and following what the gurus say is the right way to
use OO. Now if you look at the examples where I am passing Local-storage
- I can kind of see where M/F were coming from in one of their demos -
and that code made Pete Dashwood want to throw up. (I didn't throw-up,
but I was bloody close - saying to myself if this is OO 'Forget it!).

- Firstly, they were aware (having written their compiler) of the
possible penalty in using too much space for L/S

- Second, following the above thought, they then followed the 'accepted'
way of using OO by passing (Set) and getting (GET)
each individual field (from memory about 10 fields). Now turn that 10
into say 30 for a large name-address record plus credit limit, rating,
last purchase, last transaction date etc. then that gives you :-

	(a) a lot of coding doing the same thing
	(b) the obvious possibility of typos in coding - hence 
	    more desk and test-checking

In the above I'm assuming you are after a complete record for editing as
opposed to selective fields to say print a report showing last
transaction date and total purchases.

To my mind - your way represents the more practical :-
	
(a) Use an 01 to pass a whole record -
	copy "myRecord.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ls==.
	(My preferred method when editing - using three-tier
	system approach, business logic, file handling and screen
	displays)

(b) In the case of collections/dictionaries pass the
	01 Level object reference which embraces n to n+....... 
	individual objects, and which may in turn contain sub-objects.
	(Ideal approach for passing contents of droplists or
	listboxes)

Para (a) above definitely 'breaks' the cautionary note by M/F about too
much use of L/S - and doesn't sit well with the "SET" or "GET"
technique.  Take the short-cut to get the job done effectively, with
reduced code, less prone to error.

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-9651ed88eb"></a>
## Thread: Learning OO COBOL - Dissimilar Implementations. — 22 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Learning OO COBOL - Dissimilar Implementations.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38dbb53d.1351903671@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
I've read the comments here wondering whether OO on OS/390 is viable
and/or recommended.  I've also been having private discussions with
some of you here, in my onw OO COBOL quest.

I want to learn OO COBOL to decide if it's something I want to start
using for my development or if I should "Pass".  It's been
challenging.  I have written my first OO COBOL program.  It follows
this message.  I'll get into what it does etc in a few minutes.

During my research into this subject, I learned something about
collection classes.  Collection classes are a set of routines designed
to let you manage multiple instances of objects, such as "customers"
or other things.  This is a very useful thing, but something that is
decidedly not standard.  NetExpress has them, and Fujitsu has them -
with very different implementation.  With the next Fujtisu release,
the Class Collection Library will only be available with the
"Enterprise Edition" of Fujitsu COBOL.  It's really the only extra
feature of the Enterprise Edition that I really desire.  Of course,
that's the idea. I'm going to have a hard time coughing up $3000.00 to
get a class collection library, however.  So, I'll stick with my
"Standard" version.  This lead me to the realization that Jimmy is
right.  OO really does help with sharing code and re-use.  It helps to
the point where there can "easily" be created an Open Source set of
common class libraries.  We can all share them.  

Realizing this, I decided to send Jimmy my code to see if it will
compile under Merant's Net Express.  I didn't use any features of
Fujitsu that were not in the 1.8 draft standard.  I should note that
Fujitsu is very close to the standard with their 5.0 (beta) product.
The lack only a few minor modifyers for Property behavior and the
whole "INTERFACE-ID" and items related to that.  

At the same time I sent the code off to Jimmy, I uploaded it to our
OS/390 2.7 box, running COBOL for OS/390 and VM version 2.1.  

I don't yet know wich version of NetExpress Jimmy was running, but I
was under the impression it was the latest - I would really like to
hear from some Merant people on the issues I am about to discuss.  

NE does not understand "Properties".  Properties are special (declared
as properties) elementary items in Working-Storage of an object.  They
can be retrieved form the object using a "GET" method, and set to
values in objects uisng a "SET" method.  This means that data items in
an object can be manipulated with these methods, and need not be
passed to the class using parms when a method is invoked.  Now, this
is REALLY attractive to me.  It's true encapsulation, and it's how
ActiveX controls work.  You set properties, and invoke methods with
ActiveX -- it's REALLY OO sharing.  At any rate, when using Parms, you
actually have 2 copies of the data - the one you pass when invoking a
method and the one the object has under it's control.  The whole tenet
of OO is that the data is protected within the object - only methods
of the object can change the data.  So, I think you see why I really
like them.  You can see their use in my attached programs.

Well, the version of NE that Mr Gavan has doesn't know what a property
is.  In addition, it requires the Factory to be defined for a class,
even if nothing is in it -- since I don't use the Factory in my
example (Although Donald Tees did explain how I could have done
EVERYTHING this program does in a Factory, avoiding the necessity of
creating the object first with "new") I didn't include the Factory in
my code.  NE choked on this, and on the OBJECT paragraph and on
Properties.  

I had higher hopes for OS/390.  But alas, the manuals don't mention
Property, Object paragraph or even the FACTORY!  

OO on OS/390 is a small subset of the total specification in the draft
standard - and that is the primary reason in my mind that it's not
quite ready for prime time.  

The vast differences in these 3 implementations (Does anyone know of
any other OO COBOL implementations?) makes it virtually impossible for
those of us experimenting with OO to share code.  The sooner the
standard is approved - and implemented - the better.

In addition, Merant seems to have some pecular dependancies in the
invoking program that don't seem to exist in OS/390 or Fujitsu.  Jimmy
keeps telling me about a "Trigger" program - that's what I thought my
mainline was!

Anyway, this is kind of just a status report.  What follows is my
code.  This class that I have started is designed ot take a decimal
number and return the equivalent fraction in lowest terms.  It's an
EXTREMELY efficient algorithm for this process, not the normal "Brute
force" method of trying every possible combination until you get a
hit.  This isn't my "time tracking" project, but the project I am
using this for is kind of special, and is taking priority.

The algorithm I used is described in full here: 

http://forum.swarthmore.edu/dr.math/problems/lawson6.25.98.html

The mainline is Fractest, and the class is in Fraction.  This uses
those pesky properties.  

000020 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000021 PROGRAM-ID.  Fractest.
000030 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000040 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
000070 REPOSITORY.
000071     CLASS FJBASE
000072     CLASS Fraction.
000074 DATA DIVISION.
000080 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000082 01  Integer-part    Pic Z(9).
000093 01  Numerator       Pic Z(9).
000094 01  Denominator     Pic Z(9).
000107 01  Fraction-Object Object Reference Fraction.
000290 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000300 Start-Of-Program.
000310      Invoke Fraction "new" Returning Fraction-Object
000320      Move 3.14159 To Decimal-Num of Fraction-Object
000330      Move 10000   To Iterations of Fraction-Object
000340      Move .00005  To Precision of Fraction-Object
000350      Invoke Fraction-Object "Decimal-To-Fraction"
000351      If Error-Msg of Fraction-Object > Spaces
000352         Display Error-Msg of Fraction-Object
000353      Else
000360         Move Integer-Part of Fraction-Object to Integer-Part
000370         Move Numerator of Fraction-Object to Numerator
000380         Move Denominator of Fraction-Object to Denominator
000390         Display "Decimal Number Input = " Integer-Part
000400                 " " Numerator "/" Denominator
000410      End-If
000420      Stop Run
000430      .

And here is the Class.

000010*
000011* Fraction Class
000012*   Properties
000013*     Decimal-Num  - Input
000014*     Precision    - Answer is to be Accurate within this Amount
000017*     Iterations   - Input - How many passes in the Binary
search
000018*                  - to attempt
000019*     Error-Msg    - Output - If Iterations Exceeded a Warning 
000020*                  - is issued here   
000021*     Integer-Part - Output
000022*     Numerator    - Output
000023*     Denominator  - Output
000024*   Methods
000025*      Decimal-To-Fraction 
000026 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000027 CLASS-ID.  Fraction INHERITS FJBASE.
000030 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000040 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
000070 REPOSITORY.
000071     CLASS FJBASE.
000085 OBJECT.
000086 DATA DIVISION.
000087 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000098* properties
000099 01  decimal-num     pic 9v9(9) PROPERTY.
000100 01  integer-part    pic 9(9)   PROPERTY.
000101 01  numerator       pic 9(9)   PROPERTY.
000102 01  denominator     pic 9(9)   PROPERTY.
000103 01  iterations      pic 9(9)   PROPERTY. 
000104 01  error-msg       pic x(25)  PROPERTY.
000105 01  precision       pic V9(9)  PROPERTY.
000106* intermediate fields
000107 01  low.
000110    03  low-num   pic 9(9) value 0.
000120    03  low-den   pic 9(9) value 1.
000130 01  high.
000140    03  high-num  pic 9(9) value 1.
000150    03  high-den  pic 9(9) value 1.
000160 01  cntr pic 9(9) value zeros.
000170 01  break-sw pic x value spaces.
000180    88 break-out value "Y".
000200 01  test-low       pic 9(9)v9(9).
000210 01  test-high      pic 9(9)v9(9).
000220 01  test-x         pic 9(9)v9(9).
000230 01  int            pic 9(9).
000240 01  remd           pic 9(2).
000250 01  num            pic 9(9)v9(9).
000260 01  den            pic 9(9)v9(9).
000270 01  int-num        pic 9(9).
000280 01  int-den        pic 9(9).
000290 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000291 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000292 METHOD-ID.   Decimal-To-Fraction.
000293 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000300     compute Integer-part = function integer (decimal-num)
000303     subtract Integer-part from decimal-num
000310     perform try-fraction varying cntr from 1 by 1 until 
000311                         cntr > iterations or
000320                         break-out
000330     if cntr > iterations
000331        move "EXCEED MAX ITERATIONS" to error-msg
000332     end-if
000340     exit method
000341     .
000350 try-fraction.
000360     compute test-low = (low-den * decimal-num) - low-num
000370     compute test-high = high-num - (high-den * decimal-num)
000380     evaluate true
000390        when test-high <= precision * high-den
000401             move high-num to numerator
000402             move high-den to denominator
000410             set break-out to true
000411        when test-low <= precision * low-den
000420             move low-num to numerator
000430             move low-den to denominator
000440             set break-out to true
000450        when other
000460             perform setup-next-test
000470     end-evaluate 
000480     .
000490 setup-next-test.
000500     compute remd = function rem (cntr 2)
000510     if remd = 0 
000520        perform even-adjustment
000530     else
000540        perform odd-adjustment
000550     end-if
000560     .
000570 odd-adjustment.
000580     compute test-x = test-high / test-low
000590     compute int = function integer (test-x)
000600     compute num = ((int + 1) * low-num) + high-num
000610     compute int-num = function integer (num)
000620     compute den = ((int + 1) * low-den) + high-den
000630     compute int-den = function integer (den)       
000640     if int-num < 32769 and
000650        int-den < 65537
000660        compute high-num = int-num - low-num
000670        compute high-den = den - low-den
000680        move num to low-num
000690        move den to low-den
000700     end-if
000710     .
000720 even-adjustment.   
000730     compute test-x = test-low / test-high
000740     compute int = function integer (test-x)
000750     compute num = low-num + ((int + 1) * high-num)
000760     compute int-num = function integer (num)
000770     compute den = low-den + ((int + 1) * high-den)
000780     compute int-den = function integer (den)
000790     if int-num < 32769 and
000800        int-den < 65537
000810        compute low-num = int-num - high-num
000820        compute low-den = int-den - high-den
000830        move num to high-num
000840        move den to high-den
000850     end-if
000860     .
000870 END METHOD Decimal-To-Fraction.
000880 END OBJECT.
000890 END CLASS Fraction.
```

```
I know I'm talking to myself.

On Fri, 24 Mar 2000 19:00:51 GMT, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane
Hubbell) wrote:

>000310      Invoke Fraction "new" Returning Fraction-Object
>000320      Move 3.14159 To Decimal-Num of Fraction-Object
>000330      Move 10000   To Iterations of Fraction-Object
>000340      Move .00005  To Precision of Fraction-Object
>000350      Invoke Fraction-Object "Decimal-To-Fraction"

I wanted to explain a bit more about Properties.  The Moves above are
the same as saying:

Move 3.14159 to Parm-Area
Invoke Fraction-Object "Set" using Parm-Area

It's just an inline shortcut.

The move for the Display later is the same as:

Invoke Fraction-Object "Get" returning whatever.

The use of the qualification of the object causes an implicit get or
set method to be executed.

Invoke
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - Dissimilar Implementations.

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DC3664.BBFB7AFE@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38dbb53d.1351903671@news.cox-internet.com> <38dbc5af.1356114175@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> Move 3.14159 to Parm-Area
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Invoke Fraction-Object "Get" returning whatever.


I have juts read CD 1.7, this is cool.  This is an extension that
Borland does to C++, magic stuff.

Here is a 'get' method for a property:

METHOD-ID. GET PROPERTY data-name.
DATA DIVISION.
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 LS-data-name data-description.
PROCEDURE DIVISION RETURNING LS-data-name.
par-name.
SET LS-data-name TO data-name
EXIT METHOD.
END METHOD.


This means that you can use a getter and setter method that can force
the calculation.  Thane this means that you should remove the call:

	Invoke Fraction-Object "Decimal-To-Fraction"

The get property method can invoke this function as required.  This
hides the implementation details of how the object works.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - Dissimilar Implementations.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - Dissimilar Implementations.

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bl3op$l6f$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38dbb53d.1351903671@news.cox-internet.com> <38dbc5af.1356114175@news.cox-internet.com> <38DC3664.BBFB7AFE@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote in message <38DC3664.BBFB7AFE@zip.com.au>...

>I have juts read CD 1.7, this is cool.  This is an extension that
>Borland does to C++, magic stuff.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now, if we could get a real Object Oriented Cobol Compiler, it would be
smart enough to know that *every* data name in the code is a COBOL-OBJECT
with a type, length, etc. perhaps methods, etc.  Then we could do something
like:

INVOKE new using "PROGRAM-ID" returning program-object.
INVOKE program-object "paragraph-name" using ...

Taken to the logical conclusion, the code can be singlestepped and emulated
right through to final execution in a completely iterative fashion. A
compiler and an interpret become different compilation versions of the same
program, even at the source code level ....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - Dissimilar Implementations.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DE601C.CCE0D346@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38dbb53d.1351903671@news.cox-internet.com> <38dbc5af.1356114175@news.cox-internet.com> <38DC3664.BBFB7AFE@zip.com.au> <8bl3op$l6f$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey wrote in message <38DC3664.BBFB7AFE@zip.com.au>...
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> program, even at the source code level ....

I'm really fascinated by the approach you and Thane are taking to this -
and something which I'm sure appeals to Ken. You are both researching in
your own ways how to build the basic bricks. And as Thane said it is
challenging.

I have a gut feeling you are all three right in your thinking - but
here's where I have a problem - does it become so sophisticated, by
separating stuff off as separate objects with properties etc., that the
overall picture becomes unintelligible to a programmer who is not the
originator of the design concept - that's not saying it isn't going to
work - but is it easily understandable. (And Howard's very valid point -
is it easily maintainable).

I would suggest my approach is cruder, (I'll soon be sending you some
code along with Ken and others). It gets the job done and I am fanatical
about trying to fit reusability into it, as coding shortcuts. Now having
made a DOS system fit into OO - it does work. And as I go along, I can
see further shortcuts(reusability) that I can use. The point is I've got
a model working and now I hone away at it to introduce further
reusability. Time permitting I'll take a stab at Thane's fractions to
decimals - it will be considerably simpler that what he is attempting -
but the objective would be to get the job done - then it can be refined
to what he is after - again bearing in mind I don't have that Fujitsu
'Property' feature.

If we hear about bad missteps with OO in other languages - I'm prepared
to bet it's because they got 'too clever' with the coding. I
occasionally take a quick peek at comp.object - but those guys leave me
really cold - they rabbit on using all the neat words, polymorphism,
encapsulation etc..... ad nauseum - but bugger-all code in any language
to say what they are talking about. OO is not an abstract concept - it's
something that can be applied to the real world in the premier language
- COBOL.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - Dissimilar Implementations.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bgk96$nat$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38dbb53d.1351903671@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
I wanted to say one quick think about the OO implementation in IBM COBOL for
OS/390 & VM.  It was a "reasonable" subset of the OO definition in the draft
Standard - circa 1997/8.  The draft has moved DRAMATICALLY since then - and
IBM's implementation has NOT.  It is my understanding (totally UNOFFICIALLY)
that IBM is pretty well committed to NOT changing their IBM syntax UNTIL the
Standard is either "approved" or at least an approved "final committee draft"
(after which no "technical changes" are supposed to occur).

Both MERANT and Fujitsu have had "multiple" (semi-incompatible) upgrades to
the OO syntax that they originally introduced.  This means that they are both
CLOSER to the current draft (and which ever vendor's compiler is "most
recent" is most similar to the MOVING draft).

P.S.  I am fairly certain that Siemans as an OO compiler - and think that at
least one of the Japanese compiler vendors other than Fujitsu has one too.  I
am not positive of either of these.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - Dissimilar Implementations.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DBE227.755F6754@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38dbb53d.1351903671@news.cox-internet.com> <8bgk96$nat$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> 
> P.S.  I am fairly certain that Siemans as an OO compiler - and think that at
> least one of the Japanese compiler vendors other than Fujitsu has one too.  I
> am not positive of either of these.

In the Arranga/Coyle book (1996) they very SPECIFICALLY mention Hitachi.
As part of the '85 Standard A & C said Hitachi most closely followed the
standard with necessary features compared to IBM and Merant. Further, A
& C list the Hitachi classes and some examples. Now the only thing I've
found about Hitachi was a joint Press Release - Unisys/Hitachi - the
former was going to 'rent' the technology from the latter. I picked up
on it about last June/July - since then silence. (I may have Unisys
wrong but you know who I mean). 

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Learning OO COBOL - Dissimilar Implementations.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DBDE57.8AE3BC3C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38dbb53d.1351903671@news.cox-internet.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> I've read the comments here wondering whether OO on OS/390 is viable
> and/or recommended.  I've also been having private discussions with
> some of you here, in my onw OO COBOL quest.

Thane's just asked me another one - make it PUBLIC mate :-). And for the
FAQ I would suggest you have a section specifically for OO Books, Bill.
I've mentioned before I read OO COBOL books which are just blurbs, lots
of theory, copious diagrams, but they do bugger-all to get you started
in OO COBOL.

So - from my own knowledge - the two that have EXAMPLE source code :-

(1)

OO COBOL - Edmund C. Arranga & Frank P. Coyle - SIGS Books & Multimedia 
ISBN 1-884842-34-8.

Ed is Editor of cobolreport.com/objectz.com - Excellent book. Steps you
through the buzz-words, then finishes up with a very clear application
for a Library showing the source code in an appendix. (Pity no
accompanying diskette). Looking at the simple examples you might wonder
how they apply in the real world - put your thinking cap on and you see
what they are getting at.

(2) Elements of OO COBOL - Wilson Price 1997 - ISBN 0-9655945-0-5
(Here's why you can't find it - PUBLISHED by objectz.com) Contact them
to order a copy or suppose you could also query through cobolreport.com.

Will's book is very tutorial in style - but he steps you through the
process very neatly going from conventional(structured) into OO.
Remember it is the "WillPriceMethod" that I have used for my flat-file
handling. Plus his text also gave me 'oodles' of other neat tricks.
(Again following on from Will, Gene Webb has written a file-handler
directed at Windows APIs).

IMPORTANT - Neither book clogs the issue using GUIs - they just keep to
ANSI displays to get their points across. Until any more 'practical'
texts, as opposed to 'theoretical' - these two should be a 'must have'
for anybody who is the slightest bit curious about OO.

Why do I wax forth about these two books ? Having bumbled, stumbled my
way through NetExpress - these two books were the ones that did it for
me. (Merant does provide a lot of detail in their on-line help, but as
yet they haven't provided a 'book-style' as they have for their own
Dialog System. Nevertheless, even if compiler vendors do provide
book-style documentation, you do really still need a hard book that you
can curl up in a chair with and read at your own pace).

As I'll shortly be writing to Pete Dashwood - yes I've had a very LONG
process in learning OO - but the truth of the matter is OO - 20% - GUIs
80% ($%#@$ Windows) !!! Now as to progressing in OO, it just gets better
and better the more I delve into it. Given the time, I could learn to
'draw' and 'paint' - hey I could finish up with stuff I could sell as
Activex !

Jimmy
```

## Thread: NetExpress and Caps Lock issues — 7 msgs

### NetExpress and Caps Lock issues

- **From:** Aaron Cardon <aaron@amnutrition.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DBBB7D.E8EE06FA@amnutrition.com>`

```
I am running NetExpress 3.0 with all of the patches on a PC LAN.  The
runtime system is located on a Netware 4.11 file server and the clients
registry points to the shared runtime directory.  This configuration
seems to be working well for us, with one exception.  The caps lock acts
like it is on when it is off and visa versa.  This caps lock issue is
only seen when running cobol programs.

Has anyone experienced any similar problems?

Thank You,
Aaron Cardon
American Nutrition Inc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress and Caps Lock issues

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bna35$902$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38DBBB7D.E8EE06FA@amnutrition.com> <03135d23.6314a988@usw-ex0104-028.remarq.com> <38DC14DD.32B69F8D@amnutrition.com>`

```
Aaron (et al),

This is a configuration problem in Win9x rather than a problem in
NetExpress. I do not know exactly what causes it but I DO have a solution
for you. If you ensure that the following are in CONFIG.SYS and AUTOEXEC.BAT
the problem should disappear :

CONFIG.SYS:


 DEVICE=<install-dir>\command\display.sys con=(ega,,1)

AUTOEXEC.BAT:

 mode con codepage prepare=((nnn) <install-dir>\COMMAND\ega.cpi)
 mode con codepage select=nnn
 keyb xx,,<install-dir>\COMMAND\keyboard.sys

where nnn will be 437 if you are in the U.S, and xx will be us

I hope this helps.


Aaron Cardon <aaron@amnutrition.com> wrote in message
news:38DC14DD.32B69F8D@amnutrition.com...
> This is happening with Windows 95 and 98.  I'm not sure if the problem
occurs
> with WinNT since we don't have many NT workstations running these
programs.
>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > * Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion
Network *
> > The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
>
```

```
Gael,
You don't know how pleased our users are to finally have a solution to this
problem.  They have been putting up with this annoyance for over a year now.
```

<a id="t-404c0d6eba"></a>
## Thread: Serial POS Printer — 6 msgs

### Serial POS Printer

- **From:** Bob7536@aol.com
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bfl99$efo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi Folks,
I have an NCR 7193 thermal receipt serial printer. Let
me
give you a little background. We started with a DOS product and I would
handle the receipt printer by setting the following:
SELECT RECEIPT-PRINTER ASSIGN TO "LPT1:"
ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
FILE STATUS IS RECEIPT-PRINTER-FILE-STATUS.
I would then in the AUTOEXEC.BAT uses the MODE command to redirect the
serial
port to the printer port (ie. MODE LPT1:=COM1: ).
This worked great I could look at the RECEIPT-PRINTER-FILE-STATUS to
determine if the printer was "on" or "off" line.
I then converted the code to the Win9x world and found the redirection
would
not work so I made the following change:
SELECT RECEIPT-PRINTER ASSIGN TO "COM1:"
ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
FILE STATUS IS RECEIPT-PRINTER-FILE-STATUS.
This seemed to work fine, except for one thing if the printer was off-
line (
paper out or door open ) the RECEIPT-PRINTER-FILE-STATUS was still
coming
back as okay.
I have tried using the COBOL byte streaming to query the printer about
its
status but this seems to slow the printer down alot. Any thoughts ?
Thanks,
Bob Hennessey


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```

    I no longer have the original message.  I am assuming that you are
trying
to use a DOS program in a windows command prompt.  As near as I can tell,
DOS programs have NO access to windows serial printers.  You have to run the
various mode commands in the one, lone, command prompt accessing the
serial device.

    Third party serial drivers could change the rules I suppose.

    Also, MF dos runtime 3.4 seems to be unable to access
a serial port AT ALL on NT.

    Ironically, the best luck I ever had with a serial printer was
when I put the printer on a Novell server.  I called it LPT3 from
my program.
```

```
the pc.
```

## Thread: Need Some COBOL Understanding — 13 msgs

### Need Some COBOL Understanding

- **From:** "Ted Barry" <tbarry@centralsan.dst.ca.us>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38db9e32$0$22807@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
Anyone,

I'm not a COBOL programmer and need to understand some terminology.  I've
got a record layout and am having some difficulty with the packed field
types.  Can someone explain the meaning of the following? :

    9(4)V9
    9(3)V9(3)

TIA,
  Ted
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Some COBOL Understanding

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LtNC4.7314$0o4.46865@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<38db9e32$0$22807@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
In article <38db9e32$0$22807@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>,
Ted Barry <tbarry@centralsan.dst.ca.us> wrote:
>Anyone,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>    9(3)V9(3)

Those are display numeric fields, of different lengths, with implied
decimal positions, of different lengths.

Might it be asked what the situation is which requires such
'understanding'?

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need Some COBOL Understanding

- **From:** "Ted Barry" <tbarry@centralsan.dst.ca.us>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38dbaa2c$0$22811@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
As I stated before, I'm not a COBOL programmer and I believe these to be
COBOL terms.  In RPG I would refer to a length of fifteen and five decimal
positions as 15,5 .  What is does the nine in 9() or the "V" nine in V9()
represent?

<docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
news:LtNC4.7314$0o4.46865@iad-read.news.verio.net...

…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need Some COBOL Understanding

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000324184750.03333.00001570@nso-cj.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38dbaa2c$0$22811@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
In article <38dbaa2c$0$22811@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>, "Ted Barry"
<tbarry@centralsan.dst.ca.us> writes:

>
>As I stated before, I'm not a COBOL programmer and I believe these to be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Your RPG representation of a 15-digit number with 5-digits after the decimal
 (15,5) would be equivalent to PIC 9(10)v9(5).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need Some COBOL Understanding

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bh0rq$i68$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38dbaa2c$0$22811@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net> <20000324184750.03333.00001570@nso-cj.aol.com>`

```
Just because part of the original question seems to have gotten lost.  The
original post EXPLICITLY talked about "packed" number.  I am assuming that
this it means that the COBOL source code had USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL (or COMP-3
on many implementations).

This will very definitely impact how you try and "interpret" the bits (hex
values) in any field with this definition.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need Some COBOL Understanding

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<af7odskgge3am0ggfd17vmm6cmd4f07ong@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<38db9e32$0$22807@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net> <LtNC4.7314$0o4.46865@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38dbaa2c$0$22811@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
"Ted Barry" <tbarry@centralsan.dst.ca.us> wrote:

>As I stated before, I'm not a COBOL programmer and I believe these to be
>COBOL terms.  In RPG I would refer to a length of fifteen and five decimal
>positions as 15,5 .  What is does the nine in 9() or the "V" nine in V9()
>represent?

the v means implied decimal point.
packed decimal means that if there is an s, the last character in the
series, if the number is negative starts at the letter A instead of
the number 0.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Some COBOL Understanding

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DBABF8.ED5753D5@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38db9e32$0$22807@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
Ted Barry wrote:
> 
> Anyone,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>     9(3)V9(3)

The above is about all numeric types, not just packed.  Pic 9(4) has 4
numeric digits, Pic 9(3)V9(3) has 6 numeric digits.  The V is a place
holder telling the compiler where to assume a decimal.

So if your fields contained    1234  and 123456, the computer would
treat them as 1234 and 123.456;

There are some other issues in how your system does signs (even with
unsigned numbers), and where it puts slack characters.  If you want help
with these let us know your operating system.  (Or whether the file was
created by PL/I)  Actually, technical questions should always list OS,
compiler, etc., for best responses.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Some COBOL Understanding

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gmQC4.2373$QJ3.461219@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<38db9e32$0$22807@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
You can download a free tutorial on COBOL datatypes at
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html. Get file COBDATA.ZIP.

I don't think the tutorial says, though, that "by default, USAGE IS DISPLAY
is assumed". (Yeah, it should say that).
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Some COBOL Understanding

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8nRC4.749$Id7.15095@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<38db9e32$0$22807@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
1. The field consists of 5 digits, unsigned, with 1 implied decimal
place ("1234" = 123.4)
2. The field consists of 6 digits, unsigned, with three implied
decimal places ("123456" = 123.456).

There are scores of these field definitions in COBOL (signed, location
of sign, scaling, editing (blank filling, floating currency symbol,
check-protecting, imbedding (12-34-56), symbol substitution, and
others). And that's just for numbers. Alphanumerics are left for the
advanced course.

Ted Barry <tbarry@centralsan.dst.ca.us> wrote in message
news:38db9e32$0$22807@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net...
> Anyone,
>
> I'm not a COBOL programmer and need to understand some terminology.
I've
> got a record layout and am having some difficulty with the packed
field
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Some COBOL Understanding

- **From:** chankulunkwenda@aol.com (Chankulunkwenda)
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000324180648.21918.00003536@ng-cd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38db9e32$0$22807@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net>`

```
The following is my view of the syntax in question.

What you have is a numeric field edited as follows.

9(4)V9 = 9999.9

9(3)V9 = 999.999

the "v" is an assumed decimal point and all digits after the "v" are the number
of decimals available.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need Some COBOL Understanding

- **From:** "William C. Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38dd1ee1_2@nebula.superior.net>`
- **References:** `<38db9e32$0$22807@wodc7nh0.news.uu.net> <20000324180648.21918.00003536@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```

Chankulunkwenda <chankulunkwenda@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000324180648.21918.00003536@ng-cd1.aol.com...
> The following is my view of the syntax in question.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 9(3)V9 = 999.999      <==== this was 9(3)V9(3) in original post, answer is
correct for orig.
>
> the "v" is an assumed decimal point and all digits after the "v" are the
number
> of decimals available.

Finally someone got the answer right. (First example has 5 digits, not 4 as
in previous "answer.") Unfortunately, the "question" (#2) was typed wrong
above.

To try to reiterate (hopefully correctly):
   PIC 9(4)V9      is the same as PIC 9999V9, which is the same as PIC
9(4)V9(1)
         and holds five digits, assumed to be nnnn.n .
   PIC 9(3)V9(3)  is the same as PIC 999V999, PIC 9(3)V999, or PIC 999V9(3)
         and holds six digits, assumed to be nnn.nnn .
   The implied decimal point takes up no space.

But everyone knows that!
```

#### ↳ Future Cobol and colors

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bg6ke$bmg$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<8bg0fu$1gg$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I was browsing through draft proposal just to get some more insight
yesterday, and there was one thing that caught me by surprise;

Colors. It appears to me that the draft is staying with 8 colors (0..7). Is
that true?
Why haven't the committee considered RGB colors?

Just curious.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Future Cobol and colors

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bgj5f$gvg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8bg0fu$1gg$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8bg6ke$bmg$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
The colors are those that were "common" in X/Open environments (whence comes
the entire Accept/Display facility).  I think that it *is* possible to have
extensions for other colors.  HOWEVER, you might want to send a note to J4
suggesting that the values of 0...7 be changed to the (now common - I think)
0...255 for RGB.  If you have a "reference" that you could point to for those
values, I think it would probably be useful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future Cobol and colors

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bh1ec$gq1$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<8bg0fu$1gg$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8bg6ke$bmg$1@news.cerf.net> <8bgj5f$gvg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:8bgj5f$gvg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...
> The colors are those that were "common" in X/Open environments (whence
comes
> the entire Accept/Display facility).  I think that it *is* possible to
have
> extensions for other colors.

Well, RGB isn't that easy. At least from the perspective of Windows
programming, it is a mix of three bytes;
    RedByte, GreenByte, BlueByte

Which each may have a value between 0 and 255. For the Windows API, this
implies shades of the three colors, that is, if all are set to 0, the result
is black, are all set to 255, the result is white.

For the Windows API, there is a RGB macro to call, for instance;
    myRGBColor = RGB(255, 128, 144);
which expands into:
    myRGBColor =
((((BYTE)(r)|((WORD)((BYTE)(g))<<8))|(((DWORD)(BYTE)(b))<<16)))

The result of the macro is a DWORD value which is a 32-bit unsigned integer.

Obviously, this won't fit in any storage intended to hold a value ranging
from 0 to 7.

> HOWEVER, you might want to send a note to J4
> suggesting that the values of 0...7 be changed to the (now common - I
think)
> 0...255 for RGB.  If you have a "reference" that you could point to for
those
> values, I think it would probably be useful.

Well, we might end up with another delay then. May be we should wait for the
next draft.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: SP2 runtime requirements?

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-24T00:00:00

```
I see this all the time when I use the Template.  It puts the EXPLICIT
Path to your panel file in the template.  (I've modified mine not to
do that now!).  Anyway, check that in your source you don't have the
full path to the .pan file specified in the Open Panel call.  If
that's not it, sometimes I see this when I forget to include fonts.sp2
with the runtime.
```

#### ↳ Re: learning tables

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bk79u$4ook$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<38DD91CC.41A03912@sorden.com>`

```

500-EntireName needs to be subscripted.  Probably by 300-SS?
```

#### ↳ Re: learning tables

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A9vD4.1686$Id7.37509@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<38DD91CC.41A03912@sorden.com>`

```
Look at it this way: You defined storage for 100 different
"500-EntireName" entries. Your STRING statement needs to know WHICH of
the 100 possible gets the result of the string operation. Once you
code "dataname OCCURS.." in Working Storage, every occurrence of
"dataname" in the program (including the STRING verb) must be followed
by an index to particularize which entry you mean.

So,
           String   400-LastName delimited by space
                     ', ' delimited by size
                     400-FirstName delimited by space
                     into 500-EntireName (300-SS)

Of course your code won't work if the last name has an imbedded space
(Van Zandt) or a trailing modifier (Jr., M.D., Ph.D.) let alone trying
to put the name back the way it was. But it's close enough for
government work (I assume you are attending a public school).


Sorden <steve@sorden.com> wrote in message
news:38DD91CC.41A03912@sorden.com...

tell

assumed.
```

```
:>           05  Filler          Pic XX.
:>           05  400-Hours       Pic 99V99.
:>           05  400-Rate        Pic 99V99.

:>       01  500-Payroll-table.

Anything below is an array reference.

:>           05  500-Element     Occurs 100 Times.
:>               10  500-EntireName  Pic X(29).
:>               10  Filler          Pic XX.
:>               10  500-EmpCode     Pic X(5).
:>               10  Filler          Pic XX.
:>               10  500-DeptCode    Pic 99.

:>       B100-Initial.

:>                    Output 200-Payroll-Out.
:>           Perform  C100-Read.
:>           Move     Zero to 300-SS  300-Limit.

:>       B200-Load-Table.
:>           Add      1 to 300-SS  300-Limit.
:>           Move     400-EmpCode to 500-Empcode (300-SS).
:>           Move     400-DeptCode to 500-DeptCode (300-SS).
:>           Move     400-Hours to 500-Hours (300-SS).

                      not an array reference

                      not an array reference

                      not an array reference

:>                    into 500-EntireName.

                      DING. DING. DING.
```

## Thread: Learning OO COBOL — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re : Learning OO COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DC4080.C5EB116D@home.com>`

```
Thane's at it again :-)  :-

> Been doing my homework.

DD will LUVVVV you ;-)

>Can you take a quick gander in chapter 2 of your Wilson Price OO 
>COBOL book at the section titled: MicroFocus and Ansi 
>Comparison and tell me what you see there as far as Properties, 
>Object and our other apparent differences?  Gimme a summary?

Dead easy - not too much to type :-

--------------------------------------------------------------------
As you learned earlier in this chapter, Micro Focus and ANSI use
different approaches to relating class names to external files.

Micro Focus :
	Environment Division.		*> I don't use either of these.
	Object Section.			*> Am I missing something ?
	Class-Control.
		 class-name is calls "external-name"

ANSI Standard :
	Environment Division.
	Repository.
		 class-name is calls "external-name"

ANSI specifies that a class's object data is defined under the
Working-Storage Section of the Object definition. This is permissible
with Micro Focus but only if the following compiler directive is
included prior to the first statement in the program.

	$set ooctrll(+w)

Unfortunately, this directive appears to malfunction  at times, so it is
not used in this book.......

----------------------------------------------------------------------
 Personally :-

1. I stick with M/F default directives - so no future surprises.
   Similarly I don't use any $set.....

2. Unlike a structured, my global Working-Storage is absolutely minimal
and consists primarily of copyfiles

3. I freely inter-mix Working-Storage Section and Local-Storage section
in methods :-

	*>--------------------
	method-id "method-1".
	*>--------------------
	Local-storage section.
	78 ls-value1 			value 12.
	01 ls-value2			pic 9(03)v9(02) comp-3.
	Linkage section.
	01 lnk-ReceivingValue		pic 9(02)v9(01) comp-3.
	01 lnk-Result			pic 9(03)v9(02) comp-3.
	Procedure Division using lnk-ReceivingValue
			   returning lnk-Result.
           compute ls-value2 rounded = 
		lnk-Receiving-Value * ls-value1
	   move ls-value2 to lnk-Result

	OR

	*>---------------------
	method-id. "method-2".
	*>----------------------
	Working-Storage section.
	01 ws-MessageTitle pic x(22) value "Inspection Report - ".
	Local-Storage section.
	01 ls-Title2		pic x(xx).
	01 ls-Title3		pic x(xx).
	01 ls-FinalTitle	pic x(xx).
	Linkage section.
	01 lnk-SecondTitle	pic x(xx).
	01 lnk-DateTitle	pic x(xx).
	Procedure Division using lnk-SecondTitle lnk-DateTitle.
	  move lnk-SecondTitle to ls-Title2
	  move lnk-DateTitle   to ls-Title3
	  string ws-MessageTitle delimited by size
		 ls-Title2 delimited by 2spaces
		 ls-Title3 delimited by 1space 
		 into ls-FinalTitle
	  End-string
	 
	  invoke os-Prinfile "print-title" using ls-FinalTitle
	  etc.....
----------------------------------------------------------------------

Whoops !- nearly missed it because you pointed me at Chapter 2. Then
thought I'll check his index before I finish. In his Chapter 8 he talks
about working with objects, in-line invocation :: (I think Ken
referenced this somewhere to do with C++ ????) and Properties - but he
is generalizing about OO - not specifically Merant. I hope he is -
otherwise I missed it !

The nearest I find is Vocabularies a Merant technique that let's you
write code in 'shorthand' or paraphrase the syntax, if you like - and
that makes reference to PUT and FETCH - close to GET and SET - no cigar
????

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Re : Learning OO COBOL

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DD5BF0.6C31AE8@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38DC4080.C5EB116D@home.com>`

```
> 
> In his Chapter 8 he talks about working with objects, in-line
> invocation :: (I think Ken referenced this somewhere to do with C++
> ????) and Properties - but he is generalizing about OO - not
> specifically Merant. I hope he is - otherwise I missed it !

1.  Inline (as defined in C++) is a method to do very small pieces of
code in the interface copybook so that you do not have overhead.  The
compiler put the code into your program much like a macro.  Macros
have some funny features though because they are text expansion, not
programming per se.

bad example:

you set method is:
...
	set index-property up by 1.
...

This is one line of code, doing a call is horribly inefficient, put
the code inline and there is no difference.

The other thing is that compilers typically switch off inline when
debugging to make it easier to debug (inlines confuse the hell out of
a trace).

2.  I can only generalize, I am working on a backwards OO compiler on
MVS :-}  and I do not have a recent PC compiler.

I apologize for being C++ oriented, I hope I add value to the
discussions despite my obvious ignorance of cobol specifics.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Re : Learning OO COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bmte9$43g$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38DC4080.C5EB116D@home.com> <38DD5BF0.6C31AE8@zip.com.au>`

```
In the draft COBOL Standard the term "inline object invocation" is used to
distinguish between an explicit INVOKE statement (out-of-line) with a
reference to the returned value from an object reference.  Therefore, you can
have,

Out-of-line -
  Invoke Whatever "meth1" Using Parm-1
        returning temp-field
  Move temp-field to real-output.

  versus
InLine

Move Whatever::"meth1" Using Parm1 to Real-Output

(the "::" - double colons - are the "inline invocation operator")
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

> In the draft COBOL Standard the term "inline object invocation" is used to
```

## Thread: Connecting PC with API — 2 msgs

### Connecting PC with API

- **From:** "Roberto Bongini" <futura.softw@mclink.it>
- **Date:** 2000-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bi6fr$887$1@news.mclink.it>`

```
Hi,
I must develop a program that connect 2 PC and copy some file.
I have developed already the cbl but in running mode my program display a
blank screen in DOS mode over the my DialogSystem Screen because first I
launch a .BAT file with inside the command NET USE etc.. > NUL and then I
use CBL_COPY_FILE.

Anybody know an API function to substitute the NET command?

P.S.
The PC must stay indipendent and the connection is only for the copy.

Thanks
Roberto
```

#### ↳ Re: Connecting PC with API

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DD5768.38C8D732@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8bi6fr$887$1@news.mclink.it>`

```
Roberto Bongini wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Roberto

If you are using NT (probably 200 and 95 as well) and a recent
compiler you can directly use the actual reference.

for example:

net use \\server\data  d:
copy fred d:\somewhere\fred

can be replaced by

copy fred \\server\data\somewhere\fred

In your cobol program you should be able to do exactly the same thing.
The user must have the same userid and password on 'server' or it will
not work very well, file open tends to fail.

For the API reference I pulled this out of the microsloth reference:
....

The WNetAddConnection2 function makes a connection to a network
resource. The function can redirect a local device to the network
resource.

The WNetAddConnection2 function supersedes the WNetAddConnection
function. If you pass a handle to a window that the provider of
network resources can use as an owner window for dialog boxes, use the
WNetAddConnection3 function.

DWORD WNetAddConnection2(
    LPNETRESOURCE lpNetResource,	// points to structure that
					// specifies connection details
    LPCTSTR lpPassword,	// points to password string
    LPCTSTR lpUsername,	// points to user name string
    DWORD dwFlags 	// set of bit flags that specify connection
			//   options
   );	

..... 

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: Adding new Clients (Fujistu Cobol)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DE59F9.FFEF1438@home.com>`

```


John Collins wrote:
> 
> Greetings All,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> collinsj@xtra.co.nz.

John,

Here's a plug for the 'lad's book' - get yourself a copy of Thane
Hubbell "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours". His Part II covers the
various file types. I'm sure you must have it in your bookshops by now -
if not try Amazon.com , Barnes & Noble, or if you want to keep it to the
'Blitish Empire' you can always try Chapters.ca up here in Canuck-land.

As you are learning I would suggest using surnames as your PrimeKey is a
bit tricky. (It's a topic all unto itself). Start off by allocating
account numbers, say C1 = Collins, J, C2 = Cartwright, C3 = Collins,
M.J. etc.. - not alphabetical - but if you want those names in
alphabetical you do it with an "Alternate Key with duplicates" - because
you could have two or more Collins, J. (Like here in Calgary for the
Chinese surname 'Lee' there are at least five pages in the directory !)

So you create records using a Primekey - C1, C2. C3 and print them out
alphabetically using the Alternate Key - Adams, B, Adams B.G, Adams H
etc..

pseudo code :-

	Open file i-o (Random - ie. ISAM Indexed Sequential Access Mode
	Enter A/c # - read PrimeKey
		      if found display record
			 if I change this record
			    rewrite this record
		      else if notfound enter new data
			      write this new record

And you need that Alternate Key with duplicates otherwise on a write or
even rewrite (where you might have corrected the name) - you will go
belly-up without the 'with duplicates' check.

Above is very brief - but coupled with Thane's book - or any other good
COBOL text - it should put you on the right track.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Adding new Clients (Fujistu Cobol)

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UZuD4.1685$Id7.37384@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<sdkD4.824$fgT1.6226058@news.xtra.co.nz>`

```
The file has to be ORGANIZATION INDEXED right?

So, if you OPEN EXTEND you are implicitly adding higher keys. An
attempt to add a record smaller than the largest existing key yields
an 'invalid key' condition.

If ACCESS RANDOM you can't open the file as EXTEND (EXTEND is reserved
for SEQUENTIAL access).

What to do?

Answer: OPEN I-O.

Typical sequences for an ISAM (Indexed Sequential Access Method) are:

1. Unsorted input
(ACCESS I-O)
OPEN OUTPUT   (this creates the file)
CLOSE
OPEN I-O
WRITE records
CLOSE FILE

2. Sorted input
(ACCESS SEQUENTIAL)
OPEN OUTPUT (creates file)
WRITE records (in order)
CLOSE
This file may never again be opened OUTPUT without destroying the
original file.


John Collins <COLLINSJ@xtra.co.nz> wrote in message
news:sdkD4.824$fgT1.6226058@news.xtra.co.nz...
> Greetings All,
> I am teaching myself Cobol &  I am using Fujistu Cobol 4.0.
> I am entering client codes using Sequential Access.

ascending
> order

if
> Collins is followed by Cameron ( not in ascending order) .
> If I access as Random  rather the  Sequential then I am unable to
"Open
> Extend  File-Name" but only as "Open output Name-file" a proceed
which
> deletes any earlier entries.
> Any assistance would be appreciated.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Nice to see another New Zealander using Cobol!!

I agree with Jimmy from Calgary that you should get Thane Hubbell's book. I 
started with no Cobol experience, just like you, and came across similar 
problems. My code uses Relative files extensively for compatability with an 
earlier (Softbol/Dibol) system, but that means I have to keep track of the file 
size and other matters, all stored in line 1 of each file.

Thane's book really helped. Best of all, he gives what I describe as 'real life 
examples' of code. This compares with many C/C++ books which give examples to 
explain theory only. eg classes of dog, cat etc inheriting properties from the 
class animal. Pretty pointless really. The nearest I've seen yet to practical 
examples was one book which gave a class of vehicle, and the sub-classes were 
car, truck, trailer etc. Could prehaps be used in a vehicle licencing 
situation? Or for vehicle fleet maintenance? But nothing further was given to 
show how this could be accomplished, so one could not see how the alleged 
benefits work in practice.

David Stringer
Christchurch, NZ
```

```
Gunnar Opheim <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no> wrote in message
news:01bf982d$ce100590$0100007f@vaagshaugen...
> Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net> wrote in article
> <38df5fde$1$tfs$mr2ice@news>...
> > In <StxD4.603$4T2.280@news4.mia>, on 26 Mar 2000 at 18:51, "James
King"
> > <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> said:
.
> >
> > Not sure what you mean by "execute TSO".  TSO is a sub-system and
a
> > program can't execute TSO any more than it can execute CICS or
VTAM.
>
> Sure.  He probably meant executing IKJEFT01.  The sites I know use
it in
> production environments.  Why shouldn't they?  Security is taken
care of by
> RACF (or alike).
>
There are three flavors of IKJEFT01:
IKJEFT01 - intercepts all abends - gives a return code of 12
IKJEFT1A - intercepts user abends, system abends are allowed.
IKJEFT1B - allows user and system abends.

Most shops running DB2 run their batch programs through TSO.
```

<a id="t-8b7353ce2e"></a>
## Thread: Integrated systems — 15 msgs

### Integrated systems

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38DFBCFB.292FE232@cusys.edu>`

```
It seems that most of our biggest and most expensive problems in our
industry are when big, integrated systems fail.  Since everybody KNOWS
that big, integrated systems are good, we spend lots of effort
developing better ways of making it more likely that those systems won't
continue to fail the way they do.

I don't see much talk about analyzing how to eliminate dependencies, to
analyze risks in having such dependencies, or even to isolating various
systems so that a failure in one is not a show stopper.

Data marts are useful here.  If you need data from disparate systems,
these can be used as the go around.  But this cannot do everything.  If
we want to make sure a student doesn't get his diploma or refund until
we check each campus to see if he has any outstanding problems, we need
to have some way for different campus's to talk to each other in
transaction time.  So businesses demand immediate connectivity, and we
give them constant program dependencies.  Which means constant bug
dependencies.

OO seems designed to make these dependencies less dangerous.  But
somewhere in our analysis we should take time to decide which
dependencies can be avoided, which dependencies can be isolated (TCP/IP
like or via data marts), and which we just have to live with.  

Our psychology tends to be - we can solve these problems, let's be
direct and allow the dependencies.  As long as there aren't bugs (not in
MY design), it should work fine.
```

#### ↳ Re: Integrated systems

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E0B93B.E2B3CC57@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<38DFBCFB.292FE232@cusys.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I don't see much talk about analyzing how to eliminate dependencies, to
> analyze risks in having such dependencies, or even to isolating various
> systems so that a failure in one is not a show stopper.
> 
Maybe I'm a masochist, but I like the extreme Programming method.
1. TEST, before, during and after you code.
2. DONT over design or program, DONT solve tomorrow's problem today,
   because tomorrow for this application may never come.
3. Concentrate on delivering the contracted outcomes for the user.
4. Put out a thousand releases. Some of a working system maybe more useful 
   to a user than NONE of a total system.
5. Refractor. This means find common code and put it into subroutines, or
   for OO, create a superclass to contain the common methods.
6. Finally, let the design and implementation evolve over time.
   It worked for this universe!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Integrated systems

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38e0e7e3.1692065184@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<38DFBCFB.292FE232@cusys.edu> <38E0B93B.E2B3CC57@iinet.net.au>`

```
On Tue, 28 Mar 2000 21:52:59 +0800, Joseph Katnic
<josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:

>
>Howard Brazee wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>   It worked for this universe!

My personal conflict with #6 not withstanding, I can't agree.  You see
I have seen many many times when a particular necessary enhancement
leads to the necessity to totally redesign the code.  If it had been
designed to be extensible from the first, this would not occur.  That
means Planning.  The above implies that the planning for the future is
not necessary - we'll change as we need to.  I disagree.  Planning is
very important.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Integrated systems

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E0EFF5.DA12927D@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38DFBCFB.292FE232@cusys.edu> <38E0B93B.E2B3CC57@iinet.net.au> <38e0e7e3.1692065184@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> >6. Finally, let the design and implementation evolve over time.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> very important.

I was looking at a problem today.  The system of using dictionary
messages was working with some prefixes on our test system but not on
our production system.  Talking with the programmer who is solving the
problem we were discussing the "advantage" of having these error message
numbers in our code instead of hard coded messages.  We realize that
codes can be changed when we want to make a global change.  But that has
never happened in our shop.  Instead, performance has taken a hit when
it has to do dictionary lookups.  Maintenance takes a big hit when we
try to look up the message in the source code (there are some hard coded
messages), and then in the dictionary.

Error message codes were flexible planning for a future which didn't
come.  And they came with a cost which did come.


Sometimes planning should be:  "If we Keep It Simple Stupid, we can
afford to rewrite it or replace it knowing all of the consequences".  We
all know how easy it is to write a new small program as opposed to
maintaining a huge program which has been modified 30 times over the
last 20 years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Integrated systems

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E202CA.E2567C6F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38DFBCFB.292FE232@cusys.edu> <38E0B93B.E2B3CC57@iinet.net.au> <38e0e7e3.1692065184@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 28 Mar 2000 21:52:59 +0800, Joseph Katnic
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> disagree.  Planning is very important.

6.  The XP process starts with CRC cards.  The design is complete when
you begin but just very light weight.  The finer detail is worked out
with the user that must be on the spot in an XP team.

Extensibility argument:

a.  Because the system has such a heavy duty testing regime you can
safely totally reengineer the system with a guarantee that it will
work correctly in production.  The major cost of redesign is the
testing.

b.  The regular rework of the smaller details to make the system more
usable means that you should not have to make such drastic
modifications to the whole infrastructure.  It is slowly evolved to
the new infrastructure and methods that would not have occurred to you
at the outset just appear using Darwin's theory of evolution.

c.  You often make a mistake about what is going to need it.  As
outlined the flexible error messages that never get changed.  I
personally wrote a totally parameter driven formatting engine.  It was
never changed in it's entire life which terminated recently because
the upstream system was not up to scratch.  That exercise cost at
least $30K more to build and it was wasted.  Wait until you need it
and then build it.

d.  We often only know about reuse after we have developed the
solution a few times. I have a report pagination routine now, I have
literally written 100's of reports before I designed this.  If I had
refactored after the first 100 I would have saved a lot of time in the
long run.

e.  There is no firm rule that says you cannot design a better
solution in XP.  You must convince your partner and this stops some of
those side trips that programmers love to take.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

```
I wont admit to a 100 shots Ken :-) But it starts to become apparent, a
bell rings saying "I've been here before" and then you wise up with
structured sub-routines or reusable OO methods. I use M/F's PC_PRINT
routines - handles printers, font style and size, lines left to print
etc... More than adequate for the printing I do. They provide numerous
routines plus a whole daffy of printer error codes. Typically in
structured you would :-

	call PC_PRINT ....... using .....
		returning ErrorCode

I've turned the routines, plus error-codes, (and even added my own
methods "write-and-newline", "write-and-pause"), into Class Printfile
invoking from my print programs as necessary.(This is what poor Jose had
trouble with in VISOC in Portugal :-))

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Integrated systems

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

- **References:** `<38DFBCFB.292FE232@cusys.edu> <38E0B93B.E2B3CC57@iinet.net.au> <38e0e7e3.1692065184@news.cox-internet.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 28 Mar 2000 21:52:59 +0800, Joseph Katnic
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> very important.

Planning is very important indeed, whether structured or OO - but would
suggest even more important in OO. That OO planning comes right up front
at the blank drawing board stage - get the pieces assembled (three-tier
systems), into an overall design approach, looking for common elements
in file handling, file editing, screen displays, (and aiming for finite
is useless - the world just keeps changing), then you have a good chance
of using OO flexibility, to adapt more rapidly to change.

> >5. Refractor. This means find common code and put it into subroutines, or
> >   for OO, create a superclass to contain the common methods.

Uhh ! Uhh ! Don't overburden one class/program with all the common
performs/methods you deem necessary - the domino effect will make this
fall apart. (I have to admit I already see this in one of my files - I
do all the DB interface in one class - but it would be clearer and less
complex (to comprehend), if split into several classes or sub-classed). 

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> > >5. Refractor. This means find common code and put it into
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> performs/methods you deem necessary - the domino effect will make

This is not what he said.  Take out the common functionality of two or
```
