- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8buohg$drp$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <exFNYVim$GA.156@cpmsnbbsa05> <WRHE4.10617$Y4.179296@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8c0d1i$ap7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <kwSE4.11533$Y4.208763@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8c11fo$hs0$1@news.igs.net> <9NWE4.12136$Y4.226484@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
I read your message.
You're tying to do C in COBOL.
Use the OCCURS DEPENDING ON (ODO) option to Dynamically size your
table at run time.
You do NOT HAVE TO clear the table after each use, the next group will
overlay the previous data.
The ODO can be changed repeatedly, resizing as it goes along.

Keep in mind the Table size max in your environment is only 16M.

Read all of your Smiths into the table.
Process smith.
Set your index to 1.
Read all your Jones into the table.
process Jones
Set your index to 1.
Read all your ???? into the table.
process ????

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c2atn$b9p$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8buohg$drp$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <exFNYVim$GA.156@cpmsnbbsa05> <WRHE4.10617$Y4.179296@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8c0d1i$ap7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <kwSE4.11533$Y4.208763@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8c11fo$hs0$1@news.igs.net> <9NWE4.12136$Y4.226484@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```

Cosmic Knight said:

<everything snipped>

Further to my last post.  I CAN see an advantage to dynamically allocating
your array.  It gains you the advantage of not needing to know the array
size needed for the largest customer.

Having said that, however, I cannot see any advantage to then releasing the
array, and re-allocating it for the next customer. It is not only more
efficient to reuse the same array, but there is far less potential for
technical screw ups like memory creep, and a fair amount of overhead.

Even if I were writing that code in a language that used dynamic allocation
constantly, it is not the method I would choose to code a function like
that.  You are coding, from what I read in your example, a program that is
essentially batch.  It is going to be over and done with in a very short
period of time, and all memory is going to be re-allocated to the pool as
soon as the program ends.
It would be far more efficient to simply add to the array size until it
matches the number of records for the largest customer, never de-allocating
anything until the end of the program.

There are only four methods:

    method-one.
        perform each-customer. (x times)

    one-customer.
        perform allocate-for-one. (allocating just enough)
        perform one-customer.
        perform release-for-one.

This seems to be what you want to do, and is the least efficient and most
error prone of the four approaches.
**************************************
    method-two.
        perform allocate-for one. (allocating for largest)
        perform each-customer. (x times re-using)
        perform release-for-one.
**************************************
    method-three.
        perform each-customer.  (x times using statically defined memory).
**************************************
    method-four.
        perform each-customer. ( x times, using *different* static memory
for each)
**************************************

You seem to be saying that you have only method four, yet you repeat in your
example (twice), that once you are finished with one customer, you go on to
the next. I think you have a mind block because you are thinking in another
language.

Certainly method four is dumb, and not a good replacement for method one,
the one you appear to want.  However, method three works without *any*
dynamic storage, and uses no more memory than any of the other methods.
(Well, perhaps more than method one as you do not know the largest and you
have to allocate some slack space. But it does not need to hold all
customers, only the largest).
```

What exactly is being done with the 'order set' that you have to bounce around

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c155q$ee5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8buohg$drp$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <exFNYVim$GA.156@cpmsnbbsa05> <WRHE4.10617$Y4.179296@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8c0d1i$ap7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <kwSE4.11533$Y4.208763@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
The point is that (from your own definition), you do NOT need all 200,000
records in storage at one time.  What you need to do is create a SINGLE table
that can hold all the records for a single person.  Such a table (even with
2000 byte records) will *not* be too large to fit in an OS/390 environment.

Once you have filled and used the table for one person's records, you then
start over at the beginning of the table and use it for the next person's
records.

This DOES assume that your input file is already sorted in "person" order.
If it isn't (and the file is not "keyed" on the persons id), then you need to
sort it first.  This can be done with either an internal COBOL sort - or an
external sort (Syncsort, DFSort, etc).
```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

OK, If you REALLY want to load the data into memory and you have an IBM OS/390 system
try the following:

There is a manual in the OS/390 set called "High Level Language API's" or some such name.
In it you will find described how to Initialize, load and use a HyperSpace using Data
Windowing Services. Use one of these and you will have access to 16 Terabytes of address
space.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KOUE4.15756$0o4.89873@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <WRHE4.10617$Y4.179296@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8c0d1i$ap7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <kwSE4.11533$Y4.208763@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
In article <kwSE4.11533$Y4.208763@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>,
Cosmic Knight <cosmicknight> wrote:
>The amount of input data will vary wildly, but as an example, let's say
>there are 200,000 records in a file.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>So, to suck this whole file into an array would require 400,000,000 bytes.

That it would... were you to need all 2,000 characters of each and every
200,000 recs to be available immediately and unpredictably.

This is, I believe, the primary advantage of arrays (or 'tables', as some
folks call 'em) - the fact that they allow data to be repeatedly used when
they are needed immediately and unpredictably.  Since you incur
data-center chargebacks based, in part, on CPU use you want to keep your
core down as small and as tight as possible.

Perhaps you might go on a bit more about the application involved; folks
who are used to being limited by (and, when necessary, breaking) a
table-size limit of 32K might be able to offer you a bit of advice.

For example... once, long ago, I had to read a master-file which contained
another file's alternate key... and I found it to be faster to, as part of
housekeeping, build a table of alternate and primary keys so that when a
master-file rec was read the alternate key would be looked up in the table
and the file would be read on primary key; this sped up processing
appreciably.

Similarly... unusual thinking might be applied to your present difficulty
were more known about it.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OdIE4.15212$QJ3.2365333@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8buohg$drp$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <exFNYVim$GA.156@cpmsnbbsa05> <WRHE4.10617$Y4.179296@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
Cosmic Knight >removethis <<@home.com> wrote in message ...
>The problem is, if I use INITIALIZE or reset the subscript to 0, I haven't
>really destroyed the array.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Not in any current release of COBOL, assuming that the table (array) is
created with an OCCURS clause in working-storage.

Next release of COBOL will let you do dynamic memory
allocation/deallocation; when you combine that with pointers, you could do
what you are asking.

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bvtvg$4c5$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8buohg$drp$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <exFNYVim$GA.156@cpmsnbbsa05> <WRHE4.10617$Y4.179296@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <OdIE4.15212$QJ3.2365333@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:OdIE4.15212$QJ3.2365333@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
> Next release of COBOL will let you do dynamic memory
> allocation/deallocation; when you combine that with pointers, you could do
> what you are asking.

You might want to state that the next release you are talking of here is the
next standard, which does not mean that as stroke by a lightening all Cobol
vendors support it.

However, as William Klein has pointed out several times, many vendors
already support dynamic memory, and if this guy is using one of these, he
will be all set to start doing a linked list right away.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** aflinsch <avflinsch@att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E3636B.5DF9C1F0@att.net>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8buohg$drp$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <exFNYVim$GA.156@cpmsnbbsa05> <WRHE4.10617$Y4.179296@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
"Cosmic Knight >removethis<" wrote:
> 
> The problem is, if I use INITIALIZE or reset the subscript to 0, I haven't
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Not directly, but sort of possible in a roundabout kind of way. 
Note that different OSen may handle this in very different ways.

1 - Move your array processing to a sub program. You will need to code
all
    of the access to the array in this new module, by passing
parameters to it.

2 - Dynamically call the submodule from the main program.

3 - CANCEL the submodule from the calling module.

The key is to dynamically call the module, then cancel it when done.

The main problem is that the array will have to be defined as OCCURS
xx 
or OCCURS xx DEPENDING. Either way, most (if not al) compiliers will
allocate
the maximum amount in working storage.
```

#### ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

- **From:** Charles Hammond <ceh1@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
Why not just describe the flat file with a record key and use i/o, random
or dynamic file access to search the file like a database.  On the
mainframe we often read various files into a temporary file and sort and
search that file and just overwrite that area later.   Tables are nice,
but you have trouble putting a file with 100,000 records in a table.

Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program

#### ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c08oo$frr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
Here is how I would do it:
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. SLUTION.
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
FILE-CONTROL.
SELECT INPUT-FILE ASSIGN TO UT-FILEIN.
DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.
FD INPUT-FILE
LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
DATA RECORDS ARE INPUT-REC.
01 INPUT-REC.
05 IR-MAJ-KEY.
10 IR-NAME PIC X(14).
05 IR-ORDER-NUMBER PIC 9(01).
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 REC-CNT PIC 9(05) VALUE 0.
01 WS-EOF-SW PIC X(01) VALUE 'N'.
01 CUR-KEY.
05 CUR-MAJ-KEY PIC X(14).
01 PRV-KEY VALUE LOW-VALUES.
05 PRV-MAJ-KEY PIC X(14).
01 TBL-MAX PIC 9(03) VALUE 1000.
01 TBL-NBR-ENTRIES PIC 9(03) VALUE 0.
01 TBL.
05 TBL-ITEM OCCURS 1000 TIMES INDEXED BY TBL-NDX.
10 TBL-NAME PIC X(14).
10 TBL-ORDER-NUMBER PIC 9(01).
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
OPEN INPUT INPUT-FILE
MOVE 0 TO TBL-NBR-ENTRIES
MOVE SPACES TO TBL
PERFORM UNTIL WS-EOF-SW = 'Y'
READ INPUT-FILE AT END
MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO CUR-KEY
MOVE 'Y' TO WS-EOF-SW
END-READ
IF WS-EOF-SW = 'N'
MOVE IR-MAJ-KEY TO CUR-MAJ-KEY
ADD 1 TO REC-CNT
END-IF
IF REC-CNT = 1
MOVE CUR-MAJ-KEY TO PRV-MAJ-KEY
ELSE
IF CUR-MAJ-KEY NOT = PRV-MAJ-KEY
DISPLAY 'TABLE CONTAINS '
TBL-NBR-ENTRIES ' ENTRIES:'
PERFORM VARYING TBL-NDX FROM 1 BY 1
UNTIL TBL-NDX > TBL-NBR-ENTRIES
DISPLAY ' ' TBL-NAME (TBL-NDX)
' ' TBL-ORDER-NUMBER (TBL-NDX)
END-PERFORM
MOVE CUR-MAJ-KEY TO PRV-MAJ-KEY
* DESTROY THE TABLE!
MOVE 0 TO TBL-NBR-ENTRIES
MOVE SPACES TO TBL
END-IF
END-IF
IF WS-EOF-SW = 'N'
ADD 1 TO TBL-NBR-ENTRIES
IF TBL-NBR-ENTRIES > TBL-MAX
DISPLAY 'TBL BOUNDRY ERROR' GOBACK
END-IF
MOVE IR-NAME TO TBL-NAME (TBL-NBR-ENTRIES)
MOVE IR-ORDER-NUMBER
TO TBL-ORDER-NUMBER(TBL-NBR-ENTRIES)
END-IF
END-PERFORM
CLOSE INPUT-FILE
GOBACK.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

END-PERFORM
MOVE CUR-MAJ-KEY TO PRV-MAJ-KEY
* DESTROY THE TABLE!
MOVE 0 TO TBL-NBR-ENTRIES
MOVE SPACES TO TBL
END-IF
END-IF
IF WS-EOF-SW = 'N'
ADD 1 TO TBL-NBR-ENTRIES
IF TBL-NBR-ENTRIES > TBL-MAX
DISPLAY 'TBL BOUNDRY ERROR' GOBACK
END-IF
MOVE IR-NAME TO TBL-NAME (TBL-NBR-ENTRIES)
MOVE IR-ORDER-NUMBER
TO TBL-ORDER-NUMBER(TBL-NBR-ENTRIES)
END-IF
END-PERFORM
CLOSE INPUT-FILE

#### ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GuIE4.94456$Hq3.2462296@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
By default COBOL handles all the memory allocation and deallocation for you.
It is typically outside of programmer control with COBOL. This is usually a
blessing. You can do your own memory allocation/deallocation by using system
calls, pointers and the LINKAGE area but it will complicate your program
needlessly.

Stop thinking like a C programmer and ask yourself how you would do
sequential processing with a finite internal array, or no array at all.
Maybe all you need is to sort the input data before processing,  and maybe a
few accumulator variables, rather than an internal array.

- Karl Wagner

"Cosmic Knight >removethis" <cosmicknight<@home.com> wrote in message
news:RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com...
> Hi:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't want to read in the whole file into one array because it would be
a
> HUGE array, and the performance would probably be poor.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E35EC2.44F79BB3@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
What type of memory system are you using?  On a mainframe with lots of
virtual memory, I use large fixed length tables.  I move HIGH-VALUES to
MY-TABLE, before creating the table.  Processing treats a record with
HIGH-VALUES as a delimiter.

Depending on your OS, this might be more efficient than using variable
length arrays.

Alternatively, you can use called subprograms or OO.

"Cosmic Knight >removethis<" wrote:
> 
> Hi:
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> -ck
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000330101143.08424.00000023@ng-cf1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38E35EC2.44F79BB3@cusys.edu>`

```
If you are using COBOL on an IBM mainframe, you can use the Language
Environment callable services from COBOL, including:

CEECRHP - create heap
CEEGTSTG - get a storage element from a heap
CEECZST - change the size of a storage element (resize it larger or smaller)

and more.

<ad>

We cover all this and more, in COBOL, Assembler, PL/I, and C, in our course
"Applications Design Using LE Services", three days.

</ad>

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<063c668e.6a3bf462@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<38E35EC2.44F79BB3@cusys.edu> <20000330101143.08424.00000023@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
If you are using MF COBOL, you can use library routines to
allocate memory and when done, free the memory. The library
routines are CBL_ALLOC_MEM and CBL_FREE_MEM. In working storage,
you would need a memory pointer and then setup you table in the
linkage section and then set the address of you linkage section
01 level to the address of the memory pointer.

By passing the pointer to other program, the memory area can be
used for other functions too.

Regards,
Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

## Thread: Please do your own homework — 29 this month / 31 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Please do your own homework

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bu0go$vjn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Hi:

Since I intruded upon the forum I have noticed that
some people tell these revoltingly-named "newbies",
when they ask for help, to 'Please do your own homework'.

To ASSUME that that is the intent of all newbies, I think
is wrong.

Remember when YOU KNEW ABSOLUTELY NOTHING about programming,
COBOL, systems, computers - when you knew nothing at all.

Think how the newcomer, who can be understandably in deep fog
and probably being taught by someone who doesn't know much,
must feel, when turning to the elder statesmen (?) of the
community and are rebuffed in this churlish way.

Those who come here are presumably COBOL-lovers who would not
like to see their language languish. It is the newcomers
who will keep the language alive and we should nourish them.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Please do your own homework

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bu1m4$ob9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8bu0go$vjn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Tony, Newbies, and others -
  It has been a little while since I reposted what the COBOL FAQ says about
homework questions in the NG.  I hope this helps understand the "majority"
view on such posts,

"18.1 Can I get help with homework via the newsgroups?

Yes, you can get help with your homework via these newsgroups. HOWEVER, that
does mean that you will receive help - you will NOT find many participants
who will be very happy if you ask them to do your homework FOR YOU. Some
hints that you should consider when drafting your post for assistance with
homework include:

 * Make it clear from the beginning that you are asking for homework help.
(Most of the newsgroup participants are very good at sniffing out those who
try and pose homework questions as "business need" questions - and they are
not very polite in replying to such questions).

 * Make certain that you specify what compiler and operating system your
homework is targeted at. Solutions may vary significantly based on this. (You
might also want to include what text book you are using.)

 * The more information that you can give that demonstrates that you really
have tried to solve the problem yourself (using your text book, class
material/presentations, lab assistance, etc), the more likely it is that you
will get useful and friendly responses. If you let us know what you have
found on your own and why you are still uncertain or confused, you will
usually get helpful responses; if it looks like you are asking us questions
without trying to solve it yourself, you are likely to get very pointed
replies.

NOTE: if you are looking for a COBOL tutor, (and don't want to see the "Do
your own homework" notes that occur in the COBOL newsgroups), you might want
to try,

    http://www.tutoraid.org/
"
```

With all of the tools available today, a 'newbie' shouldn't have
any trouble working around their homework assignments. I really
wonder how much time they have spent on their homework before
posting. The learning process consist of 'trial and error'.
Believe me, I have had a lot of errors. By trying different
approached and 're-writing' programs, I have learned about COBOL
programming.

Suggestions to 'newbies', check for periods, get as many example
programs as possible and carfully read your compiler error
messages!

Rich

To answer on behalf of those members of this NG:

1. I can remember when I was a Newbie and sweated long and hard to debug
programs. But I managed it on my own (usually). When I had diagnosed the
problem then that light-bulb in my head would flash so bright and I
would feel good. 

2. Most of the problems posted are elementary and it only requires a
little effort and understanding for the individual to come up with the
answer. There is a saying: No sweat, no gain. If you don't struggle now
and resolve the problems encountered here by yourself then you won't cut
it as a proggie.

3. Some of the problems are clearly of the: I can't be bothered to do my
own homework so perhaps I can get some sap to do it for me...

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Please do your own homework

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ezq3lHim$GA.259@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<8bu0go$vjn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8buv8d$17h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ah, everyone *can* get information here.  But it's fairly easy to tell
whether the person asking the question has availed themselves of any number
of simple ways of answering it on their own.

If I were to go onto a physics newsgroup and start asking questions about
F=MA and how much I didn't understand it and how badly I was about to fail a
physics assignment because of my lack of understanding, and then proceed to
ask them to tell me everything about F=MA, from the beginning, I'd get some
pretty interesting responses.  Especially if I was #2 that week and #10 that
year with the same question.

Somehow, if I could *really* convince everyone on said newsgroup that I
really had tried (by reproducing my calculations online), I might get a
response where people pointed out where my simple multiplication errors
were, etc.  Then again, I might not get any response.

My analogy engine is beginning to burn out . . .

Even in newsgroups where most wouldn't dream of posting a message such as
Docdwarfs, like the Access newsgroup, many simple newbie questions just
shoot by without response.  They have pretty much the same rules here,
however, instead of telling you what the rules are, they just ignore you.

In any event, for example, I've found that the C and C++ newsgroups tend to
get really vicious when answering perceived newbie questions, not failing to
mention the beginner's newsgroup covering both languages, without any
apology for the fact that the poster probably barely found and got onto the
main newsgroup, much less found and identified the purpose of all possible
language newsgroups.  There really is a burden upon those who show up to
usenet to learn how to get around and what everything is for.

A simple reading of a newsgroup FAQ usually suffices, but how many first
timers do that?  Most of them have never even heard of a newsgroup FAQ.  But
just like the person who goes to a coat & tails ball will find themselves
barred when they show up in bluejeans, ignorance is not an excuse.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Please do your own homework

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c04g6$anp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8bu0go$vjn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8buv8d$17h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ezq3lHim$GA.259@cpmsnbbsa05>`

```
Maybe, I'm thinking to positive about people or I'm to helpful.
But the normal way for me is:
1. Having a problem
2. Thinking about a solution
3. Trying to implement the solution
4. If it fails, try the next one.....
5. If I can't get a solution by myself, I'll ask someone to help (after
reading books, searching in newsgroups and so on).

So before I started posting to this (and other) newsgroup(s) I read all
messages for some months. I don't participate to get quick results. And
hopefully others don't, too.
But when I have a solution I try to answer (not very often in this NG).
When starting with Cobol I would have been very happy to have access to
this newsgroup. I didn't and it took my long nights to solve my Cobol
language problems. Most of time the answer was so simple that it should
have taken at least 20 secs for an expert to give me a hint(!) not the
full code.
I know Cobol is something special...running for so long on so many
different plattforms. But I think and please excuse Cobol programmers
are special too. They are always confronted with thinks like 'Oh you're
using Cobol? This old language?' and so on. Sometimes I have the
impression that especially Cobol programmers are so 'intolerant' (don't
post to this word please) to see, that there are other good languages
too. Yes, all those asking people are intolerant too.
I respect Cobol and his fans but it took me a long time to see the
advantages of Cobol. On the other hand I respect other languages and
their concepts (like c, Pascal, c++, Delphi or PowerBuilder). Maybe
that's because I use all of them and worked with other languages before
starting Cobol.

And sometimes I'm a bit confused when I see 'Please do your own
homework' from people where I'm sure that they know the answer (hints,
not the full code).
Believe that I don't want to blame someone. It's only my opinion.

Bye - Stefan Meyer

P.S.: I don't want to start a discussion about Cobol Programmers. It's
only my personal impression over years.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Please do your own homework

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uZANC6hm$GA.255@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<8bu0go$vjn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Dear Foodman,

Yes, it may "look" rude of Docdwarf to go around telling people to "Do your
own homework."  But really, you're not looking at it in the correct context.

Usenet newsgroups are discussions places filled with many different people.
They cluster here in a virtual manner, checking in every so often according
to their own cycles.

In real world terms, you can think of it as a continuously occupied lounge
where people are restricted (most of the time) to discussing one thing.
However, unlike the real world, every single word uttered in the lounge can
be heard by every other person without distortion; or in some cases where
grammar is ignored, with too much distortion.

(Whispering in a corner of the lounge is accomplished by email, but in this
case, no one can see you do it; however, sometimes from the context of
discussions and the way a thread develops it is possible to tell if emailing
is going on, and really, the other poeple here come here to participate, and
emailing individuals prevents some parties from participating)

In any event, such a place is often viewed as the source of a "easy and
quick answer".  Yes, everyone who has been here awhile knows all the easy
answers, but as the years roll by (and no, I haven't been here that long,
though others have), you get to see that people are stopping by and asking
the their questions simply because they know they can get an instant reponse
from a living person without having to spend their own time on it; and
instead of taking a couple of hours out and actually reading the material,
they rely on getting it from those who already know.  After doing this over
an over for a *long* time, it tends to be very easy to look upon this as
other people unwittingly using up your time for the most basic of purposes
just because they find it easier than spending their own time.  Overall,
it's a net loss.  The regular attendees here, if they answered such
questions would wind up literally spending hours working on their newsgroup
reader posting answers to such questions.

How do we know that people are doing this?  Stick around, read the newsgroup
consistently, and you'll know that people really are stopping by for just a
moment to get the quick and easy answer to whatever is troubling them.  You
can tell the difference from a real question and a quick and easy question
after only a week or two of reading the newsgroup.

Docdwarf has taken it upon himself to stop this at the beginning with the
simple eye opener "Do your own homework."

This is not rude.  Really, It's not.  It's just the simplest and shortest
way of saying, "Please, if you spent a little of your own time looking at
it, you'd have figured it out.  Try again, and then if you still can't get
it, show us what you did, and we'll see what we can do with you."


On top of all of this, I learned COBOL plugging away at a COBOL manual for
about 6 months.  Every compiler error, every runtime error, every
misassumption about what the book was saying about how the code worked, and
figuring out the parts it left out.  I did it without any help in a shop
surrounded by other COBOL programmers.  Believe me when I say that this was
a huge bonus to me.  I can't tell you the number of other people my former
company sent through their COBOL training program who came out with only the
faintest glimmer of how to really program, much less what all the compiler
and runtime errors meant, much less how to solve them.

Because I had gone to class before these others, and because I actually knew
the answers and management wanted results now, I spent a lot of time
"handing out answers" to my colleagues.  I regret it now, despite the
popularity it gained me, as I certainly wasn't doing their programming
skills any good.

I could go on, there is literally a mountain of evidence for why Docdwarf's
statement is perfectly justified.



"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8bu0go$vjn$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> community and are rebuffed in this churlish way.

Hmm, "Elder Statesmen"?  Hmm.
Can you imagine walking into the middle of the US Senate, never having been
there, not knowing any of them, and asking them, "Hey, tell me everything
about Roberts Rules of Order."  Now think about all the different looks
you'd get from the various Senators . . .

Yes, there is a difference between the Senate and this newsgroup in terms of
a setting and what the setting exists for, but the attitude toward that type
of question is the same.  Senators don't want to waste their time telling a
citizen off the streets about the Rules of Order when that citizen could
easily open a book a read about the subject.

"Do your own homework," would be likely the most polite thing said.

Now, if you were some sort of political staffer (read as: someone who's done
some studying on COBOL), and between votes you stopped a Senator and asked
them about some technical point of the Rules of Order, you might get a much
more polite response.


Oh, and before anyone jumps down my throat, I know that in the real world
Senators are not necessarily experts on the Rules of Order.

>
> Those who come here are presumably COBOL-lovers who would not
> like to see their language languish. It is the newcomers
> who will keep the language alive and we should nourish them.
>

Anyone frightened away from COBOL by what is, to them, an out-of-the-blue
statement of a complete stranger is going to be frightened away from
anything by the ordinary events of "life".  They need to consider the
statement in it's proper context and relationship to them.


> Thanks
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Please do your own homework

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OjF2wTkm$GA.242@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<8bu0go$vjn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
When someone asks for help we give them help.

When someone asks us to do their homework for them, we either say no,
or ignore them.
Posting the specifications of a problem, and asking how to do it, is
not asking for help, it is a "do my homework" request.
Programming is the practice of solving problems. We do it every day.
We take the building blocks, and construct a solution to the problem.
Assuming that the student has been, or should have been given, the
building block in class.
The homework should be "now build me something". It is the students
assignment to figure out how to construct the solution.

A student should be willing to experiment, and either fail, or
succeed.
If their experiment fails, they can come here to ask why.

Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8bu0go$vjn$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
>
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Please do your own homework

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RaJE4.13399$0o4.86672@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<8bu0go$vjn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8bu0go$vjn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman  <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>who will keep the language alive and we should nourish them.

With all due respect, Mr Dilworth... our experiences are different and we
have come to different conclusions.

When someone posts what, for all intents and purposes, 'smells' like an
assignment, showing no exertion other than the asking of a question then
yes, I'll be among the first to jump in with a 'please do your own
homework'.

On the other hand... when someone states a difficulty and includes 'this
is what I have done to solve the problem... and yet still it does not go'
*then* I am more than willing to offer a pointer or two (unless, of
course, others have beat me to it).

What one gets out of this group depends on what one puts into it; if
someone cannot or will not show their own efforts then I feel no need to
expend any effort of my own... but that's just me, of course.

DD
```

### Mainframe Programming Opportunities - Not!

- **From:** "Dave Polzin" <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38e2d709$0$63695@news.execpc.com>`

```
I realize that I'm off the beaten path up here in central WI and there
aren't that many mainframes around, but I was a little surprised when one of
the regional contract firms laid off all their mainframe programmers this
morning.  Anybody else aware of lack of opportunities in other areas of the
US?  We're talking applications here, not systems.  Is the Y2K glory now
just a faded memory?
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe Programming Opportunities - Not!

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00

<dlpolzin@coredcs.com> wrote:

>aren't that many mainframes around, but I was a little surprised when one of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't think it has diddly to do with Y2K.  What is has to do with is
$$.  You see, we have been on the brink of recession for some time.
I've watched inflation and I have seen businesses starting to fail.
It's going to get worse before it gets better. 

That's just part of it.

The other part is kinda sorta Y2K.  You see, the mainframe systems are
stable.  They do their job.  They did for 20 years before Y2K with few
people touching the code.  Come Y2K it had to be done, so programmers
were hired.  Expensive programmers.  Now there is no need - the
systems are stable again and will run for another 20+ years with just
maintenance.  New development?  That's expensive.  Fuel prices are up,
everyone is raising prices, companies are feeling the pinch - here
come the layoffs.
```

>It seems there is a lot of work available but the comapnies are afraid

T'was e'er thus... it is my experience that when somebody *wants* me then
I am on-site and billing withing 48 hrs of the pimp's submitting of my
bragsheet.  This frequently disappoints the 'shoppers' who expect someone
to be available, waiting and unpaid, for six weeks after the initial
interview.

Pimp: 'Whaddaya ya *mean*, 'I took another contract'?!?  This one was so
*right*, all ya hadda do was wait a while... whaddamI gonna tell 'em?'

Me: 'You might tell them that given a choice between waiting for them to
decide and getting paid for the (n) weeks since the interview I chose the
latter; if they cannot see this as an acceptable alternative then I do not
think I'd want to work with them, anyhow.'

DD

## Thread: Variable field lengths — 11 msgs

### Variable field lengths

- **From:** Patti Lusk <plusk@clemson.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu>`

```
I'm trying to read in a record in which some of the fields vary in
length.  The source file only has some sort of character separating
them:

ST!130!00032001"

in case you are wondering, the data is an EDI file: student transcript.
the segment field (where ST appears), for instance, may be a length of
two, but another segment
may be 3.  I would like to read the file into a table, but I don't know
how to
tackle the problem with those variable length fields.  Anyone got any
ideas?
Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable field lengths

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j+FsZCAaro44Ew$h@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu>`

```
The EDI systems I have used rely upon either you playing computer and
manually decoding the data or else using the utility which comes with it
to read and decompress the files/records.

In article <38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu>, Patti Lusk
<plusk@clemson.edu> writes
>I'm trying to read in a record in which some of the fields vary in
>length.  The source file only has some sort of character separating
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable field lengths

- **From:** "Searcher" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BixE4.2997$4T2.514@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu>`

```
First of all, are all the records in the file the same length?
Probably not.
It is difficult in COBOL to have a file with records of different
length unless each record is proceeded by a LENGTH-ITEM.

Once you have gotten past that,
How do you know 'WHAT IS THE SEPERATOR?'?

If you can rely upon it always being some value, or always
being the FIRST n CHARACTERS,
then you set up a list of items of the maximum size each
all PIC X.

Then you do an 'UNSTRING DATA-ITEM INTO
item-1 DELIMITED BY WS-DELIM
item-2 DELIMITED BY WS-DELIM,
item-3 DELIMITED BY WS-DELIM'

This will split the DATA-ITEM into each of the item-n items
from LEFT to RIGHT, skipping whatever you have put into
WS-DELIM.

WS-DELIM can be a single character or several bytes!



Patti Lusk <plusk@clemson.edu> wrote in message
news:38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu...
> I'm trying to read in a record in which some of the fields vary in
> length.  The source file only has some sort of character separating
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable field lengths

- **From:** "Dave Polzin" <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38e2cf94$0$62472@news.execpc.com>`
- **References:** `<38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu>`

```
I have a situation where I receive an ascii file from a PICK system.  The
file comes as a single stream of characters into our LAN server which I
break up into blocks of 27990 each (for dasd efficiency) during the upload.
Each field is variable.  Each record is variable.  The separators are
x'0D0A' between fields, x'0C' between records and x'1A' as end-of file.  My
solution?  Write it in assembler.  Not only do I have the variable
field/record problem. but I also have to translate ascii to ebcdic.  I
didn't relish doing it in COBOL, so opted for assembler.  If you don't have
that luxury, you have a good challenge.

dlpolzin@coredcs.com

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable field lengths

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bubgt$smn$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu> <NY=iOITM76dE8NUIMbkDf0NffZ9h@4ax.com>`

```
>>I'm trying to read in a record in which some of the fields vary in
>>length.  The source file only has some sort of character separating
>>them:
>>
>>ST!130!00032001"

    move space to field1, field2, field3, field4, ...
    unstring
        data-input-record delimited by "!"
    into
        field1, field3, field3, field4 ...

*******Gives you data right justified in field1, etc.

You still have to parse the resultant fields to make sure things are aligned
as you want.  The results of the above will be left justified and space
filled, so you will have to unstring the unknown lengthed numerics delimited
by space into a picture 9(n) numeric field, or use the function numval.  The
function will give you more reliable results if you have it.  That would
also take care of "."'s that are imbedded if there are any.  The example you
show has only integers, or data with an implied decimal place.  If there is
an implied decimal place, though, the data would have to be of a known
format/length.  If you are talking real free format, then numval makes it
easy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable field lengths

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vCKE4.56$JL.667@client>`
- **References:** `<38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu>`

```
Please, can you tell us what compiler/system you are using.  It is helpful
to know this so that, for example, inappropriate (too new, too old, too
proprietary) language features are not recommended.  There are several ways
to do this, some relying on newer language features.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation

news:38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu...

```
Tom Morrison wrote:
> 
> Please, can you tell us what compiler/system you are using.  It is helpful
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Liant Software Corporation

It seems that UNSTRING is required to break up the EDI text into
fields.  As this is in Cobol 74 standard then I am mystified as to what
you could mean by 'newer language features' or 'too old' or 'too
proprietry'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable field lengths

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sUOE4.148$JL.2512@client>`
- **References:** `<38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu> <vCKE4.56$JL.667@client> <38E44A5E.59AB018D@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:38E44A5E.59AB018D@Azonic.co.nz...
> Tom Morrison wrote:
[snip]
> > to do this, some relying on newer language features.

> It seems that UNSTRING is required to break up the EDI text into
> fields.  As this is in Cobol 74 standard then I am mystified as to what
> you could mean by 'newer language features' or 'too old' or 'too
> proprietry'.

It is what comes after the UNSTRING.

Donald Tees suggested FUNCTION NUMVAL, which is not 85 standard, but rather
an add-on to that standard which might not be available on a mainframe
installation.  My company's product, RM/COBOL, implementing the 1985
standard MOVE statement, has a "deedit" feature when moving from a numeric
edited item to a numeric item.  Now the "deedit" is required by the standard
(Section VI, Paragraph 6.19.4 (4)), but the committee did not provide
semantic rules for de-editing.  I would suggest that each vendor's
implementation is therefore proprietary to some extent, and the response
would need to be tailored to the particular vendor.

In private e-mail, the compiler was identified as Fujitsu v3 on a PC.  I
replied with the following suggestion:
=== quote ===
Then Donald Tees' suggestion is the best one:  UNSTRING the data delimited
by whatever the delimiter is, and where you wish to retrieve numeric values,
use the NUMVAL function on the result of the UNSTRING.

Your example was:   ST!130!00032001"

I presume that the first 'chunk', i.e. "ST" indicates the format of the
remainder of the item.  I would probably set up something like this:

01  EDI-ITEM pic x(100).
01  EDI-CURSOR  pic 9(4).
01  EDI-ST-FIELD1  pic x(30).
01  EDI-ST-FIELD2  pic x(30).
01  EDI-GPA-FIELD1  pic x(30).
01  EDI-GPA-FIELD2  pic x(30).
...
move 1 to EDI-CURSOR.
unstring EDI-ITEM into EDI-INDICATOR delimited by "!"
    pointer EDI-CURSOR
    on overflow <error action>
end-unstring
evaluate EDI-INDICATOR
when "ST"
    unstring EDI-ITEM into EDI-ST-FIELD1 delimited by "!" or quote
                                     EDI-ST-FIELD2
        pointer EDI-CURSOR
        on overflow <error action>
    end-unstring
    <rest of ST processing e.g.>

when "GPA"
    unstring EDI-ITEM into EDI-GPA-FIELD1 delimited by "!" or quote
                                     EDI-GPA-FIELD2
        pointer EDI-CURSOR
        on overflow <error action>
    end-unstring
    <rest of GPA processing>
...repeat 'when' clauses for the various item types...
when other
    <error action for unexpected item type>
end-evaluate

Assuming you have to deal with each item type differently, this technique
works well.  The variable EDI-CURSOR retains the information about how many
characters have been examined in EDI-ITEM so the subsequnt UNSTRING(s) know
at which character position to start.

The UNSTRING verb also has a COUNT IN phrase that allows you to know how
many characters of the sending item (EDI-ITEM) were used in the move.  There
is also a DELIMITER IN phrase that will store the delimiter that terminated
the field.  Both of these phrases are most useful when you use a separate
UNSTRING verb for each field you are separating out of the sending item.

The bottom line is:  UNSTRING is a quite powerful tool for doing the job you
appear to have before you.

=== unquote ===

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable field lengths

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c0e00$t6n$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu> <vCKE4.56$JL.667@client> <38E44A5E.59AB018D@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
I can think of at least ONE item that is non-Standard that might be
required - i.e. LINE SEQUENTIAL.  Clearly RECORD VARYING IN SIZE (rather than
RECORD CONTAIN n TO n) which came in the '85 Standard might be needed.  I
believe (but won't swear to it) that there are actually some software
packages that are SPECIFICALLY created for handling EDI files.  These too
would be "proprietary".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable field lengths

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E4F07E.E2D2907B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<38E26B02.BD674D92@clemson.edu> <vCKE4.56$JL.667@client> <38E44A5E.59AB018D@Azonic.co.nz> <8c0e00$t6n$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> I can think of at least ONE item that is non-Standard that might be
> required - i.e. LINE SEQUENTIAL.  Clearly RECORD VARYING IN SIZE (rather than
> RECORD CONTAIN n TO n) which came in the '85 Standard might be needed. 

I process EDI files, such as EDIFACT standard BAPLIEs and such using a
SEQUENTIAL file with a 256 character fixed size.  I read blocks into
memory and tile these into working-storage 512 byte buffer.  This caters
for non-standard or no line terminator.  BAPLIEs and such are in
'segments' with an apostrophee (or other nominated character) as the
terminator. There may or may not be CR and/or LF.  Everything after an '
(or nominated) up to the next segment start is to be ignored. LINE
SEQUENTIAL is of little use.

The only problem is when the file is not an exact multiple of 256
characters and an exception occurs on the last block.  At this point the
last block has been read up to actual end of file.  The EDI file has a
segment marking end of data (UNZ) and so physical end of file is
unrequired.

This all works in ANSI 74 without any extensions.


> I
> believe (but won't swear to it) that there are actually some software
> packages that are SPECIFICALLY created for handling EDI files.  These too
> would be "proprietary".

Well, of course, unless it is entirely standard Cobol supplied as
source.
```

## Thread: VS COBOL II vs. OS/390 — 11 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### VS COBOL II vs. OS/390

- **From:** "CherylDCTX" <cymitch@flash.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nAAE4.20677$624.1595887@news.flash.net>`

```
Can anyone list the basic differences between OS/390 and VS COBOL II?
My IS shop is switching over to OS/390 in 3rd Quarter of this year.

Each application area will be responsible for re-compiling their
modules.

The common COBOL II modules are being compiled into OS/390.  The
programs that call them are still in COBOL II.

Are there any potential problems that we should look out for???
```

#### ↳ Re: VS COBOL II vs. OS/390

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bulph$s4q$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<nAAE4.20677$624.1595887@news.flash.net>`

```
Step 1 - READ the IBM migration guide that explains all of this in detail.

Step 2 - Do *not* recompile all (or even most) of your VS COBOL II programs.
There is absolutely no reason to do so - as IBM *fully* supports VS COBOL II
object code when used (including link-edited) with the LE run-time libraries

Step 3 - If you are not already using the LE run-time libraries for your VS
COBOL II modules, then plan this as your FIRST migration step - and plan on
LOTS of testing and tuning time.  (The change in compilers has minimal
impact - the change in run-times is SIGNIFICANT - with lots of "unplanned"
side-effects.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: VS COBOL II vs. OS/390

- **From:** "CherylDCTX" <cymitch@flash.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n1BE4.20689$624.1598238@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<nAAE4.20677$624.1595887@news.flash.net> <8bulph$s4q$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
My shop (systems programmers) is not that free with information.   No
guide was mentioned when blanket statement was made that all areas had
to switch to OS/390.  No one even attempted to explain as you just did
that we don't need to recompile.  We are using the LE run time
libraries.

Is the IBM migration guide available on-line?  Where would I get a copy
of this guide?
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

from EITHER OS/VS COBOL or VS COBOL II - to IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM) is at:
  http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYMG201/CCONTENTS

The entire COBOL for this-and-that bookshelf is at:
   http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/Shelves/IGYSH207

The LE migration Guide is at:
  http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/CEEA6010/CCONTENTS

Finally - if you are interested in PDF rather than bookmanager copies of
manuals, you may want to check out the IBM COBOL "library" page at:
   http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/coblib.htm

#### ↳ Clarification of Request - (was Re: VS COBOL II vs. OS/390)

- **From:** "CherylDCTX" <cymitch@flash.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UZAE4.20687$624.1598385@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<nAAE4.20677$624.1595887@news.flash.net>`

```
Can anyone list the basic differences between OS/390 and VS COBOL II
compilers?
My IS shop is switching over to OS/390 compiler in 3rd Quarter of this
year.

Each application area will be responsible for re-compiling their COBOL
modules.

The department responsible for the commonly used modules are already
re-compiling those modules using OS/390 as the compiler.   The
application areas that call these common submodules are slowly starting
to re-compile their modules using OS/390.

One problem that we recently came across was that:
* COBOL 2 program A called OS/390 common module B and ABENDed with a
SOC4.
It took a while to figure it what the program was.
Turned out OS/390 common submodule B contained a STATIC call to a COBOL
2 submodule C - that's a no, no.
Once they re-compiled OS/390 common module B - with DYNAMIC call to
COBOL 2 submodule C.  COBOL 2 program A stopped ABENDing.

Are there any other potential problems that we should be aware of???

Any help would be appreciated.

Thanks!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Clarification of Request - (was Re: VS COBOL II vs. OS/390)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8buptn$4np$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<nAAE4.20677$624.1595887@news.flash.net> <UZAE4.20687$624.1598385@news.flash.net>`

```

"CherylDCTX" <cymitch@flash.net> wrote in message
news:UZAE4.20687$624.1598385@news.flash.net...
  <snip>
>
> One problem that we recently came across was that:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I must be missing something here.  There is ABSOLUTELY no rule that you can
mix (and match) any number of dynamic and static CALLs between VS COBOL II
and COBOL for this-and-that compiled routines.

There *is* a rule that says that you cannot mix NORES VS COBOL II programs
with COBOL for this-and-that routines (where NORES is no longer available).
(Actually, there are some fairly complex "re-linking" and run-time options
that allow you to get away with this - but those rules are truly obscure.)

Therefore, if you have
   Module A (compiled with EITHER VS COBOL II (compiled with RES) or COBOL
for this-and-that) that calls Module B (this call may be either dynamic or
static),
  Then Module B (compiled with COBOL for this-and-that) makes EITHER a
dynamic or static call to:
  Module C (compiled with VS COBOL II - with the RES compiler option),

Then this absolutely, positively should NOT ABEND (or cause other problems).

P.S.  There is one other "potential" problem - but this has been true since
VS COBOL II first came out.  If you are passing data via a CALL USING
statements to a 24-bit (AMODE/RMODE(24)) module, then you need to compile the
calling program with the DATA(24) compiler option.  However, changing from a
static to dynamic CALL wouldn't solve this problem (if you had it), it would
actually make it worse.  My *best* guess is that you have some VS COBOL II
programs compiled with NORES.  This will cause you MANY problems as you move
to an LE and COBOL for this-and-that environment.  Check out the discussions
on this topic in the migration guides.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Clarification of Request - (was Re: VS COBOL II vs. OS/390)

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E31211.E7F99ABD@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<nAAE4.20677$624.1595887@news.flash.net> <UZAE4.20687$624.1598385@news.flash.net>`

```
CherylDCTX wrote:
> 
> Can anyone list the basic differences between OS/390 and VS COBOL II
> compilers?

COBOL for OS/390 & VM includes intrinsic functions and OO support not
available in VS COBOL II.  Otherwise, VS COBOL II syntax is completely
compatible with COBOL for OS/390 & VM, including PROCESS/CBL compiler
directives (except for NORES, which is no longer available).  But the
biggest difference is COBOL for OS/390 & VM requires LE/MVS.

The LE/MVS libraries include all the ILB* and IGZ* runtime modules to
support OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL II, although some of those modules are
different from the originals.  When you compile with COBOL for OS/390 &
VM, you will be calling CEE* runtime modules in the LE/MVS library. 
LE/MVS runs predominantly above the line, so 31-bit issues will become
important.  

> My IS shop is switching over to OS/390 compiler in 3rd Quarter of this
> year.
> <snip>
> 
> Are there any other potential problems that we should be aware of???

My shop recently began converting to COBOL for MVS & VM.  As recently as
1998, we were converting OS/VS COBOL to VS COBOL II.  We elected to
convert gradually.  As programs are recompiled for normal maintenance
they are compiled with the newest compiler.  Our system programmers made
the Endevor batch COBOL defaults include DATA(31),RMODE(ANY), and
DYNAM.  These can be overridden using PROCESS/CBL compiler directives. 
Since our compiles and object modules are managed by Endevor, no other
way is allowed to change compile parms.  The SP's would prefer that our
batch COBOL run above the 16-meg line.

Recently, we have experienced some problems with older assembler
subprograms that were last assembled before MVS/XA and know nothing
about memory above the line.  Our system programmers tell us that the
older assembler programs must be modified to support 31-bit addresses in
the save area chain to be compatible with LE/MVS, even if they remain
AMODE(24),RMODE(24).

Converting older assembler programs from AMODE(24) to AMODE(31) can be
tricky.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Clarification of Request - (was Re: VS COBOL II vs. OS/390)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E35DAB.CCA77FE9@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<nAAE4.20677$624.1595887@news.flash.net> <UZAE4.20687$624.1598385@news.flash.net> <38E31211.E7F99ABD@worldnet.att.net>`

```
We had some troubles with blocksize, and troubles with default values in
fields.  Make sure your fields are initialized before you use them
(sometimes old Cobols would initialize by default).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Clarification of Request - (was Re: VS COBOL II vs. OS/390)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c0cg2$t64$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<nAAE4.20677$624.1595887@news.flash.net> <UZAE4.20687$624.1598385@news.flash.net> <38E31211.E7F99ABD@worldnet.att.net> <38E35DAB.CCA77FE9@cusys.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38E35DAB.CCA77FE9@cusys.edu...
> We had some troubles with blocksize, and troubles with default values in
> fields.  Make sure your fields are initialized before you use them
> (sometimes old Cobols would initialize by default).

Normally, this is a problem moving from OS/VS COBOL to one of the newer
COBOLs.  If you are already using VS COBOL II, then just understand that the
WSCLEAR option used with VS COBOL II is now replaced by the STORAGE(00)
option (which has a few other parameters as well).

Check out your VS COBOL II environment and make certain that you use the
COMPARABLE (but not same word) run-time option with the newer (LE)
environment.
```

#### ↳ Re: VS COBOL II vs. OS/390

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000330102830.08424.00000026@ng-cf1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<nAAE4.20677$624.1595887@news.flash.net>`

```
Hi, Cheryl,

I see you've been getting lots of good input and advice, especially where to
find the pubs for free.

<ad>
We offer training for applications programmers that might speed up the process
of migration and even (Heavens!) _using_ available LE services. Quick synopsis
of related courses:

1. D204 - Beyond COBOL II; two days; covers changes in the language from COBOL
II on; introduction to LE concepts; some labs using intrinsic functions and LE
services.

2. M212 - Applications Design Using LE Services; three days; covers LE concepts
and a thorough survey of LE services and runtime options; course is
multilingual: lectures include COBOL, PL/I, C, and Assembler (languages are
included or omitted based on student input at the beginning) and labs can be
run in any of these languages.

3. M214 - Applications Design Using LE Services; four days; combines D204 and
M214.

4. M220 - InterLanguage Communciation in OS/390; three days; covers static and
dynamic calls from programs written in one language to programs written in
another (languages include COBOL, PL/I, C, Assembler) as well as dealing with
shared external data, different styles of passing and receiving data, the
linkage editor, the program binder, and DLLs.

5. D232 - COBOL Debugging and Maintenance in the LE Environment; two days;
covers debugging using CEE dumps (these are the LE dumps) as well as COBOL
calling, passing and receiving data, and the linkage editor / program binder.

</ad>

Management have no idea how rich the LE product is nor how to exploit its
capabilities, but a good starting point is training so at least everyone knows
what's possible.

Regards,





Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

The difference between static and dynamic calls was merely compiling
the called to a gnt and changing the literal in the call to a variable.

programs to achieve this?

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>

Check out the DYNAM, NODYNAM compiler option.

If you place the called program name in a variable, you will get
dynamic calling.

change:
 call 'pgm' using etc
to:
01  pgm-something  pic x(8) value 'pgm'.
 call pgm-something using etc.

The called programs need no changes, must the must reside in the LOAD
Library as load modules.

support,

>

> the called to a gnt and changing the literal in the call to a
variable.
>

> convert existing code from static to dynamic calls.  Can somebody
point

calling
> programs to achieve this?

#### ↳ Re: Convert Static to Dynamic?

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8bt9lb$450$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8bt33m$sbj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8bt33m$sbj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com> wrote:
> Back during my sabbatical from the IBM mainframe and systems support,
> I worked client/server apps on HP unix boxes using MicroFocus COBOL.
>
> The difference between static and dynamic calls was merely compiling
> the called to a gnt and changing the literal in the call to a
variable.
>
> Now back on the big box, I haven't figured out what I need to do to
> convert existing code from static to dynamic calls. Can somebody point
> me to a guide as to what changes are needed in the called and calling
> programs to achieve this?

If you are using COBOL II or later, you can put a PROCESS card with the
DYNAM option as the first record in the program.  This converts all
calls to dynamic calls.  You do not have to change the code itself.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Convert Static to Dynamic?

- **From:** Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8btci8$7nm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert Static to Dynamic?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E248A0.E029E0D1@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8bt33m$sbj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8bt9lb$450$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8btci8$7nm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Bill Thompson wrote:
> 
> Using COBOL for MVS & VM with the LE, I tried the NODYNAM to DYNAM but
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> loadlib that the calling program is cataloged.

The old technique I have used to make sure my called programs were
run-time was to have my COBOL call a variable.

01  my-program   pic 9(8)  value 'getdbkey'.
...
     call myprogram using myfields.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert Static to Dynamic?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8btotu$9le$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8bt33m$sbj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8bt9lb$450$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8btci8$7nm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
First, you need to be careful when using DYNAM/NODYNAM versus CALL
"literal"/identifier to force "dynamic" calls.  If you use DYNAM, this will
force ALL calls to dynamic - which is not acceptable under CICS, IMS, or DB2
(where "system" calls need to be static).

Next, no you should not need NCAL

Finally, if you are getting an (LE) U4039 ABEND, then it sounds like you are
not running with a "particularly useful" setting of the LE TERMTHDACT
run-time option.

See:
 http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/CEEA3010/2%2e1%2e53

for U4039 and TERMTHDACT

and see

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/CEEA6010/4%2e2

for the recommended run-time options

P.S.  The bottom-line description of U4039 is that some OTHER error has
already occurred - and wasn't "properly" handled.
```

Could anyone provide me with, or point me towards, an example of a
program that reads lines (records) from a file and loads them into a
table in memory.

The assignment I have requires me to read data about the number of
parts held in stock, their names and unit cost, and combine this with
another file that updates the number held in stock.  It looks like this:

042*Widget              *00123*01200
199*Bolt                *80000*00008

Where 042 is the part code, Widget is the part name, 00123 is the
number in stock, and 01200 is the unit cost.

Unfortunately I'm failing at the first hurdle because I don't
understand how to read the data into a table in memory.  Thanks for any
help!

Tom

## Thread: New to Cobol — 17 this month / 18 total

### New to Cobol

- **From:** "John Wolanski" <brgr88@REMOVETHISyahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_gyE4.9678$_c3.65791@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```
Hello, Group!

I was recently looking through the bargain books section of a local computer
store when I found a "Teach Yourself Cobol in 21 Days, by Mo Budlong" for
really cheap.

Being that I am a mainframe operator in my job, and that there are scant few
books, training materials, etc. readily available on the open market for an
affordable price (at least I can't find any), I thought that learning Cobol
might come in handy when looking for promotions, etc. in my job, so I
grabbed a copy.

The book says that I'm going to need a compiler to do any of the work in it,
and that's where I need help (and I highly doubt that my company will let me
"mess around" with their utilities).

The author recommended the Micro Focus Personal compiler, but also made
mention of compilers made by other companies.

I went to the MF website and their DOS version is about $50, but it also
mentioned that it doesn't make .exe files, and all programs must be run
through some other software feature.  Is there a compiler that makes
plain-old .exe files to run?  Or is this not the best way to go about it?
Is there one available that isn't too expensive?

I hunted down and found Cobol650, but I don't seem to be doing it right,
since every time I try to compile a source code file, I just get a "Please
donate to..." message and it quits.  And from what I can gather from the
instructions, it seems to be lacking a linking utility anyway.  Is there
some sort of voodoo that needs worked to get this program to run?

What about those Cobol books that come with (I believe) a compiler?  Are
those any good to work with?  I'm not looking for anything GUI or fancy or
anything.  I noticed a few books like this; is there any one in particular
that has a good software package (preferably one that isn't a demo version)?

Thanks for any help, and I hope I wasn't too annoying with all my questions.
```

#### ↳ Re: New to Cobol

- **From:** Charles Hammond <ceh1@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000330104950.3875A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`
- **References:** `<_gyE4.9678$_c3.65791@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```
Compilers for different languages often say they can not make an exe file,
this means that you can write a program in source program and you can
execute it from within the compiler. However you can run the program.
You can learn how the logic of a program works in this method.  Try
looking at Amazon.com for books on COBOL I bought one a while back written
for the AS400 COBOL.  

Mainframe COBOL is a lot different in how the programs are stored linked
and run.  You have to run programs with job control language (JCL) which
is specific to the actual mainframe environment.  Whether it be
Honneywell, Mitsubishi, IBM or others.

There is also a difference between the Batch programs you will find in
most programming books and CICS which is an on-line interactive kind of
programming.  When you get into larger organizations, you will also
see DBMS database and possible use of COBOL to maintain it.  Then you have
the object oriented crowd and the GUI interfaces.  At the present there is
a big demand for possible applications that can be run accross platforms
like the internet that interact with data live on the mainframe.

Microfocus also makes a compiler they call Workbench which I have seen
used.  I've used both versions.  The Workbench has it's benefits but the
version I saw didn't have any extra object oriented stuff in it.  The
Windows versions can help you learn quite a bit.  It is possible to run
a program and watch it step through all the steps to spot possible
problems.  The other day I was doing something with it and set it up so I
could watch the output and the program running through it's steps at the
same time in different windows.  But you need more advanced training.  Of
course you have to start somewhere.

Would be nice if they had a version that used a PC and simulated a
mainframe environment.

Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program

On Thu, 30 Mar 2000, John Wolanski wrote:

My "usually reliable source" (and boy did I need to think who to contact on
this one) says,

"To 'give someone the raspberry' or 'blow a raspberry' are both in use in UK
English.  The meaning is exactly as you describe and it is a show of
disrespect or dismissal, often considered immature or vulgar.  The practice
plays a part in slapstick humo(u)r and is highly amusing to children.  I
believe there are also some spoof awards known as the Golden Raspberries..."
```

Perhaps, Mr Klein, you might wish to try the obvious before wracking your
brain.  From http://www.m-w.com:

--begin quoted text:

2 [short for raspberry tart, rhyming slang for fart] : a sound of contempt
made by protruding the tongue between the lips and expelling air forcibly
to produce a vibration; broadly : an expression of disapproval or contempt 

--end quoted text

(rhyming slang of this nature is common amongst the 'Cockneys', or those
lower socioeconomic stature born in London within earshot of Bow's Bells)

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to Cobol

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FpME4.95036$Hq3.2468785@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<_gyE4.9678$_c3.65791@typhoon.columbus.rr.com> <38E2D5E8.3B092AA7@home.com> <WrBE4.9729$_c3.68222@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

George Carlin refers to it as a 'bilabial fricative'. It is also known as a

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to Cobol

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4qLE4.5735$Fk.83309@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<_gyE4.9678$_c3.65791@typhoon.columbus.rr.com> <38e2d936.6264047@news.cox-internet.com> <WvBE4.9731$_c3.68352@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```
The problem with "TYC in 21 days" (not Thane's book) is that it asks
the student to repeatedly key in example programs.  You can go through
a tutorial like that and still not be able to write any code yourself.
Though some people like the method, because it is easy (not much
thinking), but in my experience it is a very poor teaching technique.
Examples are important, but unless you begin thinking through the
process yourself, you are not learning how to program.  It works for
playing a musical instrument, for example, because that is basically a
see-do activity.  But programming is a highly creative mental activity,
and you can only learn to do that by thinking it through yourself, not
by blindly keying in sample code written by someone else.  That only
improves your typing skills, not your thinking skills. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c15g7$15l$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8buohg$drp$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <exFNYVim$GA.156@cpmsnbbsa05> <WRHE4.10617$Y4.179296@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <38E364F7.3911C375@zip.com.au> <EBSE4.11536$Y4.210183@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
"Cosmic Knight >removethis" <cosmicknight<@home.com> wrote in message
news:EBSE4.11536$Y4.210183@news1.rdc1.il.home.com...
> Unfortunately, this problem is not a typical one.  Although I am reading a
> sequential file, I need to move backward and forward through the records,
> which is why I need an array.  See ans.to Bill's message for more detail.
>

Are you saying that you need to move backwards and forwards among people - or
among transactions for a single person?  If it is the latter, then I still
say you should be creating the table (we don't call them "arrays" in COBOL)
to hold all the transactions for a single person.  This will do exactly what
you want.

If you really do have a need to move backwards and forwards from person to
person, then COBOL has a solution for this too - it is called "indexed files"
(accessed randomly or dynamically).  All you would need to do this (in an
OS/390 environment) is to do an IDCAMS REPRO from your sequential file to an
indexed file - and you will be able to process "across" people with no
difficulty (and probably won't need a table at all).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

- **From:** "Cosmic Knight" <cosmicknight>>removethis<<@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OVWE4.12148$Y4.226873@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8buohg$drp$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <exFNYVim$GA.156@cpmsnbbsa05> <WRHE4.10617$Y4.179296@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <38E364F7.3911C375@zip.com.au> <EBSE4.11536$Y4.210183@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <8c15g7$15l$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

an
> indexed file - and you will be able to process "across" people with no

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Destroying a COBOL array

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000331090658.20357.00000251@ng-cn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<OVWE4.12148$Y4.226873@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
Cosmic Knight,

I included a reply to this thread a few days ago about using LE callable
services but never saw a response from you. Did you see the note?

However, based on your latest information, I think you are in trouble if you
want to use IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM to solve this problem using arrays. The
reason is that this compiler has a maximum size for an array of 16MB and you
are talking about needing an array of 400MB. This is a [somewhat arbitrary]
limitation of this particular compiler.

In fact, this is the kind of thing I was talking about a few weeks ago after
SHARE: if you have requirements for compiler enhancements (such as larger table
sizes) you need to let the IBM compiler developers know, so they can build a
business case to implement these desired features.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

### Announcement: TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS2000) Conference

- **From:** uspdi@earthlink.net (USPDI)
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth

MASTER THE CHALLENGES OF FUTURE TRENDS AND DEVELOPMENTS IN SOFTWARE TESTING! 

17th International Conference and Exposition on 
TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS2000)
Theme: Testing Technology vs. Testers' Requirements
June 12-16, 2000
Washington, D.C. * Hyatt Regency (Bethesda) * USA 
(Conference: June 14-16; Workshops/Tutorials: June 12-13) 

Conference + Workshops + Tutorials + Exhibition + Networking + Book Signing 

The TCS2000 program is designed to prepare you for the challenges of the
new millennium.

- Three 2-day workshops and nine full day tutorials by leading experts of
  software testing on advanced topics
- Thirty-eight concurrent and parallel sessions in four tracks present the
  latest developments in testing
- Vendor Exhibit of products and services to increase testing productivity
- Birds-of-a-Feather sessions and Discussion Groups to share mutual
  interests and solutions
- Conference Volume provides a useful reference for solving problems 

The featured speakers of TCS2000 will be: 

"From Art to Practice: Transferring Testing (and Other) Technology"
   by Shari Lawrence Pfleeger
"When Helping Doesn't Help: Software Testing as Codependent Behavior"
   by  Lee Copeland
"Testing Object-Oriented (OO) Systems: Lessons Learned" by Bob Binder
"Is an Ounce of Test-plan Worth a Ton of Tools?" by Simon Mills
"Powerful and Pitiful Measures of Software" by Tom Gilb
"Luncheon Address: If I Knew Then What I Know Now" by Louise Tamres
"Wrap-up Session: Conference Reflections" by Marnie Hutcheson

The TCS2000 web site at http://www.uspdi.org/conference gives you all the
details of the sessions, speakers and other related information about the
Conference and location. You can also register on-line at: 
http://www.uspdi.org/conference/register.html 

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
