```
      E-mail - admin@uspdi.org (Subject: TCS2000)
         Web - http://www.uspdi.org/conference/brochure.html
Web download - http://www.uspdi.org/conference/printbrochure.html

--------------------------------------------------------------------------- 

For additional information please contact us:

              MD 20912-5400 (USA)

Fax:          (+1) 301-270-1040
```

## Thread: Planning for Fall Semester — 4 msgs

### Planning for Fall Semester

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@worf.netins.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E36722.A2DC995D@worf.netins.net>`

```
I will be teaching the COBOL class again next Fall and am
planing to use your TY COBOL book once again.  I do have a
couple of related questions:

1.  Last year I took a short course with Ed Price (of
OBJECTZ fame) using OO COBOL in the MF environment.  I know
that you have been experimenting some with OO COBOL during
this past year.  My question is this: how important is OO
COBOL going to become?  The students in the course have
already had an one year introduction to OO programming using
JAVA - is a unit on using OOP in COBOL worth adding to the
one semester COBOL course?

2.  Last year I took a short course with Ed Price (of
OBJECTZ fame) doing Web Programming using COBOL in the MF
environment.  My question is this: Given that students
already have a strong background in Web Programming, is it
appropriate to introduce students to Web Programming using
COBOL?  

I know that I am asking these questions early, but since
either or both will required our IT department to make some
adjustments, an early answer will help in the getting the
necessary support during the coming summer.

Thanks for any advice. 
```

```
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Planning for Fall Semester

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E391D2.BECB3280@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38E36722.A2DC995D@worf.netins.net>`

```


Floyd Johnson wrote:
> 
> I will be teaching the COBOL class again next Fall and am
> planing to use your TY COBOL book once again.  I do have a
> couple of related questions:

Floyd, as Rich has already pointed out it is Will Price - close but any
confusion is because you are also thinking of Ed Arranga (and Frank
Coyle) who wrote another book on OO COBOL. Ed is Editor of
cobolreport.com/objectz.com and Will is Deputy Editor.

Should you cover OO in one semester - difficult - because of your time
constraints. If as you say they are already familiar with Java, make a
passing reference to OO COBOL with coding comparisons - briefly showing
how we are doing the same thing with COBOL classes/methods. Try and
avoid the GUI issue - it will only confuse in a short course. I'm
biased, but would suggest you stick in a plug for
collections/dictionaries (as yet an underdetermined feature in COBOL 
200x) copy-catted from Smalltalk. (Should you be interested in
background on this feature - check out Adele Goldberg "Smalltalk 80").

Is OO COBOL the wave of the future - I'd like to think so - who knows ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Planning for Fall Semester

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0d7dce05.6cfbec0c@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<38E36722.A2DC995D@worf.netins.net>`

```
Floyd

It's not Ed Price, it's Wilson (Will) Price.

I can respond better to your second question. Why teach web
programming to COBOL programmers? This depends, do your student
plan on writing business applications for the web? If there is a
remote possiblity, then yes. Over time, I will be taking an
application that I market for the PC and apply it to the web. I
will be able to create forms using MS Frontpage or NetExpress,
incorporate them with NetExpress, access data bases (SQL, ISAM,
etc.), update, remove and add records over the internet.

Using COBOL for web programming gives your students another tool
to develop web applications. Will Price's "Elements of COBOL Web
Programming" is an excellent resource, but it doesn't go into
much detail about publishing a COBOL web application to the
internet. Will and I have talked and he will be providing more
information on this topic.

Regards,
Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

<a id="t-8eda05008b"></a>
## Thread: JCL Question about the RC system Variable — 18 this month / 22 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### JCL Question about the RC system Variable

- **From:** "Denis" <grossnou@antispam.skynet.be>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,bit.listserv.tsorexx
- **Message-ID:** `<8c1rst$dnl$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```
(I know that this question is off-topic but I do not know in which newsgroup
to post-it)
I have a JCL question: Is it possible to reset the RC system variable in a
JCL ? for example, with a statement like:
// SET RC=0
Obviously this statement does not work as RC it is not a normal "variable"

The goal is to have a JCL like the following:

//S1 EXEC PGM=P1
...
//  IF (RC GE 8) THEN   <--- start of the "does not work" construction
//   SET RC=4                 <--- does not work the idea is to "reset" the
RC variable to 4
// ENDIF                          <--- end of the "does not work"
construction

// IF (RC GE 4) THEN  <--- want to test with RC = 4 even if S1 ended with
RC=8
//S2 EXEC PGM=P2
...
// ENDIF

This JCL is generated via FTINCL statements from a REXX/ISPF proc and has a
(quite large) variable number of steps after the step S2. The idea is to
avoid to have to generate very complex IF/THEN/ENDIF construction for the
steps that follow S2 (for example by qualifying each test of RC with the
step name...)

Any idea?

Many thanks in advance
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL Question about the RC system Variable

- **From:** Kerry Ellis <Kerry.Ellis@msdw.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,bit.listserv.tsorexx
- **Message-ID:** `<38E4D30C.72E2FDB5@msdw.com>`
- **References:** `<8c1rst$dnl$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```
There are two ways to do this:

#1.    Write a program -- presumably authorized or sets itself
        that way -- that does "nonstandard" stuff to update con-
        trol blocks in SWA; this approach is highly problematic,
        does not lend itself to maintenance, and might require
        adjustments with various new releases of the operating
        system.

#2.    Front-end the program with a harness; i.e., a program
        that CALLs, LINKs, or ATTACHes program P1 (al-
        most any "main" program in OS/MVS can be used as
        a "subroutine") and have the harness test the contents
        of R15 (or RETURN-CODE in COBOL, etc.) and re-
        set it accordingly, as per your requirements.

Kerry

[P.S. The stuff expressed herein does not necessarily reflect
the views of my employer or client.]

Denis wrote:

> (I know that this question is off-topic but I do not know in which newsgroup
> to post-it)
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Many thanks in advance
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL Question about the RC system Variable

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,bit.listserv.tsorexx
- **Message-ID:** `<38E4A8B5.4CC1EBC@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8c1rst$dnl$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```

I assume that you look this up and maybe even tried it and it did not
work.  Ok here are a couple of suggestions:

1.  The RC variable can be associated to a step.  Might become painful
quickly though.

2.  Break the JCL up into two jobs.  The first does a iebgener (copy)
of the second JCL at the last step to the internal reader submitting
the job:
  //sysut2 dd sysout=(*,intrdr)

3.  WRite a simple program that simply calls the program in the
parameter, interrogates the return and changes an 8 to something
useful:

	procedure division using called-program.

	call called-program.
	
	if return-code = 8
	    move 1 to return-code
        end-if
	stop-run

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

```
//SYSIN        DD    *
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL Question about the RC system Variable

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,bit.listserv.tsorexx
- **Message-ID:** `<8c28kc$2a5k$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8c1rst$dnl$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```

You can't set the RC in JCL with a JCL statement, but you COULD exec a COBOL
pgm that set the value of RETURN-CODE for the step the pgm was executed in.
The value of the RC in your first step would remain that value for the
during of the job.  In other words, replace // SET RC=4 by // EXEC
PGM=SETRC4 then test the result of the step executing SETRC4.  Each step
returns it's own RC, so in ob containing 4 steps, there are 4 different RCs
you can test.

Denis <grossnou@antispam.skynet.be> wrote in message
news:8c1rst$dnl$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com...
> (I know that this question is off-topic but I do not know in which
newsgroup
> to post-it)
> I have a JCL question: Is it possible to reset the RC system variable in a
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> //   SET RC=4                 <--- does not work the idea is to "reset"
the
> RC variable to 4
> // ENDIF                          <--- end of the "does not work"
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>SNIP<
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: JCL Question about the RC system Variable

- **From:** Thomas David Rivers <rivers@dignus.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,bit.listserv.tsorexx
- **Message-ID:** `<38E4B15C.B33B23C6@dignus.com>`
- **References:** `<8c1rst$dnl$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com> <8c28kc$2a5k$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Terry Heinze wrote:

> You can't set the RC in JCL with a JCL statement, but you COULD exec a COBOL
> pgm that set the value of RETURN-CODE for the step the pgm was executed in.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> you can test.

Well - a COBOL program may be a little overboard; just write a quick
ASM program that puts 4 in R15 and does a BALR 14,15.   That will
set the 4 as the RC.

Something like:

       SET4RC  CSECT
               L    15,=F'4'
               BALR 14,15
               LTORG
               END  SET4RC

Assemble this, link it and there you go.

A fancier version might, perhaps, look at the incoming PARMS
value (off of R1) and simply set the return code to whatever
that specified, etc...

        - Dave Rivers -
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL Question about the RC system Variable

- **From:** Warren Porter <wb999port@bellsouth12.net>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,bit.listserv.tsorexx
- **Message-ID:** `<38E4BCB4.26640B6E@bellsouth12.net>`
- **References:** `<8c1rst$dnl$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com> <8c28kc$2a5k$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <38E4B15C.B33B23C6@dignus.com>`

```
Thomas David Rivers wrote:

> Terry Heinze wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>                END  SET4RC

I think you are at the wrong end of the program for that instruction.  Try
                  LA 15,4
                  BR 14
after the registers have been restored.  The BALR would load the address of the
next instruction in Reg 14 and branch to the address in Reg 15, but the system
wouldn't let you branch to low core.
```

```
Thanx for your answers, but this does not answer my question
The solutions you propose (COBOL, asm or IDCAMS) allows me to write a
program for which the RC of the execution step will be 4.

This is not what I want to achieve: What I want to do is, in the middle of a
JCL, reset the internal value of RC stored somewhere by JES
I want to say to JES is: From that execution point in the JCL, forget the
maximum return code of all the previously executed steps (i.e. the global RC
variable)  and reset it to 4 (or force the value of the global RC indicator
to 4)
By "global RC indicator", I mean the RC variable handled by JES not related
to any particular step, present in any JCL and that you can test in IF
statements without the step qualifier.The solution you give allows me to
force the value of the step.RC value for a particular step, not the "global"
one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL Question about the RC system Variable

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** vrenios@enuxsa.eas.asu.edu (Alex Vrenios)
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,bit.listserv.tsorexx,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<8c2fs1$d3k$1@eninet.eas.asu.edu>`
- **References:** `<8c1rst$dnl$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com> <8c28kc$2a5k$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <38E4B15C.B33B23C6@dignus.com> <8c2drm$ir1$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```
In article <8c2drm$ir1$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>,
Denis <grosnou@antispam.skynet.be> wrote:
>Thanx for your answers, but this does not answer my question
>The solutions you propose (COBOL, asm or IDCAMS) allows me to write a
>program for which the RC of the execution step will be 4.
>
IEFSETRC CSECT
         STM   14,12,12(13)
         LR    12,15
         USING IEFSETRC,12
         SR    15,15         ASUME RC 0
         L     1,0(,1)
         LTR   1,1           ANY PARM?
         BNZ   DONE
*
         IC    15,0(,1)      YES: GET PARM BYTE
         N     15,=F'15'     AND SET RC FROM PARM
DONE     L     14,12(,13)
         LM    0,12,20(13)
         BR    14
         END   IEFSETRC

Now you can use

         // EXEC IEFSETRC,PARM='0'
or
         // EXEC IEFSETRC,PARM='4'
or
         // EXEC IEFSETRC,PARM='8'

But I'm a bit rusty at this. Maybe someone can get you 12 and 16 by
making some small change. Hope this helps.
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL Question about the RC system Variable

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,bit.listserv.tsorexx
- **Message-ID:** `<e7yEi8xm$GA.257@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<8c1rst$dnl$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```
Don't know if this helps:

1) You can set the initial RC to 0 by executing IEFBR14.
If you test RC before a program has been executed, it is null.

2) You can nest your conditional JCL. I think 15 levels is the max.

3) There is also an ELSE condition which you can Use

4) You can break your job stream up into several jobs, and use the
internal reader to start them.

5) I don't know of any way to reset the RC for a JOB.

construction
```

<a id="t-814d86f13d"></a>
## Thread: Destroying a COBOL array — 2 msgs

### Re: Destroying a COBOL array

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qe5F4.786$lT3.36102@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<RnBE4.9926$Y4.172076@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <Pine.SOL.3.96.1000330112152.3875B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <8c0lu3$42d$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Hard drives are slow but still faster than not being able to do it at
all.

Consider the alternatives:

Table in memory: With sufficient RAM=Fast, With insufficient
RAM=Impossible
Table on file: With sufficient RAM=Fast (OS buffers entire file), With
insufficient RAM=Slower

In sum, you lose (almost) nothing using a file for only a few records.
You lose the entire job for huge internal tables.



Cheesle <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote in message
news:8c0lu3$42d$1@news.cerf.net...
> "Charles Hammond" <ceh1@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> wrote in message
> news:Pine.SOL.3.96.1000330112152.3875B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu...
> > Why not just describe the flat file with a record key and use i/o,
random
> > or dynamic file access to search the file like a database.
>
> Because hard drives are sooooooooooo slow :-)
>
> > mainframe we often read various files into a temporary file and
sort and
> > search that file and just overwrite that area later.   Tables are
nice,
> > but you have trouble putting a file with 100,000 records in a
table.
>
> No way, using dynamic memory it fits wonderful.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-e5af86af1b"></a>
## Thread: 3-tier — 2 msgs

### Re: 3-tier

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E40A24.5EA4D120@home.com>`
- **References:** `<v1t5esssoi5cim6v4a7290hgmee69i118j@4ax.com>`

```


G Moore wrote:
> 
> someone said in cobol they seperate thw tiers into file handling,
> screen and business logic. i guess my question is, how do you abstract
> file handling? like so (general skeleton)?
> 
(1) Structured :
Can't give you an example, but some 4 (?) months back a couple of people
did state they were doing this in structured - vague - but I think they
were calling individual files as separate programs. Again can't remember
specifics but they got over the problem of file-status because it was
available to both the caller and sub-program (?????). Didn't see any
source, but logically the file accesses would be made either by a flag (
1 = Open as input, 2 = Open as output etc...) coupled with EVALUATES or
getting into the file-program with entry points.

If anybody recalls that thread - perhaps they can spell it out in more
detail.

(2) OO :
If you are interested in an OO approach - see attached zip (posted
here last July).

(3) General :
The really important thing, whether structured or OO (and VITALLY
IMPORTANT for the latter, I believe), is this three-tier approach (1)
Business Logic - what you want the 'program' to do (2) Screen
Handling/Windows - display and get data from a 'dumb terminal' and (3)
DB/flat-file handling - keep the three pieces separate and you can
change one without adversely affecting the other two.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: 3-tier

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E4A67D.E1FA1F70@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<v1t5esssoi5cim6v4a7290hgmee69i118j@4ax.com> <38E40A24.5EA4D120@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> G Moore wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> can change one without adversely affecting the other two.


As Jimmy states, this is a general practice, not specific to any
implementation.

You business logic is where your real knowledge of what you do is. 
With the current shift from Mainframe CICS to windows front end we
have many programs that have all the CICS calls ripped out and
replaced by windows calls.  A long arduous and error prone task.

If instead we move all the common logic between CICS and Windows into
a module then we have a product that can run on two platforms and
produce reliable results.

The back end is another area of protection.  When we move from
mainframe CICS / DB2 we might move to ODBC drivers on Windows.  It
would be great that the database (or file or indexed file) access was
isolated in a common module.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

```
         if         LineCount < 3
                    invoke self "print-heading"
		    (Depending upon the report this may be between
		    3 - 15 'lines' subject to font styles/sizes, so
		    when heading is finished, it in turn -
                    invokes os-PrintFile "get-line-count"
               		returning LineCount)
         End-if

         invoke self "prepare-print-line"
	 (HA ! HA ! says WMK - you could GENERATE here !! )
         invoke self "print-line" :-
         	(move Line-item           to PrintBuffer
         	(move length of Line-Item to PrintBufferLen
         	(invoke os-PrintFile "write-and-newline"
                (	using PrintBuffer by value PrintBufferLen
                (	returning ws-ErrorCode)
                subtract 1 from LineCount


No, I don't 'encapsulate' the Line-Count check - but I think that is a
matter of style. (Stop using those $10 words :-) )

Bill - as I said - PC_PRINT is adequate for my needs. Other N/E users
who either don't use it/like it or use alternatives, primarily Crystal
Reports, I think, (Ken Mullins, Rich Rhode, Bob Hennessy with his POS
and Simon Hart....etc..).

Call me 'Frugal James' if you like - but all the tools I use are already
a part of NetExpress.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Subject Header: VB 5 Help
_______________________________________________________________
If someone could take 10 to 15 minutes to help me with something I am not
sure of how to do, it would be greatly appreciated.  If you can help, please
email me at tdors@yahoo.com.  Thanks!
________________________________________________________________

I'm not kidding.
Oh, it was cross posted to the following newsgroups:

microsoft.public.access.modulesdaovba,
microsoft.public.access.modulesdaovba.ado,
microsoft.public.ae.arabic.vb,
microsoft.public.il.hebrew.vb,
microsoft.public.inetsdk.programming.scripting.vbscript,
microsoft.public.office.developer.vba,
microsoft.publi

This last newsgroup was cut off in my newsreader, so I don't know how far it
went.

The tenor of the messages this post received was about exactly the same as
those seen here recently.

To continue this thread:
___________________________________________________________
Todd,

With this question and the other questions you have asked, it will make
things a whole lot easier for yourself & everyone if you consider things a
bit more carefully from the point of view of the people you are asking to
help you. For instance

1. Which program are you using? If you are using VB, don't cross-post to the
Access groups. If you are writing in English, I don't think the Arabic or
Hebrew groups will greatly appreciate you. Pick just *one* group which seems
to deal in subject matter most closely related to your question.

2. Don't ask for a private reply. These groups are for user-to-user
exchanges, and designed so that others who may have a related question can
see the answer, and so avoid having to post another similar question. If you
want your own private technical support or training program, then you may
have to go and pay for it.

The approach you have taken gives the impression that you cannot be bothered
to read the groups, and are unwilling to participate in an *exchange* of
knowledge. Instead, you have posted to every group whose name contains "vb",
whether or not it has any relevance to your question. This will greatly
reduce the motivation of people who have the answers to provide them to you.

Regards
Jonathan West - Word MVP
MultiLinker - Automated generation of hyperlinks in Word
Conversion to PDF & HTML
http://www.multilinker.com
Word FAQs at http://www.multilinker.com/wordfaq
Please post any follow-up in the newsgroup. I do not reply to Word questions
by email

________________________________________________________________________
Okay, by reading this, it looks like I don't look at the group, doesn't it!
The reason I asked for personal help is that I was going to explain my
problem further and send the code I have along to the person.  If you look
at the groups I sent it to, I took the Arabic and Hebrew groups out.  I
emailed all the newsgroups that had anything to do with visual basic in
English.  I don't think that you are "King of the newsgroups," so don't tell
me what I can and cannot do.  All questions that I put out into the
newsgroups are not similar.  Again, by replying to your email, it really
looks like I'm being bothered by it, doesn't it!  (being said
sarcastically!)  I'm sure that when people reply for help, that they look at
how many newsgroups I have posted the question to and that they decline if
there is more than a certin number.  (also being said sarcastically!)

-Todd D.

______________________________________________________________________
```

<a id="t-45d203071d"></a>
## Thread: Reentrant Cobol from TSO Call - Is it possible? — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Reentrant Cobol from TSO Call - Is it possible?

- **From:** Jeremy Coast <Jeremy.Coast@bt.com>
- **Date:** 2000-03-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38E49C3E.DAE22154@bt.com>`
- **References:** `<38E0E14E.9E4377A0@bt.com> <38E203FA.A5C13342@zip.com.au> <38E32770.45454CBD@bt.com> <38E352AA.8E0E78F3@zip.com.au>`

```
Okay. Since you asked, this is where the whole sordid story comes out!

I was being lazy. I was asked to write a generic subroutine that could
be called by any "COBOL" program. And it can. However, when I originally
came to test it I decided I wanted to play with the test data (two
records) and some standing lookup data and call the program (via clist)
all instream in my JCL, and without having to write another test module
to do the call.

That's when I found that the subroutine was doing the initialisation
every time. So I thought (dangerous that sometimes - thinking I mean) it
was obviously something to do with the environment: "What I need is a
cobol stub to setup a cobol environment". I then trawled through the
TSO/E Programming Services manual to learn how to call a clist from a
cobol program. Hit upon the TSO Services Facility and lifted the sample
program which calls IKJTSOEV to initialise a TSO/E environment (hang on,
didn't I want to create a cobol environment) and then calls TSOLNK
(alias for IKJEFTSR) passing %MYCLIST as a parameter to invoke the clist
which in turn calls my cobol subroutine. Hmmmn!

Well it all worked up to a point. I got my cobol stub to run a test
clist, and I was able to interogate the return-code from that. Myclist
was able to call the cobol subroutine, and I could tell from the journal
that the program ran once ok. However, an abend resulted from the return
of control back to the clist, and the whole house of cards collapsed.

The abend was S30A ERROR DURING FREEMAIN and
Reason-code - 14 - subpool supplied is not for private area storage
or private area storage not in indicated subpool.

I guess that's back to environments again! I have been programming for
about 8 years now (mainly standard batch stuff), but this is at the top
end of my system knowledge and I can't take it any further. So much for
being lazy eh?

The thing is I am pursuing this mainly for my personal benefit, and in
the interests of extending the boundaries of knowledge. I don't really
need it for my work. However, any comments or suggestions would be
gratefully received (such as: "suggest you quit while you're ahead").
Many thanks for the interest shown.

Regards,

Ken Foskey wrote:

> Jeremy Coast wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```
